verses.ajuz: لم أزل في دمامه المعظم المك
أنا ثاو بحمص في كل ضرب
وثنائي من قبل هذا ومن بع

translationEn: I am in the protection of the noble one, secure — the sound of heart, compassionate, concerned with lofty aims. My anxiety has been bound by his resolution to a nation's ambition, making my weight heavy upon the days. With the sharp sword of tongue and decisive judgment — more cutting when drawn than an Indian blade. A glorious man whose care has exceeded bounds, until I imagined it a vision in a dream. I have not faced any horizon of the horizons without finding you before me. Each day you see the gift of Abu Nasr — I have remained in his honored and exalted care.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: protection of the noble one
the compassionate, the tender
lofty concerns, important matters
the sword-sharp tongue
a sharp sword
glorious, noble
horizons, regions
under my protection, in my charge

context: A 14-verse panegyric in al-khafif meter addressed to Sulayman (Abu Nasr), a patron in Syria. The poem uses metaphors of sword-sharpness for the patron's intellect and protection, while Abu Tammam humorously laments being forced to greet tedious acquaintances in Homs in his patron's absence.

titleEn: Day of Separation, You Were Created Immense

verses.sadr: ما زلت بعدك يا أخي في حسرة

translationEn: Day of separation, you were indeed created immense, and you left my body ill — may you not recover! What ails separation that it has torn apart my limbs — it has never ceased to destroy union since ancient times! I have not stopped grieving after you, my brother, in remorse and confusion, until I see you well. Convey my greetings upon you whenever the winds blow and you breathe in their fragrance as a breeze.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: separation, parting
you were created, you are by nature
immense, great
may you become ill
confusion, bewilderment
breeze, gentle wind

context: A brief 4-verse poem in the kamil meter expressing grief at separation from a close companion. The poem's intimacy and personal tone — addressing the day of separation itself as a living creature — reflect Abu Tammam's skill at ikhwaniyyat (friendship poetry) alongside his renowned panegyrics.

titleEn: The Turning of Separation Is Not Long in Stay
translationEn: The turning of separation does not linger long — it stirs up what is not worth stirring. Winds blew for our loved ones that are neither still nor sluggish. The beauty of full-moon nights, eyes of sand-dunes, and gazelles of fertile lowlands. Between anklets and bracelets and arm-rings and earrings. Every full-figured woman draped in a robe of her luxuriant flowing hair. Like the slender young gazelle, its fear drove it to a lactating ewe. The she-camels pastured in my yard at 'Uwayrida among the tamarisk bushes and rough ground. I pressed them across a desert of obscure horizons, torn open on plain and marshy ground.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the turning of separation
one who lingers, stays long
stirs up, unearths
full-figured, plump woman
young gazelle
a graceful female antelope
refuge of the fearful
the distressed, the worried

context: A 28-verse poem in the makhlu' al-basit meter addressed to Abu al-Mughith (Musa). After an elaborate nasib with desert journey imagery, Abu Tammam praises the patron's inexhaustible generosity, likening him to a deadly serpent for enemies but a refuge for the distressed.

titleEn: Do You Claim the Encampment Is Not Orphaned

verses.ajuz: والدمع في دمن عفت لا يسجم !

verses.sadr: أين التي كانت إذا شاءت جرى
مقسومة في الحسن بل هي غاية

verses.ajuz: في الخلق فهو مع المنون محكم

verses.sadr: إن كان وصلك آض وهو محرم
وفتى إذا ظلم الزمان فما يرى
لولا ابن حسان المرجى لم يكن
لا يحسب الإقلال عدما بل يرى
يحتل في سعد بن ضبة في ذرا
لو كان في الدنيا قبيل آخر
ممن إذا ما الشعر صافح سمعه

translationEn: Do you claim the encampment is not orphaned and that tears at the effaced traces do not flow? O season of pleasures, separation has devastated you after me — your ruins are now a season of longing! I saw you once clothed in full-figured beauties; today you are forbidden their presence. The vicissitudes cast a glance at your radiance with a gaze I always feared would not leave it unscathed. Where is she who, when she wished, would cause a flow of tears mixed with blood from my eyelid? White she was, walking in darkness, so that darkness would be clothed in light, then walking in light so it would be darkened. The bold man finds his doom delectable in her, as you see him eager for death and marked for battle.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: orphaned, desolate
the encampment, the dwelling
flows freely (tears)
full-bosomed beauties, young women
traces of an encampment
touched with color, rosy-cheeked
son of Hassan (patron's name)
the one enslaved by love

context: A 29-verse panegyric in the kamil meter addressed to Muhammad ibn Hassan of the Raqqah region. After an amatory nasib describing the lost beauty and the grief of separation, Abu Tammam praises his patron as one who rescues poets from poverty through his magnanimity.

titleEn: This Is the Letter of a Youth Whose Ambitions Are High
translationEn: This is the letter of a youth whose ambitions drove his hope to your door. Time has shackled the hands of his resolution, and his past standing has tumbled him from a high precipice. His relatives have abandoned him to his fate, and his destitution has hidden him from his peers. A pen has conveyed his secret to you — if it could understand, its own nib would have wept.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ambitions, high aspirations
resolution, firm intention
his relatives abandoned him, left him to fate
his destitution, poverty
peers, equals
pen, reed pen

context: A brief 4-verse intermediary poem in the kamil meter — likely written on behalf of a petitioner seeking patronage. Abu Tammam's skill in composing such epistolary poems made him valuable to those seeking to approach powerful patrons.

titleEn: Abu Sa'id, My Praise Is Not to Be Doubted

verses.sadr: لئن جحدتك ما أوليت من حسن
verses.ajuz: إن لفي اللؤم أولى منك في الكرم
تبسم الصبح في داج من الظلم

verses.sadr: كذا أخوك الندى لو أنه بشر
verses.ajuz: لم يلف طرفة عين غير مبتسم
حقنت لي ماء وجهي أو حقنت دمي

translationEn: Abu Sa'id, my description is not to be doubted in praise, nor is my gratitude to be cut short. If I denied you the good you have bestowed, I would be more rightly placed among the ignoble than you among the noble. I forget your smile while colors are dim — the smile of dawn in the thick of darkness. Thus is your brother generosity: were it a person, it would never be found not smiling for even a moment. You restored the sheen of my face in its page, as the polishing restores a sharp, gleaming sword with water. I do not care — and the best of words is the most truthful — whether you preserved my honor or my very blood.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my description, my praise
cut short, interrupted
ignoble character, baseness
colors (here: of dawn)
polishing, burnishing
sharp sword
sharp-edged, cutting
honor (lit. water of the face)

context: A 6-verse poem in al-basit meter expressing Abu Tammam's sincere gratitude to a patron named Abu Sa'id who restored his honor. The poem unusually stresses the truth of the poet's praise rather than its artifice — a personal touch that distinguishes it from conventional panegyric.

titleEn: Tell the Prince You Have Bestowed Favors upon Me

verses.sadr: يا مانحي الجاه إذ ضن الجواد به
لم يلبس الله نوحا فضل نعمته
verses.ajuz: إلا لما بثه من شكره نوح

verses.sadr: لم يغلق الله باب العرف عن أحد
لن يعدم المجد من كانت أوائله

verses.ajuz: من كل جارحة في جسمه روح

translationEn: Tell the prince: you have bestowed great favors on me — my praise of them will last as long as the wind blows! O you who granted me prestige when the generous withheld it — my gratitude to you shall be gifted to all ears as long as I live. God did not clothe Noah in the gift of His grace except that what Noah spread of gratitude was worthy of it. The world blamed his generosity toward him, and he spends morning and evening only to be praised. For every affair, when views narrow upon it, there is an open vista from his counsel on the day of argument.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you bestowed on me, adorned me with
praise, eulogy
prestige, rank, influence
Noah (the prophet)
his generosity, liberality
praised, lauded
the one who kindles hearts
lamps, lights

context: A 9-verse poem in al-basit meter praising an unnamed prince for granting Abu Tammam social prestige. The poet invokes the Prophet Noah as an archetype of gratitude, suggesting that the prince's generosity, like divine grace, demands perpetual acknowledgment.

titleEn: When Has My Hearing Been a Prize for Blamers

verses.sadr: إذا المرء أبقى بين رأييه ثلمة
فإني ما حوزفت في طلب العلى
ومالي من ذنب إلى الرزق خلته
لعمر النوى لا زلت بعد محمد
إذا سار فيه الظن كان بكل ما

translationEn: When has my hearing been a prize for blamers, and how have the reproachers bent my resolutions? If a man leaves a gap between his two opinions that blame can fill, he is no wise decision-maker. I shall tread upon the army of the garrison with an army of humiliation, erasing those landmarks! For I have not been circumvented in seeking high honor — it is you who have been circumvented in noble deeds. Wait — let the matter settle in its proper place, for glory is not asleep regarding what you do. My only fault against fortune — or so I imagined — was my hope in you for great undertakings. In the eyes of high ambition, you dwell between a destroyer of its highest pillars and one who resembles such a destroyer.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: stolen opportunity, prize seized stealthily
blaming women, reproachers
resolutions, determinations
wise, resolute, decisive
the army, the garrison
landmarks, marks of distinction
you were circumvented, deprived of your share
flowing freely (tears)

context: An 11-verse poem in al-tawil meter expressing Abu Tammam's self-defense (fakhr) against patrons who failed him. This poem is unusually direct in criticizing unnamed patrons for their failure to properly reward the poet, while celebrating his own poetic superiority.

titleEn: This Covenant, Were You to Know, Is a Reproach

verses.ajuz: أن تناما عن ليلتي أو تنيما

verses.sadr: كنت أرعى البدور حتى إذا ما
لن ينال العلى خصوصا من الفت
verses.ajuz: يان من لم يكن نداه عموما

verses.sadr: لا رزيناه ما ألذ إذا هز
وجه العيس وهي عيس إلى الله
في طريق قد كان قبل شراكا
حرم الدين زاره بعد أن لم
فاض فيض الآتي حتى غدا المو
فعلمنا أن ليس إلا بشق الن
أجدر الناس أن يرى وهو مغبو
فتح الله في اللواء لك الخا
نعمة الله فيك لا أسأل الل
verses.ajuz: ه إليها نعمى سوى أن تدوما

verses.sadr: ولو أني فعلت كنت كمن يس

translationEn: This covenant, were you to know, would be a reproach — that you two should sleep through my night or lull it to sleep. I used to watch over the full moons, but when they parted from me I spent my evenings watching the stars. We passed by the house when it was empty and wept over its ruins and faded traces. We asked its encampments but turned away with sickness — we asked no wise man. The meadow of youth has become dried stubble and its fresh breeze turned to simoon. A spark at the parting lit within my very breast an orphaned grief deep in my core.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a covenant, a bond
blameworthy, reproachful
full moons
the stars
ruins, traces
faded traces, marks
dried stubble, withered vegetation
the core, the very heart

context: A 48-verse poem in al-khafif meter combining a personal nasib with praise of Abu Sa'id (a major Abbasid-era patron). The poem recounts a pilgrimage journey in vivid detail, praising the patron's battle victories against the Byzantines and celebrating his piety.

titleAr: أهد الدموع إلى دار وما صحها
titleEn: Guide the Tears to a Dwelling Though It Cannot Be Cured

verses.sadr: أهد الدموع إلى دار وما صحها
دار أجل الهوى عن أن ألم بها
verses.ajuz: في الركب إلا وعيني من منائحها

verses.sadr: ما للفيافي وتلك العيس قد خزمت
فتل إذا ابتكر الغادي على أمل
تصغي إلى الحدو إصغاء القيان إلى

verses.ajuz: لم يرتع الذم ، يوما ، في طوائحها
لم ينزل الشيب في مثنى مسائحها

verses.sadr: قوم هم أمنوا قبل الحمام بها
كانوا الجبال لها قبل الجبال وهم
verses.ajuz: سالوا ولم يك سيل في أباطحها

verses.sadr: لا تفت تزجي فتي العيس ساهمة
ذر تدرإ وإباء في الأمور وهل
لله درك في الخود التي طمحت
verses.ajuz: ما كان أرقاك يا هذا لطامحها
في الغاب والنجم أدنى من مناكحها

verses.sadr: لو أن غير أبي الأشبال صافحها
نصلان قد أثبتا في قلب شائنها

translationEn: Guide the tears to a dwelling though it cannot be healed — the abodes have a share in the flowing tears. Time unleashed every calamity upon it and a parting that darkens the world for the distant one. I swear truthfully: its beauty has diminished through those beauties who departed. Their departure is upon a heart that cannot settle, while my tears do not cease to accompany them. A house too noble for love — I never passed it in the caravan without my eyes becoming its fountain. When I described its abandonment to my soul, it lunged forth, the buried longing in the farthest reaches of my breast. She who, if she were to meet Malik ibn Abi al-Samt, would find the foundations of Mecca and the world in its virgin youth.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: guide, send as a gift
its flowing streams (of tears)
its distant one, the one far from it
its beauties, lovely ones
the one who does not leave it
its fountains, sources
passionate love, grief
the limbs, the vital organs

context: A 41-verse panegyric in al-basit meter with an elaborate atlal nasib leading to praise of a Hashimite patron. Abu Tammam praises the patron's Meccan lineage, connecting him to the sacred foundations of the Ka'ba and celebrating two sons who are described as pillars of the Hashimite family.

titleEn: May a Homeland Draw Them Near, Perhaps the Days Will Relent

verses.sadr: لهم منزل قد كان بالبيض كالمها

verses.ajuz: وقد كان مما يرجع الطرف مكرما

verses.sadr: ومن وشي خد لم ينمنم فرنده
إلى حائط الثغر الذي يورد القنا

verses.ajuz: حدا هجمات المال من كان مصرما
وكان زمانا في عدي بن أخزما
وإن رتعوا في ظلمه كان أظلما

verses.sadr: لقد أصبح الثغران في الدين بعدما
لئن كان أمسى في عقرقس أجدعا
verses.ajuz: لمن قبل ما أمسى بميمذ أخرما
قناك لما قد ضيع الشيب محكما

verses.sadr: بسافر حر الوجه لو رام سوءة
كيوسف لما أن رأى أمر ربه
verses.ajuz: وقد هم أن يعروري الذنب أحجما

verses.sadr: وقد قال إما أن أغادر بعدها
هو افترع الفتح الذي سار معرقا

verses.ajuz: بأخرى وخير النصر ما كان ملحما

verses.sadr: هما طرفا الدهر الذي كان عهدنا
رأى الروم صبحا أنها هي إذ رأوا
فلو صح قول الجعفرية في الذي
به سبتوا في السبت بالبيض والقنا
verses.ajuz: سباتا ثووا منه إلى الحشر نوما

verses.sadr: فلو لم يقصر بالعروبة لم يزل

verses.ajuz: له ابن كيوم السبت إلا تبسما

verses.sadr: ولم يبق في أرض البقلار طائر
verses.ajuz: ولا سبع إلا وقد بات مولما

verses.sadr: ولا رفعوا في ذلك اليوم إثلبا
verses.ajuz: ولا حجرا إلا رأوا تحته دما

verses.sadr: رموا بابن حرب سل فيهم سيوفه

verses.ajuz: ولم يقس منه القلب إلا ليرحما

verses.sadr: إذا أجرموا قنا القنا من دمائهم
verses.ajuz: وإن لم يجد جرما عليهم تجرما

verses.sadr: هو الليث ليث الغاب بأسا ونجدة
verses.ajuz: وإن كان أحيا منه وجها وأكرما

verses.sadr: جدير إذا ما الخطب طال فلم تنل
كريم إذا زرناه لم يقتصر بنا

verses.ajuz: فكم بك بعد العدم أغنيت معدما

translationEn: May a homeland draw them near and perhaps the days will relent and show leniency to them — for sometimes. They have an encampment that was inhabited by beauties like oryx, eloquent of places, but now it has become mute. It returned the eyes of onlookers in humiliation, though it had been what made the gaze return with honor. It exchanged its visitor for a submissive gazelle draped in the robe of beauty as a peaceable phantom. The poet addresses Muhammad ibn Humayd al-Tusi, the Abbasid commander, celebrating his victories over the Byzantine forces and the Khurramites under Babak, one of the most important battles of the Abbasid era.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: homeland, native land
draws near, approaches
the dwelling places, abodes
mute, speechless (of ruins)
mature war-horses
the frontier, border region
a battle site against the Byzantines
a type of sharp sword

context: A 60-verse poem in al-tawil meter, one of Abu Tammam's most celebrated battle odes (hamasiyyat), praising the Abbasid commander Muhammad ibn Humayd al-Tusi. The poem commemorates the defeat of the Byzantine forces and the Khurramite rebellion, demonstrating Abu Tammam's mastery of martial description.

titleAr: سقى الله من اهوى على بعد نائه
titleEn: May God Water the One I Love Despite Distance

verses.sadr: سقى الله من اهوى على بعد نائه
أبى الله الا أن كلفت بحبه

verses.ajuz: وقد غص منها كل جفن بمائه

verses.sadr: فإن مت من وجد به وصبابة
verses.ajuz: فكم من محب مات قبلي بدائه

translationEn: May God water the one I love despite his distance, his turning away from me, and his long estrangement! God has willed that I be afflicted with love for him, and so I have come to be content with His decree. I have set apart my eyes for tears so that they have become choked, every lid brimming with their water. If I die of longing and passionate love for him — how many a lover has died before me of his own malady!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may God water/shower blessings upon
his distance, his being far away
turning away, aversion
estrangement, unkindness
I was afflicted with, burdened by love
His (God's) decree
longing, passionate grief
ardent love, passion

context: A 4-verse ghazal in al-tawil meter expressing the poet's unrequited love and acceptance of his passion as divinely decreed fate. This poem exemplifies the 'Udhriyya strand within Abbasid love poetry — the concept of love as an affliction one must endure with patience and spiritual resignation.

titleEn: The Exile of Separation Has Become Fortunate Through Su'ad

verses.sadr: شاب رأسي ، وما رأيت مشيب الرأ
verses.ajuz: س إلا من فضل شيب الفؤاد

verses.sadr: نال رأسي من ثغرة الهم مالم
يا أبا عبد الله أوريت زندا
أنت جبت الظلام عن سبل الآ
verses.ajuz: ل إذ ضل كل هاد وحاد
ف وفي القلب من ضياء البلاد

verses.sadr: كان في الأجفلى وفي النقرى عر
ومن الحظ في العلى خضرة المعرو
غير أن الرب إلى سبل الأنوا
كل شيء غث إذا عاد وال
verses.ajuz: معروف غث ما كان غير معاد
ناء في قلب كل قار وباد

translationEn: The exile of separation has become fortunate through Su'ad — she is easily accessible whether going to Tihama or the highlands. She parted from us while the eyes shed rains of tears, morning after morning upon the cheeks. Every day they pour out fresh tears, milked from clouds of old-seasoned longing. Falling upon the cheeks, the heat of it falling upon hearts and livers. On the camels are restrained beauties who smile to reveal a cool, glistening smile. The thorns of the mimosa used to be beautiful but now the thorns of separation's parting are more painful. My head has gone white — and I see gray in the head only as excess of gray in the heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: became fortunate, was blessed
exile, estrangement
separation, being far apart
going toward Tihama (lowlands)
going toward Najd (highlands)
restrained, modest beauties
cool, glistening (of teeth)
the heart, the inner self

context: A 43-verse panegyric in al-khafif meter addressed to Abu 'Abd Allah (a senior Abbasid official). The poem opens with a personal nasib lamenting the departure of Su'ad before Abu Tammam addresses his patron, celebrating how the patron's patronage revived the poet's inspiration after a period of poetic dryness.

titleAr: قل للأمير أبي سعيد ذي الندى
titleEn: Tell the Prince Abu Sa'id, Lord of Generosity

verses.sadr: قل للأمير أبي سعيد ذي الندى
فمن أين أرهب أن يراني راجلا
أحمل هداك الله رجلي يا بن من

translationEn: Tell the prince Abu Sa'id, lord of generosity and glory — may God increase his honor! O you who gave horses and their saddles as gifts, and the thoroughbred with its saddle and bridle! The one who carries people upon swift horses and imitates the lion in his boldness! And he who gives as a gift the sword Samsamah that flows with the lethal venom of death along its blade. You are one who rivals the wind in his largesse and one who scorns criticism alongside generosity. From where should I fear being seen dismounted when my only hope is in his days?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: swift, soft-stepping camels
a thoroughbred horse (named after Aywaj)
swift, lean horses
the fierce lion
a famous sword (Samsamah)
lethal, instantly deadly
its blade, its steel
censure, blame

context: A 10-verse poem in the kamil meter praising Abu Sa'id for his lavish gift-giving, specifically horses, swords, and gold. Abu Tammam humorously confesses that given such generosity, he cannot fear being seen unmounted — a witty defense of accepting lavish gifts from patrons.

titleEn: I Have Exhausted in Your Presence the Meanings of Complaint

verses.sadr: فلو أن ما أشكو إلى بشر

translationEn: I have exhausted in your presence the meanings of complaint and the descriptions of what I suffer of affliction. I have turned over the horizons of speech and have not seen myself neglecting any meaning. I count what I do not complain of as a loss and return to it again and again. If my complaints went to a human being it would relieve me from the humiliation of complaining. But I complain to a stone that blunts the pickaxes — or to something even harder. A gazelle whose weeping and laughter both illuminate and darken our world.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I exhausted, I depleted
the meanings/themes of complaint
affliction, trial
horizons of speech
a loss, a deprivation
a human being
stone
pickaxes, mattocks

context: A 6-verse poem in the kamil meter expressing the poet's frustrated longing for an unresponsive beloved. The conceit of complaining to one harder than stone is a classic ghazal motif, here rendered with Abu Tammam's characteristic wit — the poem ends with the paradox of a beloved who simultaneously illuminates and darkens the world.

titleEn: May Clouds Water the Memory of the Sanctuary

verses.sadr: وإن يك من بني أدد جناحي

translationEn: May clouds water the memory of the sanctuary with generous rains, its present residents and those far away! I drew the eye's well dry when I saw tears as the best of provisions. How beautiful were the ruins — yet how time walked toward them in the forms of distance! When the incidents of fate flew in their heights as settled birds, they were the song of abundance. Race horses and drinkers of winter rain, companions of young men, and pots boiling with meat. Eyes anointed with the magic of beauty and bodies perfumed with amber wood. Through Zuhr and al-Hidhaq and the family of Burd my fire-striker lit a spark in every good deed. I rose with their wings to spread shade over those with need, and behind me flows an abundant river.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may it be watered, rained upon
rains following rains
the eye's well (wellspring of tears)
the sanctuary, the protected place
the sought-after song, abundant song
winter rain, overcast rain
amber, a yellow perfume
the Iyad tribe; supports, pillars

context: A 49-verse poem in al-wafir meter addressed to Ahmad ibn Abi Du'ad, the chief qadi (judge) of the Abbasid caliphate. After a conventional nostalgia prelude, Abu Tammam praises his patron for resolving the poet's troubles with members of the Mudar tribe — a poem rich in political and personal allusions.

titleEn: O Abu Said, Blessings Meet at Your Hands

verses.sadr: لا زال جودك يخشى البخل صولته
أشرفت منك على بحر الغنى ويدي
فسوف يثبت ركن المدح فيك أخ
verses.ajuz: لولا رجاؤك لم يثبت له قدم
شككت إذ قمت دوني أنك الحرم

translationEn: O Abu Said, all blessings converge in you — you are a mountain of refuge and firm support for us. May your generosity forever make miserliness tremble at its might, and may your noble nature ever water its garden with abundant rain. I look out from you upon the sea of wealth while poverty and destitution wander through my equal plane. The pillar of praise in you shall stand firm — but for hope in you, no foot would hold its ground. I have taken sanctuary beside you, fearing calamity, and I have no doubt, now that you stand between me and harm, that you are my sacred refuge.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: converged, gathered together
great mountain, towering peak
one who saves, deliverer
refuge, one who is clung to for protection
might, fierce attack, power
persistent rains, steady gentle rains
I entered into sacred sanctuary, I took refuge

context: A short panegyric (madih) addressed to Abu Said, praising him as a source of safety and generosity. Abu Tammam uses the imagery of mountains, seas, and sacred sanctuary to elevate his patron's stature.

titleEn: Will My Lord Strip Me of Wealth

verses.ajuz: له رب سوى ابن أبي داود !

translationEn: Will my Lord strip me of wealth's abundance — that I should seek it from a lifeless hand? I would then claim that generosity has found itself a lord other than the son of Abu Dawud!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: strips me of, robs me of
abundance of wealth, affluence
lifeless stone, inanimate object
I claimed, I alleged
generosity, munificence

context: A brief but pointed panegyric in which Abu Tammam declares that true generosity belongs solely to Ibn Abi Dawud, implying that to seek provision from anyone else would be absurd.

titleEn: I Scattered Tears Like Unstrung Pearls

verses.ajuz: مهراقة من ماء وجهي أو دمي

verses.sadr: إن شئت أن يسود ظنك كله

verses.ajuz: وأخو الكرى لو لم ينم لم يحلم
لم يبتدأ عرف إذا لم يتمم

verses.sadr: في قله كثر السماك وإن غدا
وإذا انتمى في قلة من سؤدد
ما ضر أروع يرتقي في همة

verses.ajuz: لا يرغم الأزمات ما لم يرغم

verses.sadr: لا يستطال على الخطوب ولا ترى
حلت محل البكر من معطى وقد
إن الثناء يسير عرضا في الورى
أعطيت ما لم تعطه ولو انقضى
لو قلت حصل بعضها أو كلها
زهراء أحلى في الفؤاد من المنى
verses.ajuz: وألذ من ريق الأحبة في الفم

translationEn: I scattered the unique gems of tears unstrung — for tears carry some of the weight of the passion-stricken. Her cheeks mingled tears with blood, like the embroidered hem of a patterned robe. She was consumed with longing; everything dimmed before her, yet from her everything dark was lit. Her tears at parting flowed as if poured from the water of my face or my very blood. The limbs of one whom separation has made taste the bitterness of parting grow weak — bitter as colocynth. Departure is a kind of death, save that its people are spared two things: the grave and the mourning tent. If you want your entire judgment to be sound, direct it toward this greatest darkness. A friend is not one who lends you a smiling surface while his interior scowls. Let the young men convey my message to Malik — that when they are broken, I collapse. Let the days know that I have been tried by Abu al-Husayn Muhammad ibn al-Haytham — a matchless man whose right hand deals out gifts morning and evening in twin abundance. I warned one deceived by his clemency: the sleeper who never slept would never dream. Do not let his forbearance bind you to him, for a valley may carry you away even when not brimming. Caravans drove their camels toward al-Jazira from highlands and lowlands alike. It was as if, but for the sacred rites, her courts were a place of pilgrimage or a festival ground. He seemed, in their praise, to be in a garden, and they, in his bounty, at a place of sharing. Devoted to the lord of glory, he claims no act of kindness begun unless it is completed. I strung for him the beads of eulogy — noble deeds that cast their spell upon the knotted tongue of the tongue-tied. In his thrift, the Simak star grows abundant, and even when his hand pours in torrents it surpasses the rainy Pleiades. He served nobility and nobility served him — for nobility is not served by people unless they serve it. Whenever he climbs one peak of sovereignty, another calls to him: advance, you have arrived. It harms no bold man rising in a lofty ambition if he climbs without a ladder. He refuses to expose your honor, guarding everything around it like a warrior defender. Inherited wealth, however precious, overcomes no calamity unless it overcomes. One cannot be extended over misfortunes, nor is a noble quality half-complete if it has not suffered oppression. A favor of yours, once given, comes as a virgin gift even to one who has received before, for you have sent it as a bride's procession to the once-married. Let love of generosity grow in you from the alchemy of glory — grow rich and gain. Praise spreads wide among people, yet its true station in height surpasses the stars. When gifts grow dark, you clothe them with a radiance like the lightning flash of a sharp blade. You gave what none other gave, and were your gracious reception to cease, you would withhold what others withhold not. I cut fine qualities from you as if their strips were sliced from the character of the storming cloud. Were I to say that some or all of them had gathered in Hatim, I would be called the dismisser of a debtor. You have grown so famous that your name strikes the mind before its meaning strikes the penniless. These odes set out toward you as strays, taking sanctuary in your bounty before I took my own. They did not rest until they brought you on a swift horse the first-fruits of their youth — the raid before the spoils. You made their very soul their value, and you mastered it so that the odes became assessors of the assessed. Take them — they have always, independently, been polished by one who straightens and refines. They leave the youth's hope behind them and wander under the shadow of hope like a great eagle. Radiant — sweeter to the heart than wishes, more delicious than the saliva of the beloved on the lips.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unique gem, singular pearl
tears, tear-ducts
blood, dark flowing blood
one passionately in love, the passion-stricken
colocynth, extremely bitter plant
Arcturus / Spica, bright stars
alchemy, transformative art

context: A long qasida beginning with a lyrical nasib (amatory prelude) then transitioning into a panegyric of Abu al-Husayn Muhammad ibn al-Haytham. Abu Tammam employs his characteristic metaphysical conceits to praise both his patron's generosity and the power of his own poetry.

titleEn: I Remembered You Until I Nearly Forgot You

verses.ajuz: توقد من نيران ذكرك في قلبي
كأن لم يمثل بي صدودك في القرب

verses.sadr: وهل كان لي في القرب عندك راحة
verses.ajuz: ووصلك سهم البين في الشرق والغرب

verses.sadr: بلى كان لي في الصبر عنك معول

translationEn: I remembered you so intensely that I nearly forgot you in the fires your remembrance lit within my heart. I wept for you when absence set love ablaze — as though your coldness in nearness had never wounded me. Was there ever any rest for me in your nearness? — for your union was the arrow of separation in east and west alike. Yet I had recourse to patience in your absence, and room to breathe — were it not for my excess in love.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: kindled, blazed up, lit
distance, separation, farness
turning away, disdain, estrangement
recourse, reliance, support
room, breadth, escape route
my excess, my overindulgence

context: A brief but paradoxical love lyric in which Abu Tammam reflects that intense remembrance can become a kind of forgetting, and that closeness brought no more peace than distance. A gem of ghazal poetry in four verses.

titleEn: Did You See What Cheeks and Temples Appeared

verses.sadr: لا حزم عند مجرب فيها ولا
لا أفقر الطرب القلاص ولا أرى
يا أحمد بن أبي داود حطتني
إن كنتم عادي ذاك النبع إن
هذا الذي خلف السحاب ومات ذا
ما قاسيا في المجد إلا دون ما
وتمكن ابن أبي سعيد من حجا
ما خالد لي دون أيوب ولا
من بعد أن ظنوا بأن سيكون لي

verses.ajuz: ما كان يعرف طيب عرف العود
في أرض مهرة أو بلاد تزيد

translationEn: Did you see what temples and cheeks appeared before us between the winding sands of Fazroud? Companions heedless of nights, who wove the bonds of love at Yariq and Aqoud — a fair one whom the morning breeze topples playfully, as a supple ben-tree branch is swayed. Wild-natured, she shoots hearts at dawn half-drowsy, and she hunts nothing but her prey. No veteran's prudence avails against her, and no tribal chief is stubborn before her. I retain from their abode only sorrow and the resolve of one who endures. If the favored one waters their ruins with the torrents of lament, I am not among the fortunate. They departed, and I wept a full year after them — then I restrained myself, for that is the judgment of Labid. A burning coal of anguish is more likely to be extinguished by tears only to blaze longer. I do not let gaiety impoverish my camels, nor do I tighten my loads in the company of women's musicians. A longing I have cleared its dross from my drinking-place, and a love whose bark I have stripped from my branch. My year and the camels' year pass between a scorching wasteland flooded and a stony barren track — until each day in the wilderness I leave for the birds a feast from yesterday's banquet. Far from any praiseworthy garden until it halts at Ahmad al-Mahmoud, the camping-ground of the Arabs where one finds security for the frightened and rescue for the distressed. The sons of Ishmael and Hud loose their burdens and sorrows there. Hope alighted as their delegation — and they went forth from his presence as themselves a destination for delegations. He began generosity and renewed it among them — and how many initiate kindness but never renew it! O Ahmad ibn Abi Dawud, you have guarded me in my protection and treated me with your remedy. You granted me a love whose sacred bond you protected from desertion and estrangement. How many an enemy quoted to me: how many a fond one is not cherished in return! Iyad, in all Ma'add, belongs to you — and you are the pillars of its established structure. You are elevated to the peaks of nobility and honor by radiant ancestors of radiant forebears. If you are the very origin of that noble spring in lineage, and the very cleft of that hard boulder — and you share with them what others do not — then you are our partners in generosity apart from them. Ka'b and Hatim divided the plans of glory between them, inherited and earned. This one left clouds behind him, and that one died in glory like an honored ancient warrior. Were the martyr not among them, his tribe would not yield him for a thousand martyrs. He suffered nothing in glory save less than what I suffered in justice and monotheism. Hear the words of a visitor whose views did not stray when the open desert paths bewildered others. He bartered some words from you with his deeds fully and your gracious approval with effort given. He walked as a fugitive of shame from what they alleged — yet no one flees from reverence. You were the spring before him and after him — the moon of the tribes, Khalid ibn Yazid. The rain comes from the flowering cloud of compassion, and the pillar from Shayban, a mountain of iron. Tomorrow will reveal the clearness of my innocence, if only I shook off my tihamat and highlands. This Walid saw the need for verification after they said Yazid ibn al-Muhallab was loyal — and the falsehood long established in his sight crumbled, for the edifice of this slander was never sound. The son of Abu Said was established in discernment, a king grateful to the sons of kings, fulfilled. Khalid is not preferred by me over Ayyub, nor Abd al-Aziz, and I am not inferior to Walid. My soul is your ransom — what calamity has ever befallen you without the key being tossed toward you? He who contrives slander is not the one who joins him, and the one far in tribe is not truly far. When your cloud overshadowed me, those witnesses against me became my witnesses. After they thought that a day would come for me at their hands, like the day of Ubayd — a wish whose devil they never encountered, neither as jinn nor as rebel. They drew the arrow of estrangement feathered with the wings of ingratitude — and it flew astray. When God wills to spread a virtue that was folded away, He provides it with the tongue of the envious. Were it not for fire blazing in what it neighbors, the sweet fragrance of aloeswood would not be known. Were it not for fear of consequences, the envious would forever hold the blessing over the envied. Take these verses, their rhymes perfected — their master ungrateful for no generous gift. Sharp-edged, they fill every ear with wisdom and eloquence, and they flow into every vein — like a wide lance-thrust from the hand of an avenger for his brother, or like a slashing blow that cuts a trench. Like pearls and coral strung with granules on the neck of a tender girl. Like the striped half-cloak with its intricate patterns from the land of Mahra or Tazid. The noble takes good tidings with it and wraps himself in its mantle in a celebrated assembly. Glad tidings of al-Ghani, father of daughters, multiply — tidings of a born cavalier. Like the charms against vipers and serpents that long drew out the venom of grudges and rancors.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: temples, sides of the face
twisted or winding sand-dunes
drowsy, heavy-eyed with languor
one who endures patiently, the long-suffering
intense midday heat, scorching wasteland
my medicine poured from the side of the mouth
slander, fabricated lie

context: A major qasida opening with a ghazal prelude then pivoting to an elaborate panegyric of Ahmad ibn Abi Dawud, the Abbasid chief judge (qadi al-qudat). Abu Tammam defends himself against detractors while celebrating his patron's justice, lineage, and generosity across 56 verses.

titleEn: O Malik, Grief Is But a Dreamer's Dream

verses.ajuz: إلى آدم أم هل تعد ابن سالم

verses.sadr: متى ترع هذا الموت عينا بصيرة
وإن تك مفجوعا بأبيض لم يكن
فمن قبله ما قد أصيب نبينا

verses.ajuz: خفاتا ولا حزنا عدي بن حاتم

verses.sadr: وأي فتى في الناس أحرض من فتى
وهل من حكيم ضيع الصبر بعدما
ولم يحمدوا من عالم غير عامل
verses.ajuz: خلافا ولا من عامل غير عالم

translationEn: O Malik, grief is but the dreams of a dreamer — and however long it lasts, passion does not endure forever. O Malik, excessive longing will leave crookedness and a bend in the lance of noble qualities. Reflect slowly: can you trace your lineage back safely to Adam, or count yourself among the sons of Salim? Whenever you observe death with a discerning eye, you find one who seems just, resembling one who is unjust. If you are struck by the loss of a fair-skinned one upon whose amulet cords no knots were ever tied — a cavalier of Taghlib and highland of Wail, a star of rebuke and an ember of Hashim — his loss grieved the wind so it increased in longing, and caused new grief in the doves' weeping. Before him our Prophet, Abu al-Qasim, the clear light, was struck by the loss of Qasim. Ali spoke words of consolation to al-Ash'ath and feared for him some of those sins. At the losses of the Tarfat on the day of Siffin, Adi ibn Hatim did not die of sorrow or grief. We men were created for patient endurance and for sorrow — and those women were created for weeping and mourning tents. What youth among people is more wretched than one who wades in the ditches of flowing tears? Is there any wise man who wasted patience after the sages declared patience an imperative necessity? They never praised a scholar who did not act, nor one who acted without knowledge. They saw paths of incapacity as crooked severers, and the most severing incapacity in their view was the incapacity of a resolute man. May Rabi'ah ever strike with a gentle serpent behind the serpents! You and your two brother-companions, the two supporters, are brothers created as snuff for defiant noses. Three pillars — and no sovereignty collapses when three supporting pillars stand firm within it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: intense grief, passionate longing
excessive yearning, overpowering love
crookedness, deviation from straight
one struck by sudden bereavement
doves, pigeons (symbol of mourning)
an inescapable blow, an imperative
defiant, those humiliated against their will

context: A consolation elegy (ta'ziya) addressed to someone named Malik who has lost a dear one. Abu Tammam urges patience through Quranic and historical precedents — citing the Prophet's own loss of his son Qasim — while praising the noble lineage of the deceased.

titleEn: Alone in Beauty, Empty of Love

verses.sadr: ولوع بسوء الظن لا يعرف الوفا
verses.ajuz: يبيت على سلم ويغدو على حرب

translationEn: One who stands alone in beauty, empty of love, yet keenly versed in the gates of transgression and reproach — addicted to suspicion, knowing nothing of faithfulness, sleeping in peace but rising to war.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unique, standing alone, singular
feigning guilt on others, accusation
reproach, blame, rebuke
one deeply addicted, intensely fond
faithfulness, loyalty, fulfillment of promise

context: A terse two-verse ghazal sketch of a paradoxical beloved — beautiful yet cold, suspicious yet disarming. Abu Tammam captures in miniature the psychological complexity of the unattainable beloved who brings war where peace was promised.

titleEn: O Ahmad, the Envious Are Multitudes
translationEn: O Ahmad, the envious are multitudes — but the rain-cloud's strike falls where you will it. So do not keep me, who am near, at a distance — for how long I sought you and you were not far, even when you were far away. Incline your ear and hear the finest verses, for they are stars — only these are fortunate stars. And do not let wearing thin diminish them, for the pleasure of a cloak is in its newness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: multitudes, gathered crowds
rain-cloud, clouds bearing rain
incline your ear, listen attentively
the finest of verses, the purest rhymes
wearing out, becoming threadbare
a striped garment, a cloak

context: A brief poem addressed to Ahmad ibn Abi Dawud encouraging him to listen to fresh verses and not let the passage of time dull their effect. Abu Tammam uses the metaphors of rain-clouds, stars, and new cloaks to characterize the vitality of his poetry.

titleAr: يا ربع لو ربعوا على ابن هموم
titleEn: O Abode, If They Had Halted for a Grief-Stricken Man

verses.sadr: يا ربع لو ربعوا على ابن هموم
من كل ريم لو تبدى قطعت
أما الهوى فهو العذاب فإن جرت
جاءتك في معج خوائف في البرى
ما زلت من هذا وذلك لابسا
مثل الب دور تضيء إلا أنها
في ساعة لو أن لقمانا بها

verses.ajuz: ما اهتز إلا اجتث عرش عظيم

verses.sadr: لا يأمل المال النجاة إذا عدا

translationEn: O dwelling, if only they had halted for one overcome with grief, surrendered to the burning of separation and sick at heart — you were known as the home of the finest of our dwellers, and the finest of ruins and traces. In the days when the days still held freshness in you, and time was blameless in its fidelity to you. The gazelles of your familiarity did not exchange their settled ones for the wild gazelles of your wilderness. Every one of them — were it to appear, the glances of its eyes would cut the heart of a wild gazelle. As for love — it is torment itself, and when separation runs through it, every pain is pain upon pain. The firmness of composure commanded by the anguish of restless grief ordered the still tears to flow. By the faded ruins — an oath of one deeply rooted among lovers at heart: my eyes never sought to delay a single hour, for tears became my creditor from the day separation became my opponent. The separation that scattered my ribs has not ceased until my sides were saturated with poisoned desire. Toward Abu al-Husayn's sanctuary the camels rushed with their reins, like an untamed camel bridled. They came to you swift with fearful urgency across the wilderness, familiar with the way-marked track. From every fast she-camel whose skin seemed stitched on its outer surface with sea-turtle hide — she spreads her ribs when pressed like the turning of a washer. I sought you from the lineage of al-Jadil and Shadqam — noble humped she-camels from noble humped stock. They forget the voices of the camel-drivers and their tone, entranced by the echoes and the owl. They arrived at the sea of your bounty, a watering not turned away, and they found your generosity's mother not barren. When they alighted at the pools of your flowing gift, they camped there and drank the drinking of the thirsty. The caliph and the caliph before him found you a peer in counsel and resolution. They found you praiseworthy and never spared any effort for you in deliberation or precedence. You have always been clothed from this one and that in robes of trust and honor. My soul is your ransom — with mountains and their people — in the pitch-dark night of wars. At Dadhuwayh and Khayzaj and their kind, your sword made a covenant that was never contemptible. With the Mus'abian warriors who were like lions of thickets and jinn of dark nights — like full moons that shine, yet helmeted with stars from their white armor. The forsaken one turned away blaming himself, trailing his defeated army. They sought the lesser and the greater affair — and the Imam's sword and the prayer of the oppressed held them back. I implored them by God on the day I met them, while the horses moved beneath a dust-cloud like a dark night. I gave them both your warnings — from a rugged ease, a hard-hearted mercy — until when they bolted, I tore open their tents by God, then by the infallible Eighth. The white swords bared themselves for their skulls, and monotheism bared itself for destruction. I attacked them at dawn with a battle in the east whose thunderbolts split the mountains of Byzantium. I drove them out — nay, a sedition drove them out that stripped them from freshness and ease. They were moved from sweet water and a life of plenty to bitter scalding drink and the fruits of Zaqqum. War learns when it is ignorant of a raid that boils over the firewood of shattered lances — that destinies are obedient to your might and battle is a cup of yours mixed with ruin and wounds. War in a battle rides its own head — a scene where a fool equals a thousand wise men in an hour when even Luqman, the Wise, would cease to be wise. The birds of death settled in their nests and left the bird of reason unable to settle. The sword swears that you are the sword that, whenever it shook, uprooted the throne of a great power. Misfortunes walked backward when they saw my swift trot toward you, steady as clover. I hastened to bid farewell without lingering, when I rushed to you with my submission. Time is most blameworthy when you choke on its blame — unless you illuminate it with a noble man. You stirred for me the wind of hope so my ambitions advanced upon it until they became the hunters of my cares. You awakened the generous to generosity with a speaker whose call for your bounty revealed the treasure of every ancient store. We exist and there is no noble man we can reach until a thousand base men wade through to him. You have established through goodness, from the traces of bounty, traditions that healed us from our blameworthy age. Time marked people with want, but you marked it with generosity manifest on its very snout. You looked upon it with an eye unbleared by stinginess and not squandered on the non-existent. The opening of provision through its glances falls in order upon the deprived. And a hand in which wealth ever falls like its own falling — the way a letter falls in grammatical elision. Wealth cannot hope for salvation when the turns of time drive poverty toward the destitute. Tell misfortunes: Go away from me — for I…

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: burning grief, inward anguish
fast she-camel, swift riding camel
insatiably thirsty camels, those with unquenchable thirst
pitch-dark night, thick darkness
scalding fluid in hell (Quranic term)
bitter tree of hell (Quranic term)
grammatical elision of the last letter in vocatives

context: A long and ambitious qasida addressed to Abu al-Husayn, a military commander praised for his campaigns against the Khurramite rebels. The poem opens with a classical nasib (elegiac prelude), describes the poet's camel journey, then launches into an elaborate battle panegyric.

titleAr: هي فرقة من صاحب لك ماجد
titleEn: It Is Parting from a Noble Companion

verses.sadr: هي فرقة من صاحب لك ماجد
وإذا فقدت أخا ولم تفقد له
أعلي يا بن الجهم إنك دفت لي
لو كنت طرفا كنت غير مدافع

verses.ajuz: يوما ، ولا هي رغبت في زاهد

verses.sadr: ما أدعي لك جانبا من سؤدد

translationEn: It is a parting from a noble companion of yours — and so it melted every frozen tear. Take refuge in the stored reserve of the eye's downpour and its bitterness, for tears dissolve some of the effort of the one who strives. When you lose a brother and shed no tear and have no patience for him, then you have lost nothing at all. Rejoice, O son of al-Jahm, for you have poured for me sky and wine in cool, pure water. May you never go far, and may distance never come to you — for your verdant noble qualities are not far off. Even if a new robe of brotherhood wears thin, we travel morning and night in an age-old fraternity. Or if the waters of union diverge, our water is sweet, descending from a single cloud. Or if lineage parts us, culture unites us — a culture we have established in the place of a father. Were you Taraf, you would be undisputed over al-Ashqar al-Ja'di or al-Dha'idh. Were your age before mine, I would think that Khalid's eloquence was derived from your very speech. Were I ever to believe in the stars, I would claim you are the firstborn of Mercury. You are difficult — but when treated with indulgence you become easy, your reins gentle in the hand of the guide. You clothed over the whiteness of your glory a white favor that lodged in the blackness of the envious. A love that never disdained the ardent one for a day, nor did it desire one who was indifferent. A richness in which it is no wonder that the shepherd goes to its meadow before the scout. I claim for you no aspect of sovereignty without you being its most just witness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: separation, parting, departure
channels of tears, tear-ducts
the new edge, freshly acquired end of a garment
ancient, long-established, inherited
scout sent ahead to find water and pasture
sovereignty, nobility, chieftaincy

context: An elegy for a lost companion addressed to Ibn al-Jahm, blending grief with warm celebration of an enduring friendship. Abu Tammam reflects on the nature of true brotherhood — transcending lineage and bound by culture — and praises his companion's eloquence and noble character.

titleEn: Were It Not for Abu Ya'qub's Resolve

verses.ajuz: سبب العلى لا نحل ثني ذمامه

translationEn: Were it not for Abu Ya'qub's determination in tying the ropes of nobility, we would not loosen the fold of his covenant. A lion when needs press close to his flanks — reluctant in their distaste yet charging forward in boldness. Observe how hopes graze in his thought, his sitting, and his standing. How can one complain of time and its turns when the prince's generosity surrounds you and you live in his days? This is a cloud — you drove its rain-cloud, and upon you, after God, is the flood of its downpour. The beginning of a favor is a soaring glory — but all glory, complete glory, lies in its fulfillment. This crescent moon delights the eyes of people with its beauty — yet nothing matches its beauty when it becomes full.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his determination, his resolution, his tying firm
his covenant, his bond of obligation
their grazing, their free ranging
rain-cloud, the cloud
good deed, kindness, recognized favor
crescent moon

context: A short poem of praise addressed to Abu Ya'qub, using the Quranic metaphor of a crescent moon growing to full beauty as an image for a noble deed brought to completion. Abu Tammam emphasizes that the fulfillment of generosity surpasses its beginning.

titleEn: Finding Comfort in Nothing When You Are Absent
translationEn: I find comfort in nothing when you are absent — save your remembrance, which never absents itself. You are, apart from all companions, my intimate — though you are far, grief in you is near.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: finding comfort, becoming familiar, feeling at ease
absents itself, disappears, becomes hidden
sitting companions, those who sit with one
intimate companion, one who brings comfort
grief, sorrow, sadness

context: A two-verse ghazal fragment of tender longing in which Abu Tammam finds that the beloved's absence is paradoxically filled by the ever-present memory — though the memory itself is a form of grief.

titleEn: Ruins of the Gathering, You Have Faded Honorably

verses.sadr: خضلا ، إذا العبرات لم تبرح لها

verses.ajuz: من كل لهفان زدت في أود ال

verses.sadr: من كل سابغة الشباب إذا بدت
من كل معطية على علل السرى
نسب كأن عليه من شمس الضحى
عريان لا يكبو دليل من عمى
لو لم تكن من نبعة نجدية
زهرا إذا طلعت عل حجب الكلى
ما إن ترى إلا رئيسا مقصدا

verses.ajuz: ما إن ترى الأحساب بيضا وضحا

verses.sadr: وإذا رأيت أبا يزيد في ندى ~

verses.ajuz: من أجل ذلك كانت العرب الألى

translationEn: O ruins of the gathering, you have faded honorably — and your witness to my bereavement is enough. Ruins as though separation has come to seek from you ruins among your standing stones and grudges. You drew near to hearts far in their inward burning, yet you left the course of tears in you far behind. Moist — when tears have never ceased to dwell in them, the restlessness of their troubled resting-place walked as a fugitive. O stations of young men that encompass the unfulfilled — from every grief-stricken one you increased his distress. You reminded us of the king bewildered in love, and of the two Ash'as, and Tarafa, and Labid — they untied in you the knot of the amatory prelude and embroidered from its patterns its robes and its ode. The beauties of the tribe departed from you, haughty, wearing at times distance and at times disdain. From every one full-bloomed in youth, who when she appeared left the chieftain of the two settlements chief-stricken. They were infatuated with smooth-cheeked noble youths, full-bodied and graceful, who grew accustomed to them, pliant and graceful. The sweetest of men in women's eyes is one whose cheeks most resemble theirs. Seek tranquility through movement, and rouse from beneath drowsiness sleepers by means of swift camels — from every one that gives on the pretexts of night-travel, a cheek from which sleep passes the night as a fugitive. She is ridden by a man of unbridled zeal who, when his peers tire, becomes their saddle-cloth and pack-saddle. He made darkness his camel and bade farewell contentedly with ease, adopting the sluggard's slouch. The spring of Rabi'ah's rain, for which the ground was prepared, alighted into the shade of Rabi'ah the extended — her Bakrite, Alawite, Sa'bite, fortified Shaybani stronghold clan — her Dhahali, Murrite, Mutrite clans — the right hand of them all: Khalid ibn Yazid. A lineage as if over it shone from the morning sun its light and from the dawn a pillar of radiance — bare, in which no guide stumbles in blindness, nor does it require witnesses. A nobility over the foremost of ages — for noble qualities are created to be ever-new. Were you not from a Najdi noble ben-tree, high-grown, I would think your very wood was aloeswood. Your father, father of Wa'il's crescent moons, filled the wide earth with forces and numbers. Every man takes refuge in his support — the deaths bore vipers and lions, dusty and a lions' den upon their shoulders, where you imagine their thick-fleeced to be lions' mane-pads. They inherited fatherhood and fortunes and became — combining ancestral glories in eminence with great-grandfathers. The coolness of souls when the stars of Qa'dhab struck down the insolent jinn of battle. Radiant when they rose over the veils of hearts they were ominous, yet when they set they became auspicious stars. You see nothing but a sought leader under the dust and an operator much sought after. They rushed to the double-linked mail and donned within it iron, sharp in the tear-ducts in iron. Moving with a pace that slowly breaks the firm mountains — they approach their slopes as tributaries of a lance-thrust — in a wound, and a most terrible blow that cuts a groove. You see noble qualities white and radiant only where you see destinies darkened. He put on courage — for it was for him since youth a craving and a persistent nature — a tribal might and a dignity's might. When you see Abu Yazid in generosity, feeding those who hope in him the marrow of his wealth — and the spear-tips as a gap and a jugular — you know that from generosity there is a courage that bleeds, and from courage there is a generosity. When you let your gaze wander around his tents, you meet nothing but blessing and envious ones — and noble qualities of ancient lineage, inherited. If the plateau of 'Umayatayn is ancient — and when you alight there it grants you its utmost — and you find after utmost effort therein an increase — from it time blazes while sometimes time is dull for others. Yazid and Mazid and their father remain — and his father is your pillar in glory, firm. They were forebears who saw reputation as righteous progeny and departed counting praise as immortality. Verse and endeavors are forever like a threading: they are scattered jewels — but when you assemble them with poetry they become necklaces and collars. At every battlefield and every gathering, they take from it a covenant and pledges. If the odes are not their escort, no witnessed scene from them satisfies. For that reason the early Arabs used to call this a limited sovereignty, and glory strays among them unless for one for whom the strength of the ode has made fetters for it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins of a former dwelling, vestige of a camp
its standing stones, its cairns
the amatory prelude of an ode
noble generous youths, young chiefs
double-linked chain mail
marrow at the tip of a bone, the softest and richest part
a unique pearl, a singular gem

context: A major qasida of 49 verses opening with classical ruins imagery, moving through a lyrical description of beauties and a camel journey, then arriving at an elaborate panegyric of Khalid ibn Yazid and his clan from Rabi'ah. The poem ends with reflections on the relationship between poetry and immortality.

titleEn: O Sons of Hamid Allah, Your Excellence

verses.sadr: لو كان في يوم بابك لكم
ألا اشكروا الله ذا الجلال فقد

translationEn: O sons of Hamid Allah, your excellence — Asram has preserved for you and made you fortunate. He preserved a father for you who blesses you, who aided you in battle and ennobled you. So take him for that reason as your lord — for his nobility among people has made you chiefs. Had he been with you on the day of Babak, you would not have lost your lord in the encounter. God gave you out of His compassion Asram from among us, to test you thereby. Know that you must thank God, the Majestic, for He has provided for you through the benefaction of Asram. There has always been in your people a king for you who mends your slips and guards you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: your excellence, your superiority
preserved, kept, caused to remain
battle, war, the din of fighting
your lord, your chief, your master
out of His compassion, through His mercy
mends, repairs, covers over slips

context: A short poem addressed to the sons of Hamid Allah following a loss — possibly in the campaigns against Babak the Khurramite — encouraging them to recognize Asram as their new leader and to thank God for this provision.

titleEn: You Extinguished the Fire of Your Love in My Heart

verses.sadr: ما الذنب يا كنز الذنوب معا

verses.ajuz: من لم يقل من هجره حسبي ؟

translationEn: You extinguished the fire of your love from my heart and released me from the knots of passion. You healed the wound of an anguish that had lodged between the pericardium like a wound in the side. The fault — O you who are the treasury of faults combined — in love is not yours, but mine. Farewell then, yet fare not well — for no wonder that one who never said 'enough' of your abandonment. A pearl has never escaped the piercing!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: extinguished, quenched, put out
handle, loop, knot — here: bonds
the pericardium, the membrane surrounding the heart
burning anguish, intense grief
treasury of faults, repository of sins

context: A brief but emotionally dense love poem in which Abu Tammam addresses a beloved who has somehow cooled his passion — paradoxically healing a wound while revealing that the fault in love was always the poet's own. The closing image of the pearl and the drill is a characteristically witty Abu Tammam conceit.

titleEn: What Has Befallen the Sand-Dune of the Sacred Ground

verses.ajuz: ما ناله في الحسان من خرده

verses.sadr: لبسن ظلين ظل أمن من الده

verses.ajuz: إلا أن برد الأكباد في جمده

verses.sadr: كالخوط في القد والغزالة في البه

verses.ajuz: في جيده بل حكاه في جيده

verses.sadr: لم يبق شر الفراق منه سوى

verses.ajuz: هيق إذا ما استحم في نجده
نعم لواء الخميس أبت به يو
خلت عقابا بيضاء في حجرات ال

verses.sadr: قصد لمن لم يطأ على قصده

verses.ajuz: إذ منهم من يعد ساعته الط
ألوى كثير الأسى على سؤدد ال
يا مضغنا خالدا لك الثكل إن
إليك عن سيل عارض خضل الش

verses.sadr: رى منه في رهطه وفي عدده

verses.ajuz: في غلة أوقدت على كبد ال
إيثار شزر القوى يرى جسد ال

translationEn: What has befallen the sand-dune of the sacred preserve in its cluster — what is the matter with its sandy bottom and its barren strip? What has afflicted it, what has overtaken it, what has destroyed it — what has befallen it from the beautiful ones with their trinkets — those who strip a man of his resolve through their enchantment and who breathe upon his knotted love? They wore two shades: the shade of safety from time and the shade of their amusement and tenderness. And they tell of the ease of comfortable living and are asked about its denial. And many a dark-lipped one among them, fresh-toothed — I sipped from her what does not melt from her coolness. She had little of the quenching saliva of the one dissolving in desire — except that the cooling of livers lies in its ice. Like a slender branch in grace, and a gazelle in radiance, and a gazelle's fawn in languid softness. It resembled not the gazelle's neck's grace — rather it resembled it in its necklace, not its neck. The dwelling has overcome me despite my endurance with what has faded from its soft parts and its hard skin. Nothing of the evil of separation remains from it but a fragment of its circular trench and its tent-peg. I will traverse a wide land for a wide-hearted one by a she-camel fleet as an ostrich when it preens on the highland — fronted in the halter, firm-backed, its collar bone shining from its flanks to its withers — full-humped, full-chested, close-jointed, compact, tight-girthed, and toughest. To the ransomed Abu Yazid, who confounds the dense throngs of kings in his very puddle. A shade for suppliants — he loves his visitor as a senior loves the youngest of his children. When they alight at his gate they take the two judgments — word and deed. Rain-seeker from Banu Matar who camped with them, funds until I straightened his crookedness — and their mark clear on their inheritance, so they swagger in their robes while people are in his cold. They do not mourn the slain until the full blood-price comes to them on its lead. A vessel of glory full — blessed in its pure clarity for nobility and in its cream. A plateau of might in whose descents generosity flows and in whose heights pride ascends. Yazid and the two Mazids are in war the two additions — two mountains from his ballista. The army's banner bore him on a day — and an army at the height of noon he ransomed. A white eagle appeared in the chambers of the king — it flew from him and in its perches — and troubled the air though it is its home, and fought the wind though the wind is its provision. A spear-shaft fluttering its two tresses upon a brown-backed frame on the day of battle — its shaft, its flexibility, its straightening — its wide joints in the hands its tracing. Its shade flickers over a king who sees the driving of heroes in its driving. He attained through the bare lance and its wearer a glory before which Orion spends the night short of his reach. He knows there is no easy path to glory for one who has not trodden his path. O joy of the frontier fortress at the caliph — him whom al-Murtada has increased and whose lions' fire blazes in hospitality and battle — from the edge of his swords and from his flint — full in chest and sides of mercy, those sides filled from his envy. He takes rest for work and saves the dry season of time from his fresh morning. For he, were he able in his happiest moment, would cut a joint from his today for his tomorrow — since some of them count their hour of good fortune as provision for them upon eternity. Overwrought in grief over a sovereignty whose life is little in grief over its freshness. The wellspring of reason from his strongholds and patience in calamities from his equipment. O you who nourish a Khalid's grudge — yours is the bereavement if you have kept resentment against him in your heart. Toward you — from a passing torrent, moist of cloudburst, which brings the pigeons from its bank — its floods foaming, its torrents streaming, its hail swelling, its coldness pouring. Can any king rival you in glory? Your breast is worthier of expansiveness than his land. Your brilliant noble qualities have left traces before your clan in his clan and his forces — and a scene that turned the champions in it into suitors for its honey. As if fate's decree with its messengers and death from his vantage were decrees. Inherit from Khalid the unbridled boldness of attack on the day of fury bared — beautiful as the full moon and yet revisited by the lion's scowl of the jungle lion in his anger — like the sword that gives you its fill in your eyes of its glittering pattern at one time and its tarnish at another. By God, I will never forget his defense of the false accusation from the one-eyed slander of the calumniator and from his foolishness. And not forget the Yemeni clans — what there was of his victory and his gathering — the noble of his Anmar and Hamdan, the Shamite, from his Azd and from his Adad. He preferred me when he made himself my support. Every man takes refuge in his support — in a fever kindled upon the liver of the seeker — a fire that exhausts itself upon his liver. The preferring of one's neighbor's rope — he sees the body of a good deed worthier of healing than his own body. I came to him as a visitor, and he went beyond for me — the noble qualities past his wealth to his ancestry. I departed from him with a gift which suppliants reach from his provision. Can hardship be an excuse for a man — Khalid al-Mazidi from his counting?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sand-dune, heap of fine sand
protected sacred ground, forbidden enclosure
dark-lipped, with bluish-dark lips considered beautiful
small pool of water, shallow puddle
suppliants, those who come seeking provision
the watered pattern on a sword blade
the two Mazids, referring to sons named Mazid

context: A very long qasida (59 verses) opening with the classical ruins and love prelude, then describing the poet's camel journey before arriving at an extended panegyric of Abu Yazid (Khalid al-Mazidi) — praising his generosity, his military prowess, and the nobility of his clan.

titleEn: Her Clan and Intimates Blamed Her

verses.sadr: لم تدر كم من ليلة قد خاضها
أو ما رأيت منازل ابنة مالك

verses.ajuz: في ذمها لم يدر كيف يذيمها

translationEn: Her clan and intimates blamed her — base qualities from her that have made plain their vileness. She did not know how many a dark night he waded through, while she slept it away and put others to sleep. She did not recognize a youth whose freshness of face kept calamities and blame at bay. Do not deny my anxiety — for I am ever increasing in my determined readiness for calamities and their evil omens. For before this, the polishing of a sword revealed its effect, and the hearts refined their cares. Mishaps, even when their misery strikes you — it is he who taught you what their ease is. Did you not see how the abodes of ibn Malik's daughter traced for him how sighing traces its traces? Their vessels and ruins, their highlands and lowlands, their present and their past — the winds come blowing and healing, injuring the dwelling without injuring it. It is as though separation has thrown its staff there from a distant remove and will not stir from it. I have revealed for you a hardship through dear ones, brilliant when the murky affairs fill everything dark. Through three, as the wine is known by three equally: its color and its taste and its scent. And by the three of the ripened tree that matched equally: its branches and its fruits and its roots. And by the three of the bucket that is reckoned good for a drawer: its staves and its rope and its leather. And by the three of the pot whose question is difficult: is the last of them the laden one or its foremost? When the thorny camel-halter of the pilgrims settles on them one day — then it has adopted you when you seek it. Abd al-Hamid for her, and al-Fadhl the high in it, and like the sword — Ibrahim her blade. They have surpassed noble qualities of which glory is entirely certain that they are its stars. Were the tongue-tied Baqqila to step forward to praise her, her hard places would be smoothed for him. And were the eloquent Sahban to apply himself to defaming her, he would not know how to find her faults. We came to you safeguarding purposes before which a great event's greatness seems small. With the camels we divided the open land's limbs — and the wilderness is not given its equal share in the division. To us belongs the trust of its rocks and its forms — and to it belongs the fresh fat of its hump and its meats. It took its great she-camels from the plains and their firstlings — so distance excuses them while we blame them. Indifferent to thorny plants that do not burden it — the darkness's rustling, its whistling and its whimpering. A night-traveler whose heads were laden before with the echoes of the wilderness and its owls. A Maharid she-camel whose riders have reached the utmost in leasing her — and her resting-driver has disappeared and her grazing-driver. Her trot strains her, and her amble — her pacing, her shuffle, and her measured step cause her numbness. Weariness has taken hold of her necks and her noses — so her weakness is a debt upon her and her exhaustion. As if her loose-reined one is the broken-in of another, and as if her bridle-shorn one is her bridle-led one.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: her close relations, her intimates
pitch-dark night, extremely dark night
thorny aromatic shrubs
the foremost, the leading part
name of a proverbially tongue-tied fool
name of a proverbially eloquent orator
she-camel of the Mahra breed, swift and prized

context: A 30-verse qasida that opens in a complex narrative mode — a woman blamed by her clan — before transitioning to praise of three noble patrons: Abd al-Hamid, al-Fadhl, and Ibrahim. Abu Tammam employs triple comparisons (the wine, the tree, the bucket, the pot) in a display of his signature intellectual wit.

titleEn: He Who Nurtures Grief in Hearts
translationEn: He nurtures grief in hearts and bolsters resolve in spite of faults. Whatever wonderful sight you wish is in him, and whatever eloquent speech. When he saw the watchfulness of enemies upon a meaning saddened by him — he drew for me from his love a friendship that became a watcher over the watcher.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who rears, nurtures, fosters
supporter, helper, one who aids
wise, sagacious, intelligent
watchfulness, surveillance, the neck (of surveillance)
a watcher, a guardian, a rival

context: A brief lyrical poem of four verses that celebrates a beloved who fosters love even in adversity — culminating in the witty final image of a friendship that becomes a watcher over the very spies who would disrupt it.

titleEn: People at Habina'a Say, Having Seen

verses.sadr: فقلت لهم لا ذا ولا ذاك ديدني
هي الناهد الريا إذا نعمة امرىء

verses.ajuz: له فارتقى بي في عقاب المحامد

translationEn: People at Habina'a who saw the loading of my saddle-bags with the new and the inherited say: Did you find a treasure, or did you raid in the morning those of heedless pride whose guardian was absent? I said to them: Neither this nor that is my way — but I have come from Khalid's presence. I pulled at his generosity on a Saturday morning with a pull, and it fell prostrate before my odes. I returned with a favor from him — white, supple, causing many a wound in the hearts of the envious. It is the full-figured, bountiful one — when another's favor has become flat and deflated. I grazed the heights of the earth while my poetry was in his praise — and it raised me to the heights of praiseworthiness. He clothed me from the most noble of his inherited property, and I clothed him from the noblest of my necklaces.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: filling, loading up, prosperity — here the full saddle-bags
freshly acquired wealth, new property
inherited wealth, ancient property
my habit, my custom, my way
full and firm-breasted — here: the full, generous favor
necklaces, here: the poet's odes as ornaments

context: A charming poem of eight verses in which Abu Tammam narrates a scene: people at a desert stop see his heavily loaded saddle-bags and think he has found a treasure or raided someone — but he corrects them, saying he simply came from Khalid's generous presence. The exchange between poet and patron is framed as a mutual clothing of gifts.

titleEn: May Clouds Water That Land Fully and Lightly
translationEn: O Abu Bishr, may the downpours of clouds water it abundantly, and with alternating rains, dense and sparse! I wore longing for it in grief — yet I was gladdened by it for the sake of Zamzam and the Standing Place. On the morning it went off — the most honest of inhabitants dispersing beneath a sacred great one. The noble manners have dwelt disheveled since his departure, and the pools of speech have dried after him. A trustworthy brother has gone far — and since he went far, I have remained a target for the brothers of mere greetings — those of dormant ambitions, frozen hands, and sleeping manliness. The most indifferent of them toward you nurses grudges for a dream if he should see it in sleep. And among the worst of waters when sought for drinking is the stagnant one long settled.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: abundantly, with plenty, softly and fully
showers of rain, downpours
longing, yearning, passionate nostalgia
a noble generous man, a great chief
dormant, extinguished — dead ambitions
stagnant water, water long standing and putrid

context: An elegy and farewell poem addressed to Abu Bishr — likely a companion departing for the holy city or having died there. Abu Tammam mourns the departure of a trusted friend while lamenting the empty acquaintances left behind, using the image of stagnant water for superficial companions.

titleAr: لأشكرنك إن لم أوت من أجلي
titleEn: I Shall Thank You Even If I Receive Nothing

verses.sadr: لأشكرنك إن لم أوت من أجلي
وإن توردت من بحر البحور ندى
verses.ajuz: ولم أنل منه إلا غرفة بيدي

translationEn: I shall most certainly thank you even if I receive nothing on my behalf — a gratitude that shall reach you from me at the end of all eternity. Even if you descended from the sea of seas in generosity, and I received of it nothing but a single handful in my palm.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I shall most certainly thank you (emphatic)
I was given, I received
eternity, endless time
you descended, you came down to water
a handful, a single scoop of water

context: A two-verse expression of enduring gratitude in which Abu Tammam paradoxically vows to thank his patron regardless of whether he receives any gift — even a single handful from a sea of generosity would bind him to eternal acknowledgment.

titleAr: إلياس كن في ضمان الله والذمم
titleEn: O Ilyas, Be Under God's Protection

verses.sadr: إلياس كن في ضمان الله والذمم
verses.ajuz: ذا مهجة عن ملمات النوى حرم
لم تنح أظفارها إلا على الكرم
آلاء ربك ما استشعرت من سقم

verses.sadr: إن الرياح إذا ما أعصفت قصفت
بنات نعش ونعش لا كسوف لها
verses.ajuz: والشمس والبدر منه الدهر في الرقم
تعتام إلا امرأ يشفى من القرم

verses.sadr: قد ينعم الله بالبلوى وإن عظمت

translationEn: O Ilyas, be under the guarantee of God and of covenants — one whose soul is sacred against the blows of harsh separation. A wholeness for you whose freshness is never stirred, and a shaking-off of excess in heel and foot. God has healed you of it — a passing ailment that sharpened its claws only upon the generous. The dust-storms of the frontier vanished from the moment God's blessings cleared away the illness you felt within. If a sickness displayed its fury to you — the rose is allied with the thick-maned lion of the forest. When the winds storm they break the trees of Najd but think nothing of the white broom. The daughters of the bier and the bier itself suffer no eclipse — yet the sun and the moon are forever marked by it. Misfortunes are the enemies of the noblest men — they choose only one whose cure requires satisfaction of a keen appetite. So may the reward and the blessing that have grown so great gladden you — great enough to polish the rust from the sharp-edged sword. God sometimes blesses through affliction, however great — and sometimes He afflicts some people through blessing!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: covenants, bonds of obligation, pledges
the soul, the life-blood, the innermost self
chronic illness, persistent sickness
white broom, a light desert shrub
a sharp, straight-bladed sword
intense appetite, craving, violent desire

context: A poem of well-wishing addressed to Ilyas, apparently written during or after a period of illness. Abu Tammam draws on natural imagery — the lion and the rose, the storm and the broom — to celebrate the recovery and to reflect on the paradoxical nature of divine testing through both affliction and blessing.

titleEn: O My Two Companions, Both of Whom Answer
translationEn: O my two companions — both of whom answer you when calamities call, with a noble response — help me against a gazelle whose portion I have made my own, yet who, while I live, has no portion in me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my two close friends (dual form)
calamities, misfortunes, afflictions
noble response, a noble one answering
gazelle — here: the beloved
his share, his portion

context: A short ghazal fragment addressed to two trusted friends, asking for their help in a love affair — a beautiful and sad inversion where the poet has made the gazelle-beloved his entire portion, while the beloved grants him none of their own.

titleEn: You Have Watered the Thirsty and Barren Plain
translationEn: You watered the thirsty and barren plain, and filled from your two stream-bends the eye of the scout. I came to you parched and waded in noble qualities sweeter than cool, clear water. I have prepared for your name a station and a dwelling in poetry — between rarities and witnesses. It is the watering-place for every elusive meaning, and the hobble for every stray verse. How many a favor of yours has adorned me with its strung jewels — like a necklace on the neck of a full-breasted maiden. You left them like a wall whose height was raised, struck between me and the envious one. So clasp your hands upon mine and deliver me from a murky, stagnant watering-place. I have become blind in its paths and faces — though I was once the guide's noble-born mount. Those are open wells whose edges are freely accessible, and the basin awaits the comer's watering. The bucket with its rope-length complete is filled with drink — if connected with one single arm.
