vocabularyHighlights.meaning: thirsty man, one intensely longing for water
barren, dead, parched (of land)
bend of a valley, winding part of a watercourse
scout, the one sent ahead to find water and pasture
strung jewels, necklaces, garlands
an ancient well, a natural water-hole

context: A poem of praise and supplication addressed to a patron. Abu Tammam uses extended water and well imagery — the watering of parched land, the scout finding water, the full bucket — to celebrate both his patron's generosity and the power of his own poetry to preserve his patron's name.

titleEn: Would That the Gazelles Had Told Him News

verses.ajuz: ما قيل في عمرو وفي الصمصام

translationEn: Would that the gazelles, O Abu al-'Umithil, had brought news to quench the thirst of thirsting minds! The prince, when events darken, is the light of time and the adornment of Islam. By God, one does not know in what condition his neighbor rests content across the days — whether for the wealth he shares with him or for the indigence he parts from. I see that the letter-page has been overcome by a lull — a lull that has made the very souls droop within their bodies. When thoroughbred horses are refined by craft they delight men of intellect and understanding, that the eyes may have more room in them for contemplation and careful attention to their bearing. Were it not for the prince, and that the judge of his opinion in poetry has become the most just of judges — I would have mourned all my hopes before him, or my recitation would have been the escort of my words. And I would have feared in his separating what is between us what was said of Amr and al-Samsama.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: thirsting ones, those intensely thirsty
minds, intellects, heads
boasts, prides himself, considers himself fortunate
a lull, a period of pause and weakness
thoroughbred horses, the best of horses
a famous sharp sword — here: a sword separated from its owner, proverbial

context: A poem addressed to Abu al-'Umithil, a scholar and literary figure at the Abbasid court, extolling a prince's role as the supreme judge of poetry. Abu Tammam playfully references the famous proverb about the sword al-Samsam being separated from its owner to express the danger of the prince withholding his favor.

titleEn: My Phantom Met Him in Slumber and He Avoided It

verses.sadr: ولو مرت الريح الصبا عند أذنه

translationEn: My phantom met him in slumber and he avoided it — and when one day I kissed his shadow, he was angered by it. My phantom told me that I had once passed by his gate to steal a glance — and he veiled himself from it. Had the east wind passed by his ear bearing my name, he would have cursed the wind or censured it! And no thought of mine ever ran through his heart making itself apparent without my being blamed for it. Yet his ugly behavior toward me and his turning away and avoidance only increased my love for him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: phantom, night-specter, vision in sleep
drowsiness, light sleep, slumber
he avoided it, he turned away from it
to steal, to snatch quickly and furtively
he veiled himself, he screened himself away
endearment, increased love, becoming more beloved

context: A five-verse ghazal masterpiece of Abu Tammam in which the beloved is so cold that even the poet's phantom-self is avoided in dreams — yet the beloved's cruelty paradoxically deepens the poet's love. The conceit of blaming the east wind for carrying the poet's name is memorably witty.

titleAr: يا بعد غاية العين إن بعدوا
titleEn: O How Far the Eye's Reach When They Depart

verses.sadr: يا بعد غاية العين إن بعدوا
قالوا : الرحيل غدا لاشك ، قلت لهم
verses.ajuz: اليوم أيقنت أن اسم الحمام غد

verses.sadr: كم من دم يعجز الجيش اللهام إذا
ما لامرىء خاض في بحر الهوى عمر

verses.ajuz: على النفوس أخ للموت أو ولد

verses.sadr: تداو من شوقك الأقصى بما فعلت
في موقف وقف الموت الزعاف به
في حيث لا مرتع البيض الرقاق إذا
verses.ajuz: أصلتن جدب ولا ورد القنا ثمد

verses.sadr: مستصحبا نية قد طال ما ضمنت
ورحب صدر لو أن الأرض واسعة
verses.ajuz: كوسعه لم يضق عن أهلها بلد
قد صرح الماء عنها وانجلى الزبد

verses.sadr: من كل أروع ترتاع المنون له
verses.ajuz: إذا تجرد لا نكس ولا جحد

verses.sadr: يكاد حين يلاقي القرن من حنق

verses.ajuz: جيش من الصبر لا يحصى له عدد

verses.sadr: نأوا عن المصرخ الأدنى ، فليس لهم
نجاك في الروع ما نجى سميك في

verses.ajuz: فاذهب فأنت طليق الركض يا لبد

verses.sadr: لاخلق أربط جأشا منك يوم ترى
verses.ajuz: أبا سعيد ولم يبطش بك الزؤد

verses.sadr: أما وقد عشت يوما بعد رؤيته

verses.ajuz: ما ليم أن ظن رعبا أنه الأسد

verses.sadr: من كان أنكأ حدا في كتائبهم

وفي الكلى تجد الغيظ الذي نجد

فافخر فما من سماء للندى رفعت
واعذر حسودك فيما قد خصصت به

verses.sadr: إن العلى حسن في مثلها الحسد

translationEn: O how far the reach of the eye when they depart — it is longing for all of time and sleeplessness! They said: The departure is tomorrow, no doubt. I said to them: Today I am certain that the name of death is 'tomorrow.' How much blood, which even a vast army is powerless before, will be ruled upon by hard destiny when they depart. No man who has waded in the sea of love across a lifetime but that separation has easy and difficult modes for him. It is as though separation, from its eternal insistence upon souls, is a brother or son of death. Cure your utmost longing with what the cavalry of Ibn Yusuf did while the heroes were driven in pursuit. That joy whose brightness vowed that no depression would neighbor it in the soul — I met them while destinies refused to be turned from what they were commanded, and the encounter was the very center of the battle. In a stand where death's deadly venom halted — death is found and souls are lost. Where the thin bright blades have no pasture when they are drawn in drought, and where the lances' watering-place has no puddle. Accompanied by a resolve that has long undertaken to guarantee you calamities — and fulfilled with what it promised. And a broad chest — were the earth as wide as its breadth, no land would be too narrow for its people. You cracked their course among a few — the water had cleared of them and the froth subsided. From every bold man at whose sight destinies tremble when he draws forth — no coward, no denier. He almost, when he meets his adversary in fury, sends his soul ahead before the spearhead against his foe. They were few, yet they were good — aided by an army of patience whose numbers cannot be counted. When they see death's looming cloud they don their armor of certitude — armor without rings. They turned away from the nearer cry for help — they have nothing for their enemies but swords as reinforcement. Mu'awiya turned away from them while the lances had judged him — but fate and the term of life refused. What saved you in the terror saved your namesake at Siffin when the cavalry was stripped of its horsemen. If you escape while the nostrils of death are humiliated — go then, for you run free, O Lubad! No creature is more firm of heart than you on the day when, O Abu Said, the terror has not laid a hand on you. Now that you have lived a day after beholding him — boast, for you are the courageous cavalier. Had the lion of lions beheld his sight, he would not be blamed for fearing that he was the Lion himself. How different between them in every calamity — fate's path is clear and renewed in them both. This one bears on his shoulders every calamity feared — and that one on his shoulders bears only the lion's mane-pads. It has overcome me without overcoming through a difficulty — at Sindabayya and a day of massed terror. Who was more wounding in their squadrons — you, or your sharp sword, or the One God? No day has more beautiful spectacle than it — with the Yemeni blades cutting through their skulls. Their souls he auctioned to the lances when they were leveled — and no hand will ever repel a doubt of time away from him. As if they, plunging into the jugulars and deep in the sides, find the rage that we find — from every blue-flashing one, keen of gaze with no gaze toward the vital spots, with no crookedness in its blade. As if it had been the companion of love since long ago — so no heart or liver escapes it. You left their path to the fire a well-traveled way, with a band arriving at it every day. It is as if Babak in al-Badhin after them is a camp-enclosure that remained when the tribe departed, or a tent-peg. At every winding path of Farsi Batlas — when he grew dark of entrails from his arrogance — you lodged in both his flanks a star that blazes, and a fugitive, and the inner terror drives him to his death as cash money is sought. As if his soul from its long bewilderment watches upon itself on the day of war. By God, we do not know: should Islam be grateful for it — from a battle — to Umm al-Abbas, or to Adad? A day by which Islam took its ornament entire and eternity put on glory with it. A day that will come when the Reckoning stands, and Badr will not blame it nor will anyone be shamed by it. The people of Mawqan when they resisted — no guilt saved them from you in the battle and no support. You left no polytheist woman but she learned: if she does not repent, that which she bears is for the sword. The leopard when the affair grew dark attacked them at dawn — a drop of war — and when it came to them they were extinguished. They almost had their necks severed from their skulls — had they not resolved upon the giving of judgment, they would not have settled it. But I delegated to them the opinion of Ibn Muhsana — the sword considers him a sword whenever he strives. Every day a conquest from you arrives — almost grasped in its beauty by the cold north wind. Events whose reports were sweet and pleasant — honey has become abandoned compared to them. Ibn Yusuf rescued the frontier from a famine of years — the years of Yusuf, beside which life is prosperous. The traces of your ancient wealth have grown old — but you have left blessings whose traces are ever new. So boast — for there is no sky of generosity raised without your fine deeds being its pillars. Excuse your envious one for what you have been singled out with — for in such matters envy is beautiful indeed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a strong firm she-camel — here: fate, the hard and relentless
swift-killing, deadly venom
the heart, courage, composure of heart
the ferocious lion, the powerful lion
a place-name: site of a famous battle
ancient traces, the oldest vestiges
a fortress/region in Azerbaijan, site of campaign against Babak

context: A major war ode (53 verses) celebrating the military campaigns of Abu Said (likely Muhammad ibn Yusuf or a Abbasid commander) against the Khurramite rebel Babak and frontier enemies. The poem opens with a ghazal prelude of longing then transitions into vivid battle description — one of Abu Tammam's finest fakhr-tinged panegyrics.

titleEn: You Were Detained and for Your Sake the Rains Were Held

verses.ajuz: ولم يزل نابيا عن صحبك العدم

verses.sadr: يا بن المسيب قولا غير ماكذب
verses.ajuz: لولاك لم يدرض ما المعروف والكرم
يجل شكري إذ جلت لي النعم

verses.sadr: يا من إذا قعدت بالقوم همتهم
verses.ajuz: عن اكتساب العلى قامت به الهمم
ما في جوانبه لين ولا وصم
لذمة الشعر إذ ضاعت له الذمم

verses.sadr: علوت من مجد قيس في الورى علما
verses.ajuz: أعيا الورى وعلا مجدا بك العلم

translationEn: You were detained — and the persistent rains were withheld for your sake — and destitution has never ceased to be repelled from your companions. O son of al-Musayyab — speaking only truth — were it not for you, none would know what kindness and nobility are. You have covered me in blessings that were great — and it is fitting that my gratitude be great when great blessings are granted to me. O you — when the ambitions of people sit idle from attaining glory, his own ambitions rise in its pursuit. I saw your branch from a ben-tree whose root has no softness or flaw in its sides. You are the drawn sword — draw the sword in defense of the covenant of poetry when its covenants have been squandered. You have risen in the glory of Qays among people as a standard that exhausted all people, and glory rose higher through you as a standard.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: persistent rains, steady gentle rains
repelled, keeping away, at a distance
you covered me, you robed me
ben-tree, the hardest and most prized wood for bow-making
flaw, blemish, crack in wood
the drawn (sword), also: the noble offspring

context: A seven-verse poem praising Ibn al-Musayyab as a man whose presence makes generosity known and whose absence causes even rains to be withheld. Abu Tammam urges him as a drawn sword to defend the honor of poetry, celebrating his lineage from the tribe of Qays.

titleAr: لئن جحدتك ما لاقيت فيك لقد
titleEn: If I Denied What I Suffered in You

verses.sadr: لئن جحدتك ما لاقيت فيك لقد
لكن عدوت على جسمي فبنت به
verses.ajuz: يامن رأى الظبي عداء على الديب

translationEn: If I deny what I suffered in you — the witnesses of my anguish and torment have testified truthfully. Sigh after sigh, long witness to the fact that they were torn from an anguished breast. Yet you ran upon my body and it showed it — O you who have seen the gazelle sprinting against the wolf!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I denied you, I rejected what you caused me
anguish, pains of longing, intense suffering
a deep sigh, a groan of grief
anguished, distressed, afflicted with sorrow
the wolf — here: the ravaging beloved

context: A three-verse ghazal of remarkable compression. Abu Tammam admits that even if he tried to deny his suffering, his own sighs are witnesses. The final image of a gazelle outrunning a wolf — the beloved's aggression upon the poet's wasted body — is an arresting reversal of the usual imagery.

titleAr: سرت تستجير الدمع خوف نوى غد
titleEn: She Walked Seeking Refuge in Tears from Tomorrow's Separation

verses.sadr: سرت تستجير الدمع خوف نوى غد

verses.ajuz: إلى كل من لاقت وإن لم تودد

verses.sadr: وطول مقام المرء في الحي مخلق

verses.ajuz: إلى الناس أن ليست عليهم بسرمد

verses.sadr: فتى يوم بذ الخرمية لم يكن
عدا الليل فيها عن معاوية الردى
verses.ajuz: وما شك ريب الدهر في أنه ردي
لو أن القضاء وحده لم يبرد

verses.sadr: عططت على رغم العدا عزم بابك
رآك سديد الرأي والرمح في الوغى

verses.ajuz: إذا هو لم يؤنس برمح مسدد
من الخوف والإحجام ما لم يعود

verses.sadr: وكان بعيد القعر من كل ماتح
وللكذج العليا سمت بك هم ة
وقد خزمت بالذل انف ابن خازم
لما بت في الدنيا بنوم مسهد
من الصبر في وقت من الصبر مجحد
ويا سيف لا تكفر ويا ظلمة أشهدي
لما بث في الدنيا بنوم مسهد
إذا عدد الإحسان أو لم يعدد
verses.ajuz: فمهما تكن من وقعة بعد لا تكن
رأى بابك منك التي طلعت له
هززت له سيفا من الكيد إنما
يسر الذي يسطو به وهو مغمد

verses.sadr: ويفضح من يسطو به غير مغمد
ولم يبق مذخور ولم يبق مجتد
verses.ajuz: إذا ما رحى دارت أدرت سماحة

verses.sadr: رحى كل إنجاز على كل موعد
verses.ajuz: أتيتك لم أفزع إلى غير مفزع

verses.sadr: ولم أنشد الحاجات في غير منشد
يدي عولت في النائبات على يدي

translationEn: She walked seeking refuge in tears from fear of tomorrow's separation — and every resting-place became thorny thicket to her. What rescued her from the swamp of death is that it was the turning-away of parting — not an intentional estrangement. So compassion caused to flow for her a tear reddened with blood over a rosy cheek. She is the full moon — the love her face shows to all she meets enriches her, even when she feels no love. But I have never gathered wealth entire and rejoiced in it, except in scattered, dispersed condition. And the days have never given me settling sleep to enjoy, except in the form of driven, scattered sleep. Long residence in a tribe wears thin the two sides of a man's face — so travel abroad and you will be renewed. For I have seen the sun grow more beloved to people from the fact that it does not stay above them forever. I swear by the Lord of the white blades whose backs are bloodied, and the Lord of the lances that groan and splinter — Muhammad al-Samiti's sword has stilled the anguish of the blood-revenge of Muhammad al-Samiti. May God cast upon Babak and his lieutenants from him a bone-breaker in every scene — more generous than the brilliant face of the rain-cloud in generosity, braver than the turns of time, and more resolute. When we call him by the name of the most fortunate and auspicious, he answers to it — and he is not wronged by being called the bald and wretched. A youth on the day of Badh — the Khurramite battle — he was no coward, no weakling, no retreater. He stood at Sindabayya while the lances were aimed, guided to the hidden soul and finding their way. Death overran Mu'awiya there in the night — and the doubt of time did not doubt that he was doomed to perish. By my life, I had set him free on the day I met him — were it only for fate to have not grown cold. If fate proves him refuted in his plan — it does not refute him in his followers. And in the most precise of battles with horses hurling their heroes into a blazing furnace — you cut asunder the resolve of Babak despite the enemies' noses, with your patience — a cut like the warp-threads of the supported garment. If he did not depart there with a cut-up carcass, he departed with a cut-up resolve. The lances had glimpsed his heart — and the veil of extended fate blinded them to it. Mawqan was the land of his exile — and you watered it with cavalry — what a watering! You brought down on it, on a Friday, his glory — which had been dwelling between Vulture and Farqad. He saw you correct in opinion and in the lance in battle — girdled with boldness in it and wrapped. And no ordeal is dispelled by a sound opinion when it is not companioned by a straightened lance. So he passed obedient to the lance-shafts — accustomed to fear and hesitation in what he never was before accustomed. He was the strong endurance — and you stripped him, by the beauty of pure endurance in battle, of the beauty of his endurance. By my life, you made accessible his fortress-heart — a rope-reach away for the lances, an easy watering. He was deep-bottomed from every drawer — and you left him watered and drinking by hand. For the high Kadhaj your ambition soared — an ambitious one in which victory goes out at evening and returns at morning. You humiliated by shame the nose of Ibn Khazim, and their citadels baffled Yazid ibn Mazid — when you spent your night in the world sleepless and you released upon them every bound death. At the plateau of Abarshtawim and Darrudh upon every raised, full, and round hillock — the sharp blades won you there glories — and a night on which you performed the night-raid's performance of patience at a time when patience was denied. O charge — do not deny him his dignity! O sword — do not be ungrateful! O darkness — bear witness! O night — if I were in your place after it I would not have dispersed in the world in sleepless sleep. Victories whose roots and branches of triumph — whether acts of goodness are counted or uncounted — whatever battle comes after, let it be nothing but a reiteration of the good of what you have done. The beauties of all types of singers are many — yet the prizes of precedence belong only to Ma'bad. You cleared darkness from Azerbaijan after it had been clothed in a color like the gray cloud — and there was a time when the dawn in it was not white — then it became a time when night in it was not black. Babak saw from you what rose upon him with ill-omen — and upon the true religion with the most fortunate augury. You wielded against him a sword of cunning — one whose necks it severs without being drawn: it gladdened him who wields it while sheathed, and shamed him who wields it unsheathed. And I hope to collar his neck with a necklace of a polished Indian blade — strung with death — whose wearer among people is its ornament rather than its ornament's wearer. Toward you we tore open a wing of night as if the lands had applied kohl of antimony from it — turning in the horizons like a blazing coal as if its two jaws turn a strip of a file. Your two grandfathers' generosity encompassed the seekers — until there remained no reserve and no seeker. When their wheel turns it drives generosity — the wheel of every fulfillment upon every promise. I came to you having sought refuge with none other than you — I sought my needs from no other source. Whoever hopes for the good deed of one far away — his hand has relied in calamities upon his own hand.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: thorny shrubs — here used as image for painful resting
compassion, tender concern, anxiety for another
epithet derived from lineage — al-Samit, the silent one
Badh: the mountain stronghold of Babak, the Khurramite rebel
Kadhaj: a fortress or region in the Byzantine frontier
polished at the edge — a sharp, well-honed sword
a type of Yemeni striped cloth — here used for its woven warp threads

context: A 54-verse qasida — one of Abu Tammam's most celebrated battle odes — praising Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Samit's campaigns against the Khurramite rebel Babak and the frontier fortresses of Azerbaijan. The poem opens with a tender ghazal prelude about a woman's tears before the beloved's departure, then transitions into a sustained martial panegyric rich with historical detail and metaphysical conceits.

titleEn: May Thunderclouds Water Their Ruins

verses.sadr: زعمت هواك عفا الغداة كما عفت
لا والذي هو عالم أن النوى
ملك إذا نسب الندى من ملتقى
كالليث ليث الغاب إلا أن ذا
بالسفح من همذان إذ سفحت دما
وبيان ذلك أن أول من حبا
النار نار الشوق في كبد الفتى
خير له من أن يخامر صدره

translationEn: May a thunderous downpour water their ruins, and may prosperity and bliss descend upon them. The rain-clouds have visited their dwelling-places with showers that are no disgrace to ruins. Separation has shown its foolishness on the day of their departure, yet despite what I see it remains mild toward you. An unjust woman has wronged you, an innocent man unjustly wronged — oppression from one who holds power is blameworthy. She claimed that your love has faded this morning, as the ruins at the curve of the dunes have faded. Nay, by God who knows that separation is patience, and that Abu al-Husayn is noble. I have never deviated from the path of devotion, nor has my soul ever circled around a thousand other than you. To Muhammad ibn al-Haytham ibn Shabanah belongs glory established beside the star al-Simak. A king whose generosity, if traced to its source in his nature, is found to be his own kin and intimate. Like a lion — the lion of the thicket — save that this one smiles in battle while that one scowls. You crushed the mountains of the enemy with your cavalry while disbelief was both seated and standing against guidance. On the slope of Hamadhan, where you shed blood so freely that the thirsty spears drank deep. A day you branded upon time, a battle that cooled the heat of Islam while it itself was a scorching wind. His spear-points gleamed, and by day they were suns, and by night they were stars. You bared your swords for the fray and sheathed them when the Khuramiyya's stratagem was broken. You served religion with the blessing of a bold stroke that left the chief of unbelief wounded. The lightning of your right hand flashed and dissipated the darkness from the face of disaster. You struck at the nose of drought until it retreated, and scarcity beneath its cloud became nonexistent. Generosity has a share in all noble deeds and piety — its master is neither the lazy nor the faint-hearted. Its proof is that the first to bestow hospitality was Abraham, the intimate friend of God. You gave me the blood-money of the slain, though I have no claim and no old right upon you — save the generosity that falls due like a debt, for the noble man is a debtor to those who seek him. Gratitude became a thin and slight thing in his eyes, yet it is truly immense. He concealed what he gave, and thus the gift was concealed and folded away, yet when spread abroad its shadow became vast. He walked generosity through hardship with humility, and was too great to mention it, though it was truly great. The fire of longing burns in the young man's liver, and separation kindles it with a poisoned love. Better for him that his chest harbor no secret kindness of a man kept hidden. The one who steals a favor shall continue under a curse — the wronged benefaction calls down curses upon him. Shall I withhold kindness while it is like the moon of darkness? Then I would truly be base. Enriched by the wealth you gave me, its burdens upon my neck — and deficient in loyalty? I go forth in two robes that no youth wore before me: the robe of wealth and the robe of blame.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: thunderous downpour, heavy rain with rumbling thunder
the star Spica (al-Simak al-A'zal), used as a high marker of glory
the Khurramiyya sect, heretics who rebelled against the Abbasid caliphate
innate disposition, natural qualities; here: bold stroke of fortune
one who seeks a favor, a petitioner
one who makes others weary or frustrated, the idle and fruitless
separation, parting from a beloved

context: A long panegyric qasida addressed to Muhammad ibn al-Haytham ibn Shabanah, combining a nasib (romantic prelude) mourning separation with a lengthy praise section. Abu Tammam celebrates the patron's military victories over the Khurramiyya sect — the heretical movement suppressed during the reign of al-Mu'tasim — and his generosity. The poem ends with a self-reflective section on the relationship between poet and patron.

titleEn: We Withheld Our Gazes Fearing They Would Melt
translationEn: We have withheld our gazes from you, fearing they would dissolve. Whenever we gave you a longer glance, you gave us in return more beauty and fragrance. The glances of an eye fell ill, and so they made hearts fall ill!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: we withheld, we shortened, we restrained
to melt, to dissolve (from intense beauty)
the sidelong glance, a furtive look from the corner of the eye
fragrance, goodness, pleasantness
fell ill, became sick (used metaphorically for hearts struck by love)

context: A short muqatta'a (fragment) in mujazza' al-ramal meter, composed in the ghazal tradition. The poem captures with concise wit the paradox of the beloved's beauty: to look is to be destroyed, yet looking only increases beauty. Abu Tammam was celebrated for compressing profound observations into brief pieces.

titleEn: I Shall Thank the Sons of Wahb for Their Gift

verses.ajuz: وهل لي غداة السبق عذر وأنتما
لئن رمت أمرا غبتما عند بكره
وماخير برق لاح في غير وقته
وما زلتما من نبعه إن عجمتما

verses.sadr: فلا عجب أن تأخذا من لسانه

translationEn: I shall thank the two sons of Wahb for their gift, which is sincere affection they have preserved with the finest care. Away with the calamity that they faced together as its counterpart, and as a bridle for the dark misfortune they both contended with. To the valley of Mina and the two standing-places — you both flowed like the dew of rain-clouds and their downpour, and from the prime of good deeds and their finest moment. Do I have any excuse on the morning of the contest, when both your eyes witness the day of the wager? I saw you both as a mountain range against the caprices of my time, and may you always remain among its peaks. I found for myself a prey pinned beneath the camel's chest-bone — and had it not been for you two I would have lain beneath its chest-bone. You gave me mastery over a stubborn thing and its bridle, and you enabled me to reach a soaring aspiration and its rein. If I aimed at something and you two were absent at its first attempt, then your deed delighted me in its second attempt. What good is lightning that appears out of season, and a valley that floods before its time? You two treated time gently until it answered me, though the misfortunes of time had long lamed my feet. You two are always, when tested, of the strong-wood bow when it comes to enduring oppression, and of the bamboo when it comes to generosity. By my life, you two have become the companions of benevolence, whose words guarantee your benefits. He takes from your hands, your affections — no wonder then that he takes from his tongue as well.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: away with, expiation — an exclamation wishing ill away
a great calamity, a devastating disaster
the lower part of a camel's neck resting on the ground; used idiomatically for being subjugated
bamboo; symbolizing flexibility, generosity, and ease
a hard mountain wood used for bows; symbolizes strength and resilience
the prime, the pinnacle, the first and best of something
a nose-ring or bridle used to control a camel; metaphor for mastery over difficulty

context: A poem of gratitude and praise addressed to the two sons of Wahb, important patrons of Abu Tammam. The poem weaves the poet's personal hardship with elaborate metaphors drawn from camel imagery, archery, and weather, typical of Abu Tammam's elaborate style (badi' school). The pilgrimage sites of Mina and the two standing-places (Arafat and Muzdalifah) are invoked symbolically.

titleAr: ما اليوم أول توديع ولا الثاني
titleEn: Today Is Not the First Farewell nor the Second

verses.sadr: ما اليوم أول توديع ولا الثاني
خليفة الخضر من يربع على وطن
وما أظن النوى ترضى بما صنعت

verses.ajuz: قد كان عيشي به حلوا بحلوان

verses.sadr: غصن من البان مهتز على قمر
verses.ajuz: يهتز مثل اهتزاز الغصن في البان

verses.sadr: أفنيت من بعده فيض الدموع كما
أمسكت منه بود شد لي عقدا
verses.ajuz: كأنما الدهر في كفي بها عان

verses.sadr: إذا نوى الدهر أن يودي بتالده
لو أن إجماعنا في فضل سؤدده
verses.ajuz: في الدين لم يختلف في الأمة اثنان

translationEn: Today is not the first farewell nor the second — separation is greater than my longing and my sorrows. Leave separation aside, for time has aided it until it holds more power over my body than my own soul. He is the successor of al-Khadir — who would dwell permanently in one homeland when the backs of she-camels are my true dwellings? In Syria are my family, in Baghdad my love, and I am in al-Raqqatayn while in Fustat are my brothers. I do not think that distance will be satisfied with what it has done until it tosses me to the farthest reaches of Khurasan. I left behind in the western horizon a dwelling place where my life had been sweet in the sweetness of Hulwan. A branch of the ban tree swaying upon a moon that sways like the swaying of a branch in the ban tree. After him I exhausted the flood of tears just as I exhausted in his absence my patience and my consolation. A man does not know the true depth of union until he is bid farewell with distance or with estrangement. The ill-treatment of calamities bored a tunnel into my depths — and then the kindness of Ibn Hassan shadowed you. I seized from him an affection that tied a knot for me, as if time were in my hands a captive through it. When time resolves to destroy a man's inherited wealth, it calls on no helpers other than his two hands. Were our consensus about the excellence of his nobility applied to religion, no two people in the community would differ.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Khadir, the immortal prophet-figure who wanders the earth; symbol of perpetual journeying
al-Raqqatayn, two cities on the Euphrates (Raqqa and Raqqa al-Shamiyya)
Fustat, the old capital of Egypt, near modern Cairo
the moringa or ben tree, whose slender swaying branches symbolize the beloved's graceful figure
Hulwan, a pleasant town near Baghdad; also a pun on حلو (sweet)
consolation, forgetting of grief, solace
a tunnel, a subterranean passage; metaphor for calamity penetrating the poet's innermost self

context: A nashib and madih poem combining the grief of perpetual travel with praise of Ibn Hassan, likely a patron in Iraq or Syria. Abu Tammam names actual cities — Syria, Baghdad, al-Raqqatayn, Fustat — creating an autobiographical map of his wandering life as a court poet. The al-Khadir image establishes the poet as an eternal wanderer whose only home is the saddle.

titleAr: تجرع أسى قد أقفر الجرع الفرد
titleEn: Drink the Grief of the Solitary Desolate Sandy Plain

verses.sadr: تجرع أسى قد أقفر الجرع الفرد
verses.ajuz: ودع حسي عين يجتلب ماءها الوجد

verses.sadr: إذا انصرف المحزون قد فل صبره
بدت للنوى أشياء قد خلت أنها

verses.ajuz: من الهزل يوما إن هزل الهوى جد

verses.sadr: فلا تحسبا هندا لها الغدر وحدها
وقالوا أسى عنها وقد خصم الأسى

verses.ajuz: ولا بين أضلاعي لها حجر صلد

verses.sadr: وكم تحت أرواق الصبابة من فتى
verses.ajuz: من القوم حر دمعه للهوى عبد

verses.sadr: ومن كان ذا بث على النأي طارف
verses.ajuz: فلي أبدا من صرفه حرق تلد
يجاوز بي عنه ولا رشأ فرد

verses.sadr: محمد يا بن الهيثم انقلبت بنا
verses.ajuz: نوى خطأ في عقبها لوعة عمد
إلي ولولا الشري لم يعرف الشهد
حدا بي عنك العيس للحادث الوغد
سق العهد منك العهد والعهد والعهد

verses.sadr: سحاب متى يسحب على النبت ذيله
verses.ajuz: فلا رجلق ينبو عليه ولا جعد
فلم ألق من أيامها عوضا بعد

verses.sadr: لدى ملك من أيكة الجود لم يزل
verses.ajuz: على كبد المعروف من فعله برد

verses.sadr: رقيق حواشي الحلم لو أن حلمه
verses.ajuz: بكفيك ما ماريت في انه برد
ولا يقطع الصمصام ليس له حد

verses.sadr: وداني الجدا تأتي عطاياه من عل
غدا بالأماني لم يرق ماء وجهه

verses.ajuz: بأرض ، فقد ألقى بها رحله المجد
ثوى منذ أودى خالد وهو مرتد

verses.sadr: فتى لا يرى بدا من البأس والندى
verses.ajuz: ولا شيء إلا منه غيرهما بد

verses.sadr: حبيب بغيض عند راميك عن قلى
verses.ajuz: وسيف على شانيك ليس له غمد
فأضحت جميعا وهي عن لحمه درد
ويعدو عليه الدهر من حيث لا يعدو

verses.sadr: ألم تر أن الجفر جفرك في العلى
verses.ajuz: قريب الرشاء لا جرور ولا ثمد
فأول من يروى به بعدها الأزد

verses.sadr: لهم بك فخر لا الرباب تربه
وكم لك عندي من يد مستهلة
verses.ajuz: علي ولا كفران عندي ولا جحد
وإن كنت لا مثل إليك ولا ند

verses.sadr: نظمت له عقدا من الشعر تنضب ال
verses.ajuz: بحار وما دناه من حليها عقد
وما السير منها لا العنيق ولا الوخد
بها وهي حيرى لا تروح ولا تغدو
وما ابتل منها لا عذار ولا خد

verses.sadr: أهين لها ما في البدور وأكرمت

translationEn: Drink the grief of the solitary desolate sandy plain, and let your eye's spring flow with water drawn up by longing. When the grieved one turns away with his patience broken, questioning the ruins is answered only by weeping. Things have appeared for separation that I had thought would befall me only when the calamities of time begin. A departure like the shooting of a star — it was the result of what seemed jest one day, for love's jesting is serious. Do not think that treachery belongs to Hind alone — the nature of every coquettish woman is Hind. They said grieve no more for her, yet grief has won the argument against the ribs of a longing man when they argue cleverly. An eye that when stirred returns sleep, and tears whose flocks when summoned come galloping. Behind my eyelids there are no stingy streams, nor between my ribs a hard stone for them. How many a youth lies under the canopy of passion, his tears free yet his heart enslaved to love. No one has borne separation patiently — but separation is patience itself. He who had only a recent grief at distance — for me there is an ancient burning from time's reversals. Neither a peerless king of gifts and generosity shall make me pass beyond him, nor a solitary fawn. Muhammad, O son of al-Haytham, a departure struck us in error, and its aftermath was deliberate anguish. And a grudge from the days — though they are powerful — and the worst of qualities is power neighbor to a grudge. The ill-treatment of time has reminded me of its former kindness to me — for without bitterness one would never know sweetness. By the calamities of time — a calamity drove me away from you on the she-camels to a vile accident. Calamities that veer from love — their beloved creeps while their hated rushes. Our nights at al-Raqqatayn and its people — may your covenant be refreshed, covenant upon covenant. A cloud whose hem, whenever it drags across the grass, leaves neither rocky hill to resist it nor curly plant. I have probed time's belly and its back and found in its days no substitute thereafter. In a king from the thicket of generosity who never ceases by his deeds to cool the liver of benevolence. Gentle in the margins of forbearance — if his forbearance were in your hands you would not doubt it is cold. A man of power whose blade-edges cut deeply, while the sharpest sword cannot cut without an edge. Near in bounty, his gifts come from above, while his rank has rugged and barren heights. The seeker has alighted with a noble man whose gifts are a lowland and his glory a highland. He arose with aspirations that kept his face's water unspilled by delay, and never blocked the hopes he raised. Most faithful of them in lightning when the flash fails, and truest in thunder when thunder lies. Most generous of them with saliva and hand to one who asks, and freshest in promise when promise dries up. Noble — when he pitches his tent in a land, then glory has pitched its baggage there. Through him benevolence was saved in Syria after it had lain low since Khalid perished, and was in retreat. A youth who sees no escape from valor and generosity, and nothing else has any escape from him. Beloved and loathed in the eyes of your attacker from spite, and a sword against your detractor that knows no sheath. How many a calamity rained upon him and then cleared — God be praised for its clearing, and to you the credit. For how long was he a morsel for calamities, until they all became toothless husks from his flesh. Were it not for you, every disaster would have wrestled him while time runs upon him from where he does not run. You stood as a highland among the sons of Sassan, with shelter spreading wide and a firm, lofty foundation. Where the blue-grey falcons among them soared high and the lions rose from their prey. Do you not see that the deep well of glory is your well, with a short rope, neither dragged nor scant? When all the Persians depart from it, the first to be satisfied from it after them is the Azd. Through you they have a pride that neither al-Ribab can match by claim, nor was Sa'd blessed with its days. How many a lavished gift you have bestowed on me — I hold neither ingratitude nor denial. A gift by whose breezes time is humbled, and the rosy horizon turns green from its benevolence. For one like you I have awarded praise as recompense — though you have no like and no equal. I have strung for him a necklace of poetry — seas run dry yet none of its jewels approach it. Its fringes travel like the sun's course, and the travel is neither ambling nor swift — it departs and returns, yet one departs and returns with it while it is bewildered, neither departing nor returning. It races across the horizons of lands as frontrunners, its cheeks never dampened nor its reins. Marvels whose appeal never departs, for one who sings them while driving and one who sings them extempore. When it appears at kings' courts, it receives its choicest portions untouched and fresh. What lies in the full moons is humbled before it, while its rhymes are honored in their presence as an honored delegation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sandy lowlands, sandy plains in a valley; singular: جرعة
dwelling places, habitations; places where one used to sing and live
falcons, hawks; singular: أجدل — powerful birds of prey
a deep well; also an allusion to the esoteric Jafr — here used as a metaphor for the patron's deep glory
a sharp-edged, powerful sword that does not bend
a well with scant water; contrasted here with a full flowing well
two gaits of a camel — a quick ambling pace and a heavy swinging gait

context: A long panegyric to Muhammad ibn al-Haytham, a prominent patron of Abu Tammam. The poem opens with a conventional nasib (love prelude) lamenting separation and the nights at al-Raqqatayn on the Euphrates. It then shifts to sustained praise of the patron's generosity, valor, and lineage from the Azd tribe. The poem concludes with a famous self-referential passage in which Abu Tammam praises his own poetry as poems that travel like the sun, honored at the courts of kings.

titleAr: ألقت على غاربي حبل امرئ عان
titleEn: Fate Cast Upon My Shoulders the Rope of a Captive Man

verses.sadr: ألقت على غاربي حبل امرئ عان

verses.ajuz: من كل صائبة عن قوس غضبان
حتى رمت بي في بحر ابن حسان

verses.sadr: بحر من الجود يرمي موجه زبدا
لولا ابن حسان مات الجود وانتشرت

verses.ajuz: على اعتساري يد لم تسه عن شاني

verses.sadr: يد من اليسر قدت حلتي عسري
verses.ajuz: حتى مشى عسري في شخص عريان

verses.sadr: يا من به بدنت من بعدما هزلت
كن لي مجيرا من الأيام إن لها

verses.ajuz: إذا الزمان جلا عن وجه خوان

translationEn: Separation cast upon my shoulders the rope of a captive man, and distance turned against me with the eye of a serpent. The calamities of time kept striking me relentlessly from every well-aimed shot of an angry bow. Hope stretched my reins and I guided it along until it cast me into the sea of Ibn Hassan. A sea of generosity whose waves cast up foam, its bubbles silver adorned with gold. Had it not been for Ibn Hassan, generosity would have died and the misfortunes of stinginess would have spread, folding up every kindness. When the days kept jesting with me and their wind shed the leaves of my branches. The hand of hope was joined to the hand of wealth, and my sorrows and griefs parted between the two. Until I clothed myself in the robes of ease that a hand spread over my hardship — a hand that never neglected my affairs. A hand of ease that cut the robe of my hardship until my hardship walked naked. The nights made peace with me after my sorrows had outweighed my joys in the balance by a great weight. Today my time has made peace with me and reminded me of the praises I had been caused to forget. Then the days bared their sharp sword for enemies and met them with a face devoid of beauty. Today I shall send my hopes to a king who receives praise with a heart that does not forget. My eye took a good omen from him when the lashes of my eyelids fluttered above, bringing good tidings. O you through whom my aspirations grew fat after they had wasted away and showed me the face of my own ruin. Be for me a protector from the days — for they have a hand that probes my secret and my public affairs. O son of the noble ones, and the hoped-for one among the tribe of Mudar when time uncovers a treacherous face. Toward you my hopes have driven me, guided by the cloud of your generosity from my own lands and homelands.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the hump of a camel; casting a rope on the hump means releasing control, giving someone free rein
a captive, one enslaved or bound
pure gold, native gold — used to describe the finest quality
hardship, difficulty, straitened circumstances
twitched, quivered — referring to the twitching of eyelashes as a traditional omen
grew fat, became plump — metaphor for hopes becoming robust after being wasted
Mudar, a major tribal confederation of northern Arabia from which many Arab nobles claimed descent

context: A panegyric to Ibn Hassan, likely the same patron addressed in poem 130. Abu Tammam presents his journey to the patron as driven by necessity after prolonged misfortune. The poem is structured around the metaphor of seas and clouds of generosity, and employs the common Abbasid-era trope of the poet's fortunes being entirely tied to a single powerful patron.

titleEn: My Tears Grew Beautiful and My Weeping Grew Sweet

verses.sadr: لك قد أدق من أن يحاكى
أي شيء يكون أحسن من صب

verses.ajuz: بعد ما جاز حكمه في القلوب

verses.sadr: كاد أن يكتب الهوى بين عين
غير أني لو كنت أعشق نفسي

translationEn: My tears have grown beautiful and my weeping has grown sweet — for you, O treasure of all beauty and sweetness. You have a beauty too refined to be compared to a willow branch or a sand dune. What could be more beautiful than a passionate lover — a cultured, passionate man infatuated with a cultured one? My judgment has prevailed over his mold and his desire, after his judgment had prevailed over all hearts. Love almost wrote between his eyes a text: 'This is Habib — a beloved.' Yet had I been in love with myself, I would have embittered my own life with the jealous watcher.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a tear, especially a single falling tear
loud weeping, sobbing, lamentation
a slender branch, especially of willow or ben tree — classical simile for the beloved's figure
a sand dune — classical simile for the beloved's figure and smooth skin
enslaved by love, one whose heart is completely captivated
the jealous watcher, the spy who prevents lovers from meeting

context: A witty ghazal poem that plays on Abu Tammam's own name — Habib means 'beloved' in Arabic. The poem is addressed to a refined, educated (أديب) beloved and celebrates the meeting of two cultured minds in love. The final verse is characteristic of Abu Tammam's intellectual wit, turning the poem into a self-reflective meditation on the nature of loving one so perfect.

titleEn: I Sacrifice Myself for You, Abdullah Is Dear to Me
translationEn: May I be your ransom — Abdullah is dear to me after the estrangement and distance from him. He has from the noble men of the book white (pages) who have fulfilled their right of visitation and affection. I reckon their day — if you do not find them — is like a day in which a call from them meets only stone. How many a rain-star brought the red wine flowing, and another from you brought generosity at dawn. This one rains down upon my burning thirst, and that one rains upon my inherited wealth. This one irrigates every root with rivulets, and that one fills every valley's basin. I called upon them for you, and you were among those whom we designate in the best of binding covenants.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a rain-star, one of the 28 lunar mansions believed to bring rain when they rise or set
red or golden wine; also a reddish-colored she-camel
a burning thirst, extreme inner heat from passion or thirst
inherited wealth, ancestral possessions
small rivulets, channels of water; singular: مذنب
the basin of a valley where water collects

context: A short poem of friendship and affection addressed to a patron or companion named Abdullah, combining wine imagery (the rain-star of wine) with generosity metaphors (the rain-star of the patron's benevolence). The poem exemplifies Abu Tammam's characteristic technique of drawing parallels between natural phenomena and human virtues.

titleEn: May God Grant You Bodily Health in Succession
translationEn: May God grant you bodily health in succession, for as long as the cooing birds call from the branches. How did you find the medicine — may God grant you recovery from it throughout all time? May God never remove from you a virtue you have worn from your fine tribulation. May you always glory in every health you pluck from the thickets of tribulations. Were our lifetimes to obey us, the nobles of Yemen would share their lifetimes with him. The continued life of the noble Ahmad is truly a gift upon our necks, one of God's choicest gifts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to call, to coo, to chirp — the sound of birds
the cooing doves or calling birds
you wore it out, you spent and exhausted it — said of wearing out a virtue through use
you uproot it, you pluck it out entirely — from اجتث
the generous man, the noble one; also a noble horse
a gift that creates obligation, a favor bestowed from above

context: A short congratulatory poem (تهنئة) on recovery from illness, addressed to a patron named Ahmad. The poem follows the classical convention of blessing the patron's health with formulas of prayer and praise. The final verse — calling the patron's survival a gift to the whole community — is a characteristically hyperbolic Abbasid panegyric gesture.

titleEn: My Gaze at You Signals to Me
translationEn: My gazing at you signals to me, guiding me to the fact that you are beloved to me. And my keeping distance out of fear of the talebearers — while you are close to my heart. So look at my infatuation with you whenever the jealous watcher is heedless. And look at my body — for in what has befallen me there is something wonderfully strange.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: talebearers, slanderers who report lovers' secrets to separate them
the jealous watcher, guardian, or spy who prevents lovers from meeting
obsession, intense infatuation, consuming love
a wonderful strangeness, an astonishing marvel — intensified wonder

context: A short mujazza' al-kamil ghazal presenting the classical situation of secret love conducted under the eyes of watchful talebearers. The body wasting away from love is visible proof of what the lover cannot say openly. Abu Tammam uses the compressed form to maximum effect, with each verse presenting a paradox or irony.

titleAr: أبا القاسم المحمود ، إن ذكر الحمد
titleEn: O Abu al-Qasim the Praised, If Praise Is Mentioned

verses.sadr: أبا القاسم المحمود ، إن ذكر الحمد
verses.ajuz: وقيت رزايا ما يروح وما يغدو

verses.sadr: فإن نك قد نالتك أطراف وعكة
verses.ajuz: فلا عجب أن يوعك الأسد الورد

verses.sadr: فقد أصبحت من صفرة في وجوهها
بنا لا بك الشكوى فليس بضائر
verses.ajuz: إذا صح نصل السيف ما لقي الغمد

translationEn: O Abu al-Qasim the Praised — if praise is mentioned — may you be protected from every calamity that comes by day or night. The lands where you dwell have grown pleasant, their spring pasture being the lowlands and their summer pasture being the highland. If you have been touched by the edges of a fever, it is no wonder that the rose-colored lion falls ill. You are safe — and the prayer you made was lofty in its reach, and it was fortune that achieved its fulfillment. For the pallor has now departed from their faces and banners alike — the Azd have been grieved equally with you. With us, not with you, lies the complaint — for it does not harm the sword-blade when the sheath suffers.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a fever, illness, a bout of sickness
the rose-colored or tawny lion — the most noble variety of lion, here a metaphor for the patron's nobility
the lowland, the depression; here a spring pasture region (Ghawr)
the highland plateau of central Arabia; here the summer pasture region
the Azd tribe, a major South Arabian tribal confederation
the blade of a sword or arrowhead — the essential part, contrasted with the sheath

context: A short poem of consolation and praise addressed to Abu al-Qasim on his recovery from illness. The poem is notable for its final verse — a celebrated aphorism stating that the sword-blade is unharmed even if the sheath suffers — meaning the patron's true nobility is untouched by bodily illness. Abu Tammam was known for embedding memorable epigrams in his poems.

titleEn: The Battle Has Subdued al-Badhh and It Lies Buried

verses.ajuz: ما إن به إلا الوحوش قطين

verses.sadr: لم يقر هذا السيف هذا الصبر في
كانت من الدم قبل ذاك مفازة
ولى ولم يظلم وهل ظلم امرؤ
يا وقعة ما كان أعتق يومها
لو أن هذا الفتح شك لاشتفت
هيهات لم يعلم بأنك لو ثوى

translationEn: The battle has subdued al-Badhh and it lies buried — nothing dwells in it now but wild beasts. Never did this sword maintain this patience in battle save for the glory of this religion! It had been the virginal stronghold of the west — and al-Afshin, solver of the east, pierced its maidenhead with the sword. He left it with foxes howling in its midst, though only yesterday you would see it as a lions' den. From the skulls of its people the rains poured down — their signal being severed necks and flowing blood. Before that, it had been a desert from blood — a lowland — and by evening it had become a spring of it. A sea of battle whose sails are nothing but ribcages and breastbones. A king came to meet them, greeted by high deeds — with the bells of the noble and the fortunate good omen. A king whose noble deeds illuminate when his forehead and brow appear before kingship. He governed armies with the governance of a man of experience whom the eye of kingship watched while still in the womb. His lance was made supple and so he grew mighty — for the spear's force strengthens when it bends. You see the noble man grow mighty when he humbles himself, and you see the base man grow base when he grows base. He led both death and armies until they arose with dust-pillars as their banner over Arshaq. He left Arshaq — its name enough to make the deaf hard rocks spring with water-sources. Were a city able one day to perform pilgrimage, the Ka'ba and Hajun would have made pilgrimage to it. Babak met you while roaring, but then he turned away — and his roaring had become a moan. He met from you Mu'tasimite bridles that emaciated the side of disbelief while it was fat. When he saw your two banners he fled — his disbelief with a sore eye upon him. He fled and was not wronged — for is a man wronged who hastens his escape while behind him is the dragon?! You struck in Abrashtuwaym blows that made the tooth of religion laugh while it was grieving. You struck them with blows that shatter kidneys and make a man light who was once steadfast. Blows like the jaws of a she-camel in labor, and under them thrusting like a pestilence in its strike. Valor that disperses the battle ranks, and under it steady reason that disperses minds. Your sword-fight emptied his chest — though he had carved it out and his heart was inhabited by valor. His experiences imprisoned the excess of his ferocity — for experience is a prison for minds. On the evening of al-Tall, you withdrew while guidance had a circling longing and yearning for you. He set an ambush for him and he remained to face his doom — while his hidden ambush became his own ambush! O battle-day — how noble was your day, when some days of time are base. Had this conquest been in doubt, hearts would have found relief from it — so how much more when it is certain! You took Babak bewildered, far from his hopes, while the hopes of error have muddied waters. Regret stabbed his heart, and so his heart without a warrior's thrust has been stabbed! He hoped for the land of Byzantium but was held back by a doom deaf to escape, stubborn. Far from it — he did not know that if he had dwelt in China, China would not have been far from you. Not Pharaoh nor Haman nor Qarun achieved in the world what he achieved. And Islam will thank what you have bestowed upon it — and God is guarantor of fulfilling that.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Badhh, the mountain fortress of Babak al-Khurrami in Azerbaijan, conquered in 837 CE
al-Afshin, the Abbasid general (of Sogdian origin) who defeated Babak; his name means 'prince' in Sogdian
an ambush; the verse plays on Babak setting an ambush that became his own trap
a cloud of dust raised by an army — used as a battle standard metaphor
the dragon; here used as a metaphor for the terrifying force of the Caliph's army
Abrashtuwaym, a location in Azerbaijan where a major battle against Babak was fought
Babak al-Khurrami, leader of the Khurramiyya revolt against the Abbasid caliphate, executed in 837 CE

context: One of Abu Tammam's most celebrated panegyric odes, praising al-Afshin and the Caliph al-Mu'tasim for the defeat and capture of Babak al-Khurrami in 837 CE. The poem is a historical document describing the siege and fall of al-Badhh fortress in Azerbaijan. It employs extensive military imagery and historical allusions, and contains the celebrated aphorism: 'Experience is a prison for minds.' The poem is central to the Abbasid literary canon.

titleEn: A Sun of Cloudy Skies Rose from a Willow Branch
translationEn: A sun of cloudy skies rose from a willow branch, its eye commanded to bewitch hearts. Were it to lift the veil from the sun and full moon, their light would be shamed into setting. I am wounded by the glance of its two cheeks, and I seek remedy in a tear and a sob. The burning of longing and love contend over me — women tearing their garments above me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: cloudy, overcast sky; a day of thick clouds — contrasted here with the bright sun of the beloved
a veil, a face-covering worn by women
they were shamed, they were put to shame (dual form)
a sidelong glance, looking from the corner of the eye
women tearing their garments — a classical image of extreme grief or mourning

context: A short ghazal in khafif meter combining conventional metaphors of the beloved as a sun rising from a slender figure. The final verse shifts from the beauty of the beloved to the anguish of the poet, whose longing is so intense that mourning women tear their garments for him — a striking inversion of the grief ritual.

titleAr: يا دار دار عليك إرهام الندى
titleEn: O Ruin May the Gentle Rains Drizzle Upon You

verses.sadr: يا دار دار عليك إرهام الندى

verses.ajuz: أن كاد يصبح ربعه لي مسجدا

verses.sadr: سقيا لمعهدك الذي لو لم يكن
لم يعط نازلة الهوى حق الهوى
من كان أحمد مرتعا أو ذمة

verses.ajuz: قد ساد حتى كاد يفني السؤددا

verses.sadr: عضب العزيمة في المكارم لم يدع

verses.ajuz: في غاية ما زلت فيها مفردا
لك والرماح من الرماح لك الفدا
آمالنا بك ما سلمت من الردى

verses.sadr: كم جئت في الهيجا بيوم أبيض

verses.ajuz: عنها ولم ير فيك قرنك موردا

verses.sadr: هيهات لا ينأى الفخار وإن نأى
لما زهدت زهدت في جمع الغنى

translationEn: O ruin, may the gentle drizzle of dew drizzle upon you, and may your garden sway in the moist earth gently bending. You were clothed in the robes of rain-clouds, proudly, and your wilderness was left with its wild beasts emboldened. A remnant of a dwelling I devoted myself to questioning until its quarter nearly became for me a place of prostration. I kept searching for it and searching for its people, while grief was my companion, searching or being searched. A watering upon your familiar ground — were it not to have existed, my heart would not have become familiar with passion. One who is ill has not given the falling of love its due right when love surrounds him and he forces himself to be patient. A passionate man whose heart sorrows have made an appointment — if you break the appointment with him. How do you disown my confusion in separation? — the beauty of the longing one is to be confused. O my companion in Damascus, you are not my true companion unless you smooth the way for sorrows. Bring near the strong robust she-camel and speed her on the road while the road is smoothed. Toward the sons of Abd al-Karim the mounts rushed in the gait of ostriches seeing the darkness and walking lightly. How many moons have they produced, protecting by their deeds a moon and a noble deed conversing with al-Farqad star. Smiling in battle, overflowing when the flint of the stingy miser strikes sparks and falls silent. He who has Ahmad as a pasture or a pledge — then God is Ahmad, then Ahmad is Ahmad. He became an enemy to his friend when his friend, praising him, blamed him, and a friend to his enemy. I have exhausted poetry for a praised one who has ruled until he nearly exhausted nobility itself. Sharp in resolve among noble deeds — he left in his own day no honor for tomorrow to seek. You advanced alone in seeking excellence, traveling through it toward lowland or highland. A wonder: that you are safe from loneliness in an extreme where you have always been solitary. I am your ransom when lances clash for you — and lances are your ransom against lances. You are safe — may our hopes through you always be safe as long as you are safe from destruction. How many a bright day you brought to battle while war itself brought a black day. You advanced — and chivalry allowed no turning back from it, while your adversary saw in you no ford to cross. You did not sheath the sword you had girded until its blade wished to be sheathed. Far be it — glory does not grow distant even when it grows distant from a seeker whose mount is generosity. How can what you seek escape you when your nature is to give generously and be praised? When you grew ascetic, you grew ascetic toward accumulating wealth — and when you were eager, you were most ascetic in it. So wealth, when it inclines, does not escape the grip of your generosity, reforming it or wasting it. You are nobler in origin than your gift, and your generosity is nobler in origin than your enemy. May the tribe of Tayyi never be without you — for rarely has your clan lacked a noble, leading generous man.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a gentle fine drizzle; softer than غيث (rain)
swaying gently, bending softly — said of branches or garden plants in a breeze
al-Farqad, the two stars near Polaris (Ursa Minor); a high stellar landmark of glory
to strike sparks that immediately go out; the flint fails — metaphor for the miser's futile effort
Tayyi, a major Arab tribe of northern Arabia, known for its warriors and nobles
a strong, hardened she-camel with a firm back — the best kind for long journeys
sharp in resolution — using the sword metaphor (عضب = sharp sword) for decisiveness

context: A long panegyric qasida to Ahmad, a member of the tribe of Tayyi, beginning with a conventional nasib over ruins in Damascus before the poet sets out toward his patron. The poem contains an extended play on the name Ahmad — a practice (ishtiqaq) typical of Abu Tammam — and includes a celebrated verse likening the patron to God's own praise. It praises Ahmad's valor, generosity, and his role in tribal leadership.

titleEn: By My Father the Dwelling-Places Are Truly Full of Grief

verses.sadr: قرت به تلك العيون بالملك وأشرقت
من يعش ضوء الآل يعلم أنهم

translationEn: By my father — dwelling-places are truly full of grief, and through sadness they are truly revealing. Tie your worn-out camel to the worn-out ruin, sharing the excess of passion between a gladdened companion and a grieving one. Do not prevent me from a halt by which I may heal the ailment of separation — for it is a necessity. Water the hearthstones from the stream of my tears — for one stingy with his tear is truly stingy. The tent-ditch had its half extinguished, and it is as if under the blows of calamity it is a joined eyebrow. Grief on the morning of grief stirred its burning thirst — and in the spotted ground of tender affection from you is a yearning. The mark of passion is a sigh or a tear, taken charge of by entrails and flowing streams. Were it not for the expression of grief, the plateau of al-Hima and the pure stone of al-Mishqar would be claimed to be grieving. March forth, O sons of needs — your quest shall succeed: a rain cloud of generosity whose downpour is heavy. For calamities are chained by its rain-drops and drought is imprisoned in its shower. They carried the heavy burden of worry and the journey overcame them — a march that breaks the back even when the back is strong. Until when they cast it from their shoulders by resolution — and it guaranteed their success. They found the court of the king green, and they beheld Harun in it — as if he were Harun (the Prophet Aaron). They found the Commander of the Faithful — his generosity a moist cloud whose shade is inhabited. They went forth having trusted in the compassion of al-Wathiq, by God — his augury for them auspicious. Those eyes were cooled by the king in joy, and those cheeks shone though they are dark-complexioned. They took hold of life's reins through the king whose manners are fortresses for noble deeds. A king whose very mention, when it enters the hearing, makes hope grow light toward him though hope was heavy. A lion — when the banner waves you see him rising above the heights of battle while battle is fierce. Drawn to its water-troughs with affection and toward its disasters with purpose, suckled at its breast. God whose word is 'Be and it is' placed the caliphate in him. We had seen it for him in our hearts — and its manifest and hidden signs were before him. And for that it was said from intuitions: a clear truth — and in some hearts there are eyes. We knew since his youth that he was the trustee of the Lord of the worlds who is trustworthy. O son of the caliphs — your mantle is full of nobility that melts rain-clouds, and tenderness. A light from the past upon you, as if it is a light upon it from the Prophet made manifest. Al-Saffah and al-Mansur and al-Mahdi and the Protected One and al-Ma'mun are raised by you. He who lives in the light of the Prophet's family knows they are a filled assembly before the filled assembly of heaven. A people whose inheritance has been established for them, with a wall of the Quran, a fortress upon it. In them is the serenity of their Lord and His book, and his imamate and his grieved name. And Islam will thank what you have bestowed upon it — and God is guarantor of fulfilling that.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a small ditch dug around a tent to channel away rainwater; its abandonment marks the ruined campsite
the three stones of a hearth; their survival marks where people once cooked and lived
pouring heavily, flowing copiously — describing abundant rain
a heavy shower of rain, a downpour
the divine tranquility, the Shekhinah — God's special blessing and calm presence
al-Wathiq, Abbasid Caliph (r. 842-847 CE), the son of al-Mu'tasim, praised here
caliphs, viceroys — plural of خليفة

context: A long panegyric to the Abbasid Caliph al-Wathiq (r. 842-847 CE), the son of al-Mu'tasim, beginning with a conventional nasib over ruins. Abu Tammam enumerates al-Wathiq's caliphal ancestors — al-Saffah, al-Mansur, al-Mahdi, al-Ma'mun — and grounds the caliph's legitimacy in divine appointment and prophetic light. The poem explicitly invokes the Quranic verse 'Be and it is' (kun fa-yakun) for the caliph's authority.

titleAr: يا أيها السائلي عن عرصة الجود
titleEn: O You Who Ask About the Arena of Generosity

verses.sadr: يا أيها السائلي عن عرصة الجود
verses.ajuz: إن فتى الباس داود بن داود

verses.sadr: فتى متى ما ينلك الدهر صالحة

translationEn: O you who ask about the arena of generosity — the young man of valor is Dawud ibn Dawud. A youth: whenever fate brings you something good, he says to its like from his deeds: come back again. He has become praised among the people for his nobility — may he never cease wearing the garment of the envied.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the arena of generosity, the field or court where generosity is practiced
valor, martial strength, the capacity to endure hardship and fight
a garment, a shirt, a tunic — wearing the garment of envy means being so noble others envy you
the envied man — in classical Arabic culture, being envied is a sign of true excellence

context: A brief three-verse epigram praising Dawud ibn Dawud. The tight compression of this piece illustrates Abu Tammam's ability to deliver a complete act of panegyric in minimal verses. The final wish — that the patron always be envied — reflects the classical Arabic value that envy is the shadow of true greatness.

titleEn: The Emir Is the Refuge of the Offender and the Criminal

verses.sadr: إذا ثوى جار قوم في بلادهم
كم صامت صامتي الضرب فزت به

verses.ajuz: فقد رأى محسنا من غير إحسان

verses.sadr: هل أنت صائن عرضي لي ومفلتي
ذو الود مني وذو القربى بمنزلة
لاقى إذن غرسهم أكدى ثرى وجرت

verses.ajuz: فهم وإن فرقوا في الأرض جيراني

verses.sadr: أفي أخ لي فرد لا قسيم له
verses.ajuz: في خالص الود من سري وإعلاني

verses.sadr: مسلط حيث لا سلطان لي ويدي
ما أنس لا أنس قولا قاله رجل
نل الثريا أو الشعرى فليس فتى

translationEn: The emir is the sanctuary of the criminal and the offender, and the recovery point of the aspirations of the bound captive. When a neighbor of a people dwells in their lands, his neighbor is settled at the peak of Ghumdan castle. How many a silent one — silent as the sword-blow — I won from him, and jewelry from benevolence adorned me. He gives and thus earns me praise through his gift, while my inherited wealth remains abundant and my acquired possessions intact. So whoever among all the peoples sees me has seen a benefactor without having done any benefit. The date-palm fruit of one other than him — its thousand trees were planted by another, and one other than him built the palace where he lives. Will you preserve my honor for me and release me with my face's water intact from Sulayman? A youth with all the qualities of youth, and a brother to calamities and trials and reversals of fortune. A sharpener of thought when its cutting edges grow blunt, and a polisher of minds and intellects. He who loves me and he who is kin to me are at the same rank — my brothers and companions are equal to me. Do not wear out my character among them while my fire blazes and time has renewed my condition twice over. In my first blameworthy age I knew them — shall I now disown them in my second age?! Then their planting would meet sterile ground from me, and their suspicions of me would run in the worst arena. A band whose culture neighbored my culture — they are, though scattered across the earth, my neighbors. Our souls are in one place while our bodies dwell in Syria or Khurasan. Many a man far in dwelling-places is always close in spirit to my soul — and one who is near is not truly near. Is it in a singular brother of mine who has no equal in pure affection from my secret and my open life — that my bucket and my rope-lengths return from your frequented sea with needs unmet?! Given authority where I have no authority, while my hand is bound from benefit though the authority is mine. Like fire cold in its wood, yet when separated from it it blazes with an unflagging flame. What I shall never forget — I shall never forget a saying a man spoke after which I lowered my gaze and my eyelids. Reach the Pleiades or Sirius — for no youth is truly a youth who did not support fifty men by means of one man!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sanctuary, place of refuge — a place where one is safe from retribution
Ghumdan castle, a legendary multi-story castle of the ancient kings of Yemen — symbol of lofty protection
the Pleiades star cluster — a high marker of aspiration and fame
Sirius, the brightest star — symbol of the very peak of glory
ropes used to draw water from a well — here metaphor for the means of obtaining support
one who weakens, who slackens, who becomes exhausted — نار غير وانية = unflagging fire
my acquired possessions, property earned through effort — from اقتنى

context: A poem combining praise of an emir with a lengthy meditation on friendship and loyalty. Abu Tammam distinguishes between genuine friends bonded by culture and spirit (who remain neighbors even when physically separated across Syria and Khurasan) and those who only appear close. The poem contains a celebrated final verse exhorting that a true man must support many through his single self — a statement of the Arab ethic of collective responsibility.

titleEn: I Am Dying and If I Die I Die from My Love
translationEn: I am dying — and if I die, I die from my love. For a gazelle of the clan of the Afw in whom there is divine power. A slave of creation — before his hands lies the kingdom of heaven. He withholds the kiss from the one who desires him, and the greeting is his sustenance. If you beseech him with words, then his response is silence.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: divine power, omnipotence — here applied to the beloved's irresistible beauty
the divine kingdom, the realm of sovereignty — the highest level of existence in Islamic cosmology
a gazelle — the classical Arabic metaphor for a beautiful, slender, large-eyed beloved
supplication, beseeching, humble prayer — here used for the lover's plea

context: A mujazza' al-ramal poem using the language of religious devotion (divine power, kingdom of heaven, supplication, silence) to describe erotic love — a practice rooted in the Sufi-influenced ghazal tradition and carried through in Abu Tammam's more playful compositions. The split-verse format creates a sense of breathlessness appropriate to the dying lover.

titleEn: If You Wish You May Follow Kindness with Kindness

verses.sadr: فقد - لعمري - فتقت الماء من حجر

verses.ajuz: أن يقتني مع رضوى طود ثهلان

verses.sadr: لو كان وصما لراج أن يكون له
verses.ajuz: ركنان ما هز رمح فيه نصلان

verses.sadr: ولم يعد من الأبطال ليث وغى

translationEn: If you wish, you may follow one kindness with another — and your generosity would be of breath and fragrant basil. For by my life, you have drawn water from rock in a plateau and bent the branch for the picker. So ask our Sulayman — may our lives be his ransom — O you whose Sulayman tends my Sulayman! It is enough for him through you — save that his ambition is to acquire with Radwa also the mountain of Tuhlan. Were he a permanent mark, it would be right for him to have two pillars — no lance was ever thrust that had two spearheads. No warrior from among the heroes could be called a lion of battle when two coats of mail were laced upon him on the morning of war.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: breath and fragrant basil — Quranic phrase (56:89) for Paradise's gifts; here: the best and most pleasant of generosity
Radwa, a mountain near Medina and the Hijaz — symbol of massive firm support
Tuhlan, a mountain in Najd — combined with Radwa as the two great mountains of Arab pride
a permanent brand, a scar — here metaphor for the patron's indelible impression on history
you bent down, you pulled down — used of bending a branch to bring fruit within reach
you drew water from rock — a miracle image (like Moses) applied to the patron's extraordinary generosity

context: A short madih poem with an interesting internal structure: the poet asks the patron to intercede for a third party named Sulayman with the patron's own associate named Sulayman — a play on the repeated name. The poem uses Moses-like imagery (water from rock) and Quranic language (ruh wa-rayhan) to exalt the patron's kindness.

titleEn: A Moon That Smiled Revealing Fresh Pearls

verses.sadr: ما زال يقصر كل حسن دونه

translationEn: A moon that smiled revealing fresh-grown pearls — and I kept gazing at it with the eye of one stupefied. Every beauty kept falling short of it until it exceeded all the attributes a describer might give. Beauty itself prostrated before his face when it saw the bewilderment of minds at his surpassing beauty. I truly hope to attain his union through his kindness — and to the humiliation of the gloater's nose.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pearls, or silver beads resembling pearls — the beloved's teeth
newly grown, just emerging — said of fresh teeth or new plant growth
one who is stupefied, who stares in bewilderment — struck dumb by beauty
one who describes, who attributes qualities — the poet who tries to describe the beloved
to the rubbing of the gloater's nose in the dust — a classical phrase meaning: to their humiliation and disappointment

context: A four-verse ghazal in kamil meter presenting the beloved as so beautiful that even beauty itself prostrates before his face. The conceit of beauty prostrating (sajada) before the beloved is a bold theological metaphor. The final verse strikes the common ghazal note of hope for union against the wishes of the gloating onlookers (shamitin).

titleEn: The Four Encampments of the Tender Ones Have Been Effaced

verses.ajuz: وهند بني هند وسعدي بني سعد
جواه فليس الوجد إلا من الوجد
بمهرية تنباع في السير أو تخدي
بنو الحرب لا ينبو ثراهم ولا يكدي

verses.sadr: ومن شك أن الجود والبأس فيهم
verses.ajuz: كمن شك في أن الفصاحة في نجد
لهم مثل ذاك السيف من ذاك الغمد
ولا قدحت في خاطري روعة الرد

verses.sadr: يرى الوعد أخزى العار إن هو لم تكن
فلو كان ما يعطيه غيثا لأمطرت
verses.ajuz: سحائبه من غير برق ولا رعد
له مخلب ورد من الأسد الورد

verses.sadr: من القوم جعد أبيض الوجه والندى

verses.ajuz: لكيما يكون الحر من خول العبد

verses.sadr: ليالي بات العز في غير بيته
verses.ajuz: وعظم وغد القوم في الزمن الوغد

verses.sadr: وما قصدوا إذ يسحبون على المنى
وراموا دم الإسلام لا من جهالة
verses.ajuz: ولا خطإ بل حاولوه على عمد

verses.sadr: فمجوا به سما وصابا ولو نأت
verses.ajuz: سيوفك عنهم كان أحلى من الشهد
ولم يجدوا إذ ذاك من ذاك من بد
كما أحكمت في النظم واسطة العقد

verses.sadr: وكنت هناك الأحنف الطب في بني
وهل أسد العريس إلا الذي له
فهم منك في جيش قريب قدومه
verses.ajuz: عليهم وهم من يمن رأيك في جند
وزدت غداة الروع في نجدة النجد

verses.sadr: رأيت حروب الناس هزلا وإن علا
فيا طيب مجناها ويا برد وقعها
verses.ajuz: على الكبد الحرى وزاد على البرد
وأوردت ذود العز في أول الورد
به فهو في جهد وما هو في جهد
أحق بأن يرعاه في سالف العهد

verses.sadr: فيا حسن ذاك البر إذ أنا حاضر
verses.ajuz: وياطيب ذاك القول والذكر من بعدي

verses.sadr: وما كنت ذا فقر إلى صلب ماله
verses.ajuz: ما كان حفص بالفقير إلى حمدي
فصاغ لها سلكا بهيا من الرفد

verses.sadr: فما فاتني ما عنده من حبائه
verses.ajuz: ولا فاته من فاخر الشعر ما عندي

verses.sadr: وكم من كريم قد تخضر قلبه
verses.ajuz: بذاك الثناء الغض في طرق المجد

translationEn: The four encampments of the tender soft-limbed ones have been effaced — each with a slender waist and a plaited figure. The Salma of Salaman, and the Umru of Amir, and the Hind of Banu Hind, and the Sa'di of Banu Sa'd. Dwellings that made every stingy eye pour forth, and that caused sorrows to trample every hard heart. Turn the noses of your Archabi camels and ease them onto that gentle sandy hill and the solitary landmark. And do not ask me about a love whose burning you two have tasted — for longing only comes from longing. I settled my loads toward the land of al-Jadidi on a pure-bred she-camel that either ambles or strides. She heads for Hafs, the torch of war, and his kinsmen — sons of war whose soil neither repels nor fails. He who doubts that generosity and valor are in them is like one who doubts that eloquence is in Najd. I couched my mounts in their courts and their open space, and my delegation dwelt in their homes. To their sword, Hafs — and always drawn for them is a sword like that from that very sheath. I came to no door whose dogs disowned me, nor did I cling to intermediaries thereafter. Thus I awoke — neither did the humiliation of begging touch me, nor did the shock of refusal strike my thought. He sees the promise as the greater shame if his gifts do not arrive before the promise. Were what he gives a rain, its clouds would pour without lightning or thunder. A trainer of horses — at battle he always has a lion's claw, the claw of the rose-colored lion. A man from the people — curly-haired, white of face, generous — and the fingers sought from him are not stiff. And you, when Khurasan had disgorged its disease and its regions had rotted like rotting skin. And its riffraff — narrow-eyed folk — against the Arabs who are the true ones — so that the free man would become the servant of a slave. Those nights when glory dwelt outside its own house and the base of the people grew great in a base age. And they aimed, dragging their desires on their hopes, only at the inheritor of the mantle. They sought the blood of Islam — not from ignorance nor from error, but they attempted it deliberately. So they spat venom and colocynth upon it — and had your swords kept away from them, it would have been sweeter than honey. You joined Qahtan and all of Adnan together, and they found in that no escape from that. So the tribes through you became the most united in affection, as the central bead draws the necklace together in order. You were there the Ahnaf — the skillful one — among all the tribes of Tamim, and al-Muhallab among the Azd. You were the Abu Ghassan, the lord of Wa'il, on the evening when the alliance drew near its alliance by binding. When the stars of the Arabs extinguished the darkness, they traveled as followers of your fortunate star. Is the lion of the thicket any other than the one whose excellence is where the lions gather? So they are from you in an army whose arrival upon them is near — and they are from the blessing of your counsel in a host. You weighted the skull of the coward toward ruin and you added to the valor of the brave on the morning of fear. You saw the wars of the people as jest, though their brilliance rose high — but that war was the reliance of earnest resolve. O how sweet was its plucking and how cool was its striking upon the burning liver — beyond all coolness. You raised an eye that without you would have been downcast, and you watered the herds of glory at the first watering.
