secret grief, internal anguish
depression, despondency
calamity of loss, grievous bereavement
a lion, especially a courageous lion
his two cheeks, the sides of his face
the pure-bred, the pure in lineage

context: An elegy for Ahmad ibn Harun, a close companion and friend of Abu Tammam. The poem is deeply personal — Abu Tammam calls him not merely a friend but his chosen companion above all others — and mourns the paradox that death struck precisely at the moment of his full flowering in glory, wisdom, and youth.

titleAr: وفى البكا بالعهد اذ لم يكن
titleEn: Weeping Kept the Covenant

verses.sadr: وفى البكا بالعهد اذ لم يكن

translationEn: Weeping kept the covenant when patience had neither pledge nor covenant. You have spoiled the beauty of the fresh narcissus since the narcissus and the rose come together.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: kept the covenant, fulfilled the promise
a solemn covenant, a binding pledge
the narcissus flower; also a metaphor for beautiful eyes
the rose; also the rosy blush of the cheek
you have soured, you have spoiled the pleasure of

context: A compact two-verse fragment (miqta) in which weeping takes the role of a faithful companion when patience has abandoned the field. The second verse layers imagery: 'narcissus and rose' together usually mean beauty, but here they become something painful, as the beloved's presence is now bittersweet.

titleEn: Muhammad, I Would Be Blameworthy Without You

verses.ajuz: إذا ما لساني خانني فيك أوشكري

verses.sadr: لئن بقيت لي فيك آثار منطق
verses.ajuz: لقد بقيت ثار كفيك في دهري
لأمر العلى فاخترت شكري على عذري
بدائعها ما استحسن الناس من شعري
وذكرتني ما قد نسيت من الشكر

translationEn: Muhammad — I would indeed be blameworthy after all of this if my tongue betrayed me in praising you or my gratitude failed. If there remain in me traces of speech about you, then the traces of your hands' bounty remain in my life. You have met the reversals of fate on my behalf, following the command of excellence, and I have chosen my gratitude over my excuses. You have favored me in calamities with benefactions as if their hands had been opened from the sea. Noble qualities that, if they had been part of my poetry, would have made the wonders of it outshine all that people have deemed beautiful in my verse. You have taught me to clothe praise upon its worthy recipients, and reminded me of the gratitude I had forgotten.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: blameworthy, reviled
the reversals of fate, the vicissitudes of time
calamities, misfortunes that befall
benefactions, crafted deeds of generosity
its hands, its benefits (metaphor for the reach of generosity)
wonders, exquisite novelties

context: A short but dense poem of gratitude to Muhammad, in which Abu Tammam reflects that the patron's qualities are so extraordinary that if they were expressed as poetry they would surpass all his own verse. The closing verse — 'you reminded me of gratitude I had forgotten' — is a graceful compliment suggesting the patron embodies virtue itself.

titleAr: جفوف البلى أسرعت في الغصن الرطب
titleEn: The Drying of Decay Hastened Upon the Wet Branch

verses.sadr: جفوف البلى أسرعت في الغصن الرطب
verses.ajuz: وخطب الردى والموت أبرحت من خطب

verses.sadr: لقد شرقت في الشرق بالموت غادة
verses.ajuz: تعوضت منها غربة الدار في الغرب
هلال عليه نسج ثوب من الترب
من الكرب روح الموت شر من الكرب

verses.sadr: لقد نزلت ضنكا من اللحد والثرى
verses.ajuz: ولو كان رحب الذرع ماكان بالرحب
فقد نقلت بعدي عن البعد والقرب

translationEn: The drying of decay has hastened upon the wet branch, and the calamity of ruin and death has brought the severest of calamities. In the East, a woman choked on death, and I found my compensation in the exile of her dwelling in the West. He clothed me in a garment of grief and sorrow — a crescent whose garment was then woven by the soil. I say, when they say she has found rest in her death from distress: the relief of death is worse than the distress. She has descended into the narrowness of the tomb and the earth — and even were it of wide expanse, it would not be spacious. I used to hope for nearness while she was distant — and now she has been moved beyond nearness and distance. She has a dwelling beneath the earth, yet I have long known her to have a dwelling between the ribs and the heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: decay, wearing away, the process of deterioration
choked on, was afflicted in the East
anguish, choking distress
narrowness, constriction
the ribs, the chest-cavity
a crescent moon (also a person's name here)

context: An elegy for a woman — likely a relative — who died in the East while the poet was in the West. Abu Tammam rejects the conventional consolation that death is a relief, insisting instead that her tomb is a narrow prison, while closing with the touching statement that her true dwelling was always within his chest.

titleEn: Separation Has Snatched Ahmad ibn Yazid
translationEn: Separation has snatched Ahmad ibn Yazid — the deeds of the days are not praiseworthy. Estrangement has distanced one whom I shall not name, and today I am in the near-yet-far. Two separations have struck me: one from a separation and another from a rebuffing. He who was merely absent and missed by the eye is not like the one who is present yet lost.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to snatch away, to steal by stealth
separation, parting, distance
estrangement, abandonment, cutting off
rebuffing, turning away, rejection
the one who is present yet lost — someone there in body but gone in spirit

context: A four-verse poem lamenting two simultaneous losses: Ahmad ibn Yazid who has departed, and an unnamed person (a beloved) who has turned away. The final verse — distinguishing the physically absent from the 'present-yet-lost' — is a celebrated example of Abu Tammam's philosophical concision.

titleAr: لا أنت أنت ولا الديار ديار
titleEn: You Are No Longer You, the Abodes No Longer Abodes

verses.sadr: لا أنت أنت ولا الديار ديار
إذ لا صدوف ولا كنود اسماهما
في حيث يمتهن الحديث لذي الصبا
إذ في القتادة وهي أبخل أيكة

verses.ajuz: إذ لاح أن الصدق منه نهار

verses.sadr: لولا جلاد أبي سعيد لم يزل
حتى التوى من نقع قسطلها على
إلا تكن حصرت فقد أضحى لها
لو طاوعتك الخيل لم تقفل بها

verses.ajuz: أو تثن عنه البيض وهي حرار

verses.sadr: إلا تفر فقد أقمت وقد رأت
في حيث تستمع الهرير إذا علا
فمضى لو أن النار دونك خاضها
أن لست نعم الجار للسنن الأولى
verses.ajuz: إلا إذا ما كنت بئس الجار

verses.sadr: لا يأسفون إذا هم سمنت لهم
والبيض تعلم أن دينا لم يضع
ومودتي لك لا تعار بلى إذا
ولذاك شعري فيك قد سمعوا به

translationEn: You are no longer you, and the abodes are no longer abodes — passion has lightened and desires have departed. Dwelling near the ruins and their people was once for a time the sweetness of a watering-place — now they are seas. Days when those idols would make his eye bleed within them, and the moons would madden his mind. When there was no one named Suduf or Kanud as with the two meant, nor was Nawar Nawar. Fair-skinned ones — when they glance unveiled they are images; when they are looked at, they are herds. Where speech is made plain for the young man, and secrets and hidden things are guarded. When even in the thorny qatad, the most miserly of thickets, there was fruit — and when the branch of time was still pure gold. The news has become clear from its pure substance, and the cities have rejoiced in your conquests. A report whose light has cleared the rust from hearts, when it became clear that its truth is daylight. Without the sword of Abu Said, the frontier's chest would never cease being exposed without a garment. You drove the thoroughbreds as if they were eagles in the villages of Derulia with their nests. Until the whirlwind of their dust-column twisted over the walls of Constantinople. You kindled a fire before the Bosphorus for its people, whose sparks are behind the strait. Even if you did not besiege it, it has come to endure from the fear of a sudden assault something like a siege. Had the horses obeyed you completely, you would not have returned — and the lock has sharpness and no nail. When they met you, they leaned on one another and made excuses in flight, but their excuses availed them nothing. Here is the fire of battle blazing, and here is an army with a clamor, and there is a raid. They were humbled by your assault, which is in their eyes like death that comes with no disgrace. When you issued from the mountain-passes toward them with a vast army that shook the earth. If he sets out at dawn, the landmarks of the road-markers guide him; if he travels by night, the stars are his lighthouse. The White Baths are their appointment, the Fortress is the decisive end, and the Strait is their password. They knew that their raiding was like his raiding of them, and that his raiding of them means destruction. So walking is a whisper and calling is a gesture — from fear of your retribution — and conversation is a secret. Only if the tips of spears do not reach Manuyil or the white blades do not turn him back while still hot. For he has wished that every city were a deaf mountain and every fortress a cave. Unless you flee — but you have stayed, and your eyes have seen the measure of war and how it is avoided. Where you hear the roaring when it rises high, and see the dust of death when it is stirred. Look with a brave eye — and you will know that the standing-ground is where the flight is. When their remnants came to you, you reinforced them with the foremost of tears, and they were many. You gave the examples of the humiliated, though you see that other than that is the deficiency and the bitterness. Patience is more beautiful, and fate is imposed — so accept it, for in its evil there is a choice. Far from it — a courageous man pulled at the reins against you, giving the spear-points all they choose. He went on — had fire been before him he would have waded through it with the sword, unless it were the fire. Until the right returns and it is the one that avenges itself upon you, and Islam has no blood-vengeance among you. God bless Abu Said — he is pure to the guest with no adulteration in him. When you settled at the frontier, it became high, and for the Byzantines that neighborhood was a neighborhood. They became certain, when your sea surged and that roar rose high and that sting became mighty, that you were not a good neighbor to the old ways unless you were a terrible neighbor. Vigilant — the polytheists fear his fervor; humble — the mighty submit to him. He pacifies his mounts when his journeys are delayed, and his concerns are journeys. He travels by night when worries travel, as if he were the star of darkness, and he raids when raids are made. His lineage soared through him among a people for whom the pivot of battle is set and revolving. They do not regret that their honors have grown fat while their lives grow lean. He plants in his planting ground his supporters — at the combat, they are as if they were the Ansar. He has discarded the merchants' character, though they too, by what they have stored for him, will indeed be merchants. Veteran men whom he has given to drink from his valor — yet when they meet the foe, they are as if inexperienced. He returns with joy to the spear-thrust — his encounter is a danger when the quivering spear quivers. The white swords know that no faith has been lost since they were drawn, and no honor has been more neglected. When the curved bows send their arrows flying like locusts spreading when disturbed — their shafts have guaranteed and their strings have pledged that they will snap the strings of the enemy. So leave the path, O sons of the path, to one who knows how the great army is led. Even if your long arms fell short of him, how can they reach when they are short? He is the star of Islam — whatever darkness he pierces, the core of unbelief within it turns hollow. You left their land with your cavalry in the heat of battle until even its most fortified parts were a race-track for them. You settled there calmly and at ease, until we thought it was your own home. The king is pleased with you for it, its bones are mended, and the world has stability in you. I see the meadows pregnant and giving birth since you were among them, and the clouds are ten-month camels. Our days are polished at their edges by you, and the nights are all pre-dawns. Your suppliants are moistened for the suppliants, and the visitors come gently to your visitors. My ambitions are suspended by you, their necks in chains — for loyalty is a bond. My love for you cannot be borrowed — unless it is the blood of the heart that can be lent. As for other people — my sitting-garment has not changed at their departure, whether they have gone to Najd or Tihama. And for that, my poetry about you — they have heard it as enchantment, while my poetry about them is mere verse. Live in safety — may destruction not cease to favor you among us, and may fate fall short of you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: desires, needs, things longed for
Constantinople (modern Istanbul), the Byzantine capital
the Bosphorus strait
Manuel, a Byzantine military commander
the Helpers of Medina who aided the Prophet
the blood of the heart, the innermost part of the heart
those who seek generosity, petitioners

context: One of Abu Tammam's grandest qasidas, addressed to Abu Said, a commander who led a campaign against Constantinople, reaching the walls of the Byzantine capital. The poem opens with a nasib recalling the beauty of youth and beloved women, then transitions into one of the most vivid battle descriptions in classical Arabic poetry, celebrating the terror the commander struck into the Byzantines.

titleEn: Repeat the Lamentation, O Wailing Woman
translationEn: Repeat the lamentation, O wailing woman, repeat — and add to your weeping, then add! Rise unveiled among the unveiled women, baring throats and cheeks. This is the calamity that has invented disasters and said to the eyes of both mankind and jinn: weep freely! Alas — Khorasan has been bereaved of its youth on the morning Umayr ibn al-Walid was laid to rest. Alas — bereaved of an answering, generous one! Alas — bereaved of a lavish bestower! Know that generosity and giving have settled where you have settled in the depths of the plain. My life be your ransom — a king whom his fate struck with an arrow of true destruction. The thick of battle was lifted from him with his face stained by his copious blood. O sea of death, you have taken from him the sea of generosity in a year of hardness. O lion of death, you have mauled from him — on the morning you mauled him — the lion of lions. Did you indeed maul the valiant hero from him? Yes, and also the slayer of the valiant hero. He faced the spears while the faces of death showed themselves in red and black. The veiled man among them had no head veiled — save that he had veiled himself in iron. What a battle! A grand calamity that lent grief and yearning to the patience of the patient man. What a battlefield! It gifted a burning thirst to our livers for all eternity. Yet our prince spared nothing of counsel and justice for the subjects and the soldiers. He poured the gift of his palms upon them and was generous with the newly acquired and the inherited. He faced death in their stead in the open until death gave him to drink from a pool of colocynth. They did not triumph over him until they received as their guest black vulture-eagles and desert hyenas — with a stabbing into their throats, persistent, and a striking on their heads, relentless. O Tuesday, we drank your morning as a terrible morning in its arrival. O Tuesday, we have been struck in you by the loss of the sturdy support. How many eyes of ours have you heated, and how many fortunes of ours have you made stumble! Your birds gave no omen of good crossing, and your stars did not rise auspiciously. O king clothed in the garment of death in a dug-out grave — I came to the courtyard of your gate and was seized by a grief between the throat and the jugular vein. I saw in it riderless mounts and horses standing at the threshold. They were equipment meant either for freeing a captive or for killing a stubborn tyrant. I saw your hopeful ones — upon them there have descended visitations that led them up difficult slopes. And the fortunes that were rising with you have now sunk at the side of others. All of them have prepared provisions and set a station at your side, and packed a riding-camel for a sitting-passenger. The delegations to you have come to a standstill — no benefit remains for one who seeks benefit. Indeed the eyes of generosity have been scalded at what has been intended, and the brilliant foreheads of poetry have been struck down.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a wailing woman lamenting loudly
Khorasan, the northeastern province of the Abbasid caliphate
one thrown down, felled by destruction
large black vultures, carrion birds
colocynth, a bitter desert plant; a metaphor for a lethal, bitter drink
the threshold, the door-sill of a tent or house
the two weights — mankind and jinn together

context: A long elegy for Umayr ibn al-Walid, a military commander in Khorasan. Abu Tammam mourns him as the sea and lion of generosity, curses the Tuesday on which he died, and vividly describes arriving at the abandoned courtyard where the patron's mounts stand riderless. It is one of his most dramatic elegies.

titleEn: I Shall Never Eat Apples
translationEn: I shall never eat apples all my life — even if they were plucked for me from the gardens of eternity. By God, I am not leaving them from aversion — rather, I am leaving them for the cheeks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: apples; also a metaphor for rosy, apple-like cheeks
I harvested it, I plucked it
the gardens of eternity, paradise
aversion, hatred, dislike
cheeks; here the beloved's apple-like cheeks

context: A witty two-verse epigram exploiting the resemblance between apples and rosy cheeks: the poet swears off apples not from dislike but from devotion — he saves them (metaphorically) for the beloved's cheeks. A classic example of Abu Tammam's playful wit in the shorter lyric form.

titleEn: The Season Grows Tender, Spring Arrives

verses.sadr: وندى إذا ادهنت به لمم الثرى

verses.ajuz: لو أن حسن الروض كان يعمر

verses.sadr: أولا ترى الأشياء إن هي غيرت
ماعاد أصفر ، بعد إذ هو أخضر
في الأرض من عدل الإمام وجوده
ما زلت أعلم أن عقدة أمرها
لم يبق مبدى موحش إلا ارتوى

translationEn: The edges of time have grown tender and glisten, while the earth adorns itself with its jewels and sways. The vanguard of summer has arrived, praiseworthy, while winter's hand is fresh and never ungrateful. Were it not for what winter planted with its hand, summer would have met only dry brambles bearing no fruit. How many a night did it nurse the lands with itself, and how many a day its torrential rain poured down. Rain that dissolves the clear sky after it, and a clear sky that nearly rains with its lushness. Two rains — the rain-stars are a visible rain before you, and the clear weather a hidden rain. A dew, when it anoints the earth's locks, you imagine the clouds came to it and excused themselves. This spring of ours in the nineteenth year of a pilgrimage — truly you are indeed the most flowering spring. The days would not strip away beauty, were it that the garden's splendor could last. Do you not see that when things are altered they grow ugly, and the earth's beauty turns when it changes? When it turned from yellow after being green — this is the making of One whose wondrous craftsmanship — the character of the Imam and his effortless guidance is a creation taller than the spring, as if. In the land from the Imam's justice and his generosity, and from the tender new growth, lamps blaze. Meadows are forgotten — but his deeds are forever remembered across the passage of nights. When a calamity darkens, the Caliph is the eye of guidance, and the caliphate is a sacred preserve for him. Its movements have multiplied through him, and you see it as though pausing to reflect. I have always known that the knot of its affairs has been selecting in his hand since you entrusted it. Time has settled — no blameworthy hand for misfortunes, no herd to be startled. He has ordered the lands so they now resemble a necklace in which justice is the jewel. No desolate wasteland remained without being quenched, as if from his mention he were present. A king in whose days glory itself goes astray, and what is plentiful seems little in the breezes of his generosity. After him, nights will find it hard to afflict the afflicted with their vicissitudes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to glisten, undulate, shimmer (of something tender or soft)
dry, broken sticks; withered plants bearing no fruit
pouring, flowing heavily (of rain or tears)
rain-stars, the lunar stations associated with seasonal rains
lushness, freshness, vitality
sacred enclosure, protected precinct
the one in hardship, the destitute
wonders, marvels of craftsmanship

context: A qasidat madih in the kamil meter opening with a vivid description of spring turning to summer before pivoting to praise of the Abbasid caliph, whose justice and generosity are compared to the life-giving rains. The nineteen-year reference likely marks a significant anniversary of the caliph's reign.

titleEn: Elegy for Khalid, Lament for Generosity

verses.sadr: ألا غرب دمع ناصر لي على الأسى
verses.ajuz: ألا حر شعر في الغليل مساعدي

verses.sadr: فلم تكرم العينان إن لم تسامحا
verses.ajuz: ولاطاب فرع الشعر إن لم يساعد

verses.sadr: تقلص ظل العرف في كل بلدة
أقم ثم حط الرحل والظن إنه
verses.ajuz: مضت قبلة الأسفار منء بعد خالد
على ألف ألف مقرب لا مباعد

verses.sadr: فأين شفاء الثغر أين إذا القنا
verses.ajuz: خطرن على عضو من الملك فاسد ؟

verses.sadr: وأين الجلاد الهبر إذ ليس سيد
verses.ajuz: يقي جلدة الأحساب إن لم يجالد ؟

verses.sadr: ومن لم يكن ينفك يغبق سيفه
verses.ajuz: دما عاندا من نحر ليث معاند ؟
ولا زال مهتز الربي غير هامد

verses.sadr: أقام به من حي بكر بن وائل
فكم غال ذاك الترب لي ولمعشري

verses.ajuz: فما يشتكى وجد إلى غير واجد

verses.sadr: فما جانب الدنيا بسهل ولا الضحى
حمدت الليالي إذ حمت سرحنا به
verses.ajuz: ولست لها في غير ذاك بحامد

verses.sadr: من المكرمين الخيل فيهم ولم يكن
إذا شب نارا أقعدت كل قائم
verses.ajuz: وقام لها من خوفه كل قاعد

verses.sadr: ألا القوا مقاليد البلاد وهل لها

verses.ajuz: مع السيف يدمى نصله غير مارد

verses.sadr: وما كثرت في بلدة قصد القنا

translationEn: By God, shall I endure after Khalid — he who was the lamp of glory, the star of all praiseworthy deeds? The tripod-stone of the Arabs has been torn away, by which the very boulders were split. Will no tear come flowing to assist me in grief, will no heartfelt verse aid me in my burning sorrow? The eyes are not noble unless they are generous with tears, and the branch of poetry is not sweet unless it lends help. Let the rhymes weep their lament after Khalid — the weeping of those lost to generosity, searching. Their cheek-locks, when presented before Khalid, were like the cheeks of virgin maidens standing tall. And hunting the eloquent had a sweetness for his heart not found in hunting wild things. He saw the venom of speech as if sometimes laced with the venom of black serpents. The shade of beneficence has contracted in every land, and the lamp of odes has been extinguished across the world. What shame for one who traveled to him and returned, and the embarrassment of every delegate sent and every visitor. O noble one whom death fulfilled its vow against, striking terror into every brave and noble soul! Tomorrow generosity's pearls will be withheld after you, and the giving hands' streams will run dry. O you who watch a deceiving lightning and listen to a thunderclap of false promise among the thunders — halt, unload your saddle; travel's direction has gone after Khalid. The earth's back tilted the day it was deprived of its collapsed mountain beneath the wide tracts. The frontier now has a darkened hue after a fair scene, and the sky runs unsettled and unstill. O year of calamities, you have broken faith — after the people of hope called you the year of benefits! Time has bitten the tribes after him with iron fangs dripping persistent venom. Qahtanids were draped in drought and Nizar bent under a scanty life, defiant. Upon what noble nose have we been bested, what hand and arm have parted from us! It is as if we lost a million armored warriors upon a million horses, near not far. O loneliness of the world, though it was once companionable, and the solitude of all within it for the fall of one! The pride of horses has passed, and destruction departed with the most precious of souls from Maad and the fathers. Where now is the frontier's cure, when lances quiver over a diseased limb of the kingdom? And where is the fierce swordplay, when no master guards the skin of honor without combat? Who will make authority the lifeline of his own vein, who will string the extremities like a necklace? Who never ceased but gave his sword a nightly drink of the blood of a defiant lion? My soul be ransom for him whose grave lies in Rabia's land — may the hills' swaying never be still. Khalid established among Bakr ibn Wa'il's clan a well-watered generosity, verdant in the wake of his promises. What qualities were shrouded by his burial-cloths — springs of inexhaustible sweet water! Dispositions that were like frontier-garrisons, undermined — and he had stood guard over them like a warrior. How much did that earth take from me and my circle and all people, of the new and the old! O two grey-hairs — that crescent will not rise again for us, nor will those clouds return. O two noble grey-hairs: no longer any benefit for the hateful, no longer any gain for anything when glory departs with an ascender. O two grey-hairs — their fire of calamity spread universal, so grief cannot be told except to one who grieves. If it has ulcerated the eye of friend and companion, it has shaken the pillar of enemy and envier. If it bestowed sorrow upon kinfolk, it covered the cheeks of the distant in dust. No flank of the world lies easy, no morning is bright, and water of life is not cool. Yet, by my father, Prince Muhammad is the pivot of the millstone, the lamp of those arenas. I praise the nights for sheltering our pastures with him — in nothing else do I praise them. Upon him rests the guidance of Yazid and Khalid, and two lights shining from lineage and witness. Among those who honor horses — and horses are honored only by the nobly bred. The brother of war clothes it in blood as if the backs of its hills were draped in red garments. When he kindles a fire, every standing man sits down, and every seated man rises in fear. Tell the kings of Sijistan and those in Arran or Jurzan, not beseeching — lay down the keys of your lands; is there any bolt that its people can lock against those with the master-keys? Let not the demon of war deceive you — with the sword, its blade bleeds and is not rebellious. Do not splay your necks, for around them are Rudiniyya lances gathering the heads of the fugitives. Wherever lances concentrate in a land aiming, they do not lift except from the necks of the targeted.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one of the three stones of a cooking tripod; metaphor for a central pillar of support
large hard boulders, rocks
young women with full, upright figures; maidens just coming of age
to mix or taint (especially poison mixed into food or drink)
broad, flat, hard plains; wide open tracts of land
keys; reins of authority; control over something
Rudiniyya lances, named after Rudayna, famous for making excellent spears
Sijistan (Sistan), a region in eastern Iran/Afghanistan

context: One of Abu Tammam's great elegies, mourning the death of Khalid (likely Khalid ibn Yazid or a Khalid from the Bakr ibn Wa'il tribe), a patron and military commander. The poem moves from personal grief to cosmic lamentation before closing with praise and a fierce warning to frontier enemies of the new Abbasid commander Muhammad.

titleEn: One Desire I Have in This World

verses.ajuz: يا رب فاصفح لي عن الواحد

verses.sadr: لا تتركني فيه يا ذا العلى
يا رب إن فارقته بعد ما

translationEn: I have but one desire in this world, O Lord — forgive me for this one thing. Do not leave me in it, O Most High, as the talk of those who come and go. O Lord, if I part from him after all that has been — cast me down to the gloating envier. Then let my soul and his body together descend into the hollow of the grave-digger's pit.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a story, something talked about, a subject of gossip
one departing (from a watering-place); traveler leaving
one arriving (at a watering-place); traveler arriving
one who gloats over another's misfortune
his body, his physical frame
a hollow, a depression in the ground; a grave-pit
the one who digs the lahd (the niche in a grave)

context: A brief and intensely personal ghazal in which Abu Tammam beseeches God not to separate him from his beloved, expressing the wish that separation would be preferable to death itself. The poem is unusual in its direct address to God within a love context.

titleEn: Truth Is Radiant, Swords Are Blazing

verses.sadr: حتى إذا ما الله شق ضميره

verses.ajuz: من بين باد في الأنام وقار

verses.sadr: قد خص من أهل النفاق عصابة
واختار من سعد لعين بني أبي
ما كان لولا فحش غدر خيذر
ما زال سر الكفر بين ضلوعه
وكذاك أهل النار في الدنيا هم
ورأى به مالم يكن يوما رأى

verses.ajuz: في بعض ما حفروا من الآبار

verses.sadr: وثمود لو لم يدهنوا في ربهم
ثانيه في كبد السماء ولم يكن
verses.ajuz: لاثنين ثان إذ هما في الغار

translationEn: Truth is radiant and the swords are blazing — beware, beware the lion of the lair! A king who has become the neighbor of the Caliphate from among you, and God has commanded the protection of neighbors. How many a trial for a community has been stripped bare by its tyrant in obedience to the All-Powerful. Fate circled around Khidhr with the turning of destiny and tyranny lodged him in the house of ruin. How many a divine grace was in his keeping, yet it was as if in exile and captivity. He was clothed in the rags of blame and diminished, as a beautiful woman diminishes in rags. A wronged grace that God sought vengeance for — sufficient is the Lord of vengeance to exact it. He flattered the Commander of the Faithful with ornaments concealing beneath them the sting of a fierce serpent. He built his two pillars on treachery, but founded his foundation on the edge of a crumbling slope. Until God split open his inner being to reveal the concealed infidelity and persistence in sin. And he aimed his blade at this religion — then turned back, while truth had him by its claw-tips, reddened. This Prophet, who was his Lord's chosen, among the manifest and hidden among the people, had singled out a band of hypocrites who were more harmful than disbelievers. He chose from the fortunate — the accursed sons of Abu Sarh — for God's revelation, though not among the chosen. Until he was illumined by the flame of the sura raised to lift the veil from secrets. The Hashimites' caravans departed from Karbala bearing the heaviest of grievances. Al-Mukhtar healed them from him — yet in his religion, al-Mukhtar was not among the rightly guided. Until when his secrets were unveiled, they declared themselves innocent of his ear and eyes. Had it not been for Khidhr's vile treachery, there would have been no year of wickedness in Islam. The secret of unbelief remained between his ribs until it caught fire from the flint-spark of the firesteel. A fire that wraps his body in blazing flame, as if it had saffron-dyed the side of a wrap. Flames flew from it, whose scorching demolished his pillars utterly, without even dust. A kindled fire raised for the greatest of polytheists — whose light was never raised for the night-traveler. He had worshipped it alive, and in death was its very fuel, and will enter it with the wicked. It severed every joint of his body, and struck a spine-shattering blow to every vertebra. And so those who are people of fire in this world are mostly the people of fire on Judgment Day. O spectacle of rejoicing — its news spread to the furthest cities, carrying the joy of city-dwellers! They gazed at the top of his cross as if they had sighted the crescent on the eve of Eid. They breathed in the smoke rising from him as if it were intense ambergris and Dari musk. They spoke of his death as one in the desert speaks of consecutive rains. They congratulated each other as the two sanctuaries congratulate each other in years of hardship at the cheapest prices. The gloating of the gloater was once a shame, but now through this event it sheds the very garments of shame. The Caliph had lodged him in a corner of his heart, a sanctuary against misfortunes. He gave him the water of ease, freely, and laid him to sleep in security without alarm. He saw in him what Amr ibn Sha's never saw in Ararar before him. Then suddenly — the son of an unbeliever celebrates his unbelief with a longing like al-Farazdaq's longing for Nawar. And when he remembers him, he weeps as Ka'b of former times wept for Abu'l-Mughwar. His ornaments showed the Caliph that not every green wood is good gold. O you who has seized the hand of the unjust — follow one right with another left. Press a bleeding forehead into the dust at the nape, and a treacherous chest against a breast-cloth. Know that you will only meet them in some of the pits they themselves dug. Had the Samaritan's people not conspired, their calf would not have lowed without purpose. Had Thamud not been compliant toward their Lord, his she-camel would not have been hamstrung by Qidar's sword. The bowels are healed from their anguish that Babak has become Maziyar's neighbor — his second in the very sky, though when two were in the cave, no second was needed. It is as if they withdrew in order to fold away from a spy some piece of news. Black-clad, as if the hands of the hot winds had woven for them garments of pitch. They set out at dawn and hastened on the backs of lean mounts, tethered for them from the carpenter's stable. They never cease and whoever sees them imagines them forever on a journey. They plotted against prophethood and guidance, and their necks were broken in that course. They were ignorant and did not multiply in obedience known to sustain lifetimes. So bind the Caliphate fast with Harun — he is the comfort in its loneliness and a settled abode. With this young man of the Abbasids, the moon surrounded by the stars of Yarub and Nizar. The nobility of paternal and maternal uncles flowed into him from the forebears — Quraysh and the Ansar. He is a rain-star of prosperity and felicity for them, a lamp of night for them and of day. So crush the demons of hypocrisy with a rightly-guided one whose guidance both creation and Creator approve. Let him travel the horizons with a manner of compassion, ruling them with calm and dignity. For China is linked to Andalus all the way to the walls of Byzantium, and the kingdom of Dhamar. I have always known that that wrist would not be left without a bracelet. For the earth is a desolate abode unless there is from Hashim a lord for that abode. The noble verses of the Quran were revealed in your midst, and for you the finest poems are fashioned.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: radiant, clear, shining; also described of a bright forehead
blazing, exposed (of swords drawn from scabbards), also: defective
long strips of cloth; rags, tatters
with nails reddened (as if with blood or henna); gripping fiercely
the fire-producing flint; a flint that strikes fire easily
the smoke or aroma of burning flesh; the smell of charred meat
hypocrisy, specifically referring to the Quranic concept of the munafiqun
a lamp, a torch; used in the Quran in the Verse of Light

context: A major political-religious qasida addressed to the Abbasid caliph, celebrating the execution of Khidhr ibn Kawus (Babak al-Khurami's ally) or a similar heretic-rebel. The poem draws on Quranic imagery and Abbasid legitimacy to justify the caliph's punishment of apostates and hypocrites, closing with a panegyric to the Abbasid prince Harun.

titleAr: لو صحح الدمع لي أو ناصح الكمد
titleEn: Had Tears Been True or Grief Sincere

verses.sadr: لو صحح الدمع لي أو ناصح الكمد
خان الصفاء أخ كان الزمان له

verses.sadr: لا والذي ارتكت تطوي الفجاج له

verses.sadr: لأنفدن أسى إذ لم أمت أسفا

verses.sadr: عني إليك فإني عنك في شغل
