verses.sadr: هي النوائب فاشجي أو فعي عظة
هبي تري قلقا من تحته أرق
صماء سم العدى في جنبها ضرب
verses.ajuz: وشرب كأس الردى في فمها شهد

verses.sadr: هناك أم النهى لم تود من حزن
verses.ajuz: ولم تجد لبني الدنيا بما تجد

verses.sadr: لو يعلم الناس علمي بالزمان وما
verses.ajuz: عاثت يداه لما ربوا ولا ولدوا
إن قال أودى الندى والبدر والأسد
مالم يزرك بنفسي حر ما أجد

verses.sadr: أنسى أبا الفضل يعفو الترب أحسنه
verses.ajuz: دوني ودلو الردى في مائه يرد ؟ !
لم يعتقد مثله قلب ولا جلد

verses.sadr: عاق الزمان رضيع الجود لم يقه

verses.ajuz: بل قيل أنجدها إن فرت النجد

translationEn: Had tears been true to me or grief sincere, soul and body would rarely have stayed with me. A brother whose purity betrayed him — time had been a brother to him — but grief did not betray his body. The falling of tears is the least of what afflicts you in love, since no soul and hand fell instead. No — by the One for Whom the camel-litters plough the roads, the ships of land furrowing the cheek of earth! I shall spend my grief to the last — since I did not die of sorrow — or life will be spent with me, or eternity itself. Away from me — go to him — for I am occupied away from you with something that makes my soul weep today and tomorrow. And if a calamity strikes, I cry out toward the summit of my endurance and my skin recoils in desolation. These are misfortunes — so grieve, or take heed as a lesson; for they are occasions whose fruit is wisdom. Suppose you see restlessness beneath which lies sleeplessness, both driven by a suppressed grief the body bends toward. A deaf blow to the enemies is a strike in its side, and drinking the cup of death is honey in its mouth. There the mother of reason did not lose anything to grief, nor give to worldly sons what she possesses. If people knew what I know of time and how its hands have wrought havoc, they would not raise children nor give birth. May God not distance from His mercy one in whose grave the figure of wisdom dwelled, and whom the One, the Self-Sufficient sustained. O dweller of the grave — the claim of one not returning that says 'generosity, the full moon, and the lion have all perished' is no idle boast. The earth spent the night with my brother in joyful delight, while sleeplessness ruled over my eyelids. My regret for you — yet my regret avails nothing unless the burning of what I feel visits you in my own soul. Shall I forget Abu'l-Fadl — shall the earth cover his finest qualities before mine, while the bucket of death draws at his waters?! Woe to your mother — restrain yourself, for it is an event that no heart or skin could have anticipated. Time has slaughtered the nursling of generosity — no family shielded him, no wealth or child availed him. Just when the water had quenched him and his youth had blossomed, revealing teeth that made glory smile. It was said: the most praised of them — nay, the most glorious — nay, said: the most helpful to the highlands when the plateaus fled. The freshness of youth like a sword-blade — no roughness in his palms, no crookedness in his stock. May rain water the captive and one imprisoned in his passage, from the rain-clouds of his namesake pouring down. Where Abu Saqr settled, the clarity of life and the comfort of its pleasures bade him farewell. Where the lost one of glory settled as a stranger, bequeathing regrets that are never lacking.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: deep suppressed grief, concealed sorrow that consumes inwardly
the soul, the vital spirit; the blood of the heart
wide roads through mountains, open mountain passes
one placed in a lahd-grave (a niche in the side of the grave)
the freshness and tenderness of youth; youthful suppleness
the isthmus; the realm between death and resurrection
the namesake; one who shares the same name

context: A ritha (elegy) for Abu'l-Fadl or Abu Saqr, a patron of generosity who died young. The poem moves between personal grief, philosophical resignation, and praise of the deceased's qualities. Abu Tammam uses extended metaphors of water, swords, and earth to convey the magnitude of the loss.

titleEn: Unique in Beauty, Offspring of Light
translationEn: Unique in beauty, a scion of light — through him the hand of joy found its footing. The eye of the keen-sighted wanders in the two lustrous beauties of his cheek. They have never known any beauty like his — he transcends comparison and equal.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unique, singular, unmatched
offspring, descendant, scion
luster, brilliance, radiant beauty
the eyeball; the eye itself
to be far above, to transcend, to be too great for

context: A brief lyrical fragment praising the unrivaled beauty of a beloved, characterized by radiant light and ineffable uniqueness. Its brevity and lyrical compression are characteristic of Abu Tammam's shorter ghazal pieces.

titleEn: My Night Passes Yet Never Ends

verses.sadr: لا شيء ضائر عاشق ، فإذا نأى
ما إن يخاف الخذل من أيامه
يفدي أبا العباس من لم يفده
لاتنس من لم ينس مدحك والمنى

translationEn: I have spent myself, yet my night never reaches its end — here are its inlets, but where are its outlets? The eyes of the gloating ones have slept, certain that he will not sleep while sorrows hold conversation with him. Separation has taken his consolation captive, and the one who used to protect him with shyness when he was captivated has gone far away. Nothing harms a lover — but when the beloved goes far, everything harms him. O you who ask me — I am explaining to you my hidden state until you are as if present at it. I and Nasr and contentment in his neighborhood are like the sea — it does not seek any neighbor but him. No one who is certain that Nasr is his supporter fears abandonment from his days. He would sacrifice Abu'l-Abbas from someone whom neither his own kin nor his elements would sacrifice from blamers. He stirs praise-poets to action — as if one who came to praise him came to compete with him in nobility. What do you say of one who has seen you worthy of his praise and whose destinies are in your hands? He has overpowered misfortunes until they lied against him — but fate contends with him. Time passed him keeping its hands from his sides — for time does in humility whatever he commands. Do not forget one who did not forget your praise, while hopes in the dark of night claim you remember him. Set out at dawn — for the noble first-fruits of odes have set out early to praise you: the best of things is taken early. Let their first part meet you with the first of his verse — give generously at first and the last of it shall be yours. Nothing is fairer than praise that travels while your generosity keeps it company across the horizons of the lands. And when the hoped-for youth succeeds by his own mind in himself, his generosity makes his poet succeed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its inlets, its water-sources; places where one comes to water
its outlets, its departure points; places of setting out
to contend with, to overpower, to wrestle against
the noble first-fruits of poems; the finest early odes
the root, the original stock; one's clan and origin
the elements; one's fundamental nature and lineage

context: A madih addressed to Nasr (likely Nasr ibn Mansur, an Abbasid-era notable), beginning with a ghazal-like opening about sleeplessness before pivoting to praise. Abu Tammam employs the conceit that praising this patron is itself a form of glory, making the poet's role inseparable from the patron's.

titleEn: Let Calamity Be Grave, the Matter Weighty

verses.ajuz: فليس لعين لم يفض ماؤها عذر
وأصبح في شغل عن السفر السفر

verses.sadr: وما كان إلا مال من قل ماله
verses.ajuz: وذخرا لمن أمسى وليس له ذخر

verses.sadr: وما كان يدري مجتدي جود كفه
verses.ajuz: إذا ما استهلت أنه خلق العسر

verses.sadr: ألا في سبيل الله من علطت له
فتى مات بين الضرب والطعن ميتة
verses.ajuz: تقوم مقام النصر إذ فاته النصر

verses.sadr: وما مات حتى مات مضرب سيفه
verses.ajuz: من الضرب واعتلت عليه القنا السمر

verses.sadr: وقد كان فوت الموت سهلا فرده

verses.ajuz: هو الكفر يوم الروع أو دونه الكفر
وقال لها من تحت أخمصك الحشر

verses.sadr: تردى ثياب الموت حمرا فما أتى
verses.ajuz: لها الليل إلا وهي من سندس خضر
نجوم سماء خر من بينها البدر

verses.sadr: يعزون عن ثاو ت عزى به العلى
وأني لهم صبر عليه وقد مضى
verses.ajuz: إلى الموت حتى استشهدوا هو والصبر !

verses.sadr: فتى كان عذب الروح لامن غضاضة
verses.ajuz: ولكن كبرا أن يقال به كبر !

verses.sadr: فتى سلبته الخيل وهو حمى لها
verses.ajuz: وبزته نار الحرب وهو لها جمر

verses.sadr: وقد كانت البيض المآثير في الوغى
verses.ajuz: بواتر فهي الآن من بعده بتر
ففي أي فرع يوجد الورق النضر ؟
لعهدي به ممن يحب له الدهر

verses.sadr: لئن غدرت في الروع أيامه به

verses.ajuz: لما عريت منها تميم ولا بكر

verses.sadr: سقى الغيث غيثا وارت الأرض سخصه
verses.ajuz: وإن لم يكن فيه سحاب ولا قطر

verses.sadr: مضى طاهر الأثواب لم تبق روضة
verses.ajuz: غداة ثوى إلا اشتهت أنها قبر

verses.sadr: ثوى في الثرى من كان يحيا به الثرى

verses.ajuz: رأيت الكريم الحر ليس له عمر

translationEn: Let calamity be this grave and the matter this weighty — for an eye that does not overflow with water has no excuse. Hopes have died after Muhammad, and the traveler has become occupied away from journeying. He was nothing but wealth for one whose wealth was scarce, and a treasure for one who had no reserve at evening. The one who sought the generosity of his hand did not know, when it poured, that hardship had been created. Would that in God's path lay one for whom the roads of God's path were widened and the frontier-gap was broken open. A youth — whenever a tribe's eyes overflowed with blood, tales of him and remembrance of him smiled. A youth who died between striking and thrusting a death that stands in place of victory when victory eluded him. He did not die until the striking edge of his sword had died from the blows, and the brown lances had grown heavy upon him. The escape from death was easy, but bitter loyalty and a rugged character drove him back to it. A soul that abhors shame so much that it is as if shame were apostasy on the day of alarm — or worse than apostasy. He planted his foot in the swamp of death and said: under your sole is the gathering-place. He set out in the morning, praise woven into his cloak — he did not return but with his shroud being divine reward. He wore the red garments of death, but by nightfall they had become green silk of paradise. As if the Banu Nabhan on the day of his death were the stars of a sky from which the full moon had fallen. They console themselves for one laid low — nobility consoled itself through him — while generosity, valor, and poetry weep for him. How can they have patience over him, when he went to death and they witnessed him and patience die together! A youth whose soul was sweet — not from softness, but from too great a pride to have pride attributed to him! A youth whom the horses stripped while he was their protector, and whom the fire of war despoiled while he was its very ember. The sharp keen swords in battle were cutting — now after him they are blunted. After events have folded Muhammad away, shall the garments of generosity ever be unfolded again? When the trees of beneficence have their roots cut, in which branch shall the fresh leaves be found? If treacherous time has become hateful for his loss, I have known it to be among those for whom time was loved. If days betrayed him in the alarm of battle — days have never ceased to have betrayal as their nature. If the calamity has clothed Tayyi' in mourning, neither Tamim nor Bakr have been stripped of it either. Such is it — we never cease to lose one whose loss is shared by bedouin and settled folk alike. May rain water a rain — the earth hid his body, though in him there was no cloud and no drop. How can I accept the rain's service of watering a grave, when within its niche there is an ocean! He passed on, pure of garment — not a meadow remained on the morning he was laid to rest that did not wish it were his grave. He was laid in the earth — he through whom the earth once lived, and whose overwhelming bounty submerged the turns of fate. Upon you be God's peace, continuing — for I have seen that the noble and free have no long lifespan.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who seeks generosity, a petitioner of gifts
bitter loyalty, a fierce sense of honor and protection
the swamp of death, a place where one sinks and cannot escape
fine green silk; used in the Quran to describe paradise garments
sharp, cutting swords; also noted swords of great tradition
to be marked, branded; here metaphorically: to be granted wide roads
his form, his body, his physical presence

context: A celebrated elegy for Muhammad ibn Humayda al-Tai, a warrior of the Banu Nabhan clan who fell in battle. The poem became one of Abu Tammam's most quoted elegies, famous for its verse about the warrior dying a death that stands in place of victory and for the image of his red battle-garments turning to green in paradise.

titleEn: O Sick One, Your Breast Is Filled with Fire

verses.ajuz: كان لي فيك حافظ الجار جارا

translationEn: O sick one whose breast is filled with fire — you who were a neighbor to me, with the protector of neighbors as your guardian! The mine of beauty and loveliness has now become a mine and resting-place for illness. Indeed the face of fever is a brazen face, when it assaults openly in broad daylight. It has not marred his fair face — rather it has turned the roses of his cheeks into saffron flowers.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the ribs surrounding the heart; the inner chest cavity
protector of neighbors; one who fulfills the obligation to guard those near him
brazen, shameless, thick-faced; audacious
to assault, to overpower, to pounce upon
saffron flower; pale yellow flowers; a sign of pallor from illness

context: A delicate four-verse piece addressed to a sick beloved or companion, lamenting how fever has altered his complexion. Abu Tammam wittily transforms the medical into the aesthetic: the blush of the cheeks has been replaced by the pallid yellow of saffron blossoms.

titleAr: شجا في الحشى ترداده ليس يفتر
titleEn: A Grief Repeating in My Breast Unceasing

verses.sadr: شجا في الحشى ترداده ليس يفتر
إذا درجت فيه الصبا كفكفت لها
بسيب كأن السيف من ثر نؤيه
فتى من يديه البأس يضحك والندى
أبا الفضل إني يوم جئتك مادحا
فلا شيء أمضى من رجائك في الندى
verses.ajuz: ولا شيء أبقى من ثناء يحبر

verses.sadr: إذا ما انطوى عنها اللئيم بسمعه

verses.ajuz: من الذكر لم تنفخ ولا تتزمر

verses.sadr: أبا الفضل إن الشعر مما يميته

translationEn: A grief returning in my breast that never abates — by it my hopes were sealed, though I remain fasting. I swear by the flowing course of wishes that you seek from it — a cloud-hand that rains with choice gifts. When the east wind glides through it, it restrained itself for her, and Abu'l-Fadl Jafar arose to match her with a flowing gift — as if the sword were from the overflow of his tent-peg's rivulet, and drinking-halls from which the rain-cloud's dew is pressed. The world has been adorned by the days of a noble one through whom kingship shines and glories glory. A youth from whose hands valor smiles and generosity — and in his saddle there is a full moon and a fierce lion. Through him the hopes of visiting petitioners have converged, and a multitude has risen in gratitude before him. O Abu'l-Fadl — the day I came to you as a praiser, I saw the faces of generosity and success blooming. I became certain that I was opening into a deep overflowing sea toward which oceans return bearing liberality. Nothing is sharper than the hope in your generosity, and nothing more lasting than praise finely penned. Swords do not give victory like the victory of a praise-poem that has a presence at the gates of caliphs. When the miser wraps his ear against it, it finds an unrolling at the hands of the generous. It has pipes at the kings' gates — from remembrance that needs no breath to blow and no trumpeting. His two palms held valor, generosity, and munificence, and he attained wisdom so that ignorance wanders bewildered and squinting. Let fulfillment not surrender its command, letting procrastination advance it in generosity as a delayer. To you I bring her — a virgin presented as a bride, with her jewelry trembling upon her. She is presented to you, O son of Nasr, as if she were Kisra's consort on the day he was sheltered by Caesar. O Abu'l-Fadl — what kills poetry is the youth's proud refusal of flattery; while glory lives and is buried.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the flowing course, the channel; the path of running water
desired gifts, coveted bounties; great wishes
squinting, looking sideways; confused and displaced
a virgin; used to describe a poem never previously presented to anyone
Kisra's (Khosrow's) wife; symbolic of ultimate royal honor and beauty
proud refusal, disdain, the rejection of flattery or submission
victorious in a game of chance; winner; here used as 'one entering into'

context: A panegyric addressed to Abu'l-Fadl Jafar ibn Nasr, an Abbasid official of great generosity. The poem opens with the personal grief of the poet before moving to extravagant praise, and closes with the famous image of presenting the poem as a virgin bride. The comparison of the patron's consort to Kisra's queen sheltered by Caesar signals the highest possible honor.

titleEn: Take Consolation, for None Endure

verses.ajuz: وهل أحد يبقى وإن بسط العمر ؟

verses.sadr: سيأكلنا الدهر الذي غال من نرى
verses.ajuz: ولا تنقضي الأشياء أو يؤكل الدهر
يضل إذا فكرت في كنهها الفكر
ويحزن لما صار وهو له ذخر !

verses.sadr: عليك بثوب الصبر إذ فيه ملبس
verses.ajuz: فإن ابنك المحمود بعد ابنك الصبر

translationEn: Take consolation — neither Hawiyy nor Amr endured forever, and will anyone remain even if his lifespan is stretched? Time will devour us as it has devoured those we see, and things will not cease until time itself is devoured. Most of the states of Adam's son, by nature, leave the mind astray when thought seeks their depth. He rejoices over something whose continuance is borrowed, and grieves for what has passed — when it was his very treasure! Clothe yourself in patience, for in it is a garment — for your praised son is the son of patience. God does not leave desolate the courtyard of His servant when he faces the great trial — for his companion is divine reward.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: consolation, condolence; the bearing of grief with patience
to destroy, to carry off suddenly; to be consumed
the depth of it, the ultimate nature, the essential reality
loaned, borrowed; that which is given on temporary basis
the great calamity; a catastrophic event that clears away all comfort

context: A brief consolation poem addressed to a bereaved father, offering philosophical reflection on mortality and the universal nature of loss. The poem draws on the names of historical figures (Hawiyy and Amr) as examples of great men who also perished, urging patience as the highest response to grief.

titleEn: A Wine Whose Star Blazes
translationEn: A wine whose star blazes, from which musk and ambergris radiate. Rose-colored, poured by a young gazelle as if it were being pressed from his very cheek. My heart, since it became attached to him, has remained blind from rejection, seeing nothing. Slender-waisted, he has not smiled since the dull stone became a jewel. By love for him my grave-digger buries me — he is the cord of my death, and by him I am resurrected.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: wine; in classical Arabic, coffee was later named after this word for wine
a young gazelle; used poetically for a beautiful youth
slender-waisted, willowy, of a delicate and graceful build
the dull, corroded, inferior; a stone without luster
a twisted cord; here metaphorically the thread of fate leading to death

context: A short wine-and-love poem (khamriyya-ghazal hybrid) in which the description of wine transitions into an address to a beautiful youth. The final verse is characteristic of Abu Tammam's mystical compression: the beloved is both the cause of death and the source of resurrection.

titleEn: O Ahmad, Enviers Are Many

verses.sadr: إليك تناهى المجد من كل وجهة
فما من ندى إلا إليك محله

translationEn: O Ahmad — enviers are many, yet if the noble are counted, you have no equal. You have settled in a station that is excellent and foremost, proud with glory and ancient pride. Every strong or wealthy man is, before you, a pauper — even if he reaches the sky. Glory has converged toward you from every direction it turns, and none surpasses you when you turn. You are the full moon of Iyad — they do not deny it; just so, Iyad is full moons to all people. There is no generosity whose proper place is not with you, and no caravan that does not journey toward you. You have avoided being called the Amir out of humility — yet you are the Amir of those who are called Amir.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to reach its utmost limit, to converge, to culminate
the tribe of Iyad, an ancient Arab tribe known for nobility
the full moon of Iyad; the most illustrious member of the tribe
to avoid, to keep away from, to abstain from
out of humility, as an act of modesty

context: A compact madih addressed to Ahmad of the tribe of Iyad, likely a notable Abbasid-era official or tribal leader. The poem closes with the famous paradox of a man humble enough to avoid the title of Amir yet noble enough to command even those who bear it.

titleEn: Sufficient Against All Time's Trials
translationEn: It is enough for me against all misfortunes of time to have Ishaq ibn Ibrahim as my neighbor. My Mus'ab will protect me from misfortunes — as if his forehead were a moon that has shone forth. In confidence — and you are worthy of it — I have taken hold of the rope of your protection by choice. Through Ishaq ibn Ibrahim, the sky of generosity has poured down in a continuous torrent. A youth whose bounty in every community has established a lineage for every noble quality. I have tied my rope to his rope, so its strands are now ones whose severance I do not fear. Yours are blessings that come at dawn and move at night — you have bestowed them upon me time and again. I thanked you for them secretly and openly, and my praise of you went into the highlands and the lowlands. We prefer you above all peoples — for we have seen kingship settle among you and travel with you. Your bounties have been universal and have singled out the people of Yemen as much as they have stripped Nizar. The Imam chose you above men for his community — he was not deprived of the best choice. You governed the Muslims and did not neglect their small affairs or their great ones. May God bless you with nobility and generosity, and clothe you in awe and dignity. When your neighbor is of the Mus'abiyya, you fear no harm and no defeat.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the covenant of protection; the bond of obligation and honor
pouring heavily and continuously, like torrential rain
origin, lineage, stock; the foundation and nature of a thing
severance, cutting off, rupture of a bond
awe, dignity, a sense of commanding respect in others
ruin, defeat, being overcome by an enemy

context: A madih addressed to Ishaq ibn Ibrahim al-Mus'abi, a prominent Abbasid official and governor, celebrating his governance and generosity. The poet declares him a personal protector and praises his appointment by the caliph as evidence that the best choice was made for the Muslim community.

titleAr: سهرت فيك فلم أجحد يد السهر
titleEn: I Kept Vigil for You, Not Denying Sleeplessness

verses.sadr: سهرت فيك فلم أجحد يد السهر

verses.ajuz: لما التفت إلى شيء من المطر

verses.sadr: يا من إذا قلت يا من لا نظير له
verses.ajuz: في حسنه قيل لي يا أصدق البشر

verses.sadr: ما إن أرى وجهك المكنون جوهره

translationEn: I kept vigil for you and did not deny the hand of sleeplessness — my thought grew long, and thought is blameless. I kept company with your memory while darkness brooded around me — it was, my lord, sweeter than night-conversation. If you see my tear, which longing pours out — it cares nothing for rain. O you — when I say 'O you who has no equal in beauty,' it is said to me: 'O most truthful of men!' I never see your face, whose essence is hidden, O most fair of people, except as a copy of the moon.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sleeplessness, keeping vigil, wakefulness at night
to keep company with, to drink with, to sit as a companion
deep darkness; the pitch-dark night
to pour out, to spill, to shed (tears)
hidden, concealed, stored away; something precious kept from view
a copy of the moon; an impression of the full moon's beauty

context: A graceful five-verse ghazal in which the poet addresses a beloved of incomparable beauty, spending the night in wakefulness and finding more sweetness in the memory of the beloved than in any external company. The final verse with 'a copy of the moon' is among Abu Tammam's most celebrated comparisons.

titleAr: يا وارث الملك إن الملك محتبس
titleEn: O Heir to Kingship, Reign Is Reserved for You

verses.sadr: يا وارث الملك إن الملك محتبس
verses.ajuz: وقف عليك إلى أن تنشر الصور

verses.sadr: لم يذكر الجود إلا خضت واديه
verses.ajuz: ولا انتضي السيف إلا خافك القدر

verses.sadr: ما ضر من أصبح المأمون سائسه
verses.ajuz: أن لم يسسه أبو بكر ولا عمر
أن لا تضيء لنا شمس ولا قمر

translationEn: O heir to kingship — kingship is held in reserve, endowed to you until the Trumpet is blown. Generosity is not mentioned but you wade into its valley, and no sword is drawn but fate fears you. What harm has come to one whose governance is al-Mamun's — that Abu Bakr and Umar have not governed him? What does the earth lose while al-Mamun rules it, if no sun and no moon shine for us?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: held, detained, reserved; kept in custody until needed
the Trumpet; the trumpet blown on the Day of Resurrection
to draw a sword from its scabbard
his governor, his handler; one who manages and governs
the earth while al-Mamun owns it; a reference to the Abbasid Caliph al-Mamun

context: A brief but hyperbolic madih to the Abbasid Caliph al-Mamun, asserting that his rule renders all previous governance and even natural light superfluous. The verse about the sun and moon not being needed is one of the most audacious compliments in classical Arabic panegyric.

titleAr: يا سمي النبي في سورة الج
titleEn: O Namesake of the Prophet in the Surah of Jinn

verses.sadr: يا سمي النبي في سورة الج

verses.ajuz: جمر شوق احر من كل جمر

verses.sadr: باشر الماء فهو في رقة الصن
verses.ajuz: عة كالماء غير أن ليس يجري

translationEn: O namesake of the Prophet in the Surah of the Jinn, and second of the Almighty's minister in Egypt! The night of the Sarata left in my heart an ember of longing hotter than any ember. He touched the water, and in the delicacy of his craftsmanship he was like water — except that water does not flow. The water scratched his moist skin until I imagined him wearing a wine-red gauze.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: namesake of the Prophet; the name Yahya (John) appears in Surah al-Jinn or similar
the Mighty One of Egypt; a Quranic reference to the official who sheltered Joseph
a canal in Iraq near Baghdad; a place of pleasant gardens
delicacy of craftsmanship; refinement of form and artistry
a thin, semi-transparent inner garment; a gauzy undergarment

context: A playful four-verse ghazal addressed to a beloved named after a prophet, likely Yahya or Yusuf, whose bathing scene the poet describes with remarkable sensory detail. The Quranic reference to the 'Aziz of Egypt' (minister who sheltered Joseph) elegantly doubles the addressee's identity.

titleAr: أصم بك الناعي وإن كان أسمعا
titleEn: The Death-Announcer Has Deafened Me

verses.sadr: أصم بك الناعي وإن كان أسمعا

verses.ajuz: إذا هي حيت معمرا عاد ممرعا

verses.sadr: فلم ار يوما كان أشبه ساعة
verses.ajuz: بيومي من اليوم الذي فيه ودعا
من الدمع حتى خلته عاد مربعا

verses.sadr: ووالله لا تقضي العيون الذي له
verses.ajuz: عليها ولو صارت مع الدمع أدمعا

verses.sadr: فتى كلما ارتاد الشجاع من الردى
إذا ساء يوم في الكريهة منظرا
فإن ترم عن عمر تدانى به المدى
verses.ajuz: فخانك حتى لم يجد فيك منزعا

verses.sadr: فما كنت إلا السيف لاقى ضريبة

translationEn: Your death-announcer has deafened me, though he was making others hear, and the dwelling-place of generosity has become a desolate ruin after you. For Abu Nasr's grave — a greeting from a rain-cloud: when it greets an ancient site, it turns it verdant. I have not seen a day more resembling a moment than the day it bid farewell to my day. A summer-dwelling into which grief poured rivulets of tears until I imagined it had become a spring-camp. By God, the eyes will never fulfill what they owe him, even if they themselves became tears. A youth who was a drinking-place and pasture for those who sought, and has become a pasture for white Indian swords. A youth — whenever the brave one sought an escape from destruction on the day of the tight pass, he found only a place of death. When a day in battle was ugly to the sight, he entered it knowing that its sound would be made fair. If you aim to escape through a life that drew near its end — it betrayed you until it found no release from you. You were nothing but the sword that met its target, cut through it, then turned and itself was cut asunder!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the one who announces a death; the bearer of news of someone's passing
barren, desolate, empty of all life and value
a rain-cloud; a cloud laden with rain
lush, verdant, abundant with vegetation
a tight pass, a narrow strait; a moment of extreme danger
a thing struck by a sword; the target of a blade; also a tribute

context: An elegy for Abu Nasr, a warrior and patron, closing with one of the most memorable images in Arabic elegy: the deceased likened to a sword that cuts through its target and then is itself cut in two. The poem beautifully blends grief with warrior-praise.

titleEn: Is There Any Trace Left of Their Abodes

verses.sadr: لا تسألنها فليس يسمع جرس ال
يكاد يجري الجادي من ماء عط
يترك ما مر مذ قبيل به

verses.ajuz: لا الربع في جريه ولا السدس

verses.sadr: شذب همي به صقيل ن ال
أروع لا من رياحه الحرجف ال
لا كأناس قد أصبحوا صدأ ال
القرب منهم بعد من الروح وال
تلك خلال وقف عليك ابن وه

translationEn: Is there a trace remaining of their dwellings — where sandy hollows meet the soft ground? Telling the traveler on his worn mount among the ruins: where are the dark-lipped gazelles? Do not ask them — for the echo of speech is only heard by one who has an echo himself. The blame of the foolish, reckless old woman is not relaxed except by the rough camel-litter. And settled grief is like a chronic ailment, and the house when one becomes accustomed to it is a grave. What fine provision of the world was gifted to you — a noble man, neither thick-footed nor sluggish. Smaller than the yolk of an egg in its proportions, pure as a bowstave. His forelegs like a trunk of arak-wood, and behind his haunches no stone-like hip. Saffron almost runs from the water of his nature, and Wars-dye can almost be gathered from his back. He has been refined in his kind and reached the utmost by himself — he alone is a class. His fathers guessed at excellence when the eye of discernment looked into their veins. It is neither novel nor wondrous from him that water comes to his drinking-place every five days. He leaves what passed moments ago as if its nearest acquaintance was yesterday. And when his rider whispers to him, he understands as humans understand. And his canter has not yet descended the mountain pass — he is neither at a quarter nor a sixth of his capacity. When he shoots a glance from his eye, it is black as ink darkened with pitch. When his white blaze is lent to your eyes, it shines as if it were white plaster. He was perfumed from his color and came as if the sun had been eclipsed in his skin. Every precious reward for him falls short — except praise, for all else is deficient. He has sharpened my care — a gleaming one — among the youths of every region, his flanks smooth. High-withered and high-foreheaded — when the ignoble bows his head in the ignominy of his deed. Abu Ali — his manners are flowers after rain, and his spirit is sanctified. Bright white, cut in the cut of the thong-leather — his soul and mine share the finest thong. He aspires to glory, and to the neglected adab he gives shelter, and to generosity he clings close. In the arena of eloquence he opens its breach — and people are mute in its like. He pierced its interior with a speech that came down like a sudden stealthy thrust from him. A noble one — not from the ill cold winds, nor from its unlucky stars. His tomorrow longs for him from his perfection, and yesterday increases its longing toward him. Give my gaze a respite from his face for a time — my moment away from him is a guard. Our days in his shade are always a spring season, and our age is a wedding feast. Not like people who have become the rust of life — as if the world to them is a prison. Proximity to the likes of them is distance from the soul, and loneliness from their like is companionship. These qualities Abu Ali — ibn Wahb ibn Said — has reserved for you, their finest locked away. An enhancer of praise — he sees men as the secret of earth and glory as its planting.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sandy plains, gravelly lowlands
soft sandy ground, fine loose soil
gazelle calves; young gazelles, used poetically for beautiful women
dark-lipped; describing gazelles with dark lips, a mark of beauty
saffron; here describing the intense golden color of the horse's coat
a plant used for yellow-red dye; used for dyeing cloth
the two sides of the nape of the neck; describing a horse's high carriage

context: A complex qasida for Abu Ali ibn Wahb ibn Said in the mansarih meter, opening with the classical nasib of abandoned ruins before pivoting to an extended description of a gifted horse, then to lavish praise of the patron. The horse-description section is considered among Abu Tammam's finest wasf passages.

titleEn: My Father's Life and More: An Elegy for Muhammad

verses.sadr: أنسى أبا نصر نسيت إذن يدي
إن طال يومك في الوغى فلقد ترى
كم مشهد قد جددته لك العلا
يا يوم قحطبة لقد أبقيت لي
ليث لو أن الليث قام مقامه
لما رأى جمعا قليلا في الوغى
ما زال ذاك الصبر وهو عليكم

translationEn: My father's life be ransom — and more, though that is little — for one upon whom the soil of al-Nabaj lies heaped. His clan abandoned him — as if their nobles did not know that the abandoner is the abandoned. The devourer of warriors' limbs by lances — now his own limbs are devoured by them! Enough — the killing of Muhammad is my witness that even the mighty are humbled before fate. If he is dominated after a life of refusing submission, even the sturdy tethered camel can be dominated. He found the face of death beautiful in battle — the face of life in its two arenas is lovely too. Shall I forget Abu Nasr? Then I would have forgotten my very hand in the place where a youth finds victory and attains! Far from it — time will not bring his like; time is indeed miserly with his like! You are not the one killed by patient resignation — rather my hope on the morning of your death-announcement is the one killed. The sword has a burning anguish and wailing after you, and upon you ancient glory has a smoldering grief. If your day in battle was long — you made the heads' day from your hand long indeed. The horses will remember your swift night-journey while the desert — familiar with destruction — remains unknown. Honor and wisdom are dulled after you, and the white blades are smooth with no notches in them. Who is it that comforts his soul with the thought of survival? Far from it — you are the proof of annihilation! Would that I knew, from the virtues in their entirety, what they would say now that they have lost your generosity. How many a scene has glory renewed for you — and it seems like yesterday yet is crumbled. And a regiment whose souls were written down for it — today it is red with polished blood. The firmest of them in certainty had no doubt that he was a messenger of death in the seizing of souls. O day of Qahtaba — you have left in me a burning whose days I see will be long. A lion — had the real lion stood in his place, it would have fled, hollow like a reed, in panic. When he saw a small force in battle — and those of true loyalty among the few are few indeed. He met the dire battle with his fear sheathed within him — but his sword drawn. He walked toward swift death as if he were an intimate friend of death in his love for it. Not one individual brought him to ruin — rather a small black serpent-tribe was his undoing. The courtyards of Muhammad and Muhammad and their brother have become as if they were abandoned ruins. O sons of Humayd — it is not the first time that the dark thicket has gone barren after the dark lions. That patience has always been your guardian unto death in the shade of swords. Recklessly bold — as if their souls were not theirs except on the morning they flow in battle. They have grown intimate with death so that the dead among them is one whom war does not clear away though he is slain. If the adversities of time have bereaved me of them — then time itself is a dead bereaved one.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: al-Nabaj, a place in the Arabian Peninsula; a location of significance
heaped up, poured loose; describing sand or earth piled over a grave
the stubborn, unmanageable camel; the unbreakable
swift night-charge, an unsheathed dash; moving with speed and determination
swift death, the rapid destroyer; death that comes without warning
those recklessly brave, who have abandoned care for their lives
a hollow reed; used metaphorically for a coward or hollow thing

context: A long elegiac qasida mourning Muhammad (likely from the tribe of Banu Humayd or Banu Qahtaba), killed in battle against overwhelming odds. The poem celebrates his death as warrior-martyrdom and mourns the loss that his clan's abandonment of him makes all the more tragic. The Qahtaba reference connects the poem to the Abbasid revolution's battles.

titleEn: Heavy of Hip and Slender of Waist
translationEn: Heavy of hip and slender of waist — a twin of the sun, a child of the moon. Wondrous of beauty, supple of stature, fair of cheek, pure of teeth. A branch of the ban-tree with a full moon upon it — the image of beauty, a bride of the inner curtain. O waist — I had been one of concealment in love until you tore away my veil. My tears betrayed my torment when the beauty of my patience vanished from me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the haunches, the hips; fullness of the posterior, a mark of beauty
twin, sibling; something that matches another perfectly
offspring, product; one born of something
a branch of the ban-tree (moringa); a metaphor for a slender, supple body
the inner curtain, the bridal chamber; seclusion of a noble woman

context: A brief descriptive ghazal cataloguing the physical attributes of a beloved with compressed precision. The final two verses shift to the poet's own emotional state, where tears betray his secret love. The poem is notable for its tight parallelism and accumulation of beauty-descriptors.

titleEn: She Said, Women's Wiles Are Like the Mute

verses.sadr: يكبر أن يستحم في الحر والقر
حر له سورة لدى الزجر والسو
حلفت بالبيت ذي الملبين في ال
أن ابن طوق بن مالك ملك
لا برد أدنى ولا إزار على
فإن موسى وصلى على روحه الرب

translationEn: She said — women's discernment is like the mute's, yet they can hit the mark by stealth. Will those with ropes among Rabia al-Faras ever turn but toward a horse's flank? As if I have arrived at its courtyard with a horse easy in its leading. Bay-red like a bar of metal, or dark bay with lip-darkness or dark-lipped shading. Or jet-black with an evenness in its darkness as if it were a piece of night-shade. With a wetted back and two saddle-seats leading to hooves, its spine smooth. When alarmed and among the mares, it is one of highest moisture above and driest below. It disdains being bathed in hot or cold water that would add to its foulness. Its face smoothed for racing like a bride of noble sons prepared for the wedding. A noble one, with a fierce spirit when urged and whipped, and a slave to the rein and the pulling. It delights trainers with its skittishness when calm, and its gentleness and its stubborn roughness. Sonorous in its neigh — you'd think its throat was strung upon a bell. With it you could kill ten ostriches in a single burst and a single breath. I swear by the House of the doubled talbiya during ihram, and during the permitted and the strict — that Ibn Tawq ibn Malik, son of Malik, possesses the sun — owner of the affair of noble qualities. In him are manners fresh and new, not worn out or confused. No garment brought close nor waist-wrapper over a shameful thing to be avoided, no moral stain. His wealth is a prey — yet you will not see his honor as prey for any predator. As if I already see his close standing with an Imam whose proximity brings comfort. Glory is built in his shade, and he has a share of kingship untainted by theft. For Moses — may the Lord bless his soul with abundant blessings — became a prophet and made great his desire in a firebrand for burning or a torch.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: skittishness, restless impetuousness; the fiery energy of a horse
a horse with a piercing neigh; very sonorous in its whinny
the strictly devout; the strictest sacred precinct of Quraysh in Mecca
proximity, nearness; standing close to a person of authority
the dark of the very early dawn; darkness just before first light
the saddle-place on a horse's back; the area of the withers and back

context: A qasida in the mansarih meter for Ibn Tawq ibn Malik, opening with a woman's observations about horses and pivoting to an extended equine description — a common opening device for the wasf section — before closing with praise of the patron's closeness to the caliph. The Mosaic ending alludes to the patron's divine favor.

titleEn: A Grief That Grips the Heart and Entrails

verses.sadr: إذا ما جرى مجرى دم المرء حكمه
فلو شاء هذا الدهر أقصر شره
فتى لم يذق سكر الشباب ولم تكن
فتى ينفج الأقوام من طيب ذكره
فتى لم تكن تغلي الحقود بصدره
ألم ترني أنزفت عيني على أبي

verses.ajuz: وإن كان يوم الروع غيري حامله !

verses.sadr: وآسى على جيحان إذ غاض ماؤه
verses.ajuz: وإن كان ذودا غير ذودي ناهله

verses.sadr: تعادل وزنا كل شيء ولا أرى

translationEn: A grief that has seized the entrails and the heart and locked it, and tears that wrong the eye, its lid overflowing. A devastating death — no enemy fears it enough to be spared, nor does it spare a friend it treats gently. What brother of consolation or stoic endurance could resist it or hope to wrestle with it? When its decree runs through a man's blood and its snares are spread across the paths of souls. If only this time would shorten its evil, as it has shortened its delights and its gifts toward us. We shall complain of it openly and secretly and in intention — the complaint of one who cannot fight it. Who will convey from me to Rabia that the dew and rain of generosity have dissipated from it? And that the cracks of wisdom have spread through it, and that the vital points of its munificence have been struck? Al-Qasim, giver of loaves, has gone to separation — though had he not separated from us, we would have separated from him eventually. They did not know that time desired him with grief, nor that the fates were in correspondence with him. A youth in whom love of noble qualities was kneaded into his very flesh, and who was permeated by the truth and falsehood of liberality. A youth who never tasted the intoxication of youth, and whose manners never blew a north wind toward a friend. A youth whom fate reached with his two hands of nobility as his measures, and ten noble qualities as his fingers. A youth who fills people from the fragrance of his memory with praise as if amber-rose had permeated it. He has bereft Utaba and Zuhayr and Taghalib and Wa'ila at the end of nights. He was a rain and a landmark for them — the destitute would ask him, the seeker would question him. One who hastened to good deeds — his gifts flowed toward them, and his misfortunes did not flow toward them. A youth in whose chest rancor did not boil, while his cauldrons boiled for winter guests. A sovereign for sovereigns — his guests would be hosted, his hopes would be hoped for, and his petitioners would petition. Fate has folded him like a book, and has hidden from his people his virtues and his bounties. It folded away manners that used to come and go, and petitions over which his own petitions baffled him. O cloud-bank of beneficence whose rain-cloud has cleared, and O valley of generosity whose streams have dried! Did you not see how I bled my eye for Abu Muhammad — the star whose setting is shining? And I moistened it for him as if, had I come to him as one hunted by nights, his bounties would have moistened me! Yet I praise the sword when it passes, even if on the day of alarm another was its bearer! I grieve for Jayhun when its water sank, even though its herd was not my herd to water. Upon you, Abu Kulthum, be patience — for I see patience, in its ending as in its beginning, is piety. All things are equal in weight, and I see nothing that equals the soundness of monotheism. You are the hump of glory and its withers, and your two counterparts are its shoulders and its nape. The cooking-stones of the pot are none but three, and the spear is none but its two points and its shaft.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: intense inner grief, a burning sorrow that consumes from within
loaves of bread; gifts of food; used as a synecdoche for generosity
kneaded in, mixed thoroughly into; blended with the substance itself
the Jayhun (Oxus/Amu Darya) river; used here as a metaphor for a great flow of bounty
the three cooking-stones that support a pot; a metaphor for three essential supports
the withers of a camel; the foremost part; metaphor for the highest point
the hump of a camel; the pinnacle, the highest and most prominent part

context: An elegy for al-Qasim ibn Khalil of Rabia (likely Abu Muhammad al-Qasim), a man of great generosity and wisdom who died young. The poem mourns the loss to his entire tribal confederation (Rabia's subtribes: Utaba, Taghlib, Wa'ila) and closes with a consolation to Abu Kulthum, the bereaved father, anchored in the doctrine of divine unity.

titleAr: ما في وقوفك ساعة من باس
titleEn: No Harm in Pausing an Hour at the Ruins

verses.sadr: ما في وقوفك ساعة من باس
وإذا مشت تركت بصدرك ضعف ما
فرع نما من هاشم في تربة

verses.ajuz: في حلم أحنف في ذكاء إياس

verses.sadr: لا تنكروا ضربي له من دونه
إن تحو خصل المجد في أنف الصبا

verses.ajuz: في الليل من قبس من الأقباس

verses.sadr: عدل المشيب على الشباب ولم يكن
فالآن حين غرست في كرم الثرى

translationEn: No harm in your pausing an hour — we fulfill the duty to the four effaced ruins. Perhaps your eye will assist with its water — for tears, some betray and some console. The longing one finds no ease, and the spearpoint of passion dries the tear-ducts, cools the breath. The dwellings were circled by separation that emptied every lair of its gazelles. From every one with a laughing chest, made slender as the supple ban-tree branch. A full moon that obeyed in you the sudden impulse of separation, and so — a sun enamored of evasion. A virgin — when she smiles, you see her lightning as the light of camomile in a moist earth. And when she walks, she leaves in your chest double what her jewelry carries of whispered longings. She spoke when separation was decreed — its cup was shared between the pourer and the drinker: 'Do not forget those pledges — for you are called insaan (human) only because you are naasii (a forgetter).' He who created creatures provisioned them with their sustenance for the rotation of guards. The earth is the sky's beneficence as hospitality for it, and for the hopeful there are the Abbasids. This people is the shadow of God — He settled His religion in them, and they are the immovable mountain of kings. In every jewel there is a shining watered-steel pattern, and they are the watered-steel to all these people. My aspiration has settled, hoping in Ahmad — and my imitation of him and my measuring has circled around him. The selected, the chosen, the one sought out for praise — clothed in it and wearing it. And praise is a mantle of beauty in which the noblest of deeds have strutted — not a mantle of dress. A branch that grew from Hashim in a soil that was its match among all plantings. The rain-stars do not abandon their growing-place, and the hard heart of the soil is not hard on them. Between them there is as if the nursing from a breast — from the excess of pure love — or the nursing of the cup. The light of the arara-plant is his light, and his fragrance is the spreading of lavender in the green of myrtle. You have worn this glory to its furthest reach in it — the noblest of disposition and bronze. The boldness of Amr in the liberality of Hatim, in the forbearance of al-Ahnaf, in the intelligence of Iyas. Do not reject my striking for him comparisons from those below him — comparisons that stray into generosity and valor. For God has struck for His Light a parable from the niche and the lamp. If you gather the qualities of glory in the freshness of youth, O son of the Caliph, O Abu'l-Abbas — many a fire of yours has given birth in the night from a spark of sparks. And many a trust in calamities you left as a worn saddle-cloth for their difficulties. I supported him in poverty — and poverty is consuming grief — with generosity, and generosity is the healing physician. I brought him comfort with time until he imagined time itself to be one of the wedding feasts. The joy of what you have shown of my ease and companionship has overcome my sorrows. Grey hair has come upon youth — not from old age but from despair. The pursuit of ambitions has left its mark on the heart, while the mark of years and their branding is only on the head. Now that I have planted those wishes in noble soil and built upon a foundation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the effaced, crumbled ruins; remnants of old dwellings
gazelles; wild animals that inhabit the wilderness
the watered-steel pattern on a blade; the shimmering wavy grain of fine swords
a fragrant desert plant with yellow flowers; the arara
lavender; a fragrant plant of the Arabian desert
a lamp, a torch; used in the Verse of Light (Ayat al-Nur)
Iyas ibn Muawiya, famous for his extraordinary intelligence and quick judgment

context: A great qasida for Ahmad ibn al-Mutasim (or another Abbasid prince Abu'l-Abbas), opening with the classical nasib of ruins and a beloved before transitioning into panegyric of the Abbasid dynasty and the named patron. Notable for the comparison of the patron to four heroic Arab archetypes (Amr, Hatim, al-Ahnaf, Iyas) and the Quranic Verse of Light parallel.

titleEn: Days Have Always Told the Questioner

verses.ajuz: أن سوف تفجع مسهلا أو عاقلا

verses.sadr: ما إن ترى شيئا لشيء محييا
من ذاك أجهد أن لا أراه فلا أرى
إن تزز في طرفي نهار واحد

verses.ajuz: إلا إذا ما كان وهما بازلا
أو أن تذكر ناسيا أو غافلا

translationEn: Days have always told the questioner that they will soon grieve the carefree or the wise. For when death's bitterness continues its course, the shields of people become its targets. Every day they slaughter our souls as one slaughters fine camels and she-camels before their time. You never see a thing giving life to another thing without meeting it then as a killer of something else. From this I strive not to see it — for I find no truth that is called falsehood except this world. For God's sake — what a burning anguish that kept us such that the weeping of eyes was set running free! A glory that came as a night-visitor until — when we said it has settled for all time — it became a departing one. Two stars that God willed should not rise except for the span of a glance before they set. The grief over meadows while still green is greater than the grief over them when withered. Had they grown tall, this one would have been the withers of noble qualities, and the other their nape. My regret for those signs in them — had they been given time to become their full characters! Their calm would have become wisdom, and their youth forbearance, and that eagerness would have become generous gifts. And the starry night would have followed with a sustained drizzle, and that dew would have turned to copious rain. For the crescent — when you see it growing — you become certain that it will be a perfect full moon. Say to the Prince — even if you find in him a weight of grave misfortunes bearing down heavily — if two calamities have stung in a single day's eye and stirred burning anguish and turmoil — then the burden is only doubled for a mount when it is weighty and mature. No wonder if two branches of its stalks met a death that devours creation. For the palm-tree when a pruner's hand strikes off its highest crowns, its lower parts grow thick and dense. Two sand-dunes whom fate has terrified, leaving for us summits before heaven as towering peaks. Radwa and Quds and Yathbil and Amaya, and Yarmaram and Matalic and Mawasil. The two pure ones and brothers whom a mother bore as noble as the face of a watering place for one departing or arriving. Your noble qualities have been too proud for any man to console you, or for you to remember as one forgetful or heedless — except for admonitions delivered to you gently, an easy road for your heart whether listening or speaking. Are hands ever burdened with the brandishing of an Indian sword except when the blade is the decisive cutting one?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: death, the fates; the inexorable destroyer of all living things
to slaughter prematurely; to cut down one who is still in good health
she-camels that have not yet given birth; young female camels
two crescent-shaped sand-dunes; used as a metaphor for two beloved figures
young palm trees; shoots of date palms
gentleness, ease, lenience; making a matter simple and forgiving
the decisive, cutting sword; one that severs completely

context: An elegy for two young men (possibly sons of an Abbasid prince or tribal chief) who died before reaching their potential, described as two crescent moons that set before becoming full. The poem draws on the Prophetic geography of Arabia (Radwa, Quds, Yathbil) for its mountain imagery and closes with the consolation that only the sharpest sword is worth wielding — only the greatest heart can bear such grief.

titleEn: O You by Whom Glory Itself Glories

verses.sadr: فقد صدقت الظن في كل ما

translationEn: O you by whom glory itself glories, and by whom poetry rejoices! My seeking of permission was not because I was drawn by a sun among humans or by a full moon. Rather, a silent speaking letter — more eloquent in its folding than in its unfolding. When it was unfolded, secrets spread that the open declaration conceals. The harbinger of grief came in its belly with a news that its back revealed. Then lines poured down its lines — tears, a line upon a line. So who will take pity with permission on one far from his family — whose hour is an age? For you have proven true my expectation in all that I hoped for, when the rain lied.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: glory glories; the abstract noun being glorified by a human, an inversion of normal praise
to rejoice, to be delighted, to exult
mute yet speaking; a paradox describing a letter — mute in form yet eloquent in content
the unfolding, the spreading out; also the smell released when a letter is opened
the harbinger of grief; a messenger bringing sad news
rain; here rain as a symbol of hoped-for generosity that failed to come

context: A poem of seeking access (isti'dhan) to a patron, using the conceit of a letter whose folding carries more eloquence than its unfolding. The letter brings news of grief, and the poet — exiled from his family — asks the patron for relief. The final verse distinguishes the patron from rain (which lied) by saying his expectation of the patron has proven true.

titleEn: O Time, Enough of You — My Reproach Avails Little

verses.sadr: ولقد أحيط بنا ولم نك صورة
قد كان قرم كاسمه قرما وما
نجما هدى هذاك نجم الجدي إن
إلا رأينا ذا على تلك الرحا
رزئت بنو عمرو بن عامر الذرى

verses.ajuz: لم تخل من ليث هنالك ملبد

verses.sadr: ما دام ذاك المعدن الزاكي الثرى
ولقد أصاب غليلها من لم يصب

translationEn: O Time — enough of you, though my 'enough' avails little — I see you as the tenday of thirst, bitter of watering-place. You have besieged us without our being marked by you — you prepared against us before we were born. O Time — what blossom of glory have you not withered, and what thicket have you not cut down! You filled a cup for the legendary roc in the tree-tops overflowing with black poison. There was a noble leader great as his name, noble — and the women of his father's sons have not given birth to anyone like Ahmad. Two guiding stars — this one is the star of al-Jady (the Pole Star) when the guide loses the way, and that one is the star of al-Farqad. This one is a Zaghibi spear-point in battle, and that one is as if he were the tip of an Indian sword. This one's forehead like a shooting star that dispelled darkness, and that one like a kindled shooting star. How excellent were the two shields of the community in their two days — and how excellent a treasure they were for tomorrow. They never witnessed a whispered counsel or a fire of war kindled by the ready lances, but we saw this one as the axle of that millstone and that one as the lamp of that scene. Banu Amr ibn Amir of the heights were bereaved of the two of them, and the plants of their valley's generosity dried up. Such is death — it treads its marked path only on the necks of the people of nobility. And even if they were struck — that is a burning anger that never lacked a crouching lion. As long as that mine, pure of soil, remains in our hearts' core, we do not turn to gold. All other calamities are bearable — except the calamity of Hujwa ibn Muhammad. It struck with its burning anguish even those who were not struck, and would make a loss for one who had lost nothing. Calm your innermost, Abu'l-Hubab — for these are calamities that come and go morning and evening upon all people. For Mutammim recovered from Malik, and Labid before him consoled himself for Arbad. If you are patient, you are the star of a community that has been patient — and if you weep, you are not censured. This aid is from the tongue — but were I to see the very face of doom, I would have aided you with my hand.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the ten-day interval between waterings; the most extreme thirst
the legendary phoenix-like roc of Arabic lore; a mythical predatory bird
swift-acting poison; venom that kills immediately
a noble leader, a chief; one great in tribal standing
Polaris, the Pole Star; used for navigation
a star in Ursa Minor; used alongside al-Jady for navigation
Mutammim ibn Nuwayra, whose famous elegy for his brother Malik is a touchstone

context: A great elegiac qasida lamenting two brothers of the Banu Amr ibn Amir, including Hujwa ibn Muhammad, praising them as twin guiding stars and shields of their community. The poem invokes the legendary pre-Islamic elegists Mutammim and Labid as models of patient grief, and ends with a striking offer of personal solidarity to the bereaved father Abu'l-Hubab.

titleEn: Your Hands Have Covered Me in My Grave
translationEn: Your hands have covered me in my grave-niche, and may you remain as long as the horizon stretches after me. I have been granted your compassion for as long as my eyes have carried tears and my longing has persisted for me. My soul, in its concealment, hangs suspended between the anguish of separation and the fear of rejection.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the niche cut into the side of the grave; the lahd
the horizon, the utmost extent; the furthest reach of time or space
compassion, affection, tender concern
passionate longing, intense love-grief
the pain of separation, distance from the beloved
rejection, turning away, the refusal of the beloved to respond

context: A brief and intense ghazal-fragment in which the poet expresses complete devotion to a patron or beloved who has shown compassion. The opening image of hands covering him in the grave renders the patron's protection as total and final.

titleAr: يا هذه أقصري ما هذه بشر
titleEn: O Woman, Relent — These Are No Mortals

verses.sadr: يا هذه أقصري ما هذه بشر
خرجن في خضرة كالروض ليس لها
ريم أبت أن يريم الحزن لي جلدا

verses.ajuz: ماء من الحسن ما في صفوه كدر
ما كان يحسد أعمى من له بصر

verses.sadr: حييت من طلل لم تبق لي طللا
قالوا أتبكي على رسم فقلت لهم :
verses.ajuz: من فاته العين هدى شوقه الأثر

verses.sadr: إن الكرام كثير في البلاد وإن
verses.ajuz: قلوا كما غيرهم قل وإن كثروا
هلكى تبين من أمسى له خطر

verses.sadr: لو لم تصادف شيات البهم أكثر ما
verses.ajuz: في الخيل لم تحمد الأوضاح والغرر

verses.ajuz: نابت وقلت له ' نعم الفتى عمر

وسائل عن أبي حفص فقلت له
هو الهمام هو الصاب المريح هو ال

verses.ajuz: وكل يوم ترى في مالك الغير !

verses.sadr: لله در بني عبد العزيز فكم
verses.ajuz: أردوا عزيز عدى في خده صعر

verses.sadr: ~ حتى لقد ظن قوم أنها سور
verses.ajuz: يا ليت شعري من هاتا مآثره

verses.sadr: في معشر وبه عن معشر قصر
إن لم يكن لك في تأسيسها سفر
verses.ajuz: هل أورق المجد إلا في بني أدد ~
من الندى والردى لم يعجب السمر

translationEn: O woman, relent — these are no mortals, nor are the veiled maidens among their peers any other. They came out in a green as the meadow, with nothing but jewels on their necks for flowers. A full moon surrounded by pearls about her — my passion's tears, which are pearls, are satisfied by her. A gazelle that refused to let grief leave my endurance, while the eye — the eye — overflows with the water of longing. Youth poured upon her — in its fullness — water of beauty without turbidity in its clarity. Were it not for the eyes and the apple-cheeks, no sighted person would envy the blind. You have been greeted by ruins that left me no ruin without a sorrow watered by remembrance. They said: Do you weep over a trace? I told them: one who has missed the eye is guided by longing to the trace. The noble are many in the lands, even when few — as others are few even when many. Do not be overwhelmed by the masses of the common herd — for most of them, indeed all of them, are cattle. Whenever stakes lie among them destroyed, it is clear who is one who has a true stake. Were the markings of dark horses not found most in horses, the white blazes and white foreheads would not be praised. How excellent a youth is Umar in every calamity that strikes! And I told him: 'How excellent a youth is Umar.' He gives and is praised by whoever comes to him and praises him — so his gratitude is a recompense and his wealth is spent. A drawn sword of judgment from his resolve — its polisher for time is reflection and thought. A sharp sword — when he draws it in the face of a calamity, the daughters of time come to him to apologize. Ask about Abu Hafs — I told the asker: Hold your reins — know him — he is fate itself. He is the resolute hero, the sweet bitterness, the lightning of inspiration, the sharp and keen sword. A youth — when you see him, his forehead dispels hardship as a good omen, and ease springs from its depths. A ransom for him is a shivering miser when asked — fear of the question as if his skin has bristle. Wherever you see a necklace stripped of its adornment on my honored patron, every day you see the wealth of the other depleted! By God's grace, the sons of Abd al-Aziz — how many times have they brought down a mighty enemy's proud face! Until people thought these were suras of the Quran — would that I knew whose these noble qualities are. What is it that awaits reaching the star by poetry's length when its odes knock against each other — in a community, while through it another community is curtailed. Travel your gaze to the furthest of our noble qualities — even if there has been no travel for you in founding them. Has glory ever blossomed except in the sons of Udad? Were it not for tales that our noble deeds have preserved — of generosity and destruction — the night-conversation would not wonder.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: veiled maidens, secluded women; those kept from public view
white star-markings on a horse's forehead; white spots that are marks of beauty
white blazes on a horse's face; distinctive markings that indicate noble breeding
a bitter plant; metaphorically used for something with an initial bitterness and inner sweetness
a very sharp and keen sword; a blade that strikes decisively
arrogance of the neck; a raised, proud bearing of the head in defiance
sons of Udad; a Yemeni tribal ancestor, here the lineage of the praised family

context: A long madih for Umar (Abu Hafs), likely a high Abbasid official of the Banu Abd al-Aziz, opening with a ghazal of radiant beauty before transitioning to praise. The poem contains the famous verse calling this patron 'fate itself' and uses the metaphor of the drawn sword of judgment for his decisive wisdom.

titleEn: Let Not Enemies Gloat at Death

verses.ajuz: سنخلي لهم من عرضة الموت موردا
أكلت لهم مني لسانا ولا يدا
فأصبحت إن لم يخلف الله واحدا

translationEn: Let not the enemies gloat over death — for we shall leave for them a watering-place exposed to the same death. Do not think death a disgrace — for we have seen that the fates have struck even Muhammad. Let not the enemies think that my calamity has consumed for them my tongue or my hand. My sons and brothers followed one upon the other in a single year, so I find myself — unless God replaces one for me — alone.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to gloat, to rejoice over another's misfortune
exposed to death, placed in the path of mortality
a watering-place; a place of arrival; metaphor for death as the common destination
the fates, the deaths; the plural of maniyya — the appointed death
to follow in succession, to come one after another

context: A brief but defiant personal ritha in which Abu Tammam mourns the consecutive deaths of his sons and brothers in a single year while refusing to yield to enemies' gloating. The reference to the Prophet Muhammad's death serves as consolation — even the greatest were not spared. The poem ends with a raw vulnerability unusual in Abu Tammam's public verse.

titleAr: من أين لي صبر على الهجر
titleEn: Where Can I Find Patience

verses.sadr: من أين لي صبر على الهجر
verses.ajuz: لو أن قلبي كان من صخر ؟

verses.sadr: لو كنت أرعى النجم تقوى لقد

translationEn: Where can I find patience in separation, even if my heart were of stone? Woe to my body from the stirrings of passion — a woe that shall enter the grave with me. Had I watched the stars out of piety, my longing gaze would have reached the Night of Power.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: separation, abandonment by a beloved
the stirrings / impulses of passionate love
piety, God-consciousness
to watch the stars (idiomatic: to lie awake sleepless)
the Night of Power (Laylat al-Qadr), the most blessed night of Ramadan

context: A brief ghazal (love lyric) in which Abu Tammam laments separation from a beloved with characteristic concision. The closing verse playfully fuses the imagery of sleepless vigil with religious devotion, suggesting that his longing gaze is intense enough to perceive the holiest of nights.

titleEn: Reviving a Stolen Heart

verses.sadr: سرى رداء الهوى في حين جدته
إذ لا نعطل منها منظرا أنقا
قد قلت لما اطلخم الأمر وانبعثت
لي حرمة بك أمسى حق نازلها
كم دعوة لي إذا مكروهة نزلت

verses.ajuz: يزدنه كرما إن ساس أو سيسا

verses.sadr: ما شاهد اللبس إلا كان متضحا
يحرسن بالبذل عرضا ما يزال من ال
فرع سما في سماء العز متخذا
verses.ajuz: أصلا ثوى في قرار المجد مغروسا

verses.sadr: ليث ترى كل يوم تحت كلكله
verses.ajuz: ليثا من الإنس جهم الوجه مفروسا

verses.sadr: تجري السعود له في كل نائبة
verses.ajuz: نابت وإن كان يوم البأس منحوسا

verses.sadr: له لواء ندى ما هز عامله
وقدموا منك إن هم خاطبوا ذربا

translationEn: He revives the last spark of a heart that was stolen away, and bandages with patience a reason that had been muddled. The mantle of love crept over it while it was new — what a wonder, clothed and gladdened by it! Could you but witness me enduring tears that pour down while the night has its doors barred and blackened. The heart planted from its anguish a tree of sorrows, and whispers tended it to fruit. O people of the paradises, I count your remembrance only when God waters and grazes the paradises. When we leave no beautiful view idle, and no meadow, familiar with the gazelles of pleasure. I said, when the affair darkened and a blind calamity set out in the pitch of night: I have a sacred bond with you — the right of one who comes to you is an endowment imprisoned upon you alone, may my soul be your ransom. How many a prayer of mine has gone up when a calamity descended and the affair grew grave — O 'Ayyash, O 'Isa! By God, the deeds and character of 'Ayyash surpass in generosity whether he rules or is ruled. Whenever ambiguity is witnessed it becomes clear, and whenever truth is distant it becomes obscured. Clouds of his bounties overflowed and obliterated misery until they uprooted wretchedness. They guard honour through giving — honour that is ever protected from calamities by radiant gifts. A branch that soared in the sky of glory, taking as its root one planted in the heart of nobility. A lion: every day you see beneath his paw another lion of humankind, grim-faced and mauled. An intrepid, resolute strider toward ambitions that drown the camels in their surging depths. He competed with the great and seized their minds from them, so that the right was entrusted to him and coveted by all. Fortune runs for him in every calamity that strikes, even when the day of hardship seems ill-starred. He has a banner of generosity — whenever its shaft is shaken, you see the banner of miserliness overturned. His lineage is matched among the sons of the Yemeni kings, root for root, summit for summit. Those who crowd the watering-troughs of death in groups and squadrons and more squadrons. Those who guard the watering-troughs of glory when threatened — as lions guard thickets and lairs. You grew, a mighty elephant of time, when an affair beset him, resembling ancestral giants. And they presented you when they pleaded urgently, striking the solid rock of Hadhrami stone. High-nosed, lordly, whose radiant forehead brands the hunted, and fiercely proud, dazzling the eyes of the squinters. My hopes watch your lightning-flash from Egypt, and even were I at Tus, I would not find Tus far.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: last flicker of life, remnant spark of the soul
stolen, taken away
confused, disturbed (of the mind)
intense darknesses, pitch-black nights
the Yemeni kings bearing the title Dhu (lords of)
squadrons, large cavalry divisions
a mighty, powerful male camel; used figuratively for a great man

context: A panegyric to 'Ayyash ibn Lahi'a (or a patron named 'Ayyash), in which Abu Tammam opens with a love-prelude lamenting separation before pivoting to lavish praise of the patron's generosity, lineage, and martial courage. The poem is notable for its rich South Arabian tribal vocabulary and the striking comparison of the patron's ancestors to the ancient Yemeni kings.

titleEn: Muhammad's Death Recalls Muhammad

verses.sadr: وكان الأسى قد آل فيه إلى الحشا

verses.ajuz: بما هاج من فيض التلاع القوابل

verses.sadr: ثووا في الثرى من بعدما سربلوا العلا

translationEn: The killing of Muhammad reminded me of Muhammad and of Qahtaba — a remembrance long in its torments. Grief had settled into the innermost core, and when it was stirred it flowed through every joint, like water of a pool that spreads after it falls, swelled by the flood of the receiving gullies. They were laid in the dust after they had dressed glory in a robe and after they had risen as the stars of assemblies. Their deaths bequeathed no shame — yet the gloating one gloats over them in the sight of the ignorant. By your life, they were not three brothers — they were three tribes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sorrows, anguishes, anxious thoughts
grief, deep sorrow
the innermost part of the body, the bowels; seat of deep emotion
a pool of standing water, a small lake left by rain
they clothed glory (i.e., they were the garment of nobility)
shame, disgrace, ignominy
Qahtaba ibn Shabib al-Ta'i, a famous Abbasid commander

context: An elegy lamenting the death of a man named Muhammad alongside the memory of Qahtaba ibn Shabib, a celebrated Abbasid general who died in 749 CE. Abu Tammam's closing verse — calling the three men three tribes rather than three brothers — is among his most celebrated epigrams for the power it confers on the deceased.

titleEn: Leaning Like a Blooming Branch

verses.sadr: قد قلتث لما لج في صده
إن لم تجد لي صحت بين الورى

translationEn: Leaning like a blooming bough, radiant as the shining moon. His eyelids shoot the people of passion with arrows from his languid glance. I said when he persisted in his aversion: have mercy on your slave, O you who buries me! If you show me no compassion, I will cry out among the people: woe is me from the gazelle of the tribe of 'Amir!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: leaning, reclining gracefully
radiant-faced, bright-browed
languid, heavy-lidded (of the eye)
persisted in his turning away, was obstinate in rejection
the one who buries me; a hyperbole for the beloved's cruelty
people, humankind

context: A short ghazal in which Abu Tammam paints the beloved as a graceful, moon-like youth whose languorous glance wounds lovers. The poem ends with a comic-plaintive threat to proclaim his suffering publicly if the beloved continues to be indifferent.

titleEn: Their Dwelling Once Fresh Now Worn

verses.sadr: ولئن حبست على البلى لقد اغتدى
قد أوتيت من كل شيء بهجة
ما في النجوم سوى تعلة باطل
فتن جلوت ظلامها من بعد ما
غرم امرىء من روحه فيها إذا
من بعد ما صارت هنيدة صرمة
ألوى يذل الصعب إن هو ساسه
من لم يقد فيطير في خيشومه
أعط الرياسة من يديك فلم تزل
verses.ajuz: من قبل أن تدعى الرئيس رئيسا

verses.sadr: تخذ القنا خيسا فإن طاغ طغى
لو أن أسباب العفاف بلا تقى
من دوحة الكلم التي لم تنفكك

translationEn: Their dwelling, once fresh, I see now worn to rags, and to your guests it serves anguish and fever. Though it is captive to decay, my tears over you remain captive until death. It is as though Tasm and the 'Amaliq and Jadis had been neighbours in you once. I see your dwelling now desolate — you who were once a familiar and companionable place. Bare empty expanses stripped bare, as though its former inhabitants swore an oath of destruction upon you. Does separation think I am heedless of it, when its hands have already touched me tenderly? Young women of perfect bloom struck by separation — they had been full moons in clouded nights and blazing suns. Bright-eyed ones whose eyes turn toward youth as though turning cups in their orbit. As though the anemones had been gifted to their cheeks by Abu Qabus himself. She was given splendour and warmth and beauty in youth, soaked through. Were it not for her youth and my inability to find a throne for her, I would think her Bilqis. O Damascus, you have gathered nobility through Abu al-Mughith, and an ancient sovereign glory. Time came to you with a joyful, smiling face — it had been scowling before. Those loins were blessed and those backs hallowed by his nearness. A favour bestowed, a calamity resolved, a great matter handled, a wound dressed. Now the hypocrites have evening and morning — eyes that were once fierce have become blind without you. You left that land a cool pleasant shade, after it had almost become an oven. They did not know until you rose over them like a full moon splitting the deep darkness. The stars hold nothing but the distraction of falsehood, long established and long laid as foundation. Kings are our stars, appearing and disappearing, bringing fortune and ill. You banished the darkness of seditions after they had stretched their eyes and necks toward them. A war in which the army is but the leavings of its morning draught, and the leftovers of its evening meal are a whole squadron. The sacrifice of a man's very soul in it, when the man of peace pays only with food and clothing. How different are people who spend only money, and people who spend their souls! The son of Ibrahim, Musa, marched a march that stilled time — it had been tempestuous before. He settled the heartland of Syria and resurrected from his hands a wrong long buried. Ascalon had been the bride, but by his rule Damascus became the bride. After the herd had scattered and the great sum had shrunk to a purse. As though they had gone astray with the calf for an era, and as though Musa, when he came to them, was Moses. The blessing you have wrought will be thanked — there is no blessing like one that rescues from misery. A skilful one who tames the unruly when he governs it, and whose side softens when he is governed. That is why none was made chief among them who had not been tested in judgment as a subject. One who has never led so that the dust of the army flies in his nostrils shall never lead an army. Take the leadership from your own hands — you were a leader before ever being called one. What could you want, with a serpent that cuts down lions before you and 'Isa behind you? Two lions that stormed from Damascus and tamed from Homs its most impregnable jungle. He made the spears a thicket, and if a rebel rebelled he was transferred to that thicket. Water your subjects from your cheerfulness — were it water, it would be well-seasoned. Openness and generosity are better for them than a chastity frozen stiff upon you. Were chastity without piety of any use, it would have benefited Iblis. These rhymes have come to you straining, embodying early morning and pre-dawn darkness. From every wild verse that leaves the rest of men's share of poetry meagre. With fresh meaning, when the meaning they have laboured over is long worn. You enjoy their immediate beauty and count them a precious bond for the ends of time. From the tree of words whose sturdy verse is always brought captive to you. Like a star: when you travel it accompanies you, and when you dismount it sits with you. We have sent the poem to you alone, and should you permit us we will send the white camels too. Seeking your shelter when a murky, violent calamity strikes the well-known one of battle.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fresh, new, pristine (of a dwelling or garment)
worn out, tattered (of a garment or dwelling)
intense, profound darkness
a large cavalry squadron, a fighting unit
setting out or travelling in the darkness before dawn
the Queen of Sheba (Bilqis), the legendary queen of Yemen
a dense thicket, a lion's lair; here used for a heavily fortified city

context: A long panegyric to Abu al-Mughith Musa ibn Ibrahim, governor of Damascus, celebrating his arrival and its transformation of the city. The poem opens with a conventional nasib (love prelude) lamenting a deserted encampment before transitioning to extended praise of the patron's governance, justice, and martial prowess in settling Syria.

titleAr: لا تعذلي جارتي إنى لك العذل
titleEn: Do Not Blame Me, Neighbour

verses.sadr: لا تعذلي جارتي إنى لك العذل
verses.ajuz: فلا شوى ما زريناه ولا جلل

verses.sadr: إحدى المصائب حلت في ديار بني
verses.ajuz: عمران ليست لها أخت ولا مثل

verses.sadr: ألوى به وهو ملو بالقنا لتوا
كان الذي ليس في معجومه خور
كان الذي يتقى ريب الزمان به

verses.ajuz: يحيى بن عمران لو أنسي لك الأجل

verses.sadr: أي امرئ منك أثرى بين أعظمه
ماقال كان إذا ما القوم أكذب ما
verses.ajuz: أطال من قولهم تقصير ما فعلوا

verses.sadr: يا موت حسبك إذ أقصدت مهجته
verses.ajuz: أولا فدونك لا حسب ولا بجل

verses.sadr: ماحالنا يا أبا العباس بعدك هل
