يا موت لو في وغى عاينته خلدت
بكل يوم وغى تصدى الكمأة به

verses.ajuz: إذا الرجال رأوه وهو يفعل ما
إما يدل منك بالموت العدى فبما

verses.sadr: دارت عليهم بلا موت لك الدول

verses.ajuz: إذ لابس الذلة المقطوع ذو رحم
جرعك الدهر كاس الصبر في لجج
موتا وقتلا كأن الدهر يظمأ ما

verses.sadr: مذ صال فيك الردى إلا بنا شغل
verses.ajuz: يا حلية المجد إن المجد عن عفر
يا موئلا كان مأوى الآمات به

verses.sadr: ما الناس يوم حفاظ حصلوا قلل

verses.ajuz: يعطي فيجزل أو يدعى فينزل أو
أضحى لنا بدلا منه تنوء به

verses.sadr: والشبل من ليثه إما مضى بدل

translationEn: Do not blame me, neighbour — I am the one who deserves blame. What we have lost is neither trivial nor a small matter. One of the calamities has descended upon the homes of the sons of 'Imran, without sister or equal. It swept away their crowns on the day misfortune was granted it, and Saturn kindled his fire in it. It twisted him away — and he was erect as spearshafts, with them upright and their necks curved. He was the one in whose character no weakness lay for those who tested it, and no flaw in his conduct. He was the one against whom the vicissitudes of time were guarded, when time showed its crooked fangs. Time settled us in a flat and easy valley once you, O mountain, collapsed from it. How beautiful were the states of the Ash'aris, O Yahya ibn 'Imran, had your appointed time been forgotten for you. What man among you enriched the soil between his bones — the soil of al-Muqattam or the sand of its flat plain? He did not follow his giving with reminders of it, nor did obstacles govern his beneficence. He never said "it was" when men most falsely prolonged their words to make up for the shortcoming of what they did. O Death, enough — you struck down his noble soul; take it then; no lineage nor rank shall stop you. What is our state, O Abu al-'Abbas, after you — do branches grow after the root has perished? O Death, had I witnessed him in battle, I would have kept eternal vigil over him instead of these flowing tears. He who kindled war when it was smouldering and who entered its sanctuary when it blazed. On every day of battle the truffles bowed before him as the swords and spears were quenched in blood. He charged into battle with spears, horses grim-faced, with no feeble nor sluggish horse among them. And the reliever of distresses that dark affairs drape over the lands. In an encounter no stumbling deterred him, and a speech no foolishness ever marred. Fully composed — neither delay loosening his resolve nor haste mounting his delivery. There where men could not place their judgments aright, seeing him act what perplexed their deeds, they said: such is the man! Even in death your enemies find reason to exult over you — but by what? Fortune circled against them without your death; when your sword was drawn and your sea of generosity was flowing, justice was dealt and your peer was bound in full measure. When the one stripped of honour was a kinsman of severed bond, you severed it, and when the connected one needed your connection, time made you drink the cup of patience in the surging depths of death — dying and slaughter as though time thirsted for those who lived, quenched only when they died and were slain. O you who kept time occupied away from us — what does its assault mean since it assailed you in us but to keep us busy? O ornament of glory — glory has appeared and its adornment is stripped bare of you. O refuge that was the shelter of hopes when calamities darkened with their worst afflictions. So what firm support allows a deed to thrive, and what awaited one allows a hope to live? But Husayn and those like Husayn — when all people on a day of honour were assembled on hilltops — the battlefield attests that he is a pillar, and the terror of battle declares him a hero. He gives and gives generously, or is called and descends, or is brought burdens and bears them. You would take him for an elder were it not for his youth, just as a crop shoots up alone and then grows full. He has become our substitute, by whom we are bowed — the lion's cub is the substitute for the lion when the lion is gone.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the Ash'ari tribe, a Yemeni tribal confederation
crooked, curved (of fangs or teeth)
truffles; here used figuratively for brave men who throw themselves into battle
one who kindles war, a fierce warrior
the surge and turbulence of a wave or flood
collapsed, fell apart (of a structure or mountain)
a lion cub; used figuratively for a young noble heir

context: An elegy for Yahya ibn 'Imran, a military commander from the Ash'ari tribe. The poem is addressed to a female neighbour and blends personal grief with hyperbolic praise of the deceased's military and moral virtues. It closes with consolation in the form of his son Husayn, who is presented as a worthy successor.

titleEn: I Thank Her Before Her Union

verses.ajuz: وإن زعمت أني لها مضمر غدرا
ولم أر درا قبله ينظم الدرا

translationEn: I rush to thank her before even her union arrives, and if she abandons me one day I seek an excuse for her. I consider her faithful in my heart even in treachery, even though she thinks I harbour treachery toward her. Her family's fragrance came to her and she laughed, saying: does one seek perfume while perfume itself is master here? Her speech is pearls and her words are pearls — yet I never saw pearls that strung other pearls before.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I hasten to her, I rush to precede
union, reunion with the beloved
treachery, betrayal
faithful, loyal
she smiled and laughed
pearls; also used figuratively for precious, beautiful speech

context: A brief ghazal remarkable for its wit: the lover so idealises the beloved that he thanks her in advance and finds excuses for her before any offence is committed. The closing verse employs a characteristic Abu Tammam conceit — her words are pearls so fine that they themselves string the pearls of her speech.

titleEn: Asma Lowered Him the Sun's Rope

verses.sadr: أبا علي أنت وادي الندى ال

verses.ajuz: ث الغيث في الأزمة والدار خيس

verses.sadr: وكل لون فليكن ما خلا ال

verses.ajuz: في المحل أو زفت إليهم عروس

translationEn: Asma lowered him the rope of the suns, while union and separation were bliss and misery. She found no quenching in satiety and felt no heart softened by her gentle touch. The fortunate stars of the world — misfortune came and pointed them out to you, O Abu 'Ali — you are the verdant valley of generosity, the familiar abode of noble deeds. The house where the star is and the hand where rain falls in drought, and the home is a lion's thicket. O son of Raja', you granted me the intention to ride toward you — hesitation and anxiety kept me. Extend my reins with a sound one-ribbed beast; it will stand firm while its excuse sways. I fight anxiety by driving it hard, for the war against anxiety is a fierce war. When the swift stallions sought its dust-cloud, their share of it was mere trifling scraps. A white-blazed horse that is not given to stumbling on the left leg — the left-footed ones among them are unlucky. And let any colour be as it will, except grey — greyness is a worn-out colour. With full barrel-ribs and flanks not hollowed out — excessive leanness in them is a fault. If it visits a racing-field as a winner or a council-meeting, those seated rise for it. You see the staid and dignified of the people have allowed their eyes to revel in its beauty — though they are squinting eyes. As though a flash of lightning appeared to them in drought, or as though a bride was escorted to them. Majestic when you survey it broadside: its upper parts lush and its resting place firm. When a cheek of it prances in its gait, the escort and the whole army are in its grace. As though a fit of madness had seized it, or as though wine had flirted with its head. The envious man guarded it jealously, and souls fluttered in fear over it. And like it is the sleek-maned one — I have mounted it while its haunches are smooth and firm. I left it while it stood upon an elevated distinction, imprisoned in the paths of glory. And a foolish new event I treated — its relapse was that of a misshapen one. I quelled it while time from its tribulations was as though the blazing oven had been lit in it. Until hardship turned to its ease and that scowl smoothed from its cheeks. Neither did those who sought your bounty fall short, nor did the healthy among them become prey to the nights. So grip praise firmly — for when the leech senses its target, it is a precious leech. And go early to its composer — for it is, by my life, a garment that souls choose for themselves.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the rope of the suns; metaphor for a rope so elevated it reaches the sky
swift, fully-grown stallions
old, well-aged wine
the blazing oven; used figuratively for the heat of battle or tribulation
misshapen, grotesque (of an ugly or deformed person)
fierce, savage, brutal (of a war or calamity)
smooth and firm, strong (of an animal's haunches)

context: A panegyric to Abu 'Ali ibn Raja', combining a ghazal prelude about Asma with an extended horse-description (wasf al-faras) and concluding praise of the patron's generosity. The poem is notable for its rich equestrian vocabulary and the playful final image comparing the poet's verses to a garment that souls choose to wear.

titleEn: We Slept but Time Did Not

verses.sadr: شرقنا بذم الدهر يا سلم إنه
إذا فقد المفقود من آل مالك
خليلي من بعد الأسى والجوى قفا

verses.ajuz: وحسب البكا إن قلت مصرع هاشم
به ثم قد شاركننا في المآتم ؟ !

verses.sadr: ولو عاش فينا بعض عيش فعاله
لئن كان سيف الموت أسود صارما
verses.ajuz: لقد فل منه حد أبيض صارم
عليه إذا ما سيل غير كرائم

verses.sadr: جرى المجد مجرى النوم منه فلم يكن

verses.ajuz: بأن الندى في روحه غير نائم

verses.sadr: فإن توه في الدنيا دعائم عمره
إذا المرء لم تهدم علاه حياته
وما يوم زرت اللحد يومك وحده
فكم ملحد في يوم ذلك غانم
verses.ajuz: وكم منبر في يوم ذلك غارم !

verses.sadr: لئن عم ثكلا كل شيء مصابه
بني مالك قد نبهت خامل الثرى
إذا اختل ثغر المجد أضحى جلادهم
إذا ما رماح القوم في الروع أكرمت

translationEn: We slept while the vicissitudes of time do not sleep — they bridled us by force without halters. Do you not see its hours and their division of the souls of the world's children like the division of spoils? Nights whose eyes, when they bore down upon you, showed you a lesson in the eyes of the serpents. We choked on blaming time, O Salm — it harms and spares no effort, yet it is not unjust. When the one lost from the family of Malik was lost, my heart broke for the sake of noble deeds. My two friends, stand fast after grief and yearning — but do not hold back the flood of streaming tears. Stop here, for this is the battlefield of courage and generosity — and weeping enough when you say: the battlefield of Hashim! Have you not seen how the days bereft us of him and then shared in our mourning? His generosity and valour were approached through qualities more protective than the curtains of amulets. Qualities like double-woven chainmail — the barbs of blamers could never penetrate them. Had he lived among us even a portion of the life his deeds lived, he would have exhausted the lifespans of the giant vultures. Time saw a stumble from him and would not pardon it — and does a resolute man seek refuge at the stumble of another? If the sword of death is dark and sharp, a bright sharp sword has already notched it. It struck a man whose most prized possessions, when asked, were never prized possessions. Glory flowed through him like sleep — he was never dreaming except in battle or in giving. In his radiance it was evident, even while he slept, that generosity in his soul was not asleep. If the pillars of his life have collapsed in the world, his generosity in it is not built on weak pillars. If a man's elevation is not demolished by his own life, then majestic death is no demolisher of it. O Hashim, tears have become a constant companion — they were never a constant companion but for you. O Hashim, there are twin calamities in you for both tribes — combined, they would fill some of the great sacred seasons. Striving efforts scattered across all the seasons — had they been gathered they would fill but some of the seasons. Your day with the Azd was a day when the Khuza'a broke away in the lowlands of Tihama. The day you visited the grave was not your day alone upon us — it was the day of 'Umar and Hatim. How many a heretic gained on that day, and how many a pulpit was bereft! If his calamity spread bereavement to all things, it specifically struck the edges of sharp swords. The world was stripped over him, and its character became like the dark and pitch-black mountain passes. No disaster that missed him was a great one — but it is among the mother of all great disasters. O sons of Malik, you have stirred the obscure ground: you have graves rising high in visible landmarks. Firm ones — the hand measures a short distance from their reach, and glory upon them cannot be climbed by ladders. You have fulfilled the earth's dues with the greatest bones that spent an age fulfilling the dues of the standing-places. You are deceived if you believe it true that any deep dark place opens except onto faces of the brave wanderers. I see them as the wings of a bird: when the leading feathers wilt, they are supported by new leading feathers. When a gap appears in the frontier of glory, their fighting and their giving are its guardians. Do not seek their swords in their sheaths — they have been lodged between throats and skulls. When the spears of the people in battle honour their drinking-places, they live as those who have honoured their eating-places.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the vicissitudes of time, the ever-turning wheel of fate
a type of dangerous, speckled serpent; eyes of serpents
fine, closely-woven chainmail armour
giant vultures, which are proverbially long-lived
the lowlands along the Tihama coast of Arabia
literally a necessary blow; idiom for something unavoidable or constant
dark and pitch-black mountain passes

context: An elegy for Hashim ibn Malik from the Banu Malik, combining cosmic imagery with tribal eulogy. Abu Tammam addresses both a female figure named Salm and two male friends, drawing on the traditions of the pre-Islamic nasib and the tribal elegy. The poem is praised for the verse asserting that if a man's greatness is not ruined by his own life, death cannot ruin it.

titleAr: قد صنف الحسن في خديك جوهره
titleEn: Beauty Arranged Its Jewels in Your Cheeks

verses.sadr: قد صنف الحسن في خديك جوهره

verses.ajuz: مذ خط هاروت في عينيك عسكره

translationEn: Beauty has arranged its jewels in your cheeks, and left there its reddest blush from the apple. All beauty takes its origin from your eyes, ever since Harut drew up his army in your eyes. Your cheek was long ago radiant and pure white — but the lodging of the glance has saffron-stained it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its jewel, its essence, the finest of its kind
Harut, the fallen angel of Babylon associated with magic and bewitching eyes
his army; used metaphorically for the magic/witchcraft Harut deployed
intensely white, brilliantly pure
its saffron-yellow tint; the stain left by passion on the cheek
settled, established itself firmly

context: A tightly crafted three-verse ghazal praising the beloved's beauty through the imagery of jewels, the angel Harut, and the saffron-stain of passion. The reference to Harut links to Quran 2:102, where Harut is one of the angels who taught magic in Babylon.

titleEn: Do the Proud of Bakr Boast, O Weakling

verses.sadr: في شامتين هو الشري الجني لهم

verses.ajuz: من بعد ما جاذبوه وهو معترض

verses.sadr: سهم الخليفة في الهيجا إذ سعرت
بذلك السهم ذي النصلين قد حفزا
ظل من الله أضحى أمس منبسطا
verses.ajuz: به على الثغر فهو اليوم منقبض
منه ، وليس له من خالد عوض

verses.sadr: لم تنتقض عروة منه ولا سبب

translationEn: Does the noble stock of Bakr boast, O you feeble one, and its star — you doomed, wretched creature? You lean on a deaf boulder, thinking it a limb you can whittle and hollow. In two gloating ones it is for them the bitter colocynth just harvested, the coloquint, the poisoned choke-hold, and decay. Concealers of envy that harms none but themselves — as though it were a disease in their bodies. Let not the faction with reddened eyes rejoice at the frontier of Arran in this sudden event. The grief has lodged lengthwise in their throats after they tried to pull it out when it was lying crosswise. The Caliph's arrow in battle when it blazed with swords and the belts and quivers intertwined — that arrow with the twin blades, feathered with the wing of two vultures, is what strikes that target. A shade from God that spread yesterday over the frontier has now contracted. Every region has a substitute for Khalid, but Khalid has no substitute. Not a bond nor a rope has broken from him — but the affair of those who hope breaks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the finest and most honoured stock, the nobles of a tribe
a weak, contemptible person
colocynth plant, extremely bitter; used figuratively for something bitter or harmful
a bitter plant; used as an image of bitterness and poison
Arran (Caucasian Albania), a frontier province of the Abbasid caliphate
a bone stuck in the throat; grief that is impossible to swallow
lying crosswise, obstructing the passage

context: A poem lamenting the loss of Khalid, a frontier commander (possibly Khalid ibn Yazid) in the Arran region of the Caucasus, and mocking the enemy's gloating. The poem employs the striking image of grief as a bone stuck crosswise in the throats of the gloating enemies, and closes with the famous epigram on Khalid's irreplaceability.

titleEn: Muhammad ibn Humaid, His Bones Now Worn

verses.ajuz: أريق ماء المعالي مذ أريق دمه
كالبدر حين جلت عن وجهه ظلمه

verses.sadr: في روضة قد علا حافاتها زهر
فقلت والدمع من حزن ومن فرح
ألم تمت ياشقيق النفس مذ زمن ؟
verses.ajuz: فقال لي : لم يمت من لم يمت كرمه

translationEn: Muhammad ibn Humaid — his bones have worn away; the water of noble deeds was spilt when his blood was spilt. The hand of time awoke to the sons of Nabhan the day he lay down, and ravaged them with its hand and mouth. I saw him in a dream with his sword-belt, sitting cross-legged like the full moon when the darkness clears from his face. In a garden whose edges were crowned with blossoms — I knew upon waking that they were his blessings. I spoke, tears flowing from grief and joy alike, streaming full down both cheeks: did you not die, O companion of my soul, some time ago? He answered: he who has not lost his generosity has not died.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his bones, his remains
the water of noble deeds; glory, prestige (figuratively)
the sons of Nabhan, a branch of the Tayy tribe
the sword-belt worn over the shoulder
sitting cross-legged with a rope around the knees and back (traditional seated posture)
companion of the soul, one as dear as a part of oneself

context: An elegy for Muhammad ibn Humaid al-Tusi, a distinguished Abbasid general who died in 838 CE fighting the Khurramites under Babak. Abu Tammam frames the poem as a dream vision in which the poet sees the general in paradise, closing with the celebrated aphorism: he who has not lost his generosity has not died.

titleEn: Your Teeth Like Hail and Lightning

verses.sadr: كم فتى ذل للزمان وقد ال
قد فضضنا من بيده خاتم الخو
وقواف قد ضج منها لما استع

verses.ajuz: د فإن مات الجود مات القريض

verses.sadr: كن طويل الندى عريضا فقد ساد
قل لعا لابن عشرة ما له من

translationEn: Your front teeth — they are young hail and pearls strung close together and a flash of lightning. And anemones lit up in the plains, shaken in the morning by a lush meadow. And the trembling of drowsiness in your eyes in sleep, in all its modes — while no drowsiness touches my eyes. The tumult of events overwhelmed me until I did not know which of them to wade into. Have the days been stabbing me with a crooked glance while before they kept their gaze lowered before me? How can one who rises to the heights of glory be content to begin his day with the wing of ascent broken from him? An ambition that headbutts the stars, and a serious resolve accustomed to the lowlands — it is thus lowland. How many a youth has humbled himself to time, and handed its keys over to it! A sharp-witted man who makes the well-tempered Mashrafi blade ring for him and the slender Zabi'i spear. Over a desert plain as though the mirage lay upon it and linen sheets of smooth white cloth were spread on it. The spirited, untamed, exuberant horse becomes in it like a hobbled beast. We have broken the seal of fear from the hand of the one who holds it — and not every seal is broken. The Mahri camels pace in it, their saddlebags worn down to their humps. Crossing the black, hard plains — their direction toward you, O faces of noble deeds, is bright. Swift, their riders urging them — hopes in you come one after another like lots cast in succession. They hastened, grinding the road with steady effort, chewing the exhaustion while it was still half-raw. He who is not stirred by indirect praise to glory and pre-eminence will not be stirred by direct praise. Every day spent brings a type of poem and a metre follows a metre in you. And rhymes that groaned when the nominative and the genitive were employed in them. Lavish praise and gratitude and thought, and the bitterness of reproach and incitement. The life of poetry is your giving life to generosity — if generosity dies, poetry dies. Be long and broad in giving — my praise of you has already prevailed in its long breadth. The seas catch seas — they overflow the more they are drawn from. O lover of beneficence in a time when beneficence has become despised. Tell him who grovels: the son of his tenth has no way to rise from it except through your generosity. Do not be to me — as you will not be — like people whose staff, when tested, proves rotten. They have a face of cheerfulness spread out for those who seek nothing, and their giving is withheld. The least useful thing is soundness of speech while the deeds are sick.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: young hailstones; also used for pearly-white teeth
anemone flowers; often compared to white teeth
the mirage, shimmering heat haze over the desert
a lively, restive, hard-to-control horse
Mahri camels, a superior breed known for speed
literally the nominative and the genitive case; a playful grammatical metaphor
poetry, verse (a formal literary term)

context: A long panegyric to an unnamed patron, opened with a conventional but vivid description of the beloved's teeth and eyes before transitioning to a desert journey and then extended praise of the patron's generosity. The poem contains the celebrated couplet: 'the life of poetry is your giving life to generosity — if generosity dies, poetry dies,' which became a famous maxim of Arabic literary culture.

titleEn: God Have Mercy on Ja'far
translationEn: God have mercy on Ja'far — he was proud and valiant and he was merciful. Death and humiliation appeared before his eyes, and he saw both as a momentous calamity. Then honour drove him forward and he slew the enemies and died nobly.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: proud, refusing to accept humiliation
valiant, spirited, courageous
abasement, humiliation, degradation
honour, protective zeal, the impulse of noble pride
forward, onward (adverb of direction and decisiveness)

context: A brief, elegant three-verse elegy for Ja'far, a man of valour who chose death in battle over humiliation. The poem's power lies in its compression: the choice between death and dishonour, and the protagonist's decision to kill before being killed, are captured in a single closing verse.

titleEn: This Is Your Love and Its Traces
translationEn: This is your love, and these are its traces — as for the heart, it knows no settling in its place. He joins groan to groan connected to a sigh joined to the scorching of longing whose fire is never quenched. He called upon the tears and they came pouring in torrents from yearning — that is the utmost of their capacity and of his. From an eye denied sleep, bewildered by love, equally wakeful through his night and his day.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: love, passion, desire
groan, moan of pain or longing
a deep sigh, a sigh of grief or passion
burning thirst or intense inner heat; the scorching of longing
pouring in torrents, overflowing (of tears)
one enslaved by love, completely overtaken by passion

context: A compressed ghazal enumerating the physical symptoms of the lover's state: a restless heart, chained groans and sighs, unquenchable inner fire, and sleepless nights. The poem is a catalogue of amatory suffering characteristic of Abu Tammam's lyric style.

titleEn: The Gazelle of the Dunes, But For Her Legs

verses.ajuz: وإن محض الإعراض لي منك ماحض

verses.sadr: رعت طرفها في هامة قد تنكرت

verses.ajuz: وما عائض منها وإن جل عائض

verses.sadr: ولا كشف الليل النهار وقد بدا
وأخرى لحتني حين لم أمنع النوى

verses.ajuz: وهل يفرس الليث الطلى وهو رابض
وجأش على ما يحدث الدهر خافض

verses.sadr: إذا ما رأته العيس ظلت كأنما
معيدين ورد الحوض قد هدم البلى
فلم تنصرم إلا وفي كل وهدة
verses.ajuz: ونشز لها واد من العرف فائض

verses.sadr: أخا الحرب كم ألحقتها وهي حائل
إذا عرض رعديد تدنس في الوغى
إذا كانت الأنفاس جمرا لدى الوغى

verses.ajuz: إذا جاض عن حد الأسنة جائض

verses.sadr: إذا قبض النقع العيون سما له
وقد علم الحزم الذي أنت ربه
verses.ajuz: بأن لا يعي العظم الذي أنت هائض
سيغرق في البحر الذي أنت خائض
بطاء عن الشعر الذي أنا قارض
يبارز إذ ناديت من ذا يعارض

verses.sadr: فلا تنكروا ذل القوافي فقد رأى

translationEn: The white oryx of the dunes — were it not for her legs and the hollows of her knees — and your pure turning away from me is a pure, sour milk. She grazed her glance at a hilltop that had changed and whose herbage had withered though it had just been fresh. She turned away and gained in exchange grief and longing — and there is no compensation for her however great the compensation. The Yemeni sword was never polished for a battle as those cheeks were polished yesterday. Night never uncovered day at its dawning as those hidden sorrows uncovered. No incompetent woman who unravelled her threads ever worked as those overflowing tears worked. Another blamed me when I did not restrain separation from leading me and no one cut the thread of my resolve. She wanted me to gather pleasures while at rest — but does a lion devour its prey while lying still? She is the proud, high-cheeked she-camel and the son of courage, with a breast serene at whatever time brings. When the camels see him, they stand as if shaken by a gust of the roses of Yamama. To you there journeyed with praise a people as though on smooth saddles they were like the slithering serpents of the rocky hollows. Returning to the watering-trough whose decay has demolished its stone supports and worn away its tracks. We watch lightning-flashes from your generosity; when the first of them gleams they are like pulsing veins. They kept rising until they were like gleaming swords on the horizon of the world. They did not pass without filling every hollow and every ridge with a valley overflowing with honour. O brother of war, how many times have you set it going when it was barren, and delayed it from its time when it was about to deliver. When a coward becomes soiled in the fray, your sword in battle is the washer of your honour. When breaths are embers at the battle's edge and the people's garments have narrowed though they are wide. Where the quiet hearts are throbbing and the water of the generous faces has dried. You are the one by whose name war is wakened when one who flees the edge of spearpoints flees. When the dust-cloud grabs the eyes, a hero rises, grasping on the embers of protective fury. The resolution — of which you are the master — has learned not to be broken by the bone you are crushing. And the peer who rivals you has learned that he will drown in the sea you are wading. As those who compose poetry have learned they are slow behind the poetry I recite. I am like a gold dinar calling: is there a young man to duel? — when I called, who shall challenge? Do not deny the submission of the rhymes, for their master has seen that I am their tamer for all time.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: white oryx; the standard classical comparison for a beautiful woman
the hollows behind the knees (of the oryx); joints, limbs
dried up, withered (of vegetation)
just sprouting, newly fresh (of herbage)
slithering, gliding (of serpents)
protective honour, the fury of one defending his honour
a tamer, one who breaks and trains horses or wild things

context: A polished panegyric combining a nasib about a white oryx-like beloved, a description of the patron's horse and camel-journey, and extended military praise. The poem concludes with Abu Tammam's celebrated self-praise as a poet, declaring himself the eternal tamer of rhymes — a confident assertion of his literary mastery.

titleAr: اليوم أدرج زيد الخيل في كفن
titleEn: Today Zayd al-Khayl Is Wrapped in a Shroud

verses.sadr: اليوم أدرج زيد الخيل في كفن
بني حميد لو ان الدهر متزع
verses.ajuz: لصد من ذكركم عن جانب خشن
لابل على أدد لابل على اليمن

verses.sadr: لم يثكلوا ليث حرب مثل قحطبة
verses.ajuz: من بعد قحطبة في سالف الزمن

verses.sadr: إلا تكن صدرت عن منظر حسن
verses.ajuz: حرب ، فقد صدرت عن مسمع حسن

verses.sadr: نعم الفتى غير نكس في الجلاد ولا
verses.ajuz: لدن الفؤاد لدى وقع القنا اللدن

verses.sadr: حن إلى الموت حتى ظن جاهله

verses.ajuz: يسكن سوى الميتة العليا إلى سكن

verses.sadr: لو لم يمت بين أطراف الرماح إذا
verses.ajuz: لمات إذ لم يمت من شدة الحزن

translationEn: Today Zayd al-Khayl is wrapped in a shroud and the knotted tears of the copiously-weeping eyes are loosed. O sons of Humaid, if time were reasonable it would have turned from the rough side of its nature toward your remembrance. If the vicissitudes of time sift your souls and the rest of the people remain safe between watering-trough and resting-place — the sweetest water is not strange that it runs dry while the stale and putrid water lives on. A calamity upon Tayy that cast its full weight upon it — nay, upon Adad, nay, upon the whole of Yemen. They have not lost a lion of war like Qahtaba, since Qahtaba in the ages past. If the war did not originate from a beautiful sight, it has at least originated from a beautiful ear. An excellent youth: not a coward in battle, and not soft-hearted at the clash of supple spears. He yearned toward death until the ignorant thought he was longing homesick toward a homeland. He turned toward the defenders and in his fierce charge with his zeal he was like one bound in harness. He saw death as the snares of souls, and settled for nothing but the loftiest kind of dying. Had he not died among the tips of the spears, he would have died from the intensity of grief for not having died.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: copiously weeping (of eyes), heavily flowing (of tears)
one who restrains himself, who holds back from extremes
the vicissitudes of time, its sudden and harsh events
stagnant, putrid water that has changed in colour and taste
the full weight of the chest (of a camel pressing to the ground); total oppressive weight
snares, traps set for animals
the loftiest kind of death; dying as a hero or martyr in battle

context: An elegy for a warrior named Zayd al-Khayl from the Banu Humaid, a branch of the Tayy tribe. The poem draws on the same tradition as Abu Tammam's famous elegy for Muhammad ibn Humaid, praising the warrior's hunger for death in battle. The closing verse — that had he not died among spears he would have died of grief for not dying — is among Abu Tammam's most celebrated paradoxes.

titleEn: The Day of Parting Is a Grim Day
translationEn: The day of parting is a grim day — what a river the souls flow into from it. I have always despised Thursday without knowing why — until Thursday struck me. By my father's life: she whose father, seeing her in his passionate love, said: would that we were Magians (so he could marry her).

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grim, scowling, severe (a day that is harsh and unsmiling)
Thursday; also used for an army; the pun on both meanings is intentional
struck me, overwhelmed me, came upon me suddenly
from intense love and infatuation
Magians/Zoroastrians, who were said to permit marriage between close relatives

context: A brief, witty ghazal playing on the double meaning of الخميس (Thursday and army). The final verse is a bold erotic conceit: the father sees his daughter and, in his helpless infatuation, wishes he were a Magian — a religion whose customs were (wrongly) associated by Arabs with consanguineous marriage.

titleEn: Your People Departed Raised and Dismantled

verses.sadr: إن يدج ليلك أنهم أموا اللوى
verses.ajuz: فلقد أضاء وهم على ذات الأضا

verses.sadr: لو كان أبغض قلبه فيما مضى

verses.ajuz: مما حشدت إليه من جمر الغضى

verses.sadr: ما أنصف الزمن الذي بعث الهوى
عندي من الأيام ما لو أنه
ما عوض الصبر امرؤ إلا رأى
verses.ajuz: ما فاته دون الذي قد عوضا

verses.sadr: يا أحمد ابن أبي داود دعوة
ما زلت أرقب تحت أفياء المنى
كم محضر لم مرتضى لم تدخر

verses.ajuz: أضعاف ما قد عزني فيما مضى

verses.sadr: قد كان صوح نبت كل قرارة

verses.ajuz: شيئا يعود إلى الحياة وقد قضى

verses.sadr: ثقلا لو أن متالعا حمل اسمه
verses.ajuz: لا جسمه لم يستطع أن ينهضا

verses.sadr: ما عذرها ألا تفيق ولم تزل
كن كيف شئت فإن فيك خلائفا
فالمجد لا يرضى بأن ترضى بأن

translationEn: Your people have risen as those departing and those whose tents are struck, and one with a nose-rope describing separation and one preparing to load. If your night darkens while they head to the sand-dunes, it has also brightened while they were on the shining salt-flats. You exchanged the lightning of the frontiers and their coolness for a lightning that flashes when the loved ones depart. Had anyone loathed his heart in what has passed, I would then be the one who loathes my heart. The ghada-wood must have diminished in its homeland from all the embers of ghada-longing I have gathered in it. How unjust is the time that sent love, condemned you to anguish, and then departed. I carry within me from the days something that, if laid before a man asleep in his grave, would keep his eyes from closing. Do not seek sustenance after its restiveness — you will tame it for a week once it dries up. No man was ever compensated by patience without seeing that what he missed was less than what he was compensated with. O Ahmad ibn Abi Dawud, a supplication that was tamed by your gratitude for me and was already trained. When I drew you out against calamities you sufficed them — a sword does not suffice you until it is drawn. I never ceased to watch under the shade of hopes for a day with a face as bright as yours. How many a commendable act not compromised, whose praises were not stored away before the well-pleased Imam. Had it not been for you, meeting him would be difficult for what remains — more than doubly difficult as it was difficult for me in what has passed. Every low basin's vegetation had withered until it became lush meadows in your generosity. You led me to the deep flowing spring while I saw others scraping at a scanty, grudging well. As for poetry, I have grasped it by the armpit, pulling it like a bucket-rope, openly and indirectly. I loved it when it was beloved in you, and I loved it more when it became despised. I revived it while thinking I would never see a thing return to life after it was finished. You bore the burden of glory, relying on a steady foot whose guardian of integrity kept you from slipping. A weight such that if the mountain Mata'li' bore its name alone — not its body — it could not rise. The situation had complained and I treated it, treating it so well that its healing refused to be undone. What excuse does it have not to recover — while it has never ceased being nursed for its sick by noble deeds? Be as you wish, for within you there are successors to whom all hope is now entrusted. Glory is not content that you be content with a man who hopes in you being content with anything less than full satisfaction.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fitted with a nose-rope for riding (of a camel); a camel ready for departure
a fragrant desert shrub; its embers are long-lasting, used figuratively for enduring longing
a small, sparse water source; a barely drinkable well
a deep, continuously flowing spring
the bucket-rope of a well
a specific large mountain in the Arabian Peninsula
tamed, broken in (of an animal); trained, domesticated

context: A panegyric to Ahmad ibn Abi Dawud al-Iyadi, the famous Abbasid chief judge (qadi al-qudat) and patron of Abu Tammam who supported the Mu'tazila. The poem opens with a conventional love-prelude and transitions into a meditation on patience and sustenance before addressing the patron directly with lavish praise. The poem is notable for its self-reflexive discussion of poetry's revitalisation.

titleEn: Have You Not Seen Me Leave My Soul to Its Affairs

verses.sadr: عنان من اللذات قد كان في يدي

verses.ajuz: متى ما أراد اعتاض عشرا مكانها

verses.sadr: وهل يستعيض المرء من خمس كفه
verses.ajuz: ولو صاغ من حر اللجين بنانها

translationEn: Have you not seen that I have left my soul to its own affairs and cared nothing for the world nor for its events? The misfortunes frightened me with their vicissitudes — yet had they given me safety, I would not have accepted their amnesty. How can I make my camp on the fire of the nights when the greying of my temples is their smoke! I was struck by a soft-cheeked maiden after whom I shall be dust-covered, a companion of grief weeping through one era after another. A rein of pleasures was in my hand — when the familiar companion departed, she took back its rein. The dolls bestowed their abandonment on me — I desire neither their beautiful ones nor does my heart love their fair ones. They say: does a young man weep for a virgin when at any time he wishes he can exchange ten for her? But can a man find a substitute for the five fingers of his hand — even if he cast them from pure silver?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: its events, its vicissitudes (the events of the world)
my camping at night, my halting-place
the greying of the sideburns/temples
a soft, pliant, young woman of delicate beauty
a sworn companion of grief, one wedded to sorrow
a virgin maiden, a secluded and untouched young woman
silver

context: A ghazal of resigned grief, in which Abu Tammam responds to those who tell him to replace his lost beloved with another. The celebrated closing verse — can you replace your own five fingers? — is among his most famous epigrams, arguing the irreplaceability of true love against a merely pragmatic view of women.

titleEn: Leave Me to Drink Passion, O Wine-Drinker

verses.sadr: لا يوحشنك ما استعجبت من سقمي
من خلوتي فيه مبدا كل جائحة

verses.ajuz: م كان قطع رجائي من يدي ياسي

translationEn: Leave me to drink passion, O wine-drinker — for I am a sipper of what I have already tasted. Let not what you find strange in my sickness alarm you — its dwelling is in the finest of people. My solitude in him is the source of every calamity, and my thoughts of him are the source of every whispering doubt. The severing of his words is the connecting thread of my destruction, and the joining of his words is the cutting of my breath. I was blessed with a tenderness of heart from him, spoiled by a spoiler whose heart is hard. When shall I live in the hope of expectation, when the cutting of my hope is from my hands — in despair?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a sipper, one who drinks in small sips
a devastating calamity, a complete ruin
whispering of doubt and anxiety, intrusive thoughts
tenderness of heart, sensitivity
a watcher, spy, overseer; here the rival or guardian who prevents the lover's access
in a state of despair, hopelessness

context: A ghazal addressed to a wine-drinking companion, in which Abu Tammam defends his passion as no less intoxicating than wine. The conceit that the beloved's words simultaneously destroy and sustain the lover is characteristic of the poet's paradoxical style.

titleEn: Tears Replaced the Flash of Lightning

verses.sadr: إن خيرا مما رأيت من الصف
من أبن البيوت أصبح في ثو
حل في البيت من إياد إذا عد

verses.ajuz: ظا وكانت قد نومت في الوفاض

verses.sadr: كم ظلام عن العلى قد تجلى
كم معان وشيتها فيك قد أم

verses.ajuz: روف من كان منهم ذا انقباض

verses.sadr: أنت لي معقل من الدهر إن را
أنت أمضى من أن تصد عن الرم
verses.ajuz: ي إذا ما جددت في الإنباض

verses.sadr: وإذا المجد كان عوني على المر

translationEn: Tears replaced the flash of lightning on the day they tightened the saddlebags with their straps. She turned away for a while, but when she sensed the parting she turned away from turning away. Her resolutions seized her sobbing, and they seized my patience and my closing of eyes. She looked and turned to the most beautiful dark pupil I had ever seen in a white eye. On a day she left with a sick glance and eyelid — while her tears were not sick at all. Better than what I saw of pardoning calamities and shutting eyes to them is this exile that follows the exile of Qays ibn Zuhayr and al-Harith ibn Mudhad. Two who never wove their opinion together, for they feared it would unravel. One of noble lineage found himself in a life that was not loose or comfortable. The true young man is the one gnawed at by nights and deserts like the swift, slithering serpent. His enemies, wherever they settle, are a subject always expanded upon from his resolve. Every day he has from the vicissitudes of time a treacherous blow like the blow of al-Barrad. To Ahmad I came undoing the bonds of helplessness, taking the lean, worn-out camels. It was as if, when I alighted toward him, I released my need from its hobble. He belongs to the Iyad house, counted by the long and broad high offices. A people who have become the fortresses of glory and the armour of lineages and honours. By you the competition for noble deeds returned, and the arrows found their targets. The arrows of the tribes became wakeful — they had been sleeping in their quivers. Noble deeds returned as prime, unweaned she-camels — they had been mixed in with inferior ones. How many a darkness over glory has been dispelled by you, and noble deeds are tamed for you. What man of eminence rivals you in it unjustly, when generosity is judge of that? How many meanings I have embroidered in you have become the rivals of gardens at evening and morning. With rhymes that are the lasting ones across time — but their prices are sharp cutting swords. I care nothing, after your openness with beneficence, for whoever among them was withholding. You are my fortress from time should a dread or a sharp event creep upon me. I never tightened the lashings of the saddle-girth in the knots of hardship until I arrived full to the brim. You are too resolute to be turned from the shooting when you renew the drawing of the bow. And when glory is my helper against a man, I collect it from him by giving up collecting.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the flashing of lightning; here used for the flash of a glance or beauty
the straps used to fasten the saddlebags on camels; signs of departure
al-Barrad, a pre-Islamic figure proverbial for surprise treacherous attack
the Iyad tribe, an ancient and noble Arab tribe
a quiver for arrows, an arrow-holder
the lashings used to cinch a saddle-girth tight
the drawing and releasing of a bowstring

context: A panegyric to Ahmad ibn Abi Dawud al-Iyadi, Abu Tammam's chief patron and the Abbasid chief judge. The poem opens with a separation scene, transitions to a journey of seeking, and concludes with rich praise of the patron and a self-reflexive discussion of poetic immortality. The reference to Qays ibn Zuhayr and al-Harith ibn Mudhad as exiles alludes to two pre-Islamic figures famed for their noble wandering.

titleEn: The Hand of Generosity Left Without Fingers

verses.ajuz: لم يترك الحدثان يوم سطا به
قد كنت حشو الدرع ثم أراك قد
من يدفع الكرب العظام إذا التفت

translationEn: The hand of generosity has become fingerless, and his spear has become tipless. The mountain of mountains — a calamity came upon it and left it with its pillars demolished. I mourn 'Umayr ibn al-Walid for a raid — a fresh first raid or a seasoned veteran one. And I mourn the knight of knights. The stumbling of time and the calamities of its vicissitudes rested at our resting-place — the stumbling of every time. The Two Events on the day they assailed him left no one for us to make our attack upon the Two Events. You were the filling of armour — and now I see you have become the filling of the grave and the shrouds. You occupied the hearts of the people and then their eyes, since you died, with palpitations and streams of tears. They found grief so sweet that they now envy each other the bitterness of grief. No one concerns himself with another, no person longs for another. Did death seize a moment's opportunity from you, and attack you while you two were brothers? Who then remains for the day of honour, and who remains for the day of battle? Who will repel great tribulations when they wind the belt-rings of every saddle-girth in a narrow pass? A bearer of burdens such that if the smallest of them were placed upon Thalhan, the peaks of Thalhan would not crumble.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fingertips; by extension, fingers and hands
the tip of a spear
a war that has been fought many times and is experienced; a veteran conflict
a continuous flow, a steady stream (of tears)
palpitation, throbbing of the heart
bitterness and sting, the painful sharpness of grief
a famous, large mountain in Najd, proverbial for its immovable greatness

context: An elegy for 'Umayr ibn al-Walid, a warrior from an unnamed tribe. The poem is notable for its opening image — the hand of generosity left without fingers — and for the eerie verse in which the people are so overwhelmed by grief that they envy each other their suffering. The closing verse comparing the deceased's burden-bearing capacity to the mountain Thalhan is a standard hyperbole in Arabic elegiac poetry.

titleEn: O Fawn Fashioned from the Sun

verses.sadr: في كل يوم انت في صورة

translationEn: O fawn fashioned from the sun, surpassing all humans in beauty and grace. Each day you are in a form different from the one you wore yesterday. You grow sweeter every day, as a branch of the ben tree grows in its planting. By God, were it not for God alone — no other — and my fear of hellfire for my own soul, I would have prayed five prayers for your sake out of awe, and added two more to the five!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a young fawn, a newly-weaned gazelle
was fashioned, was cast (as metal in a mould)
graces, beautiful features, elegance
the branch of the ben tree (Moringa); the classic comparison for a slender, supple physique
a planted sapling, a cutting planted to grow
I prayed the five prayers; an irreverent conceit comparing adoration of the beloved to worship

context: A playful, audacious ghazal in which Abu Tammam draws a witty parallel between the Islamic five daily prayers and his prostrate adoration of the beloved. The poem's conclusion — that he would add two rak'as to the five for this beloved's sake — is a daring blasphemous conceit characteristic of the libertine (mujun) vein of Abbasid poetry.

titleEn: Restlessness Robs the Eye of Sleep

verses.sadr: شجا بما عن للأمير أبي العبا
من الألى نستجير من شرق الده
verses.ajuz: ر بهم إن ألم أو جرضه

verses.sadr: صاغهم ذو الجلال من جوهر المج

translationEn: Restlessness has robbed the eyelids of their sleep, and has tightened this chest upon its pain. Grief at what has befallen the Amir Abu al-'Abbas has become lodged as an affliction upon one confronting it. For him whose arm is outstretched wide — his protection is obligatory upon all the world, imposed as a duty. He is among those we seek refuge with from the choke of time when it comes close or suffocates. The Glorious One fashioned them from the jewel of glory, and fashioned the rest of humanity from its surface. When they cast their bonds to you, you have come to the watering-trough of humanity through its designated ford. An arrow from the kingdom — his condition does not waste it until it quivers in its target. His recovery was the recovery of our hope, in the time of his disorder and weakening. And should he find an ailment we dwell in it until you see us visited with grief from his sickness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: made restless, disturbed from rest
sharp pain, biting anguish
grief, sorrow that is lodged and cannot be displaced
one who stretches out his arm wide; an image of generous, protecting power
the jewel of glory, the purest essence of nobility
the notch or designated ford of a watering-trough; the proper approach
his confusion, derangement (of illness)

context: A short poem concerned with the illness of Abu al-'Abbas, likely an Abbasid prince or official. The poem reflects the anxiety of those dependent on the patron's health and uses the metaphor of the patron as an arrow from the kingdom that must reach its target. The poet's wellbeing is explicitly tied to the patron's recovery.

titleAr: إني أظن البلى لو كان يفهمه
titleEn: Decay Would Spare Such Beauty

verses.sadr: إني أظن البلى لو كان يفهمه
verses.ajuz: صد البلى عن بقايا وجهه الحسن

verses.sadr: يا مؤتة لم تدع ظرفا ولا أدبا
يا هول ما أبصرت عيني وما سمعت
verses.ajuz: أذني فلا بقيت عيني ولا أذني

verses.sadr: لم يبق من بدني جزء علمت به
verses.ajuz: إلا وقد حله جزء من الحزن

verses.sadr: كان اللحاق به أولى وأحسن بي
verses.ajuz: من أن أعيش سقيم الروح والبدن

translationEn: I believe that decay, if it understood, would turn decay away from the remaining traces of his beautiful face. O Mu'ta, you left no wit nor refinement without passing judgment upon it for the grave and the shroud. By God, one who preserves himself while death shatters him — as though his eyelids are drunk with drowsiness. Death's hand forces his breaths back and bends them as the wind bends a branch. O the horror of what my eyes have seen and my ears have heard — would that neither my eyes nor my ears had remained. Not a part of my body remains that I know of but a part of grief has settled in it. It would have been better and more fitting for me to follow him than to live with a sick soul and a sick body.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: decay, putrefaction, the dissolution of the body in the grave
Mu'ta, a town in present-day Jordan; the site of the famous Battle of Mu'ta (629 CE)
drowsiness, the first onset of sleep
death, fate (a common poetic term for death)
drunk, intoxicated; here applied to eyelids drooping in their last sleep
turns back his breaths (the dying man's breathing forced back by death)

context: An elegy lamenting a beloved person who died, seemingly at or near Mu'ta in Jordan. The poem is remarkable for the physicality of its grief — the poet's entire body is inhabited by grief — and for the opening paradox that decay itself would spare such beauty if it could understand what it was destroying.

titleEn: O You Cloaked in the Sun's Garment
translationEn: O you cloaked in the garment of the sun, who shot me with five arrows: with your glance, and your teeth, and your sidelocks, and your neck, and something that is sweet to the touch. Here I am, confessing my sins — so grant pardon to my abasement for yesterday's transgression. And be generous to one whose eyelids rain blood, whom you have kept from his five prayers. I asked the descriptions to describe you, and they spoke only with tongueless tongues.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: was cloaked, wrapped oneself in a robe (of beauty)
the sidelocks, the locks of hair falling beside the face
one who causes the eyelids to rain blood (from grief and weeping)
the five daily prayers
mute tongues, tongues that are struck speechless

context: A compact ghazal echoing the theme of poem 239, again invoking the five prayers as a conceit for the lover's devotion. The 'five arrows' — glance, teeth, sidelocks, neck, and touch — enumerate the zones of the beloved's beauty that have wounded the lover. The final verse, where even descriptive language falls mute before the beloved, is a classic topos of Arabic praise poetry applied to the erotic context.

titleEn: Were It Not for the Departing Companions

verses.ajuz: من الشوق واديها من الهم مترع
بشمس لهم من جانب الخدر تطلع

verses.sadr: نضا ضوء ها صبغ الدجنة فانطوى

verses.ajuz: ألمت بنا أم كان في الركب يوشع
رأت بي سيد الرمل والصبح أدرع
طريق الردى منها إلى النفس مهيع

verses.sadr: له منظر في العين أبيض ناصع

verses.ajuz: وأنف الفتى من وجهه وهو أجدع
سدى لم يسسها قط عبد مجدع

verses.sadr: حلت نظف منها لنكس وذو النهى
verses.ajuz: يداف له سم من العيش منقعث

verses.sadr: لقد آسف الأعداء مجد ابن يوسف
verses.ajuz: وذو النقص في الدنيا بذي الفضل مولع

verses.sadr: هو السيل إن واجهته انقدت طوعه
ولم أر نفعا عند من ليس ضائرا
verses.ajuz: ولم أر ضرا عند من ليس ينفع

verses.sadr: ممر له من نفسه بعض نفسه
رأى البخل من كل فظيعا فعافه
له تالد قد وقؤ الجود هامه
إذا كانت النعمى سلوبا من امرئ
verses.ajuz: غدت من خليجي كفه ، وهي متبع

verses.sadr: مصيف من الهيجا ومن حاجم الوغى

verses.ajuz: يرى المرء منه وهو أفرع أنزع

verses.sadr: من اللاء يشربن النجيع من الكلى
هو الصنع إن يعجل فنفع وإن يرث

verses.ajuz: وفي السهم تسديد وفي القوس منزع

verses.sadr: وإن الغنى لي إن لحظت مطالبي
verses.ajuz: من الشعر ، إلا في مديحك ، أطوع

verses.sadr: وإنك إن أهزلت في المحل لم تضع
verses.ajuz: ولم ترع إن أهزلت والروض ممرع
فأضحى له في قلة المجد مطلع
وكان اسمه من قبل وهو مدفع

verses.sadr: وما السيف إلا زبرة لو تركته
verses.ajuz: على الخلقة الأولى لما كان يقطع
وإن لم تزع بي مدة فستسمع

translationEn: Were it not for the departing companions and a dwelling whose summer camp and spring pasture have been obliterated, the generosity that yearning stirred would have turned back on its heels, its valley filled to the brim with anxiety. We caught up with their rearguard while love had circled around hearts we knew as birds perching still. The sun returned to us, though the night was reluctant, as a sun rising for them from the side of the curtained litter. Its light stripped off the dye of darkness and the robe of the sky folded, variegated, before its splendour. By God, I do not know whether a sleeper's dreams visited us or whether Joshua was in the caravan. I know her well — she brings love to life and kills it, mends the cracked pieces of the heart and shatters it. I rebuke the intoxication of her reproach with approval, for wine sometimes yields retribution when it is well-diluted. She follows one gift with another gift — for only when a verse of poetry floors you does it truly delight you. Do you not see the gazelles — as if on seeing me they saw the master of the sands with the dawn clad in armour? If the wild beast among them quivered at my sight, the domestic one among them is more disturbed by the white of my hair. Anxiety has etched on my temples a path along which the road of destruction runs straight to the soul. It is old age: the visitor is cold-shouldered, the companion is disliked, the intimate is despised, the new is patched. It has an appearance in the eye that is white and brilliant, but in the heart it is black and sooty. We drive it on in unwillingness and willingness alike — the young man's nose cut off from its face. We have governed this time in a purposeless governance no slave with a cut nose has ever practised. Calamities come upon us every evening and morning, as though time is being knocked down by them. A base one gets the clean share from them while the man of reason has the extracted poison of life mixed for him. If we are neglected, how feeble is our striving; and if we are compelled, why should we stammer? The enemies have been grieved by the glory of the son of Yusuf — the deficient one in this world is always jealous of the one with excellence. I seized a rope of his when he twisted it away — perhaps the bitterness of the days kept cutting it. He is the flood: if you face it you yield willingly to it, and you guide it from both its sides and it follows. I have seen no benefit with one who does no harm, and no harm with one who does no good. He speaks and is heard; he walks and hastens; and he strikes for God's sake and it hurts. His self has a part twisted from himself, while the rest is entirely for praise and reward. He saw miserliness as an abomination from every angle and shunned it — though from him it is more forbidden and more abominable. Every eclipse in the orbs is an ugliness, but it is uglier in the sun and the full moon. The return-destination of people after death — and his bounty is our return and refuge before death. He has an ancestral estate whose head the generosity has tempered, so it settled and it never ceased to be alarmed before. When blessing is stripped from a man, it flows from the two tributaries of his hands, while it keeps following. And if the dark and bright nights stumble upon his solitude, you find them amassed. And if the wealth of a people's hands is disloyal to them from giving and bestowing, his hand is a stream that does not dry. On a day when glory preserves its midst with the brown-shafted lances and souls are scattered. He is a summer-camp from battle and from the throttle of war, but from the downpour of blood he is a spring-camp. A stern one who clothed his heroes in every helmet — a man appears from it while he is tall-crested and bare-headed. And a brown spear whose tips are blood-red on his day — its point delighting in the hearts' cores. From the lances that drink fresh warm blood from the kidneys, while others are watered by it and satisfied. You split through the thick of battle to his mighty one and crowned him with the sword while he was helmeted. At Sindabaya and the uplands and Arshaq and Muqan, and the two brown ones that shook. And Abrushtuwaym and al-Kadhaj and the meeting-place of their hooves while the horses charged and galloped. They went away lame and exhausted, and their running left behind the running of people while it was itself worn and lame. He is the skilled craftsman: if he hastens it is a benefit, and if he deliberates, deliberation is swifter in some positions. My hopes have overshadowed you, and there are people of fierce might and an arrow aimed and a bow drawn. My enrichment — if I look at my ambitions from poetry — is more yielding in nothing but your praise. If you are lean in a drought, you are not wasted, and you are not grazed at if you are lean while the meadow is lush. I see my hope in you alone as an ambition, though in the rest of people it is only an aspiration. How many a stumbler among us you seized by the armpit so that he saw in the heights of glory a sunrise. His name became a defender in calamities, while before it was one that was pushed aside. A sword is nothing but an iron ingot — had you left it in its original state it would never have cut. Take it, then — were it not for the softness of its prelude, the hardness of the rocks would have been cracked by it. It has sisters before it that you have heard — and if it has not stopped me for a while, you shall hear them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the companions who share the same place; the mixed company of tribe-members
natural generosity, a spontaneous noble impulse
Joshua (Yusha' ibn Nun), who according to legend commanded the sun to stand still
the shattered fragments of the heart (as in a cracked vessel)
the crest of a helmet; the helmet's pinnacle
fresh, warm blood flowing from a wound
a lump of iron or raw metal before it is worked

context: A long and celebrated panegyric to Muhammad ibn Yusuf al-Thaghari (or a similarly titled military governor), widely regarded as one of Abu Tammam's masterpieces. The poem opens with a complex nasib and a meditation on old age and time, before pivoting to extended military praise including specific place-names from the Caucasian and Azerbaijani campaigns. The final image — that the poem is a sword that would crack stone were it not for the softness of its opening — is a celebrated meta-poetic boast.

titleEn: My Soul Is Muhammad's Ransom

verses.sadr: لو قيل سل تعط المنى كان المنى
verses.ajuz: أن لو رأى مولاه كيف بكاؤه

translationEn: My soul is Muhammad's ransom and shield — and I deny that anything in the world could ransom him. Do you claim that anything could imitate his glance, when his figure is a branch with water coursing through it? Be silent — where is his radiance and his splendour, his perfection, his brilliance, and his modesty? The names of grace and intelligence are of no use for anyone but him — for they are his names. The lover is stripped bare by wasting — his shirt is endless sighing and sickness is his robe. If he were told: ask and be granted your wish, his wish would be to see how his master weeps for him. O his beloved companions, why do you do to his heart what even his enemies do not do to it? Tears rained on the earth of my cheek until morning — and my two eyes are its sky.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his protection, his shield (the thing that guards him)
wasting, emaciation from illness or grief
constant sighing, moaning, repeated exhalations of grief
wishes, desires, the objects of longing
his master, lord, or close patron
tears (a more literary term than the common دموع)

context: A ghazal of intense longing for a beloved named Muhammad. The poem is notable for the paradox in the sixth verse: the lover's only wish, if granted one, would be to see how much his master grieves for him. The closing image — cheeks as earth, eyes as rain-clouds — is a classic and striking variant of the tear-rain metaphor.

titleEn: O You Who Wear Grace's Garment Unknowingly

verses.ajuz: ما كنت أول من جنى من غرسه

translationEn: O you who wear the garment of grace unknowingly — you are the most worthy of all its wearers to wear it. God did not grant you what He granted you until He made the moon and the sun seem trivial by comparison. A fawn who, whenever he is about to let his nature loose in his coquetry, is commanded by modesty to restrain it. And I am the one who gave him the pure and essential heart of love, and I took from him the virginity of his intimacy. If I have reaped his fruits and planted him, I was not the first to reap from his planting. Your master, O my master, has kept company with anguish throughout his day and longing throughout his yesterday. A sick man who gives his soul away until he has become too weak to give his soul away!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unknowing, naive, innocent of his own grace
a young fawn, a newly weaned gazelle; the standard image for a beautiful youth
his coquetry, his treacherous and deadly charm
the virginity of his intimacy; the first of his close companionship
gravely ill, wasting away from love-sickness
to give one's soul away; the idiom for dying, giving up the last breath

context: A ghazal to a beautiful, modest youth who is unaware of his own grace. The poem closes with a striking paradox characteristic of Abu Tammam: the lover is so ill that he is too weak even to die — too feeble to give his soul away. The interplay between 'planting' and 'reaping' in verse five alludes to the poet's role in cultivating the beloved's awareness of love.

titleEn: Spare Your Tears for My Resolve

verses.sadr: فتى النكبات من يأوي إذا ما
فما في الأرض من شرف يفاع

verses.ajuz: على ما فيك من كرم الطباع

translationEn: Spare the tears of your eye from my resolve, and guard whatever veil you have removed. Enough — your weeping has straitened my patience, though no calamity has straitened my forearm. O you who are accustomed to lamentation — how many separations have I endured that became the cause of reunion! The joy of homecoming belongs only to one bound to the grief of farewell. She grieved to see my body grown thin, as though glory were won by wrestling. The youth of misfortunes — when they settle upon him, he turns to character both vast and ample. He raises clouds of dust at every frontier, mad with the spirit of ʿAdiyy ibn al-Ruqāʿ. Dwell with the lions of the wilderness until the lions mistake him for one of their own kin. Then clothe yourself in resolution if you ever attempt to accomplish the impossible. You did not journey like the swift she-camel on the road, nor did you ride your anxieties like a man of firm purpose. Through Mahdī ibn Aṣram my branch returned to its leafing and my reach extended wide. He lengthened my arm against the days until I repaid fortune's turns measure for measure. When the herds of poetry found no pasture, his gifts became their grazing grounds. Gardens from which fragrance never strays and which are never empty of lofty ambitions. He strove and drew down honor through power — without striving there would be no strivings. O Mahdī, I greet you on account of your gift — how much blaming sounds to one who does not heed! You intended, where lofty deeds are never disobeyed, that generosity be disobeyed and that you be obeyed. Pillar of rescue when the turns of nights assault and their champion confronts confrontation. Often the high lances fill him with longing and his ambition aspires to precious possessions. It is as though, on the morning of alarm, he had drunk wine — described to him as the soul of the brave. A beautiful death in nobility and piety is dearer to him than a beautiful defense. And the voice of a petitioner hoping in him is sweeter to his ears than melodious song. You made generosity the luster of your deeds — can a sun exist without its rays? There is nothing on earth more resistant to restraint that drives blame away than an obeyed generosity. Nothing preserves lost glory like lost wealth. May God protect you for the sake of kindness — I see you as no guardian of your wealth's pasture. There is no lofty honor on earth that you have not preceded, nor a lofty character. Your resolve is like the resolve of a flood strengthened by its channels and watercourses. Your judgment is like the judgment of a sword whose edge is sound in counsel at the clash. If you were to paint your own portrait you could not add to what is already in you of noble character.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my firm resolve, my determined intention
weeping, sobbing, lamentation
homecomings, returns from journeys
dust cloud raised in battle or swift movement
firm resolution, determined purpose
the clash of battle, the moment of confrontation
watercourses, channels that carry floodwater
strivings, noble endeavors, efforts toward glory

context: A panegyric to Mahdī ibn Aṣram in the wāfir meter, opening with an address to a beloved lamenting his departure. Abu Tammam weaves together the nasīb of departure with praise for his patron's generosity and martial resolve, celebrating him as a pillar of rescue and a man whose ambitions surpass all others.

titleAr: بيت قلبي من هواك على الطوى
titleEn: My Heart's House Starves for Your Love

verses.sadr: بيت قلبي من هواك على الطوى
لو لم يجرني الهجر منك بلطفه
لم ترع لي حرقا بقلبي قد مضت
verses.ajuz: لو لم يذدها الدمع عنه لاشتوى
في غفلة إن الهوى ينسي الهوى

translationEn: The house of my heart starves through loving you, and I have departed from the land of yearning and passion. Had separation not restrained me from you through its gentle hand, by God I would have sought refuge from you in remoteness. You showed no care for the burning in my heart that has passed — had tears not driven it away, it would have consumed me. Far from it — I was in the rashness and recklessness of youth, in a heedlessness, for love makes one forget love.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: hunger, starvation; also the state of enduring hardship
intense longing, passionate attachment
inner anguish, burning love-grief
separation, abandonment by the beloved
distance, separation, remoteness
youth, the state of being young and inexperienced

context: A brief ghazal in the kāmil meter reflecting on the poet's youthful heedlessness in love and the paradox that separation itself becomes a kind of refuge from the consuming fire of passion.

titleEn: May My Soul Ransom This Beloved

verses.sadr: جحدت الهوى ان كنت مذ جعل الهوى

verses.ajuz: من الشوق إلا أن عيني في عرس

translationEn: May my soul ransom a beloved who will bereave me of my very soul and make my body a gift to the grave and the tomb. I deny passion — if since passion made his beauty my sun I have looked upon the actual sun. The whole world has grown narrow upon me through his abandonment, until I feel as though I am in a prison. I dwell in a heart wandering — within it a mourning assembly of longing — except that my eyes are in a wedding feast.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: bereaves me, causes me to lose (as a parent loses a child)
the grave, the burial mound
abandonment, the beloved's long avoidance
a gathering of mourning, a funeral assembly
a wedding celebration, a joyous feast

context: A short ghazal contrasting the desolation of the heart with the brightness of the eyes, built around the paradox of a heart in mourning while the eyes rejoice. The poem is in the ṭawīl meter.

titleAr: قد كسانا من كسوة الصيف خرق
titleEn: A Nobleman Clothed Us in Summer Robes

verses.sadr: قد كسانا من كسوة الصيف خرق
يطرد اليوم ذا الهجير ولو شب
خلعة من أغر أروع رحب الص

translationEn: A generous man of great deeds clothed us in summer garments, himself dressed in noble deeds and strivings. A fine garment and a mantle like the sheen of summer heat, or like the mantle of the brave. Like shimmering mirage in description, except it does not deceive like the mirage. Soft and light as a reed-stalk, the wind sets its back trembling at the command of the blowing breeze. It trembles as if it were, from fate's perspective, the liver of the lovelorn or the bowels of the alarmed. Clinging close to what it wraps, you would think it part of the wearer's loins and ribs. It drives away the fierce midday heat, even if the burning of a farewell day were kindled within it. A robe of honor from a radiant, awe-inspiring man of broad chest, broad heart, and broad reach. I will clothe you in return in praise that covers it — like the craftsman's woven mantle. The beauty of that robe is in the eyes; but this praise — its beauty is in hearts and ears.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a fine, thin fabric of excellent quality
the shimmering mirage, the quivering haze of heat
the intense midday heat of summer
a robe of honor, a gift garment from a patron
the awe-inspiring, the one who fills hearts with wonder
praise, eulogy, commendation

context: A poem of thanks for a summer robe (khilʿa) gifted to the poet, in the khafīf meter. Abu Tammam describes the garment in elaborate detail before pledging to repay the gift with poetic praise that will outlast the robe itself.

titleEn: I Spent the Night at War with Sleep
translationEn: I spent the night at peace with anguish and at war with slumber, exposed to sighing and breathing. All through my night I clasped my liver in my hand, sliced through as if by razors. When griefs descended I groaned and called out: O Abū al-ʿAbbās! My grief from you — may not even a tenth of what has passed over my head from loving you ever touch you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: inner anguish, burning grief of love
slumber, drowsiness, sleep
deep sighing, the exhaling groan of distress
razors, sharp blades used for cutting
one tenth, a fraction

context: A short ghazal of sleepless longing addressed to a beloved called Abū al-ʿAbbās. In the khafīf meter, it captures an intimate night scene of physical anguish ending with a tender wish that the beloved be spared even a fraction of the poet's suffering.

titleEn: Abu Ali and His Pasturing Ground

verses.ajuz: من صاب قول يدمي ومن سلعه
من قدعه إن أمنت من قدعه

verses.sadr: ينزل في الكاهل المنيف من الأم
verses.ajuz: ر وهم تحت ذاك من زمعه

verses.sadr: قد ذاب لي في يديك ذوب السنا
verses.ajuz: م الجعد حكمت الرضف في قمعه

verses.sadr: ولم تغير وجهي عن الصبغة ال
لا بل هنيء الندى هنيء السدى

verses.ajuz: م المجد مجد الرياش في شنعه

verses.sadr: لا في ريام ولا قراه ولا
ساحر نظم سحر البياض من ال

verses.ajuz: ما شئت من تمه ومن قطعه

translationEn: Abū ʿAlī — his name matches his pasturing ground: settle at the upper part of his valley or its sandy hollow. Draw near in imagination and sense from his sight at one moment and his hearing at another. A relentless envier — I said to him: who is wounded by bitter speech and by cactus-thorns? Do not let the black serpents cut off your honor, and pretend not to notice with a conspicuous nose for the one who instigates harm. Let not your two neck-veins be startled by the slightest rebuke if you feel safe from rebuke. Beware of the lion-den — do not circle near it, for I fear for you its beast. You see the great man veiled — yet he has a retinue — and you meet the followed one from among his followers. He settles in the high shoulder-blade of command while they are beneath that in their haste and anxiety. O Lord, many a day whose dawn blazes with the radiant morning light of his generosity. In your hands my coagulated lock melted away — as the heated stones ruled over its suppression. You did not change my face from the hue most fitting for one whose color is scorched and gleaming. Nay rather, congratulations on the generosity, congratulations on the gift — one who hopes in you has not been soiled in his seeking. The messenger came to me with a splendid garment for a man's summer and his spring abode. Whoever finds fault with the strange robe of honor — glory lies in the splendor of fine garments in its strangeness. Had it been draped over Uways it would have hastened pride into his piety. Fine silk pleasant to the touch — the pouring of youthful hands upon one who wears a garment. And a secret brocade as if my poetry brought to life the genealogy of eyes from its novelty. As if the fresh flowers and clotted blood from its clinging and its yielding. And the light of the ʿarār flower running through its embroidery displayed upon its ripeness. Neither in Riyām nor in its villages nor in Zubayda its like, nor in Rumʿa. No eye of any fair-minded person passes over it without blessing its workmanship. You have left me with uplifted eyelids in a pitiless age, proud of its beauty. Habitually haughty and soaring above its festivals, towering over its gatherings. An envier of your gift — I told him, and many a crooked rib have I straightened with a word: You have described a sword but ignored its hilt, and a gazelle resting and forgot its neck. You are our brother and a great king — we bestow the extra of what we receive from him in robes of honor. So wear upon yourself its like, befitting one like you from the ample flowing garment of poetry in its expanse. Arduous are the rhymes except for their rider — the weaver of prosody that resists the incompetent. A sorcerer of arrangement who bewitches whiteness from the free colors — his enchanting deception. A garment of affection that became, before all others, his pasturing ground — we do not say from whose pasture. You preceded until you cut off before them what you wished of its fullness and its extent. And poetry is a path whose particular quality throughout the long nights belongs only to its first opener.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a pasturing ground sought for fresh grazing; figuratively a place of bounty
black serpents; figuratively dangerous, treacherous enemies
a lion's den, a thicket where predators hide
the upper shoulder-blade area; figuratively the high rank of command
a robe of honor granted by a patron as a gift
poetry, verse; the craft of measured composition
its first opener, the one who first breaks open a difficult path

context: A poem in the munṣarih meter thanking Abū ʿAlī for a splendid garment, containing a long description of the silk robe and concluding with Abu Tammam's assertion of his poetic mastery. He also addresses an envier, warning against jealousy and praising poetry as a domain conquered only by its true master.

titleEn: Distance Separated Him from His Kin
translationEn: Distance has removed him from his relatives, so the rope was cast over the back of the camel — he was set free to wander. His beloved lived on with her resistance; the hope of his seeker died upon that resistance. Beauty united in him while the paths of reason diverged in his various ways. I have never seen a full moon like you, so perfectly balanced that it needs no stars around it. How admirable is the passionate lover who hurled himself at your initial hardness until it softened with his own gentle persistence. I met you when your beginning was a wonder, and I did not think of the consequences. And one who is truly good — no wonder that people partake of his best qualities.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the hump of a camel; to cast a rope over it means to set free
resistance, the act of withholding and refusing
the paths or directions of reason and understanding
perfectly balanced, harmonious in all aspects
the finest and best qualities; sweet things

context: A short ghazal in the munṣarih meter praising an idealized beloved's beauty. The poem ends with a witty proverb-like verse: that from a truly good person it is natural for all to benefit.

titleAr: غدا يتناءى صاحب كان لي انسا
titleEn: Tomorrow a Dear Companion Parts

verses.sadr: غدا يتناءى صاحب كان لي انسا
verses.ajuz: فلا مصبح لي في السرور ولا ممسا

verses.sadr: أخ لي لو أعطى المنى باسم فقده
verses.ajuz: بلا فقده كانت به ثمنا بخسا

verses.sadr: فلو أن نفسي الف نفس لما انثنت
verses.ajuz: يد البين أو تودي بآخرها نفسا

translationEn: Tomorrow a companion who was my intimate draws far away, so I have neither morning nor evening left in joy. My sorrows over him will grow many, and my good fortune from his affection turns to ill omen. A brother to me — had the wishes been granted in exchange for losing him, without actually losing him, the price would be a pittance. Were my soul a thousand souls, the hand of separation would not desist until it claimed the very last one.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: draws far away, becomes distant
intimacy, the comfort of a close companion
ill omen, misfortune, bad luck
a paltry price, an insufficient compensation
separation, parting, the act of being torn apart

context: A brief elegy or farewell poem in the ṭawīl meter lamenting the imminent departure of a close friend. The poet declares that no price could compensate for the loss and that even a thousand souls would not satisfy separation's demand.

titleEn: Here Is the Station of the Grief-Stricken

verses.sadr: لو قيل ما كان مزورا بها
نوح صفا مذ عهد نوح له
نوح بن عمرو بن حوي بن عم
verses.ajuz: رو بن حوي بن الفتى ماتع

verses.sadr: إن يرفع السجف له اليوم ير
إن أنت لم تنهض به صاعدا

translationEn: Here is the station of the grief-stricken — it has become empty, the remnant of a devastating time. A dwelling that, after its dwellers, was watered by the turns of separation from their lethal poison. Blame not the lover — it is no novelty, this groaning of one pulled away. If it were said there was no visitor there, the ruined encampment would rejoice at the returner. Take heed and shed tears for a while — for tears are the twin of restraining grief. Its highlands were emptied by every slender, tall beauty who steals the heart of even the most kingly heart. One who is no weakling before the sword will wake in love for her, humble. Graceful — if you let your thought range through her beauty, it guides you to the Creator. Nūḥ — whose nobility has been pure since the time of Nūḥ, drinking glory from lofty lineage. His fathers unbroken in their lineage, like the dawn in its blazing radiance. Forebears you would think, from their brightness, to be the stations of the rising moon — like the Bucket, the Fish, the Pleiades, the Belly, the Star, all the way to the Swallower.
