Abu Tammam distils royal panegyric to its extreme: the first verse equates disobedience to the caliph with disobedience to God; the second verse dares to place Harun at the level of a prophet — the ultimate hyperbole of court poetry, stopping just short of actual prophecy.

titleEn: Imminent Departure and Blazing Longing
translationEn: An imminent departure and an unbinding — and the smoldering heat of longing and burning desire. May my father be ransomed for a beloved I bid farewell, whose company made the companions lose their way. A full moon who illuminates those who love him, untouched by any crescent of waning. Iraq grew distressed and dishevelled in grief at his absence. Death and separation — both of them are unbearable burdens. They conspire together against souls — this one is the death-agony and that one the final departure. Were it not that this thing is as it is, they would never have spoken of death or separation.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: distance, remoteness; the state of being far from the beloved
smoldering thirst or passion; an inner burning that is not quenched
the last days of the lunar month when the moon disappears; waning and diminishment
became distressed, turbid and disturbed; used of water or a region overcome with grief
became dishevelled, unkempt; used of one in mourning who neglects appearance
the final death-rattle, the process of the soul leaving the body

context: A mujazza al-kamil ghazal of seven verses about an impending farewell and the twin unbearabilities of death and separation. Abu Tammam draws an unusual philosophical parallel: death and separation cooperate against the soul — one kills the body and the other kills the spirit — and concludes that their names only exist because the reality is so devastating.

titleEn: You Know My Tears and My Longing
translationEn: You are aware of my tears and my yearning, and of the anguish and burning that possess me. And yours is the elegance, the grace, the beauty, the fragrance of the sleeves, and the noble character. It is shameful that my body has shown the very ruins of lovers that I can see all around me. So why this coldness when I have committed no fault? For coldness is separation before separation itself.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a tear; specifically a tear shed from grief or longing
burning anguish of the heart, the acute pain of love's longing
elegance, refined taste, courtly grace in manners and bearing
the sleeves of a garment; their fragrance is a sign of the beloved's beauty and care
turning away, coldness, aloofness; the act of refusing to engage or respond
the falling places of lovers; the ruin and destruction that love brings to the body

context: A khafif ghazal of complaint in four verses. The poet acknowledges the beloved's awareness of his suffering, praises the beloved's many graces, then protests against the undeserved coldness — ending with a memorable epigram: coldness itself is already a form of separation.

titleEn: That Passion and That Fire Have Died
translationEn: That passion and that fire have died, and a gazelle-eyed beloved who is tender toward me has felt compassion. Sleep has begun to flow through my eyelids in the same channel as tears once flowed, and the yearning heart has grown tranquil. Time has been gentle with me concerning my master — and time, when it wills, is a gentle companion to hearts. By my right and my sacred bond — do not wrongfully curse time, for it has been a friend to me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: inner passion, hidden burning grief; intense suppressed emotion
fire, burning; used for the consuming fire of love's longing
felt compassion for me, took pity on me; to be moved by someone's suffering
grew calm, found comfort and familiarity; the heart at ease after long turmoil
my sanctity, my sacred inviolability; invoked as a solemn bond

context: An unusual khafif ghazal in which Abu Tammam celebrates relief from love's torment rather than its perpetuation. Sleep returns, the heart calms, and the poet praises time itself — typically the villain of Arabic love poetry — as his friend and benefactor. The final verse is a surprising reversal of convention.

titleEn: Modesty Silences Me Before You
translationEn: Modesty holds me back from speaking to you — so glances are the messengers between you and me. Our conversation in the innermost soul is in harmony, yet our outward affair in all things is split apart. Our eyebrows convey our secrets to each other, and eyes take aim with the promise of union.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: modesty, shyness; also the redness of twilight — here used for bashful reserve
glances, the pupils of the eyes; used as secret messengers between the lovers
the innermost conscience and soul; the private realm of thought
to convey by suggestion, to signal secretly; a root connected to divine revelation
to aim and shoot; eyes aiming like arrows — a classic image of the wounding glance

context: A three-verse munsarih ghazal of remarkable economy. Abu Tammam captures the entire drama of silent love — modesty prevents speech, so glances become the language; inwardly the lovers are united, outwardly separated; and eyebrows and eyes conduct their own secret conversation of desire.

titleEn: By God, If You Felt What I Feel
translationEn: By God, if you were to experience what I experience, you would find it sinful to go beyond justice. In me is more than what one soul can bear — or do you see it cast aside at your side? She weeps for a wound she inflicts, through a connection that can neither be hoped for nor be healed. Have mercy on a wretch in love with you — he desires nothing, even if you were to set him free.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you would find it sinful, you would be in a state of religious prohibition; to feel the weight of transgression
justice, right, truth; fairness owed between two parties
a wound; a place bitten, gnawed or savaged — used of a wound inflicted by love
to be healed by incantation or charm; to be cured through magic or medicine
a miserable wretch, one destined for suffering; the opposite of the happy and blessed

context: A four-verse kamil ghazal built on the premise of empathy: if the beloved could feel what the lover feels, she would see that continuing her indifference crosses the line of justice. The poem ends with total surrender — even if freed, the lover wants nothing but to remain enslaved.

titleEn: My Father Be Ransom for Your Glance

verses.sadr: ما زلت أرجوك كما لم تزل
والله لو أعطى المنى لم أرد
قد بعدت همة من راح أو

translationEn: My father be ransom for your glance upon your cheek — and your eyes have milked out tears from other eyes. I have never ceased hoping in you, my lord, just as I have never ceased fearing you since the very beginning. By God, if all desires were granted me, I would want nothing but to press your mouth with mine. How low is the aspiration of one who goes through an evening or a morning merely wishing for you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the glance, a sideways look; the corner-of-the-eye glance of the beloved
milked out; as a cow is milked — eyes milking tears from other eyes by their beauty
I feared you; to hold in reverent awe as well as love, fearing the beloved's power
touching or kissing reverently; the act of pressing one's lips to something sacred
high aspiration, ambition, resolve; a man of himma aims for the greatest things

context: A sari' ghazal in four verses that moves from playful praise of the beloved's eyes to a declaration of the single, precise desire of the lover, ending with an ironic observation: to merely wish for the beloved is a sign of low aspiration — true longing demands more.

titleAr: لهف نفسي علي لا بك عليكا
titleEn: I Grieve for Myself, Not for You

verses.sadr: لهف نفسي علي لا بك عليكا
أنت وقف على القلوب بما أص
لا قضى الله لي وصالك إن كن

translationEn: My soul grieves for me — no, more precisely for you — that eyes should roam freely over your cheeks. It is hard for me to bear that gazes should pluck the spring flowers from your two cheeks. You are a pious endowment dedicated to hearts by virtue of your being loved, while those hearts are themselves dedicated to you. May God never grant me your union if I find myself yearning for anyone but you. The evil eye has wounded you with its glances until I have come to fear for you from your very own eyes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grief, sighing regret; an exclamation of pain and yearning
the two cheeks; a primary site of beauty description in Arabic love poetry
a pious endowment; property dedicated to a cause that cannot be retracted — here love cannot be revoked
union with the beloved, intimate meeting and closeness
the evil eye; the malignant glance that causes harm to beautiful things

context: A khafif ghazal of five verses notable for its final paradox: the poet fears for the beloved's beauty not from strangers but from the beloved's own eyes — the evil eye typically harms what one gazes upon, so the beloved's own beautiful eyes might harm their own beautiful cheeks.

titleAr: إن حزني علي لا بل عليكا
titleEn: My Sorrow Is for You, Not for Me

verses.sadr: إن حزني علي لا بل عليكا
إن قلبي عليك في كل وصل

translationEn: My grief is for me — no, rather for you — no, for the very soul that flows and melts in your presence. You take pride in a beauty that has drawn all gazes toward it and left them dedicated to you. Cursed be the eye to whose authority the matter was entrusted, and it parted from your cheeks! My father be ransom for your sweet speech, which has left hearing itself obedient in your hands. My heart in your presence — whether in union or in coldness — is more tender than your own cheeks.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the pure life-blood, the soul itself; the innermost vital essence of a person
to take pride in, to glory in; to be beautiful and know it
the eyeball, the eye in its socket; the organ of the gaze
speech, utterance, the words one speaks; here the beloved's sweet words
obedient, docile, compliant; hearing left in willing submission

context: A companion ghazal to the preceding poem (abu-tammam-294), sharing the same rhyme (-iika) and similar themes. The poet circles around the question of for whom his grief is directed — himself, the beloved, or the melting soul — and arrives at the paradox of a heart more delicate than the cheeks it adores.

titleAr: نم فإن لم أنم كراي كراكا
titleEn: Sleep, for If I Cannot Sleep My Sleep Is Yours

verses.sadr: نم فإن لم أنم كراي كراكا

verses.ajuz: نفس مثلي عن أن تكون فداكا

verses.sadr: في سبيل الهوى فؤادي وما آ

verses.ajuz: غير أني أبكي لأن لا أراكا

translationEn: Sleep — for if I cannot sleep, my sleep is your sleep; let that be witness from you that so it is. My suffering has grown long — may my soul ransom you — yet I said: a soul like mine is too low to be your ransom. My heart is on the path of love, and I have no grief over it — only over your memory. My two eyes have gone with their blood and tears — I care not for the parting with the world, but I weep not for the loss of my eyes for the sake of my eyes — only because I will no longer be able to see you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: slumber, sleep; particularly the light sleep of the night-watcher
may something be your ransom; an expression of deep love offering one's life in exchange
the path of love; the road one travels in passionate devotion
the eyeball; the eye itself rather than merely the gaze
for the sake of my eyes; the beloved's face is the reason the eyes have value

context: A khafif ghazal of striking emotional logic: the sleepless lover offers his sleep to the beloved, offers his soul as ransom yet finds it too low a price, and finally weeps not for the loss of his eyes themselves but only because without them he cannot see the beloved. The poem contains a notable OCR dislocation in the fourth verse.

titleAr: يا أبا جعفر اقر لك الحس
titleEn: O Abu Jafar, Beauty Bows to You

verses.sadr: يا أبا جعفر اقر لك الحس
يا أبا جعفر هل النأي ينجي

translationEn: O Abu Jafar, beauty has acknowledged you and its armies have encamped in your shadow. O Abu Jafar, you were created wondrous — the beauty of your nape surpasses the beauty of all faces. O Abu Jafar, does distance bring salvation from you? Far from it — it only increases one's destruction. O Abu Jafar, grant me your union — may God reward you as you deserve if you do.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: acknowledged, confessed, submitted; beauty itself admits the beloved's supremacy
wonderful, unprecedented; created in a fashion never seen before
the nape of the neck; remarkably, Abu Tammam says even the back of the head is more beautiful than all other faces
far from it! impossible! an exclamation of strong negation
union with the beloved; the sought-after intimacy of lover and loved one

context: A four-verse khafif ghazal structured as an anaphora — each verse begins with 'O Abu Jafar' — creating a litany-like effect of address, praise, complaint, and petition. The poem is notable for its comic hyperbole: even the beloved's nape surpasses all other faces in beauty.

titleEn: My Comfort Is in Weeping Until I See You

verses.ajuz: إن لي منك شاغلا عن سواكا
لست أدري ما حيلتي في رضاكا

translationEn: My comfort is in weeping until I see you — for I have in you a preoccupation that keeps me from all others. May rejection be wretched — and he who is more wretched than all people, except you. Guide me to your pleasure, for I do not know what means I have to win your approval. When they ask 'whom do you love?' my tongue stumbles — yet you are that very one in my heart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: may he be wretched, stumble and fall; a curse of misfortune
abandonment, estrangement, the beloved's refusal to engage
guide me, show me the right path; a plea for direction
approval, satisfaction, being pleased with someone
my tongue stumbled, erred, failed to speak correctly

context: A khafif ghazal of four verses in which the poet finds comfort only in weeping, curses rejection, begs for guidance to win favor, and ends with a beautiful paradox: when asked who he loves, his tongue stumbles even though the beloved is enshrined in his heart. The name cannot be spoken though the love cannot be concealed.

titleEn: I Am Stripped of Love Unless I Punish My Eyes
translationEn: I would be stripped of love and cured of it — if only I did not punish my own two eyes. I sent you as a scout, and you stole from him his beauties with a single glance. You came back saying 'I did not see him' — yet his beauties shine visibly upon your cheeks. If you, O messenger, kept him concealed from me, then his beauties have manifested themselves openly upon you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I was stripped bare, left naked; deprived of something that covered and protected
a scout, a vanguard; one sent ahead to explore and report back
beauties, fine qualities; the attractive features of a face or character
the eye, the pupil; the organ of sight and the seat of the glance
to appear clearly, to shine; visible on the surface for all to see

context: A wafir ghazal of conceit and wit. The poet sends his eyes as scouts to observe the beloved, but they return having stolen the beloved's beauty which now shines visibly on the eyes themselves — betraying the lie that they never saw him. A masterpiece of playful self-reproach directed at one's own senses.

titleEn: A King Who Oppresses When He Rules
translationEn: A king who oppresses when he rules — he feels no pity for those who perish. Your beloved's hand tore away the veil of my consolation, and it was rent asunder. O little king — when your slave weeps in love, he laughs! My grief matches in measure the wondrous beauty you possess.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to oppress, to act tyrannically; to exceed just limits
to feel compassion or pity for; to mourn and show sympathy
consolation, forgetfulness of grief; the easing comfort of forgetting
was torn apart, was exposed; the veil was forcibly removed
a little king, a diminutive sovereign; an affectionate or ironic form of malik

context: A four-verse mujazza al-khafif ghazal with a witty political metaphor: the beloved is a tyrant-king who rules without mercy. The poet's grief is proportionate to the beloved's beauty — the greater the beauty, the greater the suffering. The image of a king laughing at his weeping slave is a pointed commentary on the cruelty inherent in beauty.

titleEn: Longing Has Kept Me Company Since You Left
translationEn: Longing has kept me company since your departure — and a tear that strikes in the evening or comes at dawn. They have never gathered at once for my eyes — and since you left my gaze recoils from it — can the narcissus and the rose ever truly unite?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: kept me company, made me feel less alone; to provide companionship in solitude
intense longing, passionate yearning; the overpowering emotion of love's absence
a tear; a single drop of weeping caused by grief
strikes, visits; like a night visitor who arrives unexpectedly
the narcissus and the rose; classical metaphors for black eyes and red cheeks — two beauties that cannot coexist

context: A three-verse sari' ghazal, fragmentary and affected by OCR dislocations in the source. The poem uses the image of the narcissus and the rose — classical symbols of dark eyes and rosy cheeks — to suggest that the two aspects of the beloved's beauty are incompatible: they cannot be present simultaneously for the bereft lover.

titleEn: The Envious Found Something to Say About Us

verses.sadr: عجبوا أن قانصا بث في الآ

translationEn: The envious have found occasion to speak about us — they have aimed arrows and darts at us. They marveled that a hunter spread his snares in the horizons and caught a gazelle who fills my eyes with grace and beauty and my heart with awe and majesty. So blame me for him however you wish and say: God has spared the believers the need to fight!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the envious ones, those who begrudge the good fortune of others
they aimed, they notched an arrow; to prepare an arrow at the notch for shooting
snares, traps; nets spread by a hunter to catch prey
grace, charm, elegance; the quality that makes the beloved irresistibly attractive
awe-inspiring presence, dignity that commands reverence and respect

context: A khafif ghazal in which Abu Tammam addresses the envious gossipers who criticize his love. He reframes his successful capture of the beloved as a hunter's triumph, filling the poet's eyes with beauty and heart with awe — then dismisses the critics with a Quranic phrase (Q. 33:25), applying a divine verse about battle to his personal love affair with characteristic wit.

titleEn: A Gazelle Whose Rose Dwells in His Cheek

verses.sadr: ما كنت أحسب أن لي مستمتعا

translationEn: A gazelle whose rose dwells in his cheek — a cheek upon which fine shirts of rose-petal are laid. I never imagined I had any enjoyment in his nearness until I was afflicted by his distance. Nothing is more beautiful than him on the night of our union, when I have made a pillow of his cheek. And my mouth on his mouth, conversing with his saliva while my hand strolls through the gardens of his skin.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: fine inner garments, silken shirts worn next to the skin; here petals of rose on the cheek
enjoyment, benefit, pleasure taken from something
a pillow, a cushion; here the beloved's cheek used as a pillow
to converse intimately at night; to pass the night in close conversation
the gardens of his skin; the body of the beloved imagined as beautiful gardens to wander through

context: A sensual ghazal in the anonymous meter of the source. Abu Tammam describes a night of union with the beloved, using a series of unusually physical and intimate images: the cheek as pillow, the saliva as conversation partner, and the skin as gardens. One of his more erotic short poems.

titleEn: How Long Drags My Endless Night

verses.sadr: يا غافلا عني ما لي أرى

verses.ajuz: في النوم من كثرة ما نقتل

translationEn: How endless drags my longest night! How ceaselessly races my streaming tear! O length of rejection that has no end — you have a reproach that has no beginning. O one heedless of me — why do I see your glance never heedless of killing me? I see you cannot stop being alarmed in your sleep from the sheer number we are slain.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: drags on, extends beyond all limits; to persist far beyond what is bearable
flowing freely downward, pouring in streams; tears that pour without pause
estrangement, rejection, the deliberate refusal to engage
heedless, inattentive, neglectful; paying no attention to the lover's suffering
a sudden alarm, a start of terror; being jolted awake by fright in the night

context: A sari' ghazal of four verses built on the rhetorical device of paradox and reversal. The beloved is called heedless of the poet yet the beloved's own glance is the instrument of killing; and the final verse turns it into dark comedy: the beloved wakes in alarm at night because so many lovers have been slain.

titleAr: شد ما استنزلتك من ربعك الأظ
titleEn: How Deeply the Ruins Drew You Down

verses.sadr: شد ما استنزلتك من ربعك الأظ

translationEn: How deeply the abandoned ruins drew you down from your dwelling until the tears of the gazelle-eyed poured down. What beauty among those who have departed and gone — and what grace upon the backs of their riding camels. And coquetry tenting itself at the summits of the pavilions, and an anklet-wearing woman tormented behind the curtains. And oryx-eyed women from the seclusion of the chambers — gazelles set running toward their fates. A vision visited you on the night of the sandhill from a sandy place between the sacred enclosure and the place of long waiting. Sleep — for no phantom visited you; rather, you yourself journeyed to the phantom of the imagination.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the howdahs and litters of departing women; the caravan of loved ones leaving
coquetry, playful allure; the deliberately charming behavior of the beloved
an ankle bracelet, a jingling anklet; its sound and sight mark the beloved's presence
curtained chambers, the screened enclosures where women dwell
white oryx; plural of maha — a classical metaphor for beautiful wide-eyed women
the phantom of imagination; the dream-vision of the beloved that visits the sleepless lover

context: A khafif nasib poem of six verses evoking the departure of beautiful women from their encampments. The final verse delivers a philosophical inversion: the lover did not receive a dream-visit from the beloved's phantom — rather, the lover's longing soul traveled out to meet it. A meditation on the dream-vision (tayf al-khayal) convention in Arabic poetry.

titleEn: A Singular Beauty, Offspring of Light
translationEn: A singular beauty, offspring of light — by him the hand of joy has stood upright and made itself independent.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: singular beauty, one unique and unmatched in beauty
offspring of light, born of radiance
stood independent, became self-sufficient, rose on its own
the hand of joy — joy personified as capable of standing upright
the broken basit meter — a short truncated variation of the basit

context: A single-verse fragment in the makhlu' al-basit meter, preserved as a standalone gem in the diwan. Abu Tammam declares the beloved a unique product of light itself and credits him with giving joy its independence.

titleEn: My Thought Summoned Him in Dreams

verses.sadr: مجلس لم يكن لنا فيه عيب

translationEn: My thought summoned him to visit in the dream, and he came to me in fear and concealment. The nights are kinder to my heart than the days when they make it swallow the bitterness of separation. What a night it was — when souls went walking free and something drifted secretly out of bodies! A gathering that had no fault for us — except that we were assembled only by the invitation of dreams.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: summoned him to visit, invited him to come — the thought calling the beloved
fear, apprehension, a state of being afraid
concealment, hiding, suppression of oneself
separation, distance, the condition of being apart from the beloved
the souls went walking, the spirits went on an outing
the invitation of dreams — dreams as a host calling souls to gather

context: A four-verse ghazal in the khafif meter about a dream visitation from the beloved, whose soul visits the poet secretly while their bodies sleep apart. Abu Tammam celebrates this dream gathering as perfect but for its ephemeral, dream-bound nature.

titleEn: Cheeks Like Apples, Lips Like Wine
translationEn: His cheeks are like the apple in color, and his saliva is like wine. A wonder of beauty, composed from sun and from moon. He has a face — when you behold it, it whispers to you in apology. Exalted is God — how deeply his eyes have struck fire into my heart!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the apple — red and round, a classic image for rosy cheeks
saliva, the moisture of the mouth — compared to wine for its sweetness
a wonder of beauty, a marvel of fine looks
whispered to you, spoke confidentially to you
an excuse, an apology — the face seems to apologize for its own beauty
strikes fire in it, ignites it — the eyes striking sparks into the chest

context: A four-verse ghazal in the hazaj meter describing the beloved through natural comparisons: apple-red cheeks, wine-like saliva, a face composed of sun and moon. The final verse marvels at how deeply the beloved's eyes have penetrated the poet's heart.

titleEn: Love Is Unjust and You Are More So
translationEn: Love is unjust and you are even more unjust — how can the wronged one prevail against you both? Love has its boldness and you have your shunning — from neither of you do I have a merciful lover. Love has worn me down and deranged my mind — upon me from you both has descended a great affliction. Only one whose rope has been cut knows sleeplessness and the length of nights.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: unjust, oppressive — love personified as an oppressor
greatly unjust, intensely oppressive — the beloved even more so than love itself
wore me down, whittled me away, made me thin — from bara
confused, deranged, unsettled the mind
sleeplessness, insomnia, inability to sleep
one whose rope has been cut — severed from hope and connection

context: A four-verse ghazal in the khafif meter personifying love as an oppressor working in league with an unjust beloved. The poet declares himself doubly wronged and closes with the haunting image of the sleepless one whose rope of hope has been severed.

titleEn: Your Suspicion About What I Conceal Is Wise
translationEn: Your suspicion about what I conceal is a judgment I accept for myself — and your glance is itself the perceptive one. How can I find consolation when you show me no mercy? This is no way for blessings to dwell as neighbors. I have entrusted my heart with your love, and my heart is not suspect in what it has been trusted with. I have shown distress from the burning of love — yet patience, except in the face of love, is a virtue.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: what I conceal, what I keep secret — the hidden love
a judgment, a ruling, a wise decree
the understanding, the perceptive one — the glance that comprehends
consolation, forgetting a sorrow, moving on from grief
the neighborhood of blessings, where blessings dwell together
distress, anguish, inability to endure — the outward show of grief
virtue, nobility, generosity of spirit

context: A four-verse ghazal in the munsarih meter elegantly arguing that the lover's heart is a trustworthy guardian of love. The poet contends that showing distress before one's beloved is no dishonor, since patience is only a virtue in matters other than love.

titleEn: The Beloved Visitor Has Come
translationEn: The beloved visitor has come — the dazzling crescent has risen. My abundant tears are guided by it while my heart wanders bewildered. I have a flowing tear on my cheek and a verse that travels far. If you were to line your eyes with his face, though his glance is languid, and with the wonders on his cheeks that are rivals to the pomegranate blossom, you would behold fatal headwaters that have no place of return.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: arrived, came, fulfilled an expectation
the dazzling, the brilliant, the overwhelming in beauty
abundant, copious, plentiful
a tear, a single flowing teardrop
pomegranate blossom, a classic image for the red color of cheeks
rivals, co-wives; things that compete with one another
fatal headwaters, death-bringing sources with no outlet

context: A six-verse ghazal in the mujazza' kamil meter depicting the arrival of the beloved like a rising crescent moon. Abu Tammam plays on the paradox of the lover's tears being guided by the beloved's face while his heart remains lost, culminating in the image of the beloved's cheeks as deadly springs.

titleEn: Namesake of the Prophet
translationEn: O namesake of the Prophet when he is named, and he who was singled out with beauty and grace. He whose waist nearly broke from slenderness, yet the flanks bent it back when it was nearly gone. I shall never forget a secret whisper between us: the best of love is that which stays hidden. May God preserve for me the soundness of his love, and may what has grieved me from his affection suffice me!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: namesake, one who shares the same name
severance, breaking off; nearly snapping from extreme slenderness
the flanks, the sides of the torso
hidden, encoded, concealed — as in a riddle or secret
the soundness of his love, unbroken and uncorrupted affection
grieved me, caused me worry and sorrow

context: A short ghazal addressed to a beloved whose name is Muhammad, a namesake of the Prophet. Abu Tammam praises his beauty and slender figure, then reflects on their shared secret that the best love is that which is concealed.

titleEn: Sleep Is Forbidden to My Eye

verses.sadr: ويا كبدي الحرى التي قد تصدعت
verses.ajuz: من الوجد ذوبي ما عليك ملام

verses.sadr: ويا وجه من ذلت وجوه أعزة
أجر مستجيرا في الهوى بك باسطا

translationEn: Sleep is forbidden to you, O my eye — let tears flow in a streaming downpour. For in tears is the quenching of a fire of longing that blazes between the ribs. O my scorching liver, cracked apart by passion, melt — there is no blame upon you. I have fulfilled an obligation to love that was due from me, and I too have a claim upon it in return. O face of one before whom the faces of the noble have been humbled, girded by honor that none can attain. Shelter one who seeks refuge in you through love, stretching his hands toward you while all eyes sleep.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sleep, slumber, rest
streaming, flowing freely — describing heavy continuous tears
intense longing, passionate love, burning yearning
blazing fire, fierce flames
an obligation, a right, a bond of protection or duty
one who seeks protection or refuge
ecstatic grief, overwhelming passion

context: An intimate monologue of the sleepless lover, addressing his own eye, liver, and finally the beloved's face. Abu Tammam frames love as a mutual obligation while pleading for shelter from the beloved under cover of night.

titleEn: O Gazelle With Rose-Petal Cheeks

verses.sadr: لا سألت الخلاص منك وإن كن

translationEn: O gazelle whose cheek is a plucked rose, and whose mouth holds scattered pearls. No — and yet he sways like a tender bough when his full figure sways. I shall not ask for release from you, even if you stir upon me the trial of love.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: plucked, gathered — like fruit being picked from a branch
the rose; rosy color of the cheek
scattered pearls, pearls scattered loosely — for teeth
the tender bough, a fresh and supple branch
the full, heavy haunches — a classical image of physical beauty
release, freedom, escape from love's grip

context: A brief ghazal praising a youthful gazelle-like beloved through images of roses, pearls, and swaying boughs. The poet concludes with a declaration that he will not seek release even from the torment of love.

titleEn: Your Love Dwells Within My Sides
translationEn: Your love dwells settled within my sides, O gentle young gazelle. By a cheek upon which a rose has bloomed, with fragrance that paradise itself has perfected. You have truly taken hold of a heart that your languid glance has made ill.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the inner sides, the flanks, the interior of the chest
a young gazelle, a fawn — a classic term of endearment
gentle, soft, melodious; tender in voice and manner
bliss, paradise, divine abundance
the languid, sickly glance — paradoxically sickening others
you have taken hold, you have become firmly established

context: A three-verse ghazal in the munsarih meter, compact and tender, describing the beloved as a young gazelle whose rose-like cheek and languid eyes have captured the poet's heart.

titleEn: Time Is a Day of Joy, a Day of Blame
translationEn: Time is a day of joy and a day of blame, and life is a mix of excuse and reproach. So limit yourself to what you desire, and let there be no hesitation from you. Do not incline your ear to what base people say about you. And a slender figure — the soul's own wish — no bidding can raise his price. With drowsiness in his eyes, yet no sleep is truly there. I broke my fast upon him, though before him I had kept a long fast.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: time, fate, the ages — often personified in Arabic poetry
hesitation, circling about, wavering indecision
slim-waisted, slender, willowy
the soul's desires, the heart's deepest wishes
bidding, pricing, haggling — the beloved's worth beyond price
drowsy, heavy-lidded, having sleepy eyes
fasting, abstinence — here metaphorically, abstaining from love

context: A philosophical ghazal in the mujtathth meter reflecting on the duality of time, interspersed with advice to seize joy and ignore critics, culminating in the image of breaking a long fast of abstinence on the beloved.

titleEn: His Locks Are Alif and Lam
translationEn: His locks are like alif and lam, and in his glance lies a sharp cutting sword. His speech is the pearl of love whose thread has not yet betrayed it. He has not diminished in beauty at all — to him belong perfection and completeness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sideburns, the locks that fall along the cheeks
the Arabic letters alif (straight) and lam (curved) — describing the shapes of the locks
a sharp-edged sword, a keenly honed blade
pearls; figuratively for beautiful speech
the thread that strings pearls; also order and arrangement
completeness and perfection, wholeness in beauty

context: A witty three-verse ghazal playing on Arabic calligraphy: the beloved's sidelocks are compared to the letter-shapes alif and lam. Abu Tammam celebrates the beloved's perfection through images of swords, pearls, and flawless beauty.

titleEn: Do Not Shun, For Shunning Is Grave
translationEn: Do not shun me, for shunning is a grave matter — and have mercy, for God is righteous and compassionate. Is it just that your heart has flowed away while my love remains fixed and dwelling? Then you have heaped injury and oppression upon me, while another is the truly guilty and oppressive one. We have committed no wrong against you, but love in this age does not endure.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: turning away, shunning, repelling a lover
flowed away, dissolved — the heart moving on from love
wrongdoing, injury, mistreatment
we committed a crime, we wronged — from jarama
oppression, injustice, wrongful treatment
does not last, does not endure — the transience of love

context: A plea to a beloved who has turned away, appealing to divine mercy and to the injustice of being abandoned without fault. The poem closes with a melancholy observation on the impermanence of love in the poet's age.

titleEn: Sheathe the Sword of Your Glance

verses.sadr: كيف اعتدلت مع اعتدال الغصن في

translationEn: Sheathe the sword of your glance away from souls, for hearts have grown weary of your languid side-glances. How can you be upright like a gracefully swaying bough, yet act with the injustice of an oppressor? You knew the sin of sorcery and censured it — yet I see you have taken up the tools of the sorcerer. O you who are a poet in your glance and radiance and beauty — you have tormented the heart of the poet!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sheathe, put back in the scabbard
souls, lives, hearts — the plural of muhja
the languid side-glance, a drooping half-closed look
the unjust, the oppressor, one who deviates from right
the sin of sorcery — forbidden magic
a poet; one who perceives finely — pun: the beloved tortures the actual poet

context: A four-verse ghazal in the kamil meter playing on the paradox of a beautiful beloved who is as graceful as a bough yet as unjust as an oppressor. The final verse turns the word 'sha'ir' on the poet himself — the beautiful one is a 'poet' in his beauty who has tormented the literal poet.

titleEn: A Fair Abode Appeared and He Wandered Mad

verses.ajuz: أي الدموع وقد بدت لم يجرها !
لما بدا أو وجهها من شعرها

translationEn: A fair abode appeared to him, and he wandered mad with her memory — which of his tears did not flow when she appeared! A fair one — you would think her dark hair came from her face's radiance when it shone, or her face came from her hair. Varied in elegance is the inner expanse of her breast, varied in beauty is the outer surface of her chest. She gives you her speech and you know that its sweetness comes from the fruit that passes over her lips. I believe the rope of her union with her lover is flimsier and weaker than her own slender waist.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: appeared, became visible, presented itself
an abode, a dwelling; also beloved — one who brings tranquility
wandered mad with love, became delirious with passion
speech, articulation, the manner of speaking
fruit, that which is gathered; sweetness derived from a source
the mouth, the lips, the front teeth
the rope of union, the bond of connection with the beloved

context: A five-verse ghazal in the kamil meter admiring a fair woman whose radiant beauty confuses hair with light and light with hair. Abu Tammam praises her elegance, sweet speech, and slender figure, ending with the bitter observation that the bond of her love is as fragile as her waist.

titleEn: You Are Absolved, So Increase My Illness
translationEn: You are absolved — so increase my illness, exhaust my patience, and turn my tears to blood. Accept for me death through your abandonment; and if I do not die of longing, then increase my pain. The trial of the lover is humiliation in love, and when entrusted with a secret, he keeps it hidden. He is not truly one of us who, disguised by his ailment, complains of a beloved's injustice — that itself is injustice!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: absolved, free of guilt, permitted — you are not to blame
illness, sickness — here the sickness of love
exhaust, deplete, cause to waste away
abandonment, separation, desertion by the beloved
trial, tribulation, ordeal
he kept it hidden, he concealed it — from katama
disguised by his ailment, concealing a complaint behind a pretense

context: A masochistic ghazal in which the lover grants his beloved full absolution and invites further torment, framing such total submission as the mark of a true lover. The final verse establishes a code: the genuine lover never publicly complains of his beloved's injustice.

titleAr: إذا راح مشهور المحاسن أو غدا
titleEn: When the Renowned Beauty Passes By

verses.sadr: إذا راح مشهور المحاسن أو غدا
فمن لم تفز عيناه منه بنظرة
إذا ما انتضى سيف الملاحة طرفه
verses.ajuz: ونادى قلوب القوم هل من مبارز
على أنه عن غيره غير عاجز

translationEn: When the one renowned for beauty goes out in the evening or in the morning, with softness before the hinting sidelong glances of eyes. Whoever's eyes have not won a glance from him has not won any good fortune in life. When his glance draws the sword of grace and calls out to the hearts of people: Is there any challenger? My heart grew helpless and submitted in peace to his glance — though it is not helpless before others.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: renowned for beauties, famous for his fine qualities
hinting glances, meaningful looks made with sidelong eyes
drew a sword, unsheathed — used for the glance as a sword
grace, elegance, the quality of being beautiful and charming
a challenger in single combat, a duelist
submitted, surrendered — laid down arms in peace

context: A four-verse ghazal depicting the beloved's public appearance as a martial display of beauty, drawing the sword of his glance against all hearts. The poet's heart alone surrenders, though it claims to be undefeated by any other.

titleEn: Distance Began As the Sin of Drawing Near
translationEn: His distance began as the sin of drawing near — he is one stolen from the houris of paradise. On his cheeks are subtle wonders — if you could see them, you would ask about their meaning. We were silent together, yet both our hearts spoke in the language of love. We warred against the burning of longing until we surrendered in humiliation to its safe conduct.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: distance, remoteness, being far away
drawing near, coming close — the closeness that caused the distance
the houris of paradise, the dark-eyed maidens of the garden
subtle details, fine points, delicate features
we were mutually silent, we kept silent together
the burning thirst of longing, smoldering desire
humiliated, abased, in a state of submission

context: A four-verse ghazal describing a silent encounter between the lover and the beloved, whose distance is itself a result of his irresistible closeness. The poem ends with both parties surrendering to the overwhelming force of longing.

titleEn: If Only You Could See Him, Abu al-Hasan
translationEn: If only you could see him, O Abu al-Hasan — a moon rising atop a bough. A moon whose jewels have cast into my heart the jewel of sorrow. Every part of his beauty contains within it parts of trial and temptation. In his very composition are wonders that have kept my heart from the straight path. By my father — those helpers who sided with my illness over my body!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rose above, appeared atop a bough — the moon-like face above the slender body
jewels, gems; here the radiant qualities of the beloved
temptations, trials, seductions — plural of fitna
composition, construction, the way he is put together
wonders, innovations, unprecedented things
helpers, supporters — here ironically: the illness supported against the body

context: A five-verse ghazal addressed to an unnamed friend (Abu al-Hasan), describing the beloved as a moon perched on a bough whose beauty has destabilized the poet's inner life. The final verse turns to dark humor as the poet notes his illness has found more supporters than his body.

titleEn: O Sleepless Eyelids I Have Deprived

verses.sadr: أين منك الدما فقد نفد الدم

translationEn: O sleepless eyelids — I have deprived you of the pleasure of sleep and rest, O eyelids. Where now are your tears? For the blood that the sorrowful one draws from you has run dry. The body has worn away, yet longing lives on — it does not wear away, nor do the griefs wear away. Truly God has, for His servants, death-bearing agents He has appointed over hearts: eyes!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: eyelids, the coverings of the eyes — plural of jafn
sleepless, wakeful, unable to sleep — plural of sahir
draws out, milks, extracts — here extracting tears like milk
griefs, sorrows — plural of shajan
fates, deaths, doom — plural of maniyya
appointed them in power, gave them authority over

context: A four-verse ghazal addressing the poet's own sleepless eyelids and dried-up tears, contrasting the body's decay with the immortality of longing. The poem ends with a striking theological conceit: God has appointed beautiful eyes as instruments of death for human hearts.

titleAr: ومحتكم في الخمص طورا وفي البدن
titleEn: Ruling Between Slenderness and Fullness

verses.sadr: ومحتكم في الخمص طورا وفي البدن
verses.ajuz: فقد دق في حقف وقد جل عن غصن

verses.sadr: بتدى فأبدى لي الجوى من صدوده

verses.ajuz: وأحسن ما تستوضح الشمس في الدجن

verses.sadr: فأغضبته أن قلت يا أحسن الورى
verses.ajuz: وكاد بأن يفضي إلى الشتم واللعن

verses.sadr: إذا غاظ وصف الناس بالحسن أهله
verses.ajuz: فلم لم يخرق ثوبه يوسف الحسن ؟

translationEn: A figure that rules between slenderness and fullness — too fine for a sand dune and too grand for a bough. His shunning me made apparent to me the inward burning, and the finest gifts a heart can give are wrought from grief. He had blackened the court register with some of his garments — and how beautiful the sun appears in darkness. Verses that matched his face came to meet him; I called upon my thought for them and set my mind to serve them. Yet it angered him when I said: O most beautiful of all creation — and he nearly descended to insult and curse. When praising people for beauty angers those who have it, why did Joseph of beauty not tear his own garment?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: slenderness, thinness of the waist, lean elegance
a curved sand dune, a winding dune crest
inward burning, hidden grief, inner passion
the court register, the official record; also a collection of poems
I called upon, I summoned, I assigned for a task
Joseph of beauty — the prophet Yusuf, Quranic archetype of perfect beauty

context: A six-verse ghazal recounting the poet's attempt to praise the beloved in verse, only to have his praise met with anger. The poem ends with a witty reference to the Prophet Yusuf, asking why beauty should be angered by its own description.

titleEn: By My Life, Some Eyes Found Rest Near You
translationEn: By my life — if some eyes have found comfort in your nearness, then other eyes have burned hot with your parting. Go or stay — my love for you stands firm; your place in my heart is preserved for you. I truly fear that if affairs between us continue to escalate against our will, time with its hostile turns will grind us apart.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: became cool, found relief, settled in comfort — said of eyes finding rest
became hot, burned — opposite of finding cool comfort
separation, parting, departure
escalated, rose higher, intensified — affairs growing worse
hostilities, hidden grudges, enmities — plural of ihna
the vicissitudes of time, the turning blows of fate

context: A short three-verse ghazal balancing the comfort some find in the beloved's nearness against the burning of those left behind in separation. The poet reassures that his love is preserved while fearing the relentless hostility of fate.

titleEn: Beauty Is But a Part of Your Beautiful Face

verses.sadr: إن كنت في الحسن واحدا فأنا

translationEn: Beauty itself is but a part of your beautiful face, O moon rising above a bough. If you are singular in beauty, then I, O singular beauty, am singular in sorrow. Every illness you see in anyone — that is a branch, while the root is in my own body. The phenomena of love did not exist in the hearts of lovers before you came into being.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rising above a bough, appearing over the top of a branch
the singular one in beauty, the matchless in beauty
the branch, the derivative — as opposed to the root
the root, the origin, the primary source
the phenomena of love, the entities and events born of love
hearts, the plural of fuad — the deepest seat of feeling

context: A four-verse ghazal in the munsarih meter, making the bold claim that all beauty in the world is merely a part of the beloved's face, and all illness derives from the poet's own love-sickness. It closes with a hyperbolic cosmological claim: love itself only began when this beloved came into being.

titleEn: I Ransom Muhammad From All Harm

verses.sadr: فلو أن الصبا حملتك ما إن

translationEn: I offer myself as ransom for Muhammad against every harm he fears in going out or coming home. O moon of the sky — you have descended so low, as if you have grown weary of your height. I see you distant from the one who loves you, yet near to one who does not love you at all. Had the east wind carried you, nothing tomorrow would outrun me to consolation. And sufficient sorrow for you is a friend whose reins you see held in an enemy's hand.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I ransom, I sacrifice myself for — an expression of extreme devotion
descended, came down — the moon descending from its height
grew weary of, became bored with, tired of
the east wind, the gentle morning breeze — associated with messages of love
consolation, forgetting, moving on from grief
reins, bridle — here control over a friendship or relationship

context: A five-verse ghazal addressed to a beloved named Muhammad, compared to a moon who has descended to earth. Abu Tammam laments that the beloved is close to those who do not love him and distant from those who do, ending with a bitter image of a friend whose affairs are controlled by an enemy.

titleEn: Be Gentle With Him If You Are His Master

verses.sadr: ويل له إن دام هذا به

translationEn: Be gentle with him if you are his master, and have mercy — for you have already delighted his enemies. Woe to him if this continues — from a burning that splits his inner organs apart. O bough of the ban tree, supple in his stature, swaying above a soft dune at its highest. You have deprived my eye of the sweetness of sleep — may God beautify you as He has beautified you!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his master, his lord — one with authority over him
you delighted his enemies, gave occasion for their mockery
burning, scorching — the heat of passion
splits apart, cracks open, bursts
bough of the ban tree (moringa) — a classical image of slender swaying beauty
a pure white soft sand dune — symbol of lightness and grace
slumber, drowsiness, the sweetness of sleep

context: A four-verse ghazal appealing to the beloved to show mercy before the lover's suffering becomes fatal. The poem concludes with a benedictory prayer in the last hemistich — 'may God beautify you as He has beautified you' — a paradox that is simultaneously both blessing and complaint.

titleEn: She Gave, Then Lent, Then Took It Back
translationEn: She gave and lent to me — then she took it back and saw my humiliation and became proud. She has a face by which she is made mighty, while I have a burning that humbles me before her. Delicate beauties have joined the beauties of her cheeks with them. I gaze at the beauty of her cheeks, and they wound me — and I wound them!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: she lent to me — suggesting her affection was only ever on loan
became proud, was vain, exulted — from zaha
is made mighty by it, finds honor through it
burning, a scorching flame of passion
delicate beauties, subtle fine features
I wound her — the intense gaze itself wounds the cheeks it gazes upon

context: A four-verse ghazal in the mujazza' wafir meter, built on paradox: the lover gazes at the beloved's cheeks and wounds them with his gaze, while they wound him. The poem ends on a cycle of mutual wounding between the gazer and the gazed-upon.

titleAr: منحتك ودا كان طفلا فقد نشا
titleEn: I Gave You Love That Was a Child Now Grown

verses.sadr: منحتك ودا كان طفلا فقد نشا
verses.ajuz: وأبديت لي جسما من الود موحشا

verses.sadr: أرى ثمر الحسن الذي قد غرسته
ولي يا خلي الصدر من لوعة الهوى
verses.ajuz: حشا لست أدري جمرة هي أم حشا

verses.sadr: فداو سقاما منه في الجسم فاشيا
verses.ajuz: كما الحسن في ساحات وجهك قد فشا

translationEn: I granted you a love that was a child — it has now grown up, while you have shown me a body of affection that is desolate. I see the fruit of the beauty I planted trained upon the trellis of your cruelty. I, O you whose breast is free of love's burning, have an inner fire — I cannot tell whether it is an ember or my own vitals. Heal an illness that has spread throughout my body, as your beauty has spread across the courts of your face. I swear — if you appeared before the eye of al-Murasqish, you would truly have made him forget Asma'!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: grew up, matured — of a child becoming an adult
feigning wrongness, false accusations, cruelty disguised as grievance
trained on a trellis, arbored — vines growing over a framework
the burning pain of love, the anguish of passion
spreading, widespread, pervading — an illness that has spread everywhere
al-Murasqish — a pre-Islamic poet famous for his love of Asma'

context: A five-verse ghazal in the tawil meter using the extended metaphor of love as a child that has grown up while the beloved's affection remains barren. Abu Tammam closes by invoking the pre-Islamic poet al-Murasqish and his legendary beloved Asma', claiming this beloved would eclipse even that famous beauty.

titleAr: أشهى إلي من الدنيا وما فيها
titleEn: More Desired Than All the World

verses.sadr: أشهى إلي من الدنيا وما فيها

verses.ajuz: جاءت بها قينة من عند غانية

translationEn: More desired to me than the world and all it contains is a red wine with a golden tint, perfumed with aloe. It is as if it were plucked from the cheek of the one who presented it — a singing-girl brought it from a beautiful woman. My soul ransoms it from sickness and sorrows — had I been dead and she called me with her melody, I would have answered her from my grave in longing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: more desired, more appetizing, more longed for
red wine with a golden tint — a classic description of aged wine
an expensive perfume of aloe, musk, and ambergris
a singing-girl, a female musician and entertainer
a beautiful self-sufficient woman, one who needs no adornment
the grave, the niche in a tomb
I would answer her, I would say labbayk — responding to a summons

context: A wasf poem describing wine brought by a singing-girl, so beautiful in color it appears to have been plucked from her cheek. The poet declares his devotion so absolute that even death could not stop him from answering her call.

titleEn: O You Who Shows No Mercy to Your Lovers
translationEn: O you who shows no mercy to your lovers, who has blended shunning with pride toward us. Before whom beauty itself has bowed in submission, and whose grace has encompassed all those near him. O offspring of the sun — may my soul be your ransom — and what comparison can an offspring of the sun have? You have attained perfection in grace and surpassed all in elegance; you are a refined one, in whom there is no fault.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: shows mercy, softens, becomes gentle
turning away, shunning, rejecting the lover
pride, haughtiness, self-conceit
offspring of the sun, child of the sun — used to describe radiant beauty
grace, charm, beauty — the quality of elegant attraction
refined, cultivated, purified of faults

context: A four-verse ghazal in the wafir meter praising a beloved who combines unmerciful pride with unparalleled beauty. Abu Tammam calls him an offspring of the sun, a being without peer or blemish.

titleEn: He Carries My Life in His Hands
translationEn: He carries my life in his two hands — O my grief, and O my longing for him! Exalted is God — blessed is the eye that lets its gaze linger freely on his two cheeks. I believe that separation sought to bereave me of him, since it has now become envious of my claim on him. I shall weep, as long as tears obey my eye, for his beauties and the languor of his two glances.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: carries, bears — he bears the poet's very life in his hands
my grief, my sorrow, my regret
Exalted is God — an exclamation of wonder at the divine creation
blessedness, bliss — a Quranic word for a state of happiness
bereavement, devastating loss, bitter grief
the languor of the two eyes, the drowsy half-closed glance

context: A closing ghazal of this batch in the wafir meter, depicting the beloved as holding the poet's very life. Abu Tammam personifies separation as an entity jealous of his relationship with the beloved, and vows to weep as long as tears permit.

titleEn: I Revealed a Longing I Had Hidden

verses.sadr: إن كان وجهك لي تترى محاسنه

verses.ajuz: حتى إذا كملت تاهت على التيه

verses.sadr: ما استجمعت فرق الحسن التي افترقت
verses.ajuz: عن يوسف الحسن حتى استجمعت فيه

translationEn: I have spread before you a longing I used to fold away, and I have revealed my anguish that I used to conceal. If your face shows me its beauties one after another, your treatment of me shows me its faults one after another. His lower part sways as he glides along, his upper part trembles as he bends. His form surpassed the forms of all things, and when it reached perfection it surpassed even perfection itself. The scattered parts of beauty that had been dispersed since the time of Joseph the Beautiful were not gathered together until they gathered in him.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: residue of love, smoldering passion
I fold it up, conceal it
anguish, burning pain of love
one after another, in succession
swaying, shaking (of the lower body)
graceful swaying gait
vanity, self-conceit; also the desert of wandering

context: A short ghazal in which Abu Tammam confesses a passion he had long suppressed. The final verse draws a celebrated comparison to the Quranic figure of Joseph (Yusuf), suggesting the beloved has gathered all beauty that was once scattered.

titleAr: لو كنت عندي أمس وهو معانقي
titleEn: Had You Been with Me Yesterday

verses.sadr: لو كنت عندي أمس وهو معانقي

translationEn: Had you been with me yesterday while he was embracing me, my tears flowing down his cheeks — his cheeks were quenched by my tears, and my lips took pleasure in his lips — you would have seen a weeping that love makes easy, and that the releasing of tears over him makes light. And you would have seen something more beautiful than my weeping: his words, 'This young man — his eyes are the ones being stubborn!'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: embracing me, my companion in embrace
tear ducts, tears
were quenched, became saturated
a tear
cheeks
took delight, refreshed itself
stubbornly obstinate, willful

context: A tender ghazal imagining an intimate scene of embrace and tears. The final verse turns on a witty reversal: the beloved playfully accuses the poet's eyes of being the obstinate ones, not his heart.

titleEn: His Beauty Surpassed All Description
translationEn: His beauties have excelled so greatly that words are too inadequate to describe him. Beauty itself spoke in defense of his lover before the female censurers, and silenced their preaching. Souls have obtained from him nothing more than what the glance has gained from his cheeks. What harm would it do to one whose beauties are perfect if his harsh heart were to become tender?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: excelled, surpassed all others
was too great, was above
female censurers, blaming women
silenced, struck dumb
to demean, to obtain cheaply
the side glance, a brief look
harsh, rough in character

context: A compact ghazal in which Abu Tammam uses beauty itself as an advocate: the beloved's perfection silences all censure. The final verse is a classic reproach — the beloved's cruelty is his only flaw.

titleEn: My Opinion of Him Is Good Despite His Wrong

verses.sadr: لم يلهني عنه ما ألهاه بل عذبت

translationEn: My opinion of him is good, were it not for his wrongdoing and his failure to honor the right of his intimate friend. Nothing has distracted me from him as it has distracted him from me; rather, yearning has been sweet to me as I have drunk of it for his sake. His beauties have pardoned his ill treatment of me, so much so that even his faults have become beautiful to me. This is your lover — longing has made his soul bleed; how then can you deny that his eyelids should bleed?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: his wrongdoing, his unjust aggression
his close friend, companion
intense longing, fervent love
pardoned, overlooked, erased
the soul, the life-blood
corners of the eyes, eyelids
caused to bleed, wounded

context: A short ghazal of complaint against an indifferent beloved. The final verse is a powerful plea: if longing has made the lover bleed internally, surely the beloved cannot deny the visible tears as blood.

titleEn: Trials of Time and the Poet's Perseverance

verses.ajuz: يا هذه اعذري في هذه النكب

verses.sadr: في صدره من هموم يعتلجن به

verses.ajuz: فذاب هما وجمد العين لم يذب
ما الدهر في فعله إلا أبو العجب

verses.sadr: فما عدمت بها لا جاحدا عدما
ما يحسم العقل والدنيا تساس يه

verses.ajuz: والعقل عار إذا لم يكس بالنشب

verses.sadr: ما أضيع العقل إن لم يرع ضيعته
verses.ajuz: وفر واي رحى دارت بلا قطب

verses.sadr: كم ذقت في الدهر من عسر ومن يسر
verses.ajuz: وفي بني الدهر من رأس ومن ذنب

verses.sadr: أغضي إذا صرفه لم تغض أعينه
verses.ajuz: عني وأرضى إذا ما لج في الغضب
علما بأني ما قصرت في الطلب
أدراك رزق إذاما كان في الهرب

verses.sadr: ماذا علي إذا لم يزل وتري
verses.ajuz: في الرمي ان زلن أغراضي فلم أصب
تستنبط الصفر لي من معدن الذهب
عن ليلة القدر في شعبان أو رجب
على قواصيه في بدء وفي عقب
لم يخلق العرض مني سوء مطلبي

verses.sadr: إذا عنيت لشأو قلت إني قد
ما آب من آب لم يظفر ببغيته
verses.ajuz: ولم يغب طالب للنجح لم يخب !

translationEn: She came with troubles, and my chief concern turned away from her hints — O you, excuse me in these calamities. Away with you! Woe upon whoever was filled with woe upon you and compassion without cessation — in his breast, griefs that struggle within him, and whispers that shred the minds of white-complexioned women. The constant succession of nights has driven back the sharpness of his tears, so he melted with grief, yet his eyes did not melt. Not because behind you lies a dawn for pleasures — but before you joy and mirth have died. And strange events, both odd and even — time in its acts is nothing but the father of wonders. They overpower the warriors of marked heroes and lead away knights on reed-like steeds. So I have not lost through them — I deny it not — a patience that stands in place of revealing distress. Reason does not cauterize what patience cauterizes in calamities and misfortunes. Patience is a garment and the palm's inner face is naked; reason is bare when not clothed with wealth. How wasted is reason if it does not tend its domain — and what use is a millstone turning without its axle? I became entangled in the depths of the world, and it bereaved me of my wealth and came back with honor unsoiled. How many a hardship and ease have I tasted from time, and among time's children how many a head and tail have I known. I overlook when time's turning does not overlook with its eyes toward me, and I am content when it persists in anger. And if I am tried by a severity whose roughness is hard, I smooth it so that I seem to be in play with it. I cut short the paths of grief through my body, knowing that I have not fallen short in seeking. By what swift trot of she-camels and crossing of open land does a provision reach you when it is in flight? What blame falls on me if my bowstring has not slackened in shooting, though my targets slip and I do not hit? Every day my nails are chipped as they extract poverty for me from the mine of gold. I was not like one who wanders asking the days about the Night of Power in Sha'ban or Rajab; rather, I seize the forelocks of affairs, encompassing their furthest reaches at beginning and end. I have always aimed my hopes at their targets — no vicissitude has made my seeking bad through baseness. With an estrangement like the estrangement of generosity when it flashes with a return, then betrays with disappointment and lies. When I labor for a distant goal and say I have reached it — the misfortune of being a man of letters overtakes me! And a failure that springs from an absence that has stretched in the most ominous way, rising in every place of turmoil. No one who has returned has returned without attaining his desire, and no seeker of success who has set out has been disappointed!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: calamities, disasters, misfortunes
intense longing / patience, endurance
the misfortune of being a man of letters, the ill-luck of poets
the axle-pin of a millstone; a pivot figure
chipped, notched (of nails)
forelocks; metaphor for the beginnings/controls of affairs
warriors clad in armor, champions

context: A lengthy philosophical qasida in which Abu Tammam reflects on the vicissitudes of fortune, the misery of the poet's profession, and the virtues of patience and resolute pursuit. The poem contains a famous lament: 'The misfortune of being a man of letters,' expressing the frustration of the poet-intellectual who strives yet is denied worldly reward.

titleEn: Gloom and Weeping Were Our Mounts

verses.ajuz: وكم عدوية من سر عمر و

translationEn: We rode gloom and weeping as our mounts — yet neither these eyes nor these hearts can endure the habit of this. For indeed, a stranger when the bitterness of separation drags on finds solace in the stranger's company. And what a dwelling-place of hope — it settled there and stayed — is the pouring tear. How many an Adawite woman of the secret of Amr, whose lineage when traced is noble, has a chaste mother from Tayy, of a distinguished clan, and a noble father, hoped that a beloved would return to her — she hoped in vain; and where is a beloved for her? Had she truly seen him, she would have seen one eager, adorned by time's water with pallor — like a sword-blade stripped of its sheath, its striking-edge notched by misfortunes — a guarantor of wealth, or a hero of weeping over a land of barren fields and numerous offspring, who has reached a place where there is no water for the thirsty, no wealth to take refuge in — stripped bare, in Egypt. And what purpose is there in Egypt, now that death has taken away its eldest?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: gloom, dejection, sadness
loud weeping, sobbing
the stranger, the exile
the flowing, pouring tears
pallor, emaciation from hardship
death (personified as a separator of clans)
stripped of belongings, bereaved

context: A fragment of an elegy (ritha) lamenting the death of a noble figure in Egypt, interwoven with themes of exile, the suffering of a waiting woman, and the poet's own estrangement. The poem is preserved without its opening verses.

titleEn: Days Sought Him as He Sought Them

verses.ajuz: أو راح من سلب الزمان سليبا
أن شام من حكم الزمان عجيبا

translationEn: Days sought him, and he, the seeker of their like, sought others in return, so he became at once the sought and the seeker. It is a resolution fit for a sword, yet it has been made a reed-stalk for the ropes of time. Time's calamities wooed from him a condition that bore as its offspring trials and disasters. Time's ropes cut from him a decisive cut that left misfortunes palpitating. Many a time the blow of an event grieved him, reopening scars on the inner face of his two sides. Not that the means of wealth failed him, or that he went home stripped by time's plundering; rather, it is a wonder — and no wonder — for one who has glimpsed in the decree of time a marvel: that one day his dwelling is at the point where the east ends, and another day he dwells at the west as a stranger. May hopes whose fulfillment is guaranteed by a generosity that shows you only a frowning, scowling face not exist!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: sought after, pursued
firm resolution, determination
great calamities, grave afflictions
disasters, misfortunes
scars, wounds
frowning, scowling
scowling, having a furrowed brow

context: A meditation on the reversal of fortune, praising a figure who has endured time's trials with steadfastness. The poem plays on the paradox of one simultaneously seeking and being sought, and ends with a bitter irony about generosity that only frowns.

titleEn: When I Saw the Matter Was Serious
translationEn: When I saw the matter was truly serious and found no escape from rising to it, I donned the skin of a leopard in pride and the skin of a lion split clean through. I gathered the Arab assembly, the mightiest of them, a gathering that fells the most powerful of oppressors. It demolishes the pillars of mountains in utter demolition — Tamim was a slave to our father; lions whose vigor of attack was curly-haired, and we were an army for the Prophet. On the day of Buzakhat they came to the watering place — and I compare it to Badr and compare it to Uhud. And Tayy clothed me in a mantle of glory, so that I boasted and I routed the slave.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: skin of a leopard; courage and ferocity
a lion, a fierce warrior
he splits, cleaves
the strongest, the most powerful
gushing, overflowing with vigor
a mantle, a robe of honor
a place name, site of a famous battle in the Ridda wars

context: A boasting (fakhr) poem in rajaz meter where Abu Tammam celebrates his tribal lineage from Tayy and their heroic deeds, comparing the battle of Buzakhat to the Prophet's battles of Badr and Uhud in terms of importance.

titleEn: She Appeared as the Rope of Separation Tightened

verses.ajuz: وقد سهل التوديع ما وعر الهجر
خليء وما يخلو له من جوى صدر
إذا الشمس لم تغرب فلا طلع البدر
على الصدر إلا أن صائغها الشفر

verses.sadr: وما الدمع ثان عزمتي ولو أنها
verses.ajuz: سقى خدها من كل عين لها نهر
بحزم له في كل مظلمة فجر

verses.sadr: وصارعت عن مصر رجائي ولم يكن
verses.ajuz: ليصرع عزمي غير ما صرعت مصر
من الهم لم يفرغ على زبره قطر
فتى وافر الأخلاق ليس له وفر
على متنها والبر من آله بحر

verses.sadr: وما الققر بالبيد القواء بل التي
verses.ajuz: نبت بي وفيها ساكنوها هي القفر !
فأحج به أن ينجلي ولها القمر

verses.sadr: فإن كان ذنبي أن أحسن مطلبي
verses.ajuz: أساء ففي سوء القضاء لي الغدر

verses.sadr: قضاء الذي ما زال في يده الغنى
verses.ajuz: ثنى غرب آمالي وفي يدي الفقر

verses.sadr: رضيت وهل أرضى إذا كان مسخطي
verses.ajuz: من الأمر ما فيه رضا من له الأمر !

verses.sadr: أبى لي بحر الغوث أن أرأم التي
وهل خاب من جذماه في ضنء طيىء

verses.ajuz: إذا نجمت ذلت لها الأنجم الزهر

verses.sadr: لنا جوهر لو خالط الأرض أصبحت

verses.ajuz: صغت أذن للمجد ليس بها وقر
مدى اللين الا أن أعراضنا صخر
ولا نسب يدنيه منا ولا صهر

verses.sadr: اذا زينة الدنيا من المال أعرضت
وكور اليتامى في السنين فمن نبا
verses.ajuz: بفرخ له وكر فنحن له وكر

verses.sadr: أبى قدرنا في الجود إلا نباهة

verses.ajuz: عوان لهذا الناس وهو لنا بكر

verses.sadr: جرى حاتم في حلبة منه لو جرى
verses.ajuz: بها القطر شأوا قيل أيهما القطر !

verses.sadr: فتى ذخر الدنيا أناس فلم يزل
verses.ajuz: لها باذلا فانظر لمن بقي الذخر !

verses.sadr: فمن شاء فليفخر بما شاء من ندى

verses.ajuz: اذا نطقوا في مشهد خرس الدهر

verses.sadr: على كل طرف يحسر الطرف سابح
طوى بطنها الإسآد حتى لو أنه
verses.ajuz: بدا لك ما شككت في أنه ظهر
بني أسد إن كان ينفعك الخبر
لنا خطوة في أرضها ولهم فتر

verses.sadr: مساع يضل الشعر في كنه وصفها

translationEn: She appeared as the rope of separation was tightly twisted, and the farewell had been made easy by what the hardship of parting had made rough. She wept over him with the tears that the days of her breast had made him weep — one who is empty yet whose breast is never empty of burning passion. She said, 'Will you forget the full moon?' I said in feigned composure, 'When the sun does not set, the full moon does not rise.' She then displayed pearls of tears strung on her breast, but their jeweler was her eyelashes. Tears do not shake my resolve, even if each of her eyes were a river watering her cheek. I gathered the rays of my judgment and branded it with a resoluteness that has a dawn in every darkness. I wrestled my hopes away from Egypt — and nothing could have thrown my resolve except what Egypt threw. I leveled a dam like Gog's barrier before it, from grief — rain did not pour over its heights. Mounted on a swift camel that had lost the fullness of its flesh, I am a youth of perfect character but without wealth. How many a desolate wilderness I have loved, traversing its back, while the land from its kin was a sea. The true desert is not the empty wasteland; rather, it is the place that sent me away — and its inhabitants make it the desert! Whoever gambles with the days for their fruits, it is all the more fitting that the moon shine clear for them. If my fault is that I excel in my seeking, and that is an offense, then in the ill decree the betrayal falls on me. The decree of One in whose hand wealth has always been turned my hopes' sharpness while poverty was in my hand. I was resigned — but how can I be resigned when what provokes me is the very thing that contains the satisfaction of the One who commands? So I made my days bitter with a patience whose outcomes were sweet — and patience is true to its name, for it is bitter. The sea of rescue refused to let me suckle from what I have cursed, and lineage resembles lineage. Has anyone who traced his descent to the noblemen of Tayy, to Adiyy the two Adiyys, al-Qalmas or Amr, ever been disappointed? We have brilliant distinguished marks of Zaydi and Addid descent — when they rise like stars, the brightest stars bow to them. We have an essence that, had it mingled with the earth, its plains and heights would have turned to gold. Jadila and al-Ghawth, to whom the ear of glory has leaned — an ear with no deafness. Our stations are endowed with forbearance and wisdom; our youths are sages and our white-haired are learned scholars. We have softened hands with giving until they exceeded the limit of softness, yet our honor is rock. It is as if our gifts are kin to whoever comes, though no lineage or marriage brings him near to us. When the world's adornment of wealth turns away, what is more beautiful to us than praise and gratitude. He shelters the orphans in years of drought — whoever has a nestling with a nest, we are a nest for him. Our worth in generosity refuses anything but renown, so money has no worth to us ever. Let whoever wishes compete in generosity — for others it is a tame thing, but for us it is virgin. Hatim ran in its arena — had rain run in it a single stretch, one would ask: which of the two is the rain? A youth for whom people stored the world's wealth, yet he never ceased to lavish it — see then for whom the treasure remains! Whoever wishes may boast of whatever bounty he pleases, for no living tribe other than us has that boast. We gathered glory through generosity after it had dispersed, gathering it to us as the month gathers the days. By our valor, the clouds of death dropped their burden upon Najd — dark and turbid clouds — with every armored warrior whose chest is the target of lances, when entrails are ablaze and the liver swells. Escorted by the sons of death to battle, they are escorted by patience, escorted by victory. Warriors while other warriors linger at the battlefield, their lances red and their faces yellow. With cavalry in which Zayd al-Khayl has horsemen — when they speak at a great assembly time falls silent. On every swift horse that dims the eye, a swimmer — and a swimming mare whose swimming is a gallop. Night-journeying has folded her belly so that if it appeared to you, you would not doubt it was her back. A Dhabba mare that never reminds herself of what lies behind as long as a target lies ahead. Though enemies curse the evil of her morning's onset, neither wolf nor vulture can repay her gratitude. Through her, Qays ibn Aylan and al-Fazr came to know their true worth after ignorance of it. Taghlib met in her a conqueror of every conqueror, and Bakr found in our war a full-grown, first-time combatant. You know well how our lions have left the Banu Asad — if the news is of any use to you. Our unfair partition in Najd and its people: for us a step in their land and for them a span. Deeds that poetry loses itself in trying to describe fully — and yet poetry finds its way only to the least of them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: tightly twisted, said of a rope
pearls; used metaphorically for tears
Hatim al-Tayy, the legendary pre-Islamic figure of generosity
armored warriors, champions in battle
night-journeying, continuous travel
our valor, our courage in battle
a swift she-camel

context: A grand qasida in al-tawil meter that opens with a nasib (love prelude) lamenting departure, transitions through the poet's personal hardships and tribal pride, and culminates in an extended madih (praise) of his tribe Tayy and their legendary generosity exceeding even Hatim al-Tayy.

titleAr: ألا صنع البين الذي هو صانع
titleEn: Separation Did What Separation Does

verses.sadr: ألا صنع البين الذي هو صانع
verses.ajuz: فإن تك مجزاعا فما البين جازع

verses.sadr: هو الربيع من اسماء والعام رابع
verses.ajuz: له بلوى خبت فهل أنت رابع

verses.sadr: ألا إن صبري من عزائي بلاقع

verses.ajuz: إلى الغيث حتى جادها وهو هامع

verses.sadr: لئن كان أمسى شمل وحشيك جامعا
verses.ajuz: لقد كان لي شمل بأنسك جامع

verses.sadr: أسيء على الدهر الثناء فقد قضى
أبو منزل الهم الذي لو بغى القرى
verses.ajuz: لدى حاتم لم يقره وهو طائع

verses.sadr: له همم ما إن تزال سيوفها
ألا إن نفس الشعر ماتت وإن يكن
ترقت مناه طود عز لو ارتقت
verses.ajuz: به الريح فترا لانثنت وهي ظالع

verses.sadr: أنا ابن الذين استرضع الجود فيهم
سما بي أوس في السماء وحاتم
فأي يد في المحل مدت فلم يكن

verses.ajuz: لأيقنت أن الرزق في الأرض واسع

verses.sadr: رياح كريح العنبر الغض في الندى

verses.ajuz: كشفت قناع الشعر عن حر وجهه

verses.sadr: فيدنو إليها ذو الحجى وهو شاسع

translationEn: Behold, separation has done what separation does — if you are one who grieves, separation itself does not grieve. He is the spring to Asma, and this is the fourth year of his trial in the burnt wasteland — are you then a fourth? Indeed, my patience from my resolves is a barren wasteland, on the evening when the empty abodes stirred my longing. As if the white clouds had concealed beneath them a beloved, so their tears never cease. Hills whose meadows the east wind interceded for before the rain until it poured down on them, pouring in torrents. The face of the forenoon became for them a scene of smiling, and the damp flank of night became for them a bed. It clothed you in blossoms — a pure yellow, a brilliant white, a radiant red. Even if tonight the community of your wild creatures is united, there was a time when the community of your companions was united around me. I speak ill of time in praise, for it has judged against me with the injustice of its continuous turning. Does it crush us like the crushing of date-pits while it is solid, and devour us like locusts' devouring while it is famished? Whenever time lodges its load in my dwelling, I startle it in its flock as it grazes. Father of the dwelling of grief — one whom Hatim himself, were he to seek hospitality, would not have entertained even while willing. When the nights set upon him with disaster they tear themselves apart on him while he is armored with patience. When a calamity charges him one day, it meets its own points while he is armored with patience. He has resolves whose swords never cease to be keen-edged if only they had things to cut. Behold, the very soul of poetry has died — and if death's doom has not yet reached it, it is in its death-throes. I shall mourn the rhymes with rhymes, for they rightfully lament over it. The guardian of the shades of manliness is abandoned, and the preserver of the days of generosity is lost! A howler howled, yet glory stands between me and him with a barrier before me and a buttressing pillar. His ambitions climbed the mountain of might — had the wind climbed it even a hand's span it would have turned back limping. I am the son of those among whom generosity was suckled and named, both as a youth and as a mature man. Aws in the sky has raised me high, as have Hatim, Zayd al-Qana, the two Athrams, and Rafi. And Iyas was what Iyas was, and Arif and Haritha — the most loyal of all creatures and in their deeds. Stars that rise and mountains that tower, rains that pour and floods that surge.
