They passed away, yet noble deeds were to them like religious rites, for all they bequeathed them. What hand stretched out in time of drought that was not a bountiful south wind, with floods that have no resting place? They entrusted beneficence as a well-guarded deposit — what belongs to us has been squandered, yet the deposits are never lost with us. Glorious lords — had you seen the excess from their open hands, you would have been certain that provision on earth is abundant. When the spirits of their generosity flutter with giving, bounty drives them on, and aspirations inhale them. Winds like the wind of fresh ambergris in the dew, yet on the day of meeting they are howling gales. Behind them in earnest is a determined wrestling; they rose with fertile south winds as if they were south winds and north winds with no resting place. I have unveiled poetry's veil from the noble face of her visage and startled her from her nest while she was perched. With a glory that whoever hears it sees it — and a wise man draws near to it even from far away, wishing fervently that the members of his body were ears yearning to hear it when it is recited.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: empty, deserted places; barren wastelands
pouring, gushing (of rain)
young locusts that cannot yet fly
howling gales, violent winds
glorious lords, noble and generous men
aspirations, desires, ambitions
limping, lame

context: A grand multi-section qasida opening with a nasib on the abandoned dwelling, moving to reflection on time and patience, then lamenting the death of poetry itself, and culminating in a genealogical madih of the poet's tribal ancestors from Tayy — Hatim, Aws, and others — praising their legendary generosity and valor.

titleEn: Tomorrow You Shall Die and Be Buried

verses.ajuz: ولا حول محتال ولا وجه مذهب
لقد قدر الأرزاق من ليس عادلا

verses.sadr: عن العدل بين الخلق فيما يقدر
verses.ajuz: فلا تأمن الدنيا وإن هي أقبلت
فما نم فيها الصفو يوما لأهله
وما لاح نجم لا ولا ذر شارق

verses.sadr: على الخلق إلا حبل عمرك يقصر

verses.ajuz: وشمر فقد أبدى لك الموت وجهه

verses.sadr: فيظهر عنه الطرف ما كان يستر
verses.ajuz: تذكر وفكر في الذي أنت صائر

verses.sadr: إليه غذا إن كنت ممن يفكر
verses.ajuz: فلا بد يوما أن تصير لحفرة

translationEn: And tomorrow in it you shall die and be buried — yet you fertilize hopes and await their offspring! Your lifespan is shorter than what you hope for. You circle around attaining what has already been provided for you, advancing toward it with hopes and retreating. This very morning's light announces your death to you, and its night announces your death too if you have any awareness. Your provision will not escape you — it is either hastened to come on a given day, or it is deferred — and there is no scheme for the schemer, no way out, and no measure to convey it but the One who decrees. Indeed, One who is not just between creatures has apportioned provisions as He decrees. Do not trust the world even if it turns toward you, for it has always betrayed and deceived. Serenity has never slept in it for its people, nor ease, except for as long as it takes to change. No star has appeared, nor has a sun risen over creatures, without the rope of your life shortening. Purify yourself and attach repentance to your sin today — perhaps if you purify yourself you shall be purified of it. Gird yourself, for death has shown you its face, and none attains salvation but one who girds himself. These nights herald your decay — they come in the evening and the days likewise come at dawn. Be sincere to the religion of God in your heart and your intention, for what you conceal will one day be revealed. A person may conceal his deed with words, but the eye reveals what was hidden. Remember, and reflect upon what you are heading toward tomorrow, if you are one who reflects — for inevitably one day you shall end up in a pit, folded within it until the Day of Resurrection.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: to fertilize, to impregnate; metaphor for nurturing hopes
to circle around, to hover
one who girds himself, the resolute and prepared
decay, decomposition after death
heralds, announcers
a pit, the grave
you are resurrected, raised up (Day of Judgment)

context: A zuhdiyya (ascetic poem) — a fragment whose opening verses are missing — meditating on the shortness of life, the inevitability of death, the unreliability of worldly provision, and the necessity of repentance and sincere devotion to God before it is too late.

titleAr: أرى ألفات قد خططن على راسي
titleEn: I See White Lines Drawn Upon My Head

verses.sadr: أرى ألفات قد خططن على راسي
وقد أمس من وصل الكواعب آيسا

translationEn: I see alif-letters drawn upon my head by the pens of white hair on parchment pages. If you ask me who draws these letters — it is the hand of the nights, drawing ink from my very breaths. In the hearts of the young women a shuddering has run for my white hair, after softness and intimacy. And I have come now to despair of union with young maidens — for the last of all hopes leads to despair.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the letter alif (ا); white hair strands resembling the letter
erased and re-used parchments
a shudder, a shiver of aversion
young women who are self-sufficient in beauty, charming maidens
young women with rounded breasts, maidens
one who has despaired, hopeless
white hair, old age

context: A brief meditative poem on aging, in which Abu Tammam uses the elegant conceit that the strands of white hair on his head are like alif-letters written by the pen of time, drawing ink from his own life-breaths. The poem concludes with resigned acceptance of old age's estrangement from youth and love.

titleAr: ألم يأن تركي لا علي ولا ليا
titleEn: Is It Not Time I Left Frivolity Behind

verses.sadr: ألم يأن تركي لا علي ولا ليا
verses.ajuz: وعزمي على ما فيه اصلاح حاليا

verses.sadr: وقد نال مني الشيب وابيض مفرقي

verses.ajuz: وآل ثمود بعد عاد بن عاديا

verses.sadr: فيا ليتني من بعد موتي ومبعثى
verses.ajuz: أكون رفاتا لا علي ولا ليا

verses.sadr: لما ساغ لي عذب من الماء بارد
verses.ajuz: ولا طاب لي عيش ولا زلت باكيا

verses.sadr: على إثر ما قد كان مني صبابة

verses.ajuz: وإن كنت لم أشرك بذي العرش ثانيا

translationEn: Is it not time to abandon what is neither to my benefit nor to my advantage, and to resolve upon what will reform my condition? White hair has taken hold of me, my parting has turned white, and a greyness in the back of my head has consumed my darkness. Conditions have changed me from what I used to know them — the nights in their first youth — for the nights are not as they were. I call out to the world and it does not answer me; I try to remain — yet how can I remain? The days never cease to trim my lifespan, beyond reckoning, not like my own counting. They erase my traces, wear out my newness, and at their earliest opportunity vacate my dwelling of my place — as they did before me to Tasm and Jurhum, and the house of Thamud after the clan of Ad son of Ad. I shall be left prostrate among my family as a funeral bier, and the heirs will take possession of my pure wealth. I say to my soul when it inclines with its craving toward thoughts that have given birth to desires: are not the nights robbers of my soul's blood, as they robbed the past generations before me? And will they not lodge me in a niche at the edge of a pit in which the stretch of further nights prolongs my dwelling — as they lodged Ham, Shem, and Japheth, and Noah, and whoever took up abode in Mecca? I have thus grown familiar with death, because I have seen the fates snatching away my life. Would that after my death and resurrection I could be mere dust — neither burdening another nor burdening myself! I fear my God, then I hope in His bounty — but my fear overpowers my hope. Were it not for my hope and my reliance upon the One who has singled me out alone with His grace, both as a mature man and as a youth, not even sweet cold water would have been pleasant to me, no life would have suited me, and I would not have ceased weeping. I store up piety to the utmost of my capacity, and in my guidance I ride against the current of my own desire — following upon what was once in me of passion, on nights when I was disobedient to God. For I am worthy of fearing and being mindful of God — even though I never associated any partner with the Lord of the Throne.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the parting of the hair, the crown of the head
the back of the head, the nape
extinct ancient Arab tribes, examples of vanished civilizations
dust, scattered remains after death
to snatch away, to cut off (life)
piety, God-consciousness, righteousness
intense passionate longing

context: A lengthy zuhdiyya (ascetic poem) in which Abu Tammam reflects on old age, the transience of life exemplified by vanished ancient peoples, the fear of death and judgment, and his sincere turn toward God. The poem ends with a declaration of monotheism and readiness for accountability.

titleEn: Separation Fed Me Colocynth's Bitter Brew

verses.sadr: ما حسرتي أن كدت أقضي إنما
نقل فؤدك حيث شئت من الهوى
كم منزل في الأرض يألفه الفتى

translationEn: Separation made me gulp down the brew of colocynth, and separation bereaved me even though I have not truly lost a child. My regret is not that I nearly died — my soul's regret is rather that I did not. Transfer your heart wherever you wish in love — love is only for the first beloved. How many a dwelling on earth does a young man grow fond of, yet his yearning is forever for the first dwelling.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the soaked brew of colocynth, an intensely bitter medicinal plant
bereaved me of a child, caused me to grieve a loss
regret, anguish, burning grief
the heart, the inner self
the first beloved
yearning, nostalgic longing
becomes attached to, grows fond of

context: A celebrated short ghazal containing some of Abu Tammam's most quoted verses. The third and fourth lines became proverbs: love is only truly felt for the first, and the heart's truest longing is always for its first home.

titleEn: A Visitor Who Stirred a Phantom
translationEn: A visitor visited me and stirred a phantom — were it not for him I would be in the worst state of all people. I delighted in a gazelle — though God forbid that that figure should truly be a gazelle! How can I hope for a meeting with one who dwells in Baghdad while I am in Egypt — truly I have hoped in vain! My desires presented him to my eyes, my thoughts, and my heart until I accepted the impossible. I see myself never ceasing to be the target of a nocturnal phantom — or until my body becomes a phantom itself!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a phantom, a night vision of the beloved
a gazelle; metaphor for a beautiful beloved
God forbid, far be it
the impossible
wishes, desires, hopes
a nightly visitor, one who comes by night
a target, a fixed object

context: A light ghazal playing on the motif of the nocturnal phantom (khayāl) of the beloved. Abu Tammam laments being in Egypt while his beloved is in Baghdad — a detail that situates the poem in his years of sojourn in Egypt.

titleEn: I Am Jealous of My Own Kiss
translationEn: I am jealous of you even on account of my own kiss, even if you grant me my wish. And I fear to see your cheek as a fixed target for the falling of gazes.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I am jealous, I feel possessive jealousy
my kiss
I fear, I am apprehensive
a fixed target, something set up
falling places, landing spots
eyes, eyeballs

context: A tiny but exquisite two-verse ghazal expressing extreme jealousy: the poet is jealous even of his own kisses and dreads that others' eyes will rest upon the beloved's cheek.

titleEn: Seeking My Death Through His Rejection
translationEn: He seeks my death through his rejection, while his face — unrivaled in beauty — is my sole occupation. His glances move swiftly among all people toward what he desires, as swift as an arrow.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: rejection, turning away, estrangement
unique in beauty, unrivaled in handsomeness
glances, sideways looks
swift, moving quickly
arrows

context: A concise two-verse ghazal in which the beloved is depicted as a master of swift, lethal glances — his very rejection is a pursuit of the lover's death, and his gaze moves like an arrow.

titleEn: Handsome Yet Unjust to His Lover
translationEn: Well-proportioned, yet his justice was not well-proportioned toward a lover whose infatuation with him has long endured. Is his charm the more beautiful, or his elegance? Is his face the more beautiful, or his mind? Look — whatever beauty you have seen in anyone else, all of it belongs to him alone. If Beauty itself were asked to make a wish, it would wish to be like him. What qualities has my master gathered — had only his delay not clouded their serenity!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: well-proportioned, balanced, handsome of figure
madness, infatuation, derangement from love
elegance, charm of extremities (fingers, eyes)
wit, elegance, grace
delay, procrastination in fulfilling a promise
purity, serenity, clearness

context: A playful ghazal cataloging the beloved's virtues with rhetorical questions, culminating in the witty hyperbole that even Beauty itself would wish to resemble him — marred only by his one flaw: keeping his lover waiting.

titleEn: How My Heart Was Humbled
translationEn: Misery to my heart — how it has been humbled, becoming a dwelling for sickness! I did not fear what has come to pass, and I was once carefree. I have wasted away until I see no shadow of myself in the mirror of the sun. May God pardon the one who wrongs me for what he has deemed lawful!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: misery, wretchedness, hardship
was humbled, was brought low
carefree, left alone, unburdened
I wasted away, I melted from grief
the mirror of the sun, the bright face of sunlight
pardon, forgiveness
deemed lawful, considered permissible

context: A short lyric of extreme wasting away from love, written in a truncated meter that enacts the fragmentation of the lover's self. The final verse is a prayer of forgiveness for the beloved who has wronged him.

titleEn: O Sickness of the Eyelid from My Beloved
translationEn: O sickness of the eyelid from my beloved — it has clothed me in the robe of illness! How many a heart-stricken lover have your two glances wrongfully killed! O you on whose account I am overcome by passion — you have drawn my doom close to my soul. You have drunk your fill of my blood — enough of your well-aimed arrows and darts!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the languor of the eyelid, heavy-lidded eyes of the beloved
the robe of illness; metaphor for love-sickness
one driven mad by love, one whose heart has been stolen
passionate love, obsessive infatuation
death, doom
you were quenched, you drank your fill
well-aimed, hitting the mark

context: A passionate short ghazal personifying the beloved's heavy-lidded glance as the source of the poet's illness. The arrows and blood imagery culminates in a plea to stop, as the beloved has already drunk enough of the poet's blood.

titleEn: My Eye Translates What My Tongue Cannot Say

verses.ajuz: فيظهر من وجدي الذي كنت أكتم

translationEn: My eye translates on behalf of my tongue in secret, revealing the passion I have been concealing. Is it not strange that one house contains both me and you, yet we are never alone and never speak? Lips gesture, eyebrows signal, glances are cast and a gaze offers greeting — while our tongues are prevented from expressing what we desire, and our eyes answer for us and understand!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: translates, interprets, speaks on behalf of
intense passion, love-grief
a signal with the eyebrow, a wink
casting sidelong glances, broken looks of longing
prevented, forbidden
gesture, sign

context: A refined ghazal on the theme of concealed love in a shared social space where lovers cannot speak openly. The eye becomes the eloquent translator of what the tongue is forbidden to say, communicating through gestures and glances.

titleAr: كيف بعدي لا ذقتم البين أنتم
titleEn: How Have You Fared Since My Departure

verses.sadr: كيف بعدي لا ذقتم البين أنتم
يا منى النفس إن قلبي وإن با
verses.ajuz: ن بي البين عندكم حيث كنتم

translationEn: How have you fared after me — may you never taste separation — tell me, since I parted from you and you parted from me! Are you as I knew you, or have the misfortunes of treacherous time changed you so that you have been faithless? O you who are my soul's desires — my heart, though separation parted me from you, is with you wherever you may be.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: separation, parting
I parted, I was separated
the treacherous, the deceitful (epithet of time)
the desires of the soul, the beloved
misfortunes, catastrophes

context: A short ghazal of parting in which the poet addresses distant companions or a beloved, asserting that though his body is separated, his heart remains with them. The treachery of time is invoked as the agent of separation.

titleAr: سلام على من لا يرد سلامي
titleEn: Greetings to One Who Does Not Return Them

verses.sadr: سلام على من لا يرد سلامي

translationEn: Greetings to one who does not return my greeting and who does not see me as worthy of conversation. And what would it cost him to answer one who greets him? It is not as though responding with a greeting would settle any obligation he owes me.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: greeting, salutation
returns, responds to
a fitting place for speech, worthy of being spoken to
an obligation, a responsibility, a bond of honor
is settled, is discharged

context: A compact two-verse ghazal expressing the bitterest form of the beloved's indifference: he will not even return a greeting. The second verse is a restrained reproach — even a mere salutation is refused, though it would cost nothing.

titleEn: Given the Finest Breath of Beauty

verses.sadr: من كان لم ير شمسا من سنا بشر

translationEn: You were given the most radiant of beauty's fragrances, and you surpassed the most fragrant of sweet scents. Beauty has been cast aside and perfume put to shame, and the houris of paradise have become, after God, your clients. Whoever has never seen a sun made of human radiance — we have seen it in Ali.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: breaths, wafts, fragrances
the most radiant, the most brilliant of them
the most fragrant, the most intense of them
cast aside, abandoned, disgraced
the houris, the beautiful maidens of paradise
radiance, brilliance, gleam

context: A short madih for a patron named Ali, employing hyperbolic praise: his beauty surpasses all earthly beauty and even that of the paradise houris, and seeing him is like seeing the sun made of human splendor.

titleEn: On Honor, Shame, and Character

verses.sadr: إذا ما رأس أهل البيت ولى
فلا والله ما في العيش خير

translationEn: If you emulate a base person in character, then you and the one you emulate are equal. I have seen that the free man avoids disgraceful acts, and faithfulness guards him from treachery. There is no hardship without an ease that will come after its severity. I have tested this time until trials and toil have benefited me. When the head of a household departs, people begin to show coldness toward them. A man lives well for as long as he feels shame, and a branch endures as long as its bark remains. By God, there is no good in life nor in the world when shame is gone. If you fear not the consequences of the nights and feel no shame, then do as you please. A base-acting man from a noble people will forever hear the howling of censure from among them.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you emulated, you ran alongside in character
a base, low person
shameful acts, disgraceful deeds
shame, modesty, sense of honor
the bark of a tree
howling; metaphor for constant censure
ease, prosperity, comfort after hardship

context: A wisdom poem (hikam) in al-wafir meter containing famous proverbial verses on character, honor, and shame. The verse 'A man lives well as long as he feels shame, and a branch endures as long as its bark remains' became one of the most quoted sayings in Arabic literary tradition.

titleEn: A Scathing Invective Against a Rival
translationEn: O Utayb, son of a vile and filthy woman — have you felt safe from my arrogance and my fury? I swear a solemn oath that you deserve your fate and the disgrace that is rightfully yours. Is your claim among the tribe of Kalb a broader scandal and more particular, or is your claim among the poets? What wonder — a hunter of invective through his own honor, yet his sanctuary is forever exposed! His poetry is no match for mine — let him die of rage — and this low creature is not my peer. How could he escape my talons in a land where my earth and my sky are spread wide, with the elders of Kahlan and the strongholds of Himyar like a flood both before me and behind? These are my paternal uncles who wrapped themselves in noble deeds — and these are my fathers!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: foul-smelling, vile; an insult to the opponent's mother
arrogance, pride, haughtiness
fury, excess, unbridled rage
talons, claws; metaphor for poetic attack
one of the two main branches of the Yemeni Arab tribes
ancient Yemeni tribe of great prestige
wrapped themselves (in turbans of nobility); achieved honor

context: A fierce hija (invective) against a poet named Utayb, combining personal insults with tribal boasting. Abu Tammam asserts the superiority of his poetry and the nobility of his lineage through the great Yemeni tribal confederations.

titleEn: I Was Told Utba Is the Poet of the Rabble

verses.ajuz: قد ضج من عودي ومن إبدائي

verses.sadr: ما كان جهلك تاركا لك غيه
ما الشمس أعجب حين تطلع للورى
إن كنت لست بمنته عن بذلها

translationEn: I was informed that Utba, the poet of the rabble, has been wailing from my returning and my appearing. When I was angered by verse, I satirized him and made his very nature the satire of my satire. Your ignorance was never going to leave you your aberration, even if you were to become the hen of al-Raqa. My forbearance toward the forbearing is unclouded, but death lies in my recklessness toward the reckless. How weak is one whose entire affair has become morning and evening a following of the worm among poets! I am amazed at people who have the forms of men yet have the private parts of women! God knows it is truly a calamity that has descended — especially upon poets. The sun rising in the west is no more astonishing than a poet who sells himself. If you are not going to stop offering yourself, then I am more entitled to it than the strangers!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the rabble, the riffraff, the common mob
poetry, verse
the hen of al-Raqa; a proverbial insult for a base person
death, doom, destruction
the hairy caterpillar; used as an insult for a vile poet
offering it, giving it cheaply; selling oneself

context: A continuation of Abu Tammam's satirical attacks on a rival poet named Utba (or Utayb), full of obscene insults typical of the hija genre. The poem escalates to accusations of effeminacy and moral degradation.

titleEn: Tell Abdun Where Has His Shame Gone
translationEn: Tell Abdun: where has that shame of yours gone? The disease of licentiousness is an incurable disease! For long you were in my estimation impregnable and guarded, as a robe is carefully preserved. Then you pushed me away without cause — so I am now equal to al-Mubaraki in your eyes. My sincere advisers told me — and it is a true saying — that to criticize one who was obscure is to praise him. They spoke truth: in invective there is an elevation for rabble-like peoples — so I have no satire for you.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: licentiousness, shamelessness, debauchery
an incurable disease
impregnable, well-protected, beyond reproach
preserved, protected, kept with care
pushed me aside, turned your flank away from me
the rabble, the lowest of people
obscure, unknown, of low station

context: An invective against a figure named Abdun, notable for its restrained conclusion: Abu Tammam withholds his full satirical power, arguing that satirizing an obscure person only raises that person's profile — so the ultimate contempt is to refuse to write hija at all.

titleEn: Utba the Most Cowardly of All Creation

verses.sadr: رميت بمن لو أن الجن ترمى

verses.ajuz: إذا ما كنت أسفل منه جنبا
إذا ما كان مثلك كان كلبا

translationEn: O Utba — you who blame most cowardly of all creatures — through your ignorance you have become a target for misfortune. You have been struck by one who, were the jinn to be struck by him, humankind would plunder them as spoils. If you compete with me you will find me a boulder for your head and dust for your mouth. You will find a fire-brand with every limb of his thrashing so violently it seems to have a heart of its own. A brother of the open wastelands who has sustained and destroyed camel-loads and their riders in its vast plains. He nearly made a new east for the East, and nearly made a new west for the West. You, meanwhile, spin the pivot of a superior millstone — yet you have never seen the pivot of that high millstone. You think you gain victory in every wrestling match with an equal when you are beneath him on your side. May my qasidas be bereaved of me if a day passes and I have not fulfilled my vow against you! And then you would be what you are — for one like me, when there exists one like you, becomes a dog.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the two weighty ones: humans and jinn
to compete with me, to challenge me in poetry
a boulder, a heavy stone
a fire-brand, glowing coal
open deserts, barren wastelands
the pivot of a millstone; a metaphor for the center of power
a vow, a solemn pledge

context: Another fierce invective against Utba in al-wafir meter, where Abu Tammam presents himself as a desert warrior of cosmic stature while reducing his opponent to an absurd figure who only wins when already beneath his opponent. The final verse is a crushing concluding insult.

titleEn: My Poetry Pursues You Wherever You Flee

verses.sadr: يا ابن أبي عاصم ولا عاصم
لو كنت من غرة الموالي إذن
verses.ajuz: لم تنث سوءا في غرة العرب

verses.sadr: أي مناد إلى الندى وإلى الهي
إن رمت تصديق ذاك يا أعور ال
لن يهدم الناس ما بقواأ بدا
verses.ajuz: ما قد بنوه من ذلك الحسب

translationEn: My poetry will find you wherever you flee in your seeking, even if you were to ascend the heavens by a rope. O son of Abu Asim — and there is no protector — woe to you from my force and my anger! Had you been from the vanguard of the client-tribes, you would not have spoken ill of the vanguard of the Arabs. What noble man would accept the insulting of the sons of Abd al-Karim, the noble and honorable chiefs? What caller to generosity and to valor has called out to them and not been answered? What youth among them has averted his face and not been struck on the morning of battle and not struck in return? What newborn who has seen their swords bared in war has not grown grey from fear? If you wish to verify this, O one-eyed false prophet, then observe them closely and do not melt away. People will never demolish what the noble ones have built, as long as they remain in view — that lineage of theirs. These bright stars are not like one who yesterday was a foundling in poetry and in lineage.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: force, power, might, assault
client-tribes, freedmen, non-Arab allies
noble chiefs, men of high standing
the noble, the excellent
the one-eyed false messiah; a severe insult
the bright stars; metaphor for noble ancestors
a foundling, one of unknown or false lineage

context: A madih and invective combined in al-munsarih meter, defending the noble family of Banu Abd al-Karim against a rival's attacks. Abu Tammam uses a series of rhetorical questions to praise the family's valor and generosity before concluding with a crushing dismissal of the opponent as a false claimant to both poetry and noble lineage.

titleAr: من بنو عامر من ابن الحباب
titleEn: Who Are the Sons of Amir?

verses.sadr: من بنو عامر من ابن الحباب
من طفيل من عامر ومن الحا
verses.ajuz: ث أم من عتيبة ابن شهاب !

verses.sadr: من غدت خيله على سرح شعري
يا عذارى الكلام صرتن من بع
قد جرى في متونهن من الإف

translationEn: Who are the sons of Amir? Who is Ibn al-Habbab? Who are the sons of Taghlib on the morning of al-Kilab? Who is Tufayl, who is Amir, who is al-Harith — or who is Utayba ibn Shihab? The only true lion, the crusher, is Abu al-Ashbal, the one who bars every thicket and every grove. It is he whose cavalry raided the pastures of my poetry while it grazed freely in my book. A raid that scorched the eyes of meanings and made the sanctities of literary propriety permissible. If you could see my speech held captive, you would yourself become captive to tears and sorrow. O virgins of speech, after me you have become captives sold among the desert Arabs! Fragrant breezes that reveal to the ear faces like the faces of young companions of equal age. Through their spines has run the water of camphor, like the water of youth itself. The censuring of Muhammad ibn Yazid for what has befallen him is not just. Leave him to enjoy among people my poetry and my odes — that is the easier path.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the lion — a word of strength and ferocity
the crusher, the one who seizes and breaks — epithet of the lion
thicket, dense undergrowth — a habitat of lions
grove, forest — another word for lion's habitat
virgins of speech — a metaphor for original, untouched poetic compositions
fragrant breezes, wafts of perfume
the watered pattern on a sword blade — used metaphorically for beauty and luster

context: A satirical poem in which Abu Tammam defends his patron Muhammad ibn Yazid against the disparagement of rival tribal claimants. The poem pivots between challenging their lineage claims and lamenting how his poetry has been seized and scattered — treating his verses as captive maidens, a striking metaphor for the violation of intellectual property.

titleEn: By Him Who Covered the Blessed with Disgrace

verses.ajuz: يغني على الأيام ركب بها ركبا

verses.sadr: إذا ما عصت من رامها وسما لها
verses.ajuz: أطاعت فتى عضبا يسوس حجا عضبا
ولم يدر أن الليث يفترس الكلبا
فكان به رفعا وكنت به نصبا !
إليك ومسرورا كأن قد رأى زبا
على ما بدا لي منه لم يفهم الضربا

verses.sadr: إذا كان وجه المرء يبسا فإنه
verses.ajuz: يقاسي عجانا لا امتراء به رطبا

translationEn: By Him who covered the Blessed One with a disgrace that will be sung by riders for days to come — Miqran has spent his time scratching his honor against rhymes of poetry that, if a mangy person attempted them, they would become him. Whenever those rhymes resist one who tries them and aspires high, they obey the sharp-tongued youth who drives his arguments with precision. He hoped that the lowness of his rank would save him, not knowing that a lion devours the dog. O Miqran, how many a peer have you met at a gathering, and he was raised nominative by it while you were put in the accusative! You see him, when you come to him, beaming at you and joyful, as if he had seen a treasure pit. His thinking runs thick; if I struck him for what I have perceived of his nature, he would not understand the blow. When the face of a man is dried out, it is because he endures a dry perineum — no doubt about it.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: disgrace, shameful event — a noun of deep dishonor
scratches his honor against — a vivid idiom for degrading one's reputation
mangy, scabrous — afflicted with mange (jarab)
sharp, cutting — referring to both wit and argument
grammatical terms: nominative (raised) and accusative (lowered) — used here for social elevation vs. humiliation
a treasure pit, a hidden cache of wealth
dry, hardened — describing a hardened, unfeeling face

context: A cutting satire against a figure named Miqran, ridiculed for his intellectual dullness, low social status, and moral bankruptcy. Abu Tammam cleverly deploys grammatical terminology (rafa'/nasb) to mock Miqran's social inferiority, a hallmark of his witty hija style.

titleEn: Honour Overflowed and Lineage Dried Up

verses.sadr: هب من له شيء يريد حجابه
verses.ajuz: ما بال لا شيء عليه حجاب ؟ !

verses.sadr: ما إن سمعت ولا أراني سامعا
ما كنت أدري لا دريت - بأنه

verses.ajuz: لك لم يقولوا قم فأنت مصاب

translationEn: The veil overflowed while honors dried up, and nobility and refinement were uprooted. It was as if the Day of Resurrection had surprised them — no lineages between them, no bonds. O Muways, your apology seeking my forgiveness is useless, for after rebuke there is no further punishment. Grant that whoever has something wants to veil it — why does nothing have a veil over it? I have never heard, nor do I think I ever will hear, of a desert that has a gate over it! He who has lost all shame — his face serves as its own doorkeeper without any gatekeeper. My inner whispering never ceased deceiving my reason until it hoped for rain with no clouds. I did not know — and may you never know — that a mirage runs through the courtyards of houses. How strange is the people who hear my praises of you yet do not say: Rise, for you are afflicted! They dismissed the description of Musaylima the Liar, but they erred and went astray — nay, it is I who am the liar. I tore apart my religion, then covered myself with repentance — so I am the one who confesses his sin and repents!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the veil, the face-covering — here used metaphorically for shame or dignity
honors, noble deeds counted to one's credit — inherited and personal distinction
uprooted, torn out by the roots — from جثث, a powerful image of total destruction
inner whispering, obsessive thoughts, self-delusion
mirage — the illusion of water in the desert, used metaphorically for false hope
Musaylima the Liar — the infamous false prophet; Abu Tammam uses his name ironically
the one who repents repeatedly, the penitent — self-deprecating use of a divine epithet

context: A poem combining self-satire and invective against a certain Muways, with sharp wit about the nature of shamelessness and self-deception. Abu Tammam ends with a striking self-deprecating declaration — comparing himself to Musaylima the Liar — a bold rhetorical move common in his ironic style.

titleEn: Fire, Disgrace, and Ruin

verses.sadr: أحلى وأعذب من سيب تجود به
verses.ajuz: ولن تجود به يا كلب يا كلب !

verses.sadr: كذبتم ، ليس ينبو من له حسب
verses.ajuz: ومن له أدب عمن له أدب
فيكم ، وفي عجبي من لومكم عجب

verses.sadr: يا أكثر الناس وعدا حشوه خلف

translationEn: Fire, shame, hateful things, and ruin — death, crucifixion, spear-shafts, and wood — all of these are sweeter and more pleasant than a gift you would give generously. But you will never give generously, O dog, O dog! You caused me to complain, and when I complained of you, you grew angry — may that wrath and anger last forever! O sons of Luhay'a, what have I to do with you when the land is wide with opportunities to praise and move about? The stubbornness I have toward you resembles nothing so much as your own stubbornness in claiming to be Arabs! You lie: one of breeding and refinement does not turn away from another of breeding and refinement. I am a man of wonders about you that I keep repeating, and in my wonder at your blame there is wonder upon wonder. Ayyash, you have no desire for a noble deed, and no noble deed has any desire for a lowly person. O you who make the most promises — all of them broken — and who say the most — all of it lies. You spent the day plundering the world and its glitter, while your own honor was being plundered and ruined!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruin, destruction, perdition — total loss
spear-shafts, flexible lances — the wood of war
a spontaneous gift, free-flowing bounty
stubbornness, insistence, obstinacy
a noble deed, an act worthy of the generous
to turn away, to drift apart — said of a person who distances himself
to be plundered, to be seized by looting — passive form of انتهاب

context: A fierce hija directed at the Banu Luhay'a clan and a figure named Ayyash, attacking their stinginess, false promises, and baseless claims to Arab nobility. The opening catalogue of horrors — fire, death, crucifixion — declared sweeter than the addressee's gift, is a masterstroke of satirical hyperbole characteristic of Abu Tammam.

titleEn: O Yusuf, You Have Brought a Wonder

verses.sadr: أما لو أن جهلك كان علما

translationEn: O Yusuf, you have come with an astounding wonder — you left the people in suspicious doubt! I have heard of every weighty catastrophe, but I have never heard of a lamp that is illiterate! Had your ignorance been knowledge, you would have penetrated into the knowledge of the unseen! You have no hand in what is rare, but your reaching for the rare is itself the rare thing! If the graves were dug up for Zuhayr, he would cry out with wailing and lamentation — when did his rhymes ever depend on the interpretation of Hippocrates the physician? How could they, when poetry has always had a water that makes the sweet basil of hearts shimmer over it! Step back from the heights of reason until you can at least orient yourself toward what is near. I see your oppression of me as justice and equity, and my only offense against you is the expiation of sins!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a heavy catastrophe, an overwhelming calamity — nāʾid means weighty, grave
a lamp, a light — here used ironically of an illiterate person pretending to illuminate
the rare, the unusual; rare vocabulary — a technical term in Arabic philology
Zuhayr ibn Abi Sulma — the revered pre-Islamic poet, evoked as the standard of true poetry
wailing and sobbing — the sounds of grief at what has been done to poetry
the sweet basil of hearts — a fragrant metaphor for the delight poetry brings
expiation of sins — used ironically: Abu Tammam's only fault is excessive praise of Yusuf

context: A satirical poem mocking a certain Yusuf who apparently tried to explain or interpret poetry through medical or scientific commentary, perhaps citing Hippocrates. Abu Tammam defends the autonomy of poetry against pedantic interpretation, invoking the pre-Islamic master Zuhayr as the gold standard.

titleEn: I Have Exhausted My Trials Among People

verses.sadr: ما كنت أول آخر في قدره

verses.ajuz: من أن تراه زاهدا في راغب

verses.sadr: خذ من غدي الجائي بخزيك ضعف ما

verses.ajuz: مني فأيري في حر أم الكاذب !

translationEn: I have exhausted my trials among people and tested them with the probing paths of my ways. I have trotted through the days until both flanks of my hump wore thin and bent toward my withers. Taking upon myself the paths of ambition, seeking in them and from them the sustenance prize of the victor. My two affairs — good and evil — know that they are both collars around the neck of overcoming fate. Let an enemy get from an enemy what they may; it is only a companion who pardons and forgives another companion. Satire was absent, and now its finest form has returned through you — so congratulate yourself, O Musa, on the return of the absent! Do not stun me with your curtain, for I am quick of instinct and fully aware of your devious ways. Do not strain yourself — spare your face the expense of a gatekeeper where there is no benefit. I was not the first latecoming man of high rank who grew wealthy and then fell short of what was obligatory. There is no witness more shameful for a man who denies his baseness than to be seen withholding from one who desires his favor. Take from my tomorrow's coming disgrace of yours double what you gave me in the beginning of yesterday gone by. Do not deprive travelers on your account of stray-wandering companions who stand in the place of provisions. You claimed you are my destroyer and my deliverer from myself — well, my curse upon the mother of the liar!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: I have worn out, exhausted — from نضو: to thin an animal by overworking it
I trotted at a steady pace — from ذمل: a sustained travel gait
the hump of a camel — used as a metaphor for the poet's nobility and strength
the withers, the hump's forward slope — an image of physical and spiritual wear
aspirations, lofty ambitions — singular: مطمح
spontaneous wit, quick intuition — the ability to respond instantly and aptly
my devious paths, indirect schemes — from وارب: to act obliquely

context: A complex poem mixing complaint, satire, and self-assertion directed at a figure named Musa. Abu Tammam draws on the extended metaphor of an exhausted camel to describe his own wearied quest for patronage, then shifts to biting attack on Musa's stinginess and deceitfulness. The closing curse is characteristically blunt.

titleAr: إمرأة مقران ماتت بعد ما شابا
titleEn: Miqran's Wife Died After He Aged

verses.sadr: إمرأة مقران ماتت بعد ما شابا
لم يبق خلق بباب الشام نعرفه
verses.ajuz: بالفتك مذ هلكت إلا وقد تابا ! !

verses.sadr: يا نكبة هشمت أنف السرور به

translationEn: Miqran's wife died after he had already grown old — and thus the market stalls tasted the young men and the aloes. No creature that we know of at the gate of Syria for its licentiousness has remained since her death without having repented! What a calamity that broke the nose of joy in him, and what a death that left bachelors still bachelors!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: she tasted, she tried — from حسّ: to sense or taste
the market stalls, goods for sale
the aloes plant — bitter plant often associated with bitter experiences or youthful indiscretion
audacity in wrongdoing, brazen licentiousness
calamity, catastrophe, disaster — singular
broke the nose of joy — idiom: utterly humiliated or destroyed joy
bachelors, unmarried men — the irony being she served as their entertainment

context: A satirical mock-elegy on the death of Miqran's wife, continuing Abu Tammam's long satirical campaign against Miqran. The poem subverts the ritha genre by turning the death into an occasion for bawdy mockery rather than genuine grief, noting that the depraved woman's death forced even her clients to repent.

titleEn: Companions, Halt — I Have Had Enough of You

verses.ajuz: عذر الفتى إن هام أو حبا

verses.sadr: في حيث تلقى الرمح يشرع في

translationEn: Companions, halt — I have had enough of you as companions. Fulfill for us from her quarter a vow. A dwelling whose decay was spread by time as if a knowing hand scattered books across it. Where are those who once dwelt in her courtyards while fate poured out its waters in streams? When in it was every slender, bashful maiden — an excuse for the young man who fell in love or crawled in longing. The belt hung loose on her while her limbs filled the anklet and the gold bracelet. When she swayed in her walk, you would think her a supple, moist branch toyed with by the east wind. An affliction — a soft, pampered one — set itself up for him, making his gazing eye itself a target for affliction. She aimed at him before the parting and left him neither liver nor heart. Say to al-Juludy — whose hand carried off the wealth of his soldiers in scattered portions — God gave you defeat when the causes of ruin pulled you with a mighty pull. You faced heroes who urged lean, hollow-backed warhorses toward the tight confines of battle. You descended among their midst arrogantly, and they served you with stabbing and with striking, as a guest — but one to whom no one said: welcome to your lodging, and no: freely enter! Where spears are plunged into the fat of the kidneys and the sharp blade is filled with rage. With the horses galloping to the right and to the left, and death covering east and west. The white swords gleaming in their hands at the early light of forenoon, such that you would take them for shooting stars. Then your two eyes turned and saw what filled the entrails with terror. And you were distracted from the tanning of hides by what spread calamity and draped catastrophe. And a cavalry raided, such that had you stood firm against them, they would have plundered your very soul in the fray. Far from it — when you saw them, they clothed you in the garment of effort and anguish. And you took them for black lions, or for mange-stricken bull camels charging. From the tribe of Adnan and their brothers of Qahtan — neither deviating nor stumbling. And you saw that riding them as you wished was hard, and that pressing their backbone was solid. And you cast your eye gazing, and it saw in every land a fire of war kindled. And you were saved by the pitch-black night, which cast upon you its veils of darkness. So you traveled at night, covering the wasteland, cutting across its firm and flat ground with your white camels. And you left your soldiers for the spears as slaughter-meat, with the swords pulling at their skulls. Killed and captured in iron at once, awaiting death and crucifixion. So give thanks to the favors of a night that granted you survival and your cavalry rode it as a steed. Nay — you can never fully render its gratitude until you make it a god for you!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a vow, a solemn pledge — fulfilling a vow at the ruins of the beloved's camp
a slender, bashful maiden of delicate beauty — archaic terms of feminine ideal
the sash, the ornamental belt worn across the chest and waist
lean, gaunt warhorses — horses that have shed excess weight for battle
horses/omens going right (fortunate) and left (ill-fated) — traditional Arabic omen-reading
the white (swords) — common metonymy for gleaming swords in battle poetry
white or tawny camels — the finest riding camels
a lord, a god — hyperbolic gratitude: you can never thank the night enough

context: A long qasida opening with nasib (romantic prelude at ruined campsite) before pivoting to a satirical-invective section against al-Juludy, a military commander who fled battle and abandoned his troops. The poem is a masterwork of Abu Tammam's technique of blending ghazal nostalgia with devastating military satire, spanning 30 verses.

titleEn: Your First Justice in My View
translationEn: The first act of justice from you, in my view, is that you do not accept the word of a liar. I praised you with lies, and you rewarded me with stinginess — you have been fair, O Muttalib!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: you have been just, you have given what is due — from الإنصاف: fairness
with miserliness, with stinginess
proper name: al-Muttalib — the mocked addressee
you recompensed me, you rewarded me in return
justice, equity — used with bitter irony here

context: A short epigrammatic hija (two verses only) that perfectly encapsulates Abu Tammam's wit: the poet admits he praised al-Muttalib with false flattery, and congratulates him ironically for repaying lies with miserliness — the just exchange of two frauds. A gem of compressed satirical logic.

titleEn: O Abd Allah, Leave Off Your Regrets
translationEn: O Abd Allah, leave off your 'if only' and 'would that' — for you have become, O wretch, a dead man. You used to strut about because of two misers until you were cast down from the sky just as you yourself had cast others down. Softened once, and by the measure of Awn's assistance — so the face of Awn was blackened while you were plastered with tar. And you today are in tremendous disgrace — so how will you be tomorrow when you grow a beard?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: 'if only' and 'would that' — expressions of futile regret and wishful thinking
two misers — referring to patrons renowned for stinginess
to strut, to be emboldened, to swagger in confidence
you were coated, plastered — from طَلَى: to smear or cover a surface
tremendous disgrace, great shame
when you grow a beard — from لحية: beard; implies further degradation with age

context: A brief satirical poem mocking Abd Allah for his association with two notorious misers. The poem plays on the imagery of being cast from the sky and being tarred — a vivid metaphor for public humiliation. The closing question about what tomorrow brings when he grows a beard adds a darkly comic dimension.

titleEn: O Wife of the Wretch Miqran

verses.sadr: لو كان أحصن بابه أو داره

translationEn: O wife of the wretch Miqran, she whose death loomed great over those who frequented her! The grave has been left to a gazelle whom — so it is said — her private moments were sweet! She left upon the wretch a group of children like fledglings whose mothers have perished. Had he but secured his door or his house, his sons and daughters with him would have been fewer! When lands are repeatedly swept by floods across their courtyards, the open space fills up with vegetation! He pretended to sleep when her brothers visited her, and woke alert when her sisters came! His wife's affairs prevailed over him until we thought he was her wife!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: those who knock at the door, those who frequent a place — here with lewd implication
a gazelle — a common term of feminine beauty, here used satirically
her private moments, her seclusions — with obvious double meaning
whose mothers have been cut off, died — from خَرَم: to cut through
pretending to sleep, feigning slumber — showing deliberate ignorance
alert, wide awake — the ironic contrast with the previous word
her affairs prevailed over him, she dominated him completely

context: A continuation of Abu Tammam's satirical campaign against Miqran, now directing mockery at his dead wife and at Miqran himself for his cuckoldry and powerlessness. The poem escalates to a brutal final couplet declaring that his wife so dominated him that he might as well have been married to her. Bawdy satire at its sharpest.

titleEn: Hold On — Brace for My Fury's Impact

verses.sadr: دع ما مضى واستأنف العدد الذي
يا ابن الخبيثة لا تعرض صخرة
ما إن سمعت ولا أراني سامعا
ما إن تزال لهم مراود ساسم

translationEn: Hold on — nay, brace yourself for the impact of my fury! For you will surely tire of my sweetness and my bitter salt! Leave what is past and start fresh the counting you have wasted, O counter of the waves! My enmity has long been mixed and I shall now make you inhale it unmixed. O son of a vile woman, do not oppose a deaf boulder from my glory with a face of glass. You have become hollow of mind — so be branded with a hot iron that exposes the most tenacious in the ripening. I have never heard, nor do I think I shall hear until death, of a shining poet. He whose head was crowned, let him hold back the parted hair that stands in the crown's place for him. Time was stubborn with him, so his flanks swaggered in the gait of a partnership in a smooth-gaited mule. For a man in the Quran there are four wives — and for that woman there are four husbands! White women among white vessels circling black ones amid deep black vessels of dense weave and sewn sides. Kohl-sticks of ebony still remain for them, penetrating deep into ivory kohl-pots!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my rage, my excited fury — from هيج: violent arousal of emotion
my bitter salt, my biting sharpness — أجاج: intensely bitter or salty water
grew stale from delay, became distasteful through waiting — said of held-back anger
I shall make you sniff it as a nasal remedy — to force something unpleasant into the nose
a branding iron — used metaphorically for sharp, revealing wit
a lamp, illuminating light — here used for a brilliant, guiding poet
walked with a smooth swaggering gait — from هملجة: the amble of a mule

context: A violently satirical hija full of personal insults and bawdy insinuations. Abu Tammam attacks the unnamed addressee's intellectual hollowness, debauched wife, and base lineage. The closing verses with Quranic allusion to the four wives — turned on its head to mock a promiscuous woman — exemplifies Abu Tammam's daring use of sacred texts in satire.

titleEn: A Mind That Profanes Idleness's Sanctuary

verses.ajuz: به في المجد يغدو أو يروح

translationEn: A reason that profanes the sanctuary of idleness, and a worth that begs from noble deeds. No wounded heart did a far-flung departure overturn, and no weeping eye. But rather a wild, boundless ambition — a dream that goes and returns with him in pursuit of glory. I shall rebuke Utba with rhymed poems that are equal to bitter aloes in their sharpness. They spend their nights traveling and their days recited as poems like the conquests of old are recited. The sons of Abd al-Karim are stars of glory that one sees always gleaming in Tayy. You have no sound lineage among them to increase their number by your addition, nor sound reason. If satire of them is a reward, then tell me — for whom was praise created? Do you hate the refined essence of the Arabs when not even a pure mawla hates them? And you have no device against them that would avail you — well yes, one: die, and find rest!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: reason, mind, understanding — also: restraint, rational control
the sanctuary of idleness — metaphor for the protected domain of sloth
one who begs for generosity, a supplicant for noble deeds
the crushed colocynth — bitter medicinal plant used as metaphor for sharp, painful verse
traveling, circulating widely — said of famous poems that travel from mouth to mouth
the conquests — the great military victories recited as historical poetry
the refined essence of the Arabs — the pure, noble core of Arab identity

context: A hija directed against a figure named Utba, contrasted with praise for Banu Abd al-Karim, stars of the Tayy tribe. The poem concludes with devastating logic: if satire of these noble men is the reward for the addressee, then praise was created for someone else — and the only useful thing the addressee can do is die.

titleEn: What Opinion or Sound Mind

verses.sadr: خلق الله لحية لك لو تح
سار في التيه عقل من ظن أني
ياحرونا في البخل قد وأبي بخ

verses.ajuz: ها ولو كنت في سفينة نوح

translationEn: What opinion and what sound mind has not made you fear my lucky omens and my ill omens? Your own soul lied to you when it told you that I would increase my shots and my wounded. God created a beard for you that, were it shaved, the price of woolen cloaks would not be known! Scatter it in the wind if you hope that my poetry in describing it will travel in the wind. A lost mind was his who thought that my praise of you moves along by wishful thinking. O you who are stubborn in miserliness — by my father, indeed! — you have been punished by one who is deaf and headstrong. With far reach and near meanings, and heavy of reason but light of spirit. His palm has filled the seas of rhymes for you at both the allusive and the explicit. By reason — you are not safe from its heights, even if you were in Noah's Ark!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my right-side omens and my left-side omens — the two directions of omen-reading in Arabian tradition
coarse woolen cloaks, hair-cloth garments — worn in asceticism or poverty
the trackless wilderness, straying in perdition
stubborn in miserliness — حرون: a horse that refuses to move, applied to stinginess
his palm filled to overflowing — from سجر: to fill a body of water
the allusive and the explicit — two rhetorical modes: indirect hint vs. direct statement
Noah's Ark — the symbol of ultimate safety; even in it you would not be safe from my wit

context: A satirical poem in which Abu Tammam mocks an unnamed stingy addressee, playing on the themes of omens, beards as signs of age and dignity, and the power of his satirical poetry to penetrate any refuge. The closing reference to Noah's Ark gives the poem a memorable hyperbolic finale.

titleAr: يا ابن تلك التي بحران لما
titleEn: O Son of That Woman in Harran

verses.sadr: يا ابن تلك التي بحران لما

verses.ajuz: طيت ما شئت من أداة النطاح !

verses.sadr: بخ بخ لم يدان جودك يا أز
كذت تدعى لو أن خلفك قدا
verses.ajuz: مك في الحرب يا حديا الرماح

translationEn: O son of that woman in Harran who, when she grew, sprouted the branches of fornication! Do not be frightened by the rams — for you have been given all the gear you desire for butting! You gave from the rear while the old woman gave from the front — so congratulations, you both made off with generosity! Bravo, bravo! Your generosity is unmatched, O Azhar Kab, and cannot be rivaled even by the rival of the winds. You would rightly be called a spearhead in war had your back supported you in battle. My bad opinion of him protected me from desire for him — so I placed the divorce before the marriage.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: Harran — an ancient city in the Jazira region, here used to ground the insult geographically
branches of fornication — a metaphor for illegitimate children born of licentiousness
rams — used as a double-entendre for aggressive masculine rivals
the equipment for butting — literal: ram gear; figurative: the passive role in debauchery
the back, the rear — a euphemism in bawdy satirical contexts
an exclamation of admiration — here deployed with heavy irony
kite of the spears — a compound insult: rapacious but also a prey-scavenger

context: A thoroughly obscene hija combining insults about the addressee's mother's promiscuity in Harran, his own passive role in debauchery, and mockery of his boasts about military valor. The final line — divorcing before marrying — is a brilliant epigrammatic closure: Abu Tammam's better judgment protected him from any attachment to such a person.

titleAr: قلبت أمري في بدء وفي عقب
titleEn: I Turned My Affairs in Beginning and End

verses.sadr: قلبت أمري في بدء وفي عقب
فما فتحت فمي إلا كعمت فمي
verses.ajuz: ولا مددت يدي إلا رددت يدي !

verses.sadr: لا ذنب لي غير ما سيرت من غرر
verses.ajuz: شرقا وغربا وما أحكمت من عقدي
فكر يجول مجال الروح في الجسد

verses.sadr: إذا دجاها أحاطت بي أحطت بها
verses.ajuz: قلبا متى أسر في مصباحه يقد
حتى بقيت كأني لست من أدد
حتى توهمت أني من بني أسد !
على سواكم فلم تهشش إلى أحد

verses.sadr: ومدح من ليس أهل المدح أحسبه
verses.ajuz: عضوا تفضل من قلبي ومن كبدي

verses.sadr: ما إن ترى غير منشور على قدم

verses.ajuz: في المنع إن عن لي منع أو الصفد

verses.sadr: يحصن بها سندي أو يمتنع عضدي
verses.ajuz: أو يدن لي أمدي أو يعتدل أودي

verses.sadr: إن كنت في المطل ذا صبر وذا جلد
verses.ajuz: فلست في الذم ذا صبر وذا جلد !

verses.sadr: فقل وراءك في سحق وفي بعد

translationEn: I turned my affairs over in beginning and end and trained my condition in tyranny and moderation. Every time I opened my mouth it was muzzled, and every time I stretched out my hand it was pushed back! My only fault is the brilliant poems I have sent east and west, and the bonds I have tightened. A spreading fame by which poetry travels, refined by a thought that ranges as the spirit ranges through the body. Hours of gratitude nourished by his lasting existence — they are of longer life than eternity itself! When darkness enveloped it and surrounded me, I surrounded it — a heart that whenever it travels in its own lamp, it ignites. I have worn out my time and my likenesses for you and through you, until I am left as if I am not of the tribe of Adad. Then you cast aside my kinship and my bonds, until I imagined myself of the Banu Asad! Then I turned to myself to wean it onto someone other than you, but it was not inclined toward anyone. And praising one who does not deserve praise I count as an excess of my heart and my liver. People whose hopes' eyes alight upon them return kohl-lined with the wandering eye-disease of trachoma! The appearance of poetry is more hateful to their eyes than the appearance of a lion. You see nothing among the articulate but one spread out on standing feet and one folded up on envy. Speak a decisive word whose judgment passes in withholding — if withholding faces me — or in fetters. By which my support is fortified or my arm is made impregnable, or my hope draws near, or my crookedness is made straight. Or that which has long led through its difficulty from affairs to its smooth, new path. If you are patient and enduring in delay, I am not patient or enduring in blame! So say: behind you — in crushing distance and remoteness! For I am fully deserving in your regard of crushing and remoteness.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: my mouth was muzzled — from كعم: to muzzle a camel's mouth to prevent it from biting
brilliant poems, shining verse — from غَرَّة: a white blaze on a forehead; brilliance
I wore out, I ground down with exhausting service — from Hadramawt as a byword for distant toil
to wean it, to make it adopt another — from ظأر: a she-camel that nurses another's young
the wandering inflammation of trachoma — a painful eye disease; eyes filled with pain not reward
fetters, chains, bonds — used metaphorically for a constraining commitment
prolonged delay in fulfilling a promise, procrastination in paying a debt

context: A long complaint-poem (shakwa) in which Abu Tammam laments his wasted service to ungrateful patrons, his alienation from his own tribe, and his inability to transfer his poetic loyalty elsewhere. The poem moves from personal anguish to a proud declaration of poetic power and ends with a formal severing of ties — a majestic example of the classical Arabic fakhr-hija hybrid.

titleEn: I Was Told Utba Howls to Provoke Me

verses.sadr: ما كنت أحسب أن الدهر يمهلني
verses.ajuz: حتى أرى أحدا يهجوه لا أحد !
لو كان في أسد لم يفرس الأسد

verses.sadr: لو اعتدى أعوج يعدو به المرطى
لو كان يكره أن تبدو فضيحته
verses.ajuz: ما كان أكثر ما في شعره العمد

verses.sadr: فإن سمعت له نعت القنا عبثا
verses.ajuz: فقد أراد قنا ليست لها عقد !
من المني بحور كيف لا يلد ؟

verses.sadr: لو أن عشر الذي أمسى وظل به

verses.ajuz: إلا بأن يجدوا بعض الذي يجد !
أتأرت قلت له إني أنا الرمد

verses.sadr: أنا الحسام أنا الموت الزؤام أنا ال

translationEn: I was told that Utba howls so that I might exchange insults with him — God is great, how has the worn-out coin come to play the lion! I never thought that time would spare me long enough to see someone being satirized by no one! Sufficient affliction has Utba within himself — had it been in a lion, the lion would not have pounced. Had the noble horse Awaj run carrying a swift runner, or had al-Lahiq run, they would have wished they were tent pegs! Had he disliked his disgrace being exposed, his poetry would not contain so many deliberate faults. If you hear him describing spears in vain, he meant spears that have no joints! I marvel at one in whose saddlebag are seas of semen — how does he not give birth? Had a tenth of what he carries and what afflicts the world through him been known, the world would have been corrupted. They invoke no fervent curse against enemies except that they should find some of what he finds! And a questioner says: why do they close their eyes to you when you provoke them? I tell him: I am the eye-disease trachoma itself. I am the sharp sword, I am the voracious death, I am the blazing fire, I am the lion who is the slave!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: he howls — from عوى: the howl of a dog or wolf, used to demean the opponent
worn-out, clipped, defective coin — used metaphorically for a base, valueless person
acted like a lion, pretended to lion-like ferocity — from أسد: lion
intentional faults, deliberate errors — as opposed to accidental mistakes in poetry
spears with no joints — a metaphor for flaccid, knotless, structureless poetry (or the body)
trachoma, eye inflammation — Abu Tammam claims to be the disease that afflicts the eye of any opponent
the lion — from ضرغم: a ferocious, powerful lion

context: A fierce poetic duel with a rival poet named Utba, mocking his poetic ineptitude, personal debasement, and the absurdity of his attacking Abu Tammam. The poem culminates in one of Abu Tammam's greatest self-glorification passages — a serial self-identification with sword, death, fire, and the lion — a classic fakhr conclusion to a hija poem.

titleEn: Now That the Watering Trough Is Exposed

verses.ajuz: دع ذا أتعرف درب عبد الواحد ؟ !

translationEn: Now that the watering trough for travelers has come about, and tomorrow and today it stands exposed to every scout — the calamities have sent him a greeting in which lies the reform of the corrupt young man? Today instead of sorrow there is joy, and today instead of an envious man there is a compassionate one. He made writing a cover for his for-hire work and made excuses, then came with a cold pretext. And when he busied himself with talk, say to him: leave that — do you know the road of Abd al-Wahid?

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the watering trough of the one who arrives — a metaphor for a resource now open to all
exposed to the scout, open to the seeker — something once hidden now visible
calamities, events, misfortunes — the vicissitudes of time
the corrupt youth, the depraved young man
made excuses, feigned illness as a pretext
a cold excuse — a lame, unconvincing pretext (bārid: cold/insipid/feeble)

context: A short satirical poem mocking a certain youth who hides depraved activities behind a pretense of clerical or scribal work. The final verse with its reference to 'the road of Abd al-Wahid' is likely a topical allusion to a known disreputable location in Baghdad, giving the insult a concrete, street-level specificity.

titleEn: Ayyash, O Master of Miserliness
translationEn: Ayyash, O master of miserliness and stinginess, and offspring of constriction and vexation! Cold kills and convulsive shivering — even without the severity of chilling you have perfected. A baseness you profess in its sweet and its bitter, as if it were part of the declaration of monotheism! My speech will surely blacken the prominence of your face many times over what your face blackened my poem's face. And my chest's opening will expose you in every gathering, just as your two hands exposed my arriving verses. Analogy had not informed me of falsehood about you — but I fell into imitation! I cast in my greed a hand that took it out of the obedience of guidance and correction. I hoped for your generosity as you hope for high glory — by remembering the colocynth and the weed. And I forgot your evil deeds as you forget your own foundations in the district of al-Bushrūd!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: giving sparingly, doling out in drips — extreme miserliness in dispensing what one has
convulsive shivering, tetanic spasm from cold — a severe physical affliction
the height/surface of your face — يفاع: a high peak or prominent surface
the colocynth plant — a bitter desert plant; a metaphor for futile hope
a type of bitter desert weed — associated with barren, useless terrain
imitation, following precedent without judgment — the poet blames his own credulity
the district of al-Bushrūd — a geographical reference used to anchor the insult in specificity

context: A sustained hija against Ayyash (also targeted in index 364/369) for his legendary miserliness. The poet declares that Ayyash practices stinginess with religious devotion as if it were a creed, and vows to blacken his reputation as thoroughly as Ayyash's stinginess blackened the poet's poems. The self-critical note — admitting he fell into imitation in hoping for generosity — adds rare ironic depth.

titleEn: Ayyash, Strenuous Effort Has Come to You

verses.sadr: ما اللؤم لؤما إن عداك لبانه
سمجت بك الدنيا فما لك حامد

translationEn: Ayyash, strenuous effort has been presented to you and stagnant affliction has settled in your courtyard. Baseness would not be baseness had it bypassed your milk and you bypassed it — but Luhay'a is your father! He has grown accustomed to satire, so his honor cares not whether a thousand satirized him or one. The world has grown ugly by you — so you have no one praising you; and you have grown ugly by the world — so you have no one envying you! I shall discipline you so that no poet after this shall find you a target — given your corrupt origins. And I shall publicly brandish against you rare, enduring horrors that are taken for swords but are poems. In them are collars for the necks of the base that will endure, and for the necks of the noble — necklaces! They shall stick to the surface of your nape and the brand of a disgrace that Khalid, by Abu Uyayna, did not suffer. And God knows that a poem that has satire mixed with praise in your regard is a failed poem. So wear the garments of scandals you wove in arrogance — and your cold brother shall be their weft.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: was presented to you as a bride — from زفّ: the bridal procession; here ironic
stagnant affliction — a misfortune that settles and does not pass
rare, enduring wild things — originally wild animals; applied to remarkable, lasting poems
collars, fetters — iron rings for the necks of the base; metaphor for poems that bind in shame
necklaces, ornaments — the same poems become honor-ornaments for the noble
the brand of disgrace — a mark of shame burned permanently into reputation
the garments of scandals — metaphor: wearing one's own shameful deeds as clothing

context: A second major hija against Ayyash, building on the previous poem. Notable for the celebrated verse about poems that are 'collars for the necks of the base and necklaces for the noble' — one of Abu Tammam's most quoted lines on the dual nature of his satirical and panegyric poetry. The poem threatens lasting poetic punishment.

titleEn: I Came to Yahya, Once My Friend
translationEn: I came to Yahya when he was my friend and companion. I said: what has made this young man recoil and turn away? He shrank back from me as a prisoner shrinks back on seeing the executioner's block. He said to me: one given to joking turns jest into seriousness. Such is the noble man when he wants to have his midday meal!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: shrank back, recoiled in disgust or fear — from اشمئزاز: aversion, recoiling
turned away, averted — from صدود: turning the face away
the executioner's block or chopping board — the sight that makes a prisoner faint
joking, banter, humor
jest, light talk — contrasted with جدّ: seriousness
to have midday dinner — the ironic punchline: Yahya's 'seriousness' is just hunger-driven stinginess

context: A short mujathathth-meter piece mocking a friend named Yahya who recoiled from Abu Tammam as if from an executioner's block — the punchline being that this grand show of seriousness was simply Yahya wanting to eat his lunch alone without the poet present. A gem of comedic precision in five short verses.

titleEn: Do You String Together False and Senseless Words?

verses.ajuz: وأنت أنزر من لا شيء في العدد ؟

verses.sadr: أشرجت قلبك من بغضي على حرق
verses.ajuz: أضر من حرقات الهجر في الجسد

verses.sadr: أنحفت جسمك حتى لو هممت بأن
verses.ajuz: ألهوا بصفعك يوما لم تجدك يدي !

verses.sadr: لا تنتسب قد حويت الفخر مجتمعا
verses.ajuz: والذكر إذ صرت منسوبا إلى حسدي

verses.sadr: أطلت روعك حتى صرت لي غرضا
verses.ajuz: قد يقدم العير من ذعر على الأسد !

translationEn: Is it you who strings together false and senseless words, when you are more scarce than nothing in all counting? You sealed your heart with hatred of me upon a burning more harmful than the burning of separation in the body. You have emaciated yourself until, were I inclined to amuse myself by slapping you one day, my hand would not find you! Do not claim lineage — you have gathered all pride together, and all fame, since you became attributed to my envy. You have prolonged your fear of me until you have become a target for me — an ass sometimes advances from terror upon a lion!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: falsehood and senselessness — زور: lie; فند: foolish, worthless speech
more scarce, more meager — from نزر: scarceness, worthlessness in small quantity
you sealed up, you buttoned closed — from شرج: to tie or lace shut
you have become emaciated, wasted away — from نحف: extreme thinness
a target, a butt for arrows — the one who makes himself conspicuously available to attack
the donkey, the ass — proverbially timid; used here for the terrified opponent

context: A brief but sharply compressed hija against a minor opponent who dared to compose false satire against Abu Tammam. The poet ridicules his physical emaciation, intellectual worthlessness, and the irony that his envy of Abu Tammam is the only fame he will ever achieve. The final proverb about the ass charging the lion is a classical image of desperation.

titleEn: You Are Nothing but the Circulating Proverb

verses.sadr: يا ساحر اللفظ على أن من

verses.ajuz: قد ذل من ليس له ناصر !

translationEn: You are nothing but the circulating proverb — known to the ignorant and the expert alike. A fruit whose orchard was ruined and neglected, so those coming and going helped themselves to it. O sorcerer of the word — and yet the one who bewitched you with the word is the true sorcerer! A wolf of the wasteland whose tricks are armored meets a gazelle whose tricks are unshielded. Whenever I remember you, I remember: humiliated is one who has no supporter!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the circulating proverb — an adage everyone knows; to become a proverb is to become a byword for something
one arriving and one departing — meaning everyone, all passersby
sorcerer of the word — one who bewitches with eloquent language; here ironic
armored, wearing a coat of mail — metaphor for one whose cunning is protected
bare, unarmored — the opposite of dāriʿ; vulnerable, exposed
humiliated is one who has no helper — a proverbial saying used as the poem's devastating close

context: A concise five-verse hija using a series of brilliant metaphors: the circulating proverb, the abandoned orchard, the armored wolf vs. the bare gazelle. The poem concludes with a proverbial formula that works as both a general truth and a specific verdict: the opponent's complete lack of support leaves him utterly defenseless against Abu Tammam's wit.

titleEn: She Departed and Tears Replaced Pearls
translationEn: She departed and my tears exchanged for pearls, while the sorrows and the brooding belong to another. Had you unveiled her veil, the news of her departure would have reached me from you before it did. Let me summarize her ugliness for you: the face of Ibn Aʿmash is compared to her — a moon. And let me clarify her shabbiness: the speech of Ibn Aʿmash compared to her is dusky charm!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: pearls — here replacing tears; crying pearls instead of water drops is a metaphor for precious mourning
her veil — the face covering worn by women
the news of departure, the proof of separation
one who summarizes, condenses — the rhetorical mode of إجمال
ugliness, repulsiveness, unpleasantness of appearance
shabbiness, poorness, weakness — of speech or quality
dusky, dark-complexioned — used in classical Arabic poetry as a term of beauty

context: A brief satirical love poem with an unusual twist: the poet seems to mock the departing beloved (or a rival) by saying her ugliness is such that even Ibn Aʿmash — presumably a byword for plainness — appears handsome and eloquent by comparison. A sharp comic subversion of the ghazal genre.

titleAr: أبو تماملا تعجلن عليك بعد نهار
titleEn: Abu Tammam, Do Not Hurry — Tomorrow Comes

verses.sadr: أبو تماملا تعجلن عليك بعد نهار
شعر مقيل السم فيه ولم يقع

verses.ajuz: أن لم يكن لك والد عطار

translationEn: Abu Tammam — do not rush; after the day there will be another day, and tomorrow the poems are being prepared for you. Leaving a base man without tearing his honor is a deficiency and a shame for a noble man. You have plunged headlong into the sea of ignorance wandering — and ignorance in some failings is a cure! So drink, for you will soon know it is a cup whose intoxication strikes the honor. The choicest speech came to you at dawn with scattered helpers of the qasida — its fatalities are virgins never touched before. It is rock that deprives you of both your ears until you think that the call to prayer is whispering. Poetry in whose midst the poison is poured — no antidote has touched it and no fingernails have scratched it. Brilliant poems — whenever you wish, let them be my witnesses that your father was not a perfumer. Do not think I have lightened my load for a slip — and indeed the featherlight blunder in your case is dignity. Two things that cannot be trusted in their sharpness: I — when my fury burns — and fire.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: headlong, heedlessly, rashly — from سدر: to go forward without caution
a remedy, a medicinal cure — ignorance as a cure: the bliss of not knowing
a cup, a drinking vessel — here: a cup of satirical verse whose intoxication is shame
its fatalities are virgins — the devastating poems are fresh, unprecedented, never before deployed
the resting place of poison — poetry that holds venom within it
fingernails — here used in the phrase 'no fingernails have scratched it': no one has neutralized it
a slip, a blunder, a lapse — here used to suggest Abu Tammam's restraint is itself a loaded pause

context: A poem in which Abu Tammam addresses himself in the third person — 'O Abu Tammam, do not rush' — as a rhetorical device to build anticipation for the devastating attack to come. The closing couplet equating the poet's burning anger with fire itself is one of his most celebrated self-glorifying lines.

titleEn: I Am Patient Despite What Has Befallen Me

verses.sadr: ما زال غل الذم ثاني عطفه
من بعد ما نزهت في سوآته

translationEn: I am patient despite what has befallen me, and fully entitled to endurance without any display of fine forbearance. How rare and precious it is that Ayyash is now absent from me — buried somewhere other than in the grave of reason and goodness! The hands of death freed my poems from the chain of his censure — and their lion still snarls over him. The chain of blame never ceased turning his neck until death came to him while he was its prisoner. After my good poems — whose seas are oceans — had been strolled through his vices. I remained — were it not that in Tayy I am a known landmark — and the people would have said: you are Jarir himself. O strange lesson of God — from whose lineage they grew up to become the monkey and the pig! Had the draped camel a single feather, no creature would have doubted it would fly. And I see Nakir turning away from you and Munkar, thinking that you yourself are Munkar and Nakir. The grave that took you in has been groaning until we thought it was the one buried!

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: feigned endurance, performing patience — showing toughness without inward feeling
a chain, a fetter, shackles — the chain of blame that bound his neck in life
the lion — here Abu Tammam's satirical poetry personified as a lion
Jarir ibn Atiyya — the greatest hija poet of the Umayyad era; the highest standard of satire
Nakir and Munkar — the two angels who question the dead in the grave; used for comic effect
the draped, blanketed — a camel covered with a blanket; metaphor for pretense of grandeur
writhed in pain, groaned in agony — the grave itself groans under Ayyash's presence

context: A mock-elegy on the death of Ayyash, the same figure satirized in the preceding poems. Abu Tammam combines relief at his enemy's death with continued posthumous mockery — even the grave groans under Ayyash's presence, and the angels of the grave mistake him for the agents of punishment themselves. A brilliantly savage piece blending ritha and hija.

titleEn: May Your Faded Ruins Not Be Watered
translationEn: May your faded ruins not be watered, and may your stumbling disaster never end! No grave that receives your burial niche contains but a scarce portion of filth — it is not pure either. No soil ever accepted your polytheism one day nor your unbelief — except that the earth itself is an unbeliever. You charged against miserliness with what vexed it and exhausted it — your losing charge! You kept the eye of baseness awake since your garments were folded over you on the plain of resurrection. Who shall launch poetry's raids after you or the likes of him circulating? The world had almost cured my anguish over you — but you took refuge in the afterlife!
